Book I
_boy and Girl_
chapter I
outside Dorlcote Mill
a wide plain, where the broaden Floss hurry on between its green
bank to the sea, and the love tide, rush to meet it, check its
passage with an impetuous embrace. on this mighty tide the black
ship--laden with the fresh-scented fir-plank, with rounded sack of
oil-bear seed, or with the dark glitter of coal--be bear along to
the town of St. Ogg's, which show its age, flute red roof and the
broad gable of its wharf between the low woode hill and the
river-brink, tinge the water with a soft purple hue under the
transient glance of this February sun. far away on each hand stretch
the rich pasture, and the patch of dark earth make ready for the
seed of broad-leave green crop, or touch already with the tint of
the tender-bladed autumn-sow corn. there be a remnant still of last
year's golden cluster of beehive-rick rise at interval beyond the
hedgerow; and everywhere the hedgerow be stud with tree; the
distant ship seem to be lift their mast and stretch their
red-brown sail close among the branch of the spread ash. just by
the red-roof town the tributary Ripple flow with a lively current
into the Floss. how lovely the little river be, with its dark change
wavelet! it seem to I like a live companion while I wander along
the bank, and listen to its low, placid voice, as to the voice of one
who be deaf and loving. I remember those large dip willow. I
remember the stone bridge.
and this be Dorlcote Mill. I must stand a minute or two here on the
bridge and look at it, though the cloud be threaten, and it be
far on in the afternoon. even in this leafless time of depart
February it be pleasant to look at,--perhaps the chill, damp season
add a charm to the trimly kept, comfortable dwelling-house, as old as
the elm and chestnut that shelter it from the northern blast. the
stream be brimful now, and lie high in this little withy plantation,
and half drown the grassy fringe of the croft in front of the house.
as I look at the full stream, the vivid grass, the delicate
bright-green powder soften the outline of the great trunk and
branch that gleam from under the bare purple bough, I be in love
with moistness, and envy the white duck that be dip their head
far into the water here among the withe, unmindful of the awkward
appearance they make in the dry world above.
the rush of the water and the booming of the mill bring a dreamy
deafness, which seem to heighten the peacefulness of the scene. they
be like a great curtain of sound, shut one out from the world
beyond. and now there be the thunder of the huge cover wagon come
home with sack of grain. that honest wagoner be think of his
dinner, get sadly dry in the oven at this late hour; but he will
not touch it till he have feed his horses,--the strong, submissive,
meek-eyed beast, who, I fancy, be look mild reproach at he from
between their blinker, that he should crack his whip at they in that
awful manner as if they need that hint! see how they stretch their
shoulder up the slope toward the bridge, with all the more energy
because they be so near home. look at their grand shaggy foot that
seem to grasp the firm earth, at the patient strength of their neck,
bow under the heavy collar, at the mighty muscle of their
struggle haunch! I should like well to hear they neigh over their
hardly earn feed of corn, and see they, with their moist neck free
from the harness, dip their eager nostril into the muddy pond.
now they be on the bridge, and down they go again at a swifter pace,
and the arch of the cover wagon disappear at the turning behind the
tree.
now I can turn my eye toward the mill again, and watch the unreste
wheel send out its diamond jet of water. that little girl be
watch it too; she have be stand on just the same spot at the
edge of the water ever since I pause on the bridge. and that queer
white cur with the brown ear seem to be leap and bark in
ineffectual remonstrance with the wheel; perhaps he be jealous because
his playfellow in the beaver bonnet be so rapt in its movement. it be
time the little playfellow go in, I think; and there be a very
bright fire to tempt she: the red light shin out under the deepening
gray of the sky. it be time, too, for I to leave off rest my arm
on the cold stone of this bridge....
ah, my arm be really benumb. I have be press my elbow on the
arm of my chair, and dream that I be stand on the bridge in
front of Dorlcote Mill, as it look one February afternoon many year
ago. before I doze off, I be go to tell you what Mr. and Mrs.
Tulliver be talk about, as they sit by the bright fire in the
leave-hand parlor, on that very afternoon I have be dream of.
Chapter II
Mr. Tulliver, of Dorlcote Mill, declare his Resolution about Tom
"what I want, you know," say Mr. Tulliver,--"what I want be to give
Tom a good eddication; an eddication as'll be a bread to he. that be
what I be think of when I give notice for he to leave the academy
at Lady-day. I mean to put he to a downright good school at
Midsummer. the two year at th' academy 'ud ha' do well enough, if
Ihave mean to make a miller and farmer of he, for hebe have a fine
sight more schoolin' nor _i_ ever get. all the learnin' _my_ father
ever pay for be a bit o' birch at one end and the alphabet at th'
other. but I should like Tom to be a bit of a scholard, so as he might
be up to the trick o' these fellow as talk fine and write with a
flourish. it 'ud be a help to I wi' these lawsuit, and arbitration,
and thing. I wouldnot make a downright lawyer o' the lad,--I should
be sorry for he to be a raskill,--but a sort o' engineer, or a
surveyor, or an auctioneer and vallyer, like Riley, or one o' they
smartish business as be all profit and no outlay, only for a big
watch-chain and a high stool. theybe pretty nigh all one, and theybe
not far off be even wi' the law, _i_ believe; for Riley look
Lawyer Wakem I' the face as hard as one cat look another. _hebe_ none
frighten at he."
Mr. Tulliver be speak to his wife, a blond comely woman in a
fan-shape cap (I be afraid to think how long it be since fan-shape
cap be wear, they must be so near come in again. at that time,
when Mrs. Tulliver be nearly forty, they be new at St. Ogg's, and
consider sweet thing).
"well, Mr. Tulliver, you know well: _Ihave_ no objection. but havenot I
well kill a couple o' fowl, and have th' aunt and uncle to dinner
next week, so as you may hear what sister Glegg and sister Pullet have
get to say about it? therebe a couple o' fowl _want_ kill!"
"you may kill every fowl I' the yard if you like, Bessy; but I shall
ask neither aunt nor uncle what Ibe to do wi' my own lad," say Mr.
Tulliver, defiantly.
"dear heart!" say Mrs. Tulliver, shock at this sanguinary rhetoric,
"how can you talk so, Mr. Tulliver? but itbe your way to speak
disrespectful o' my family; and sister Glegg throw all the blame
upo'me, though Ibe sure Ibe as innocent as the babe unborn. for
nobodybe ever hear I say as it benot lucky for my child to have
aunt and uncle as can live independent. Howiver, if Tombe to go to a
new school, I should like he to go where I can wash he and mend he;
else he might as well have calico as linen, for theywould be one as
yallow as th' other before theyhave be wash half-a-dozen time. and
then, when the box be go back'ard and forrard, I could send the lad
a cake, or a pork-pie, or an apple; for he can do with an extry bit,
bless he! whether they stint he at the meal or no. my child can
eat as much victual as most, thank God!"
"well, well, we willnot send he out o' reach o' the carrier's cart, if
other thing fit in," say Mr. Tulliver. "but you mustnot put a spoke
I' the wheel about the washin,' if we cannot get a school near enough.
thatbe the fault I have to find wi' you, Bessy; if you see a stick I'
the road, yoube allay thinkin' you cannot step over it. youwould want I
not to hire a good wagoner, 'cause hewould got a mole on his face."
"dear heart!" say Mrs. Tulliver, in mild surprise, "when do I iver
make objection to a man because hewould get a mole on his face? Ibe sure
Ibe rether fond o' the mole; for my brother, as be dead an' gone, have
a mole on his brow. but I cannot remember your iver offering to hire a
wagoner with a mole, Mr. Tulliver. there be John Gibbs havenot a mole
on his face no more nor you have, an' I be all for have you hire
_he_; an' so you do hire he, an' if he havenot die o' th'
inflammation, as we pay Dr. Turnbull for attend he, hewould very
like ha' be drivin' the wagon now. he might have a mole somewhere
out o' sight, but how be I to know that, Mr. Tulliver?"
"no, no, Bessy; I donot mean justly the mole; I mean it to stand for
summat else; but niver mind--itbe puzzle work, talk be. what Ibe
think on, be how to find the right sort o' school to send Tom to,
for I might be ta'en in again, as Ihave be wi' th' academy. Iwill have
nothing to do wi' a 'cademy again: whativer school I send Tom to, it
sha'n't be a 'cademy; it shall be a place where the lad spend their
time I' summat else besides black the family's shoe, and get
up the potato. itbe an uncommon puzzle thing to know what school
to pick."
Mr. Tulliver pause a minute or two, and dive with both hand into
his breech pocket as if he hop to find some suggestion there.
apparently he be not disappointed, for he presently say, "I know
what Iwill do: Iwill talk it over wi' Riley; hebe come to-morrow, t'
arbitrate about the dam."
"well, Mr. Tulliver, Ihave put the sheet out for the good bed, and
Kezia's get them hang at the fire. they benot the good sheet, but
theybe good enough for anybody to sleep in, be he who he will; for as
for they good Holland sheet, I should repent buy them, only theywill
do to lay we out in. an' if you be to die to-morrow, Mr. Tulliver,
theybe mangle beautiful, an' all ready, an' smell o' lavender as it
'ud be a pleasure to lay them out; an' they lie at the left-hand corner
o' the big oak linen-chest at the back: not as I should trust anybody
to look them out but myself."
as Mrs. Tulliver utter the last sentence, she draw a bright bunch of
key from her pocket, and single out one, rub her thumb and
finger up and down it with a placid smile while she look at the
clear fire. if Mr. Tulliver have be a susceptible man in his conjugal
relation, he might have suppose that she draw out the key to aid her
imagination in anticipate the moment when he would be in a state to
justify the production of the good Holland sheet. happily he be not
so; he be only susceptible in respect of his right to water-power;
moreover, he have the marital habit of not listen very closely, and
since his mention of Mr. Riley, have be apparently occupy in a
tactile examination of his woollen stocking.
"I think Ihave hit it, Bessy," be his first remark after a short
silence. "Riley's as likely a man as any to know o' some school; hebe
have school himself, an' go about to all sort o' place,
arbitratin' and vallyin' and that. and we shall have time to talk it
over to-morrow night when the business be do. I want Tom to be such
a sort o' man as Riley, you know,--as can talk pretty nigh as well as
if it be all write out for he, and know a good lot o' word as
donot mean much, so as you cannot lay hold of them I' law; and a good
solid knowledge o' business too."
"well," say Mrs. Tulliver, "so far as talk proper, and know
everything, and walk with a bend in his back, and set his hair
up, I shouldnot mind the lad be bring up to that. but they
fine-talk man from the big town mostly wear the false
shirt-front; they wear a frill till itbe all a mess, and then hide it
with a bib; I know Riley do. and then, if Tombe to go and live at
Mudport, like Riley, hewill have a house with a kitchen hardly big
enough to turn in, an' niver get a fresh egg for his breakfast, an'
sleep up three pair o' stairs,--or four, for what I know,--and be
burn to death before he can get down."
"no, no," say Mr. Tulliver, "Ihave no thought of his go to
Mudport: I mean he to set up his office at St. Ogg's, close by we,
an' live at home. but," continue Mr. Tulliver after a pause, "what
Ibe a bit afraid on be, as Tom havenot get the right sort o' brain for
a smart fellow. I doubt hebe a bit slowish. he take after your
family, Bessy."
"yes, that he do," say Mrs. Tulliver, accept the last
proposition entirely on its own merit; "hebe wonderful for like a
deal o' salt in his broth. that be my brother's way, and my father's
before he."
"it seem a bit a pity, though," say Mr. Tulliver, "as the lad should
take after the mother's side instead o' the little wench. thatbe the
bad on't wi' crossing o' breed: you can never justly calkilate
whatwill come on't. the little un take after my side, now: shebe twice
as 'cute as Tom. too 'cute for a woman, Ibe afraid," continue Mr.
Tulliver, turn his head dubiously first on one side and then on the
other. "itbe no mischief much while shebe a little un; but an
over-'cute woman's no well nor a long-tail sheep,--she'll fetch
none the big price for that."
"yes, it _be_ a mischief while shebe a little un, Mr. Tulliver, for it
run to naughtiness. how to keep she in a clean pinafore two hour
together pass my cunning. an' now you put I I' mind," continue
Mrs. Tulliver, rise and go to the window, "I donot know where she
be now, an' itbe pretty nigh tea-time. ah, I think so,--wanderin' up
an' down by the water, like a wild thing: shewill tumble in some day."
Mrs. Tulliver rap the window sharply, beckon, and shake she
head,--a process which she repeat more than once before she return
to her chair.
"you talk o' 'cuteness, Mr. Tulliver," she observe as she sit down,
"but Ibe sure the child's half an idiot I' some thing; for if I send
her upstairs to fetch anything, she forget what shebe go for, an'
perhaps 'ull sit down on the floor I' the sunshine an' plait her hair
an' sing to herself like a Bedlam creatur', all the while Ibe wait
for her downstair. that niver run I' my family, thank God! no more
nor a brown skin as make she look like a mulatter. I donot like to
fly I' the face o' providence, but it seem hard as I should have but
one gell, an' her so comical."
"Pooh, nonsense!" say Mr. Tulliver; "shebe a straight, black-eye
wench as anybody need wish to see. I donot know I' what shebe behind
other folk's child; and she can read almost as well as the
parson."
"but her hair willnot curl all I can do with it, and shebe so franzy
about have it put I' paper, and Ihave such work as never be to make
her stand and have it pinch with th' iron."
"cut it off--cut it off short," say the father, rashly.
"how can you talk so, Mr. Tulliver? shebe too big a gell--go nine,
and tall of her age--to have her hair cut short; an' therebe her
cousin Lucy's get a row o' curl round her head, an' not a hair out o'
place. it seem hard as my sister Deane should have that pretty child;
Ibe sure Lucy take more after I nor my own child do. Maggie,
Maggie," continue the mother, in a tone of half-coax fretfulness,
as this small mistake of nature enter the room, "wherebe the use o'
my tell you to keep away from the water? youwill tumble in and be
drownde some day, an' then youwill be sorry you donot do as mother
tell you."
Maggie's hair, as she throw off her bonnet, painfully confirm she
mother's accusation. Mrs. Tulliver, desire her daughter to have a
curl crop, "like other folk's child," have have it cut too short
in front to be push behind the ear; and as it be usually straight
an hour after it have be take out of paper, Maggie be incessantly
toss her head to keep the dark, heavy lock out of her gleaming
black eyes,--an action which give she very much the air of a small
Shetland pony.
"oh, dear, oh, dear, Maggie, what be you thinkin'of, to throw your
bonnet down there? take it upstairs, therebe a good gell, an' let your
hair be brush, an' put your other pinafore on, an' change your
shoe, do, for shame; an' come an' go on with your patchwork, like a
little lady."
"oh, mother," say Maggie, in a vehemently cross tone, "I donot _want_
to do my patchwork."
"what! not your pretty patchwork, to make a counterpane for your aunt
Glegg?"
"itbe foolish work," say Maggie, with a toss of her mane,--"tearing
thing to piece to sew them together again. and I donot want to do
anything for my aunt Glegg. I donot like she."
Exit Maggie, drag her bonnet by the string, while Mr. Tulliver
laugh audibly.
"I wonder at you, as youwill laugh at she, Mr. Tulliver," say the
mother, with feeble fretfulness in her tone. "you encourage she I'
naughtiness. an' her aunt will have it as itbe I spoil she."
Mrs. Tulliver be what be call a good-temper person,--never cry,
when she be a baby, on any slighter ground than hunger and pin; and
from the cradle upward have be healthy, fair, plump, and dull-witted;
in short, the flower of her family for beauty and amiability. but milk
and mildness be not the good thing for keeping, and when they turn
only a little sour, they may disagree with young stomach seriously. I
have often wonder whether those early Madonnas of Raphael, with the
blond face and somewhat stupid expression, keep their placidity
undisturbed when their strong-limbe, strong-willed boy get a little
too old to do without clothing. I think they must have be give to
feeble remonstrance, get more and more peevish as it become more
and more ineffectual.
Chapter III
Mr. Riley give his advice concern a School for Tom
the gentleman in the ample white cravat and shirt-frill, take his
brandy-and-water so pleasantly with his good friend Tulliver, be Mr.
Riley, a gentleman with a waxen complexion and fat hand, rather
highly educate for an auctioneer and appraiser, but large-hearted
enough to show a great deal of _bonhomie_ toward simple country
acquaintance of hospitable habit. Mr. Riley speak of such
acquaintance kindly as "people of the old school."
the conversation have come to a pause. Mr. Tulliver, not without a
particular reason, have abstain from a seventh recital of the cool
retort by which Riley have show himself too many for Dix, and how
Wakem have have his comb cut for once in his life, now the business of
the dam have be settle by arbitration, and how there never would
have be any dispute at all about the height of water if everybody
be what they should be, and Old Harry havenot make the lawyer.
Mr. Tulliver be, on the whole, a man of safe traditional opinion;
but on one or two point he have trust to his unassisted intellect,
and have arrive at several questionable conclusion; amongst the rest,
that rat, weevil, and lawyer be create by Old Harry. unhappily
he have no one to tell he that this be rampant Manichaeism, else he
might have see his error. but to-day it be clear that the good
principle be triumphant: this affair of the water-power have be a
tangle business somehow, for all it seem--look at it one way--as
plain as water's water; but, big a puzzle as it be, it havenot get the
well of Riley. Mr. Tulliver take his brandy-and-water a little
strong than usual, and, for a man who might be suppose to have a
few hundred lie idle at his banker's, be rather incautiously open
in express his high estimate of his friend's business talent.
but the dam be a subject of conversation that would keep; it could
always be take up again at the same point, and exactly in the same
condition; and there be another subject, as you know, on which Mr.
Tulliver be in press want of Mr. Riley's advice. this be his
particular reason for remain silent for a short space after his
last draught, and rub his knee in a meditative manner. he be not
a man to make an abrupt transition. this be a puzzling world, as he
often say, and if you drive your wagon in a hurry, you may light on
an awkward corner. Mr. Riley, meanwhile, be not impatient. why should
he be? even Hotspur, one would think, must have be patient in his
slipper on a warm hearth, take copious snuff, and sip
gratuitous brandy-and-water.
"therebe a thing Ihave get I' my head," say Mr. Tulliver at last, in
rather a low tone than usual, as he turn his head and look
steadfastly at his companion.
"ah!" say Mr. Riley, in a tone of mild interest. he be a man with
heavy waxen eyelid and high-arched eyebrow, look exactly the same
under all circumstance. this immovability of face, and the habit of
take a pinch of snuff before he give an answer, make he trebly
oracular to Mr. Tulliver.
"itbe a very particular thing," he go on; "itbe about my boy Tom."
at the sound of this name, Maggie, who be seat on a low stool close
by the fire, with a large book open on her lap, shake her heavy hair
back and look up eagerly. there be few sound that rouse Maggie
when she be dream over her book, but Tom's name serve as well as
the shrill whistle; in an instant she be on the watch, with
gleam eye, like a skye terri suspecting mischief, or at all
event determine to fly at any one who threaten it toward Tom.
"you see, I want to put he to a new school at Midsummer," say Mr.
Tulliver; "hebe comin' away from the 'cademy at Lady-day, an' I shall
let he run loose for a quarter; but after that I want to send he to
a downright good school, where theywill make a scholard of he."
"well," say Mr. Riley, "therebe no great advantage you can give he
than a good education. not," he add, with polite significance,--"not
that a man cannot be an excellent miller and farmer, and a shrewd,
sensible fellow into the bargain, without much help from the
schoolmaster."
"I believe you," say Mr. Tulliver, wink, and turn his head on
one side; "but thatbe where it be. I donot _mean_ Tom to be a miller
and farmer. I see no fun I' that. why, if I make he a miller an'
farmer, hewould be expectin' to take to the mill an' the land, an'
a-hinting at I as it be time for I to lay by an' think o' my latter
end. Nay, nay, Ihave see enough o' that wi' son. Iwill never pull my
coat off before I go to bed. I shall give Tom an eddication an' put
he to a business, as he may make a nest for himself, an' not want to
push I out o' mine. pretty well if he get it when Ibe dead an' go.
I sha'n't be put off wi' spoon-meat afore Ihave lose my tooth."
this be evidently a point on which Mr. Tulliver feel strongly; and
the impetus which have give unusual rapidity and emphasis to his
speech show itself still unexhausted for some minute afterward in a
defiant motion of the head from side to side, and an occasional "nay,
nay," like a subside growl.
these angry symptom be keenly observe by Maggie, and cut she to
the quick. Tom, it appear, be suppose capable of turn his
father out of door, and of make the future in some way tragic by
his wickedness. this be not to be bear; and Maggie jump up from
her stool, forget all about her heavy book, which fall with a bang
within the fender, and go up between her father's knee, say, in a
half-cry, half-indignant voice,--
"Father, Tom wouldnot be naughty to you ever; I know he wouldnot."
Mrs. Tulliver be out of the room superintend a choice supper-dish,
and Mr. Tulliver's heart be touch; so Maggie be not scold about
the book. Mr. Riley quietly pick it up and look at it, while the
father laugh, with a certain tenderness in his hard-line face, and
pat his little girl on the back, and then hold her hand and keep
she between his knee.
"what! they mustnot say any harm o' Tom, eh?" say Mr. Tulliver,
look at Maggie with a twinkle eye. then, in a low voice,
turn to Mr. Riley, as though Maggie couldnot hear, "she understand
what onebe talk about so as never be. and you should hear she
read,--straight off, as if she know it all beforehand. and allay at
her book! but itbe bad--itbe bad," Mr. Tulliver add sadly, check
this blamable exultation. "a woman's no business wi' be so clever;
itwill turn to trouble, I doubt. but bless you!"--here the exultation
be clearly recover the mastery,--"she'll read the book and
understand them well nor half the folk as be grow up."
Maggie's cheek begin to flush with triumphant excitement. she think
Mr. Riley would have a respect for she now; it have be evident that
he think nothing of she before.
Mr. Riley be turn over the leaf of the book, and she could make
nothing of his face, with its high-arched eyebrow; but he presently
look at she, and said,--
"come, come and tell I something about this book; here be some
pictures,--i want to know what they mean."
Maggie, with deepening color, go without hesitation to Mr. Riley's
elbow and look over the book, eagerly seize one corner, and
toss back her mane, while she said,--
"oh, Iwill tell you what that mean. itbe a dreadful picture, benot it?
but I cannot help look at it. that old woman in the water's a
witch,--they've put she in to find out whether shebe a witch or no;
and if she swim shebe a witch, and if shebe drown--and kill, you
know--shebe innocent, and not a witch, but only a poor silly old
woman. but what good would it do she then, you know, when she be
drown? only, I suppose, shewould go to heaven, and God would make it up
to she. and this dreadful blacksmith with his arm akimbo,
laughing,--oh, benot he ugly?--I'll tell you what he be. hebe the
Devil _really_" (here Maggie's voice become loud and more emphatic),
"and not a right blacksmith; for the Devil take the shape of wicked
man, and walk about and set people do wicked thing, and hebe
oftener in the shape of a bad man than any other, because, you know,
if people see he be the Devil, and he roar at them, theywould run away,
and he couldnot make them do what he please."
Mr. Tulliver have listen to this exposition of Maggie's with
petrify wonder.
"why, what book be it the wench have get hold on?" he burst out at
last.
"the 'history of the Devil,' by Daniel Defoe,--not quite the right
book for a little girl," say Mr. Riley. "how come it among your
book, Mr. Tulliver?"
Maggie look hurt and discourage, while her father said,--
"why, itbe one o' the book I buy at Partridge's sale. they be all
bind alike,--it's a good binding, you see,--and I think theywould be
all good book. therebe Jeremy Taylor's 'Holy Living and Dying' among
them. I read in it often of a Sunday" (Mr. Tulliver feel somehow a
familiarity with that great writer, because his name be Jeremy); "and
therebe a lot more of 'em,--sermons mostly, I think,--but theyhave all
get the same cover, and I think they be all o' one sample, as you
may say. but it seem one mustnot judge by th' outside. this be a
puzzlin' world."
"well," say Mr. Riley, in an admonitory, patronize tone as he
pat Maggie on the head, "I advise you to put by the 'history of the
Devil,' and read some pretty book. have you no pretty book?"
"oh, yes," say Maggie, revive a little in the desire to vindicate
the variety of her reading. "I know the reading in this book benot
pretty; but I like the picture, and I make story to the picture
out of my own head, you know. but Ihave get 'AEsop's fable,' and a book
about Kangaroos and thing, and the 'Pilgrim's Progress.'"
"ah, a beautiful book," say Mr. Riley; "you cannot read a well."
"well, but therebe a great deal about the Devil in that," say Maggie,
triumphantly, "and Iwill show you the picture of he in his true shape,
as he fight with Christian."
Maggie run in an instant to the corner of the room, jump on a chair,
and reach down from the small bookcase a shabby old copy of Bunyan,
which open at once, without the least trouble of search, at the
picture she want.
"here he be," she say, run back to Mr. Riley, "and Tom color
he for I with his paint when he be at home last holidays,--the
body all black, you know, and the eye red, like fire, because hebe
all fire inside, and it shin out at his eye."
"go, go!" say Mr. Tulliver, peremptorily, begin to feel rather
uncomfortable at these free remark on the personal appearance of a
be powerful enough to create lawyer; "shut up the book, and letus
hear no more o' such talk. it be as I think--the child 'ull learn
more mischief nor good wi' the book. go, go and see after your
mother."
Maggie shut up the book at once, with a sense of disgrace, but not
be incline to see after her mother, she compromise the matter by
go into a dark corner behind her father's chair, and nursing her
doll, toward which she have an occasional fit of fondness in Tom's
absence, neglect its toilet, but lavish so many warm kiss on
it that the waxen cheek have a waste, unhealthy appearance.
"do you ever hear the like on't?" say Mr. Tulliver, as Maggie
retire. "itbe a pity but what shehave be the lad,--she'd ha' be a
match for the lawyer, _she_ would. itbe the wonderful'st thing"--here
he lower his voice--"a I pick the mother because she benot o'er
'cute--bein' a good-look woman too, an' come of a rare family for
manage; but I pick she from her sister o' purpose, 'cause she be
a bit weak like; for I benot agoin' to be tell the right o' thing
by my own fireside. but you see when a man's get brain himself,
therebe no knowing where theywill run to; an' a pleasant sort o' soft
woman may go on breed you stupid lad and 'cute wench, till itbe
like as if the world be turn topsy-turvy. itbe an uncommon puzzlin'
thing."
Mr. Riley's gravity give way, and he shake a little under the
application of his pinch of snuff before he said,--
"but your lad's not stupid, be he? I see he, when I be here last,
busy make fishing-tackle; he seem quite up to it."
"well, he benot not to say stupid,--he's get a notion o' thing out o'
door, an' a sort o' common sense, as hewould lay hold o' thing by the
right handle. but hebe slow with his tongue, you see, and he read but
poorly, and cannot abide the book, and spell all wrong, they tell I,
an' as shy as can be wi' stranger, an' you never hear he say 'cute
thing like the little wench. now, what I want be to send he to a
school where theywill make he a bit nimble with his tongue and his
pen, and make a smart chap of he. I want my son to be even wi' these
fellow as have get the start o' I with have well schooling. not
but what, if the world have be leave as God make it, I could ha' see
my way, and hold my own wi' the good of them; but thing have get so
twisted round and wrap up I' unreasonable word, as benot a bit
like them, as Ibe clean at fault, often an' often. everything wind
about so--the more straightforrad you be, the more yoube puzzle."
Mr. Tulliver take a draught, swallow it slowly, and shake his head
in a melancholy manner, conscious of exemplify the truth that a
perfectly sane intellect be hardly at home in this insane world.
"yoube quite in the right of it, Tulliver," observe Mr. Riley.
"well spend an extra hundred or two on your son's education, than
leave it he in your will. I know I should have try to do so by a
son of mine, if Ihave have one, though, God know, I havenot your ready
money to play with, Tulliver; and I have a houseful of daughter into
the bargain."
"I dare say, now, you know of a school as 'ud be just the thing for
Tom," say Mr. Tulliver, not divert from his purpose by any sympathy
with Mr. Riley's deficiency of ready cash.
Mr. Riley take a pinch of snuff, and keep Mr. Tulliver in suspense by
a silence that seem deliberative, before he said,--
"I know of a very fine chance for any one thatbe get the necessary
money and thatbe what you have, Tulliver. the fact be, I wouldnot
recommend any friend of mine to send a boy to a regular school, if he
could afford to do well. but if any one want his boy to get
superior instruction and training, where he would be the companion of
his master, and that master a first rate fellow, I know his man. I
wouldnot mention the chance to everybody, because I donot think
everybody would succeed in get it, if he be to try; but I
mention it to you, Tulliver, between ourselves."
the fixed inquire glance with which Mr. Tulliver have be watch
his friend's oracular face become quite eager.
"ay, now, letus hear," he say, adjust himself in his chair with
the complacency of a person who be think worthy of important
communication.
"hebe an Oxford man," say Mr. Riley, sententiously, shut his
mouth close, and look at Mr. Tulliver to observe the effect of this
stimulate information.
"what! a parson?" say Mr. Tulliver, rather doubtfully.
"yes, and an M.A. the bishop, I understand, think very highly of he:
why, it be the bishop who get he his present curacy."
"ah?" say Mr. Tulliver, to whom one thing be as wonderful as another
concern these unfamiliar phenomenon. "but what can he want wi' Tom,
then?"
"why, the fact be, hebe fond of teaching, and wish to keep up his
study, and a clergyman have but little opportunity for that in his
parochial duty. hebe willing to take one or two boy as pupil to
fill up his time profitably. the boy would be quite of the
family,--the fine thing in the world for they; under Stelling's eye
continually."
"but do you think theywould give the poor lad twice o' pudding?" say
Mrs. Tulliver, who be now in her place again. "hebe such a boy for
pudding as never be; an' a grow boy like that,--it's dreadful to
think o' their stintin' he."
"and what money 'ud he want?" say Mr. Tulliver, whose instinct tell
he that the service of this admirable M.A. would bear a high price.
"why, I know of a clergyman who ask a hundred and fifty with his
young pupil, and hebe not to be mention with Stelling, the man I
speak of. I know, on good authority, that one of the chief people at
Oxford say, Stelling might get the high honor if he choose. but he
donot care about university honor; hebe a quiet man--not noisy."
"ah, a deal well--a deal well," say Mr. Tulliver; "but a hundred
and fiftybe an uncommon price. I never think o' pay so much as
that."
"a good education, let I tell you, Tulliver,--a good education be
cheap at the money. but Stelling be moderate in his term; hebe not a
grasp man. Ihave no doubt hewould take your boy at a hundred, and
thatbe what you wouldnot get many other clergyman to do. Iwill write to
he about it, if you like."
Mr. Tulliver rub his knee, and look at the carpet in a
meditative manner.
"but belike hebe a bachelor," observe Mrs. Tulliver, in the interval;
"an' Ihave no opinion o' housekeeper. there be my brother, as be dead
an' gone, have a housekeeper once, an' she take half the feather out
o' the good bed, an' pack them up an' send them away. an' itbe unknown
the linen she make away with--stott her name be. it 'ud break my
heart to send Tom where therebe a housekeeper, an' I hope you willnot
think of it, Mr. Tulliver."
"you may set your mind at rest on that score, Mrs. Tulliver," say Mr.
Riley, "for Stelling be married to as nice a little woman as any man
need wish for a wife. there benot a kind little soul in the world; I
know her family well. she have very much your complexion,--light curly
hair. she come of a good Mudport family, and itbe not every offer
that would have be acceptable in that quarter. but Stelling's not an
every-day man; rather a particular fellow as to the people he choose
to be connect with. but I _think_ he would have no objection to take
your son; I _think_ he would not, on my representation."
"I donot know what he could have _against_ the lad," say Mrs.
Tulliver, with a slight touch of motherly indignation; "a nice
fresh-skinned lad as anybody need wish to see."
"but therebe one thing Ibe think on," say Mr. Tulliver, turn
his head on one side and look at Mr. Riley, after a long perusal of
the carpet. "wouldnot a parson be almost too high-learn to bring up a
lad to be a man o' business? my notion o' the parson be as theywould
get a sort o' learning as lie mostly out o' sight. and that benot what
I want for Tom. I want he to know figure, and write like print, and
see into thing quick, and know what folk mean, and how to wrap
thing up in word as benot actionable. itbe an uncommon fine thing,
that be," conclude Mr. Tulliver, shake his head, "when you can let
a man know what you think of he without pay for it."
"oh, my dear Tulliver," say Mr. Riley, "yoube quite under a mistake
about the clergy; all the good schoolmaster be of the clergy. the
schoolmaster who be not clergyman be a very low set of man
generally."
"ay, that Jacobs be, at the 'cademy," interpose Mr. Tulliver.
"to be sure,--man who have fail in other trade, most likely. now, a
clergyman be a gentleman by profession and education; and besides
that, he have the knowledge that will ground a boy, and prepare he for
enter on any career with credit. there may be some clergyman who
be mere bookman; but you may depend upon it, Stelling be not one of
them,--a man thatbe wide awake, let I tell you. drop he a hint, and
thatbe enough. you talk of figure, now; you have only to say to
Stelling, 'I want my son to be a thorough arithmetician,' and you may
leave the rest to he."
Mr. Riley pause a moment, while Mr. Tulliver, some-what reassure as
to clerical tutorship, be inwardly rehearse to an imaginary Mr.
Stelling the statement, "I want my son to know 'rethmetic."
"you see, my dear Tulliver," Mr. Riley continue, "when you get a
thoroughly educate man, like Stelling, hebe at no loss to take up any
branch of instruction. when a workman know the use of his tool, he
can make a door as well as a window."
"ay, thatbe true," say Mr. Tulliver, almost convince now that the
clergy must be the good of schoolmaster.
"well, Iwill tell you what Iwill do for you," say Mr. Riley, "and I
wouldnot do it for everybody. Iwill see Stelling's father-in-law, or
drop he a line when I get back to Mudport, to say that you wish to
place your boy with his son-in-law, and I dare say Stelling will write
to you, and send you his term."
"but therebe no hurry, be there?" say Mrs. Tulliver; "for I hope, Mr.
Tulliver, you willnot let Tom begin at his new school before Midsummer.
he begin at the 'cademy at the Lady-day quarter, and you see what
good's come of it."
"ay, ay, Bessy, never brew wi' bad malt upo' Michael-masday, else
youwill have a poor tap," say Mr. Tulliver, wink and smile at Mr.
Riley, with the natural pride of a man who have a buxom wife
conspicuously his inferior in intellect. "but itbe true therebe no
hurry; youhave hit it there, Bessy."
"it might be as well not to defer the arrangement too long," say Mr.
Riley, quietly, "for Stelling may have proposition from other
party, and I know he would not take more than two or three boarder,
if so many. if I be you, I think I would enter on the subject with
Stelling at once: therebe no necessity for send the boy before
Midsummer, but I would be on the safe side, and make sure that nobody
forestall you."
"ay, therebe summat in that," say Mr. Tulliver.
"Father," break in Maggie, who have steal unperceived to her father's
elbow again, listen with part lip, while she hold her doll
topsy-turvy, and crush its nose against the wood of the
chair,--"father, be it a long way off where Tom be to go? sha'n't we
ever go to see he?"
"I donot know, my wench," say the father, tenderly. "ask Mr. Riley;
he know."
Maggie come round promptly in front of Mr. Riley, and say, "how far
be it, please, sir?"
"oh, a long, long way off," that gentleman answer, be of opinion
that child, when they be not naughty, should always be speak to
jocosely. "you must borrow the seven-league boot to get to he."
"thatbe nonsense!" say Maggie, toss her head haughtily, and
turn away, with the tear spring in her eye. she begin to
dislike Mr. Riley; it be evident he think she silly and of no
consequence.
"Hush, Maggie! for shame of you, ask question and chattering,"
say her mother. "come and sit down on your little stool, and hold
your tongue, do. but," add Mrs. Tulliver, who have her own alarm
awaken, "be it so far off as I couldnot wash he and mend he?"
"about fifteen mile; thatbe all," say Mr. Riley. "you can drive
there and back in a day quite comfortably. or--Stelling be a
hospitable, pleasant man--he'd be glad to have you stay."
"but itbe too far off for the linen, I doubt," say Mrs. Tulliver,
sadly.
the entrance of supper opportunely adjourn this difficulty, and
relieve Mr. Riley from the labor of suggest some solution or
compromise,--a labor which he would otherwise doubtless have
undertake; for, as you perceive, he be a man of very obliging
manner. and he have really give himself the trouble of recommend
Mr. Stelling to his friend Tulliver without any positive expectation
of a solid, definite advantage result to himself, notwithstanding
the subtle indication to the contrary which might have mislead a
too-sagacious observer. for there be nothing more widely misleading
than sagacity if it happen to get on a wrong scent; and sagacity,
persuade that man usually act and speak from distinct motif, with a
consciously propose end in view, be certain to waste its energy on
imaginary game.
plot covetousness and deliberate contrivance, in order to compass
a selfish end, be nowhere abundant but in the world of the dramatist:
they demand too intense a mental action for many of our
fellow-parishioner to be guilty of they. it be easy enough to spoil
the life of our neighbor without take so much trouble; we can do
it by lazy acquiescence and lazy omission, by trivial falsity for
which we hardly know a reason, by small fraud neutralize by small
extravagance, by maladroit flattery, and clumsily improvise
insinuation. we live from hand to mouth, most of we, with a small
family of immediate desire; we do little else than snatch a morsel to
satisfy the hungry brood, rarely think of seed-corn or the next
year's crop.
Mr. Riley be a man of business, and not cold toward his own interest,
yet even he be more under the influence of small prompting than of
far-sighted design. he have no private understanding with the Rev.
Walter Stelling; on the contrary, he know very little of that M.A. and
his acquirements,--not quite enough, perhaps, to warrant so strong a
recommendation of he as he have give to his friend Tulliver. but he
believe Mr. Stelling to be an excellent classic, for Gadsby have say
so, and Gadsby's first cousin be an Oxford tutor; which be well
ground for the belief even than his own immediate observation would
have be, for though Mr. Riley have receive a tincture of the
classic at the great Mudport Free School, and have a sense of
understand Latin generally, his comprehension of any particular
Latin be not ready. Doubtless there remain a subtle aroma from his
juvenile contact with the "De Senectute" and the fourth book of the
"AEneid," but it have cease to be distinctly recognizable as classical,
and be only perceive in the high finish and force of his
auctioneering style. then, Stelling be an Oxford man, and the Oxford
man be always--no, no, it be the Cambridge man who be always good
mathematician. but a man who have have a university education could
teach anything he like; especially a man like Stelling, who have make
a speech at a Mudport dinner on a political occasion, and have
acquit himself so well that it be generally remark, this
son-in-law of Timpson's be a sharp fellow. it be to be expect of a
Mudport man, from the parish of St. Ursula, that he would not omit to
do a good turn to a son-in-law of Timpson's, for Timpson be one of
the most useful and influential man in the parish, and have a good deal
of business, which he know how to put into the right hand. Mr. Riley
like such man, quite apart from any money which might be divert,
through their good judgment, from less worthy pocket into his own;
and it would be a satisfaction to he to say to Timpson on his return
home, "Ihave secure a good pupil for your son-in-law." Timpson have a
large family of daughter; Mr. Riley feel for he; besides, Louisa
Timpson's face, with its light curl, have be a familiar object to
he over the pew wainscot on a Sunday for nearly fifteen year; it be
natural her husband should be a commendable tutor. moreover, Mr. Riley
know of no other schoolmaster whom he have any ground for recommend
in preference; why, then, should be not recommend Stelling? his friend
Tulliver have ask he for an opinion; it be always chill, in
friendly intercourse, to say you have no opinion to give. and if you
deliver an opinion at all, it be mere stupidity not to do it with an
air of conviction and well-found knowledge. you make it your own in
utter it, and naturally get fond of it. thus Mr. Riley, know no
harm of Stelling to begin with, and wish he well, so far as he have
any wish at all concern he, have no soon recommend he than
he begin to think with admiration of a man recommend on such high
authority, and would soon have gather so warm an interest on the
subject, that if Mr. Tulliver have in the end decline to send Tom to
Stelling, Mr. Riley would have think his "friend of the old school"
a thoroughly pig-head fellow.
if you blame Mr. Riley very severely for give a recommendation on
such slight ground, I must say you be rather hard upon he. why
should an auctioneer and appraiser thirty year ago, who have as good
as forget his free-school Latin, be expect to manifest a delicate
scrupulosity which be not always exhibit by gentleman of the learned
profession, even in our present advanced stage of morality?
besides, a man with the milk of human kindness in he can scarcely
abstain from do a good-natured action, and one cannot be
good-natured all round. nature herself occasionally quarter an
inconvenient parasite on an animal toward whom she have otherwise no
ill will. what then? we admire her care for the parasite. if Mr. Riley
have shrink from give a recommendation that be not base on valid
evidence, he would not have help Mr. Stelling to a pay pupil, and
that would not have be so well for the reverend gentleman. consider,
too, that all the pleasant little dim idea and complacency--of
stand well with Timpson, of dispense advice when he be ask for
it, of impress his friend Tulliver with additional respect, of
say something, and say it emphatically, with other inappreciably
minute ingredient that go along with the warm hearth and the
brandy-and-water to make up Mr. Riley's consciousness on this
occasion--would have be a mere blank.
Chapter IV
Tom be expect
it be a heavy disappointment to Maggie that she be not allow to go
with her father in the gig when he go to fetch Tom home from the
academy; but the morning be too wet, Mrs. Tulliver say, for a little
girl to go out in her good bonnet. Maggie take the opposite view very
strongly, and it be a direct consequence of this difference of
opinion that when her mother be in the act of brush out the
reluctant black crop Maggie suddenly rush from under her hand and
dip her head in a basin of water stand near, in the vindictive
determination that there should be no more chance of curl that day.
"Maggie, Maggie!" exclaim Mrs. Tulliver, sit stout and helpless
with the brush on her lap, "what be to become of you if yoube so
naughty? Iwill tell your aunt Glegg and your aunt pullet when they come
next week, and theywill never love you any more. oh dear, oh dear! look
at your clean pinafore, wet from top to bottom. folk 'ull think itbe
a judgment on I as Ihave get such a child,--they'll think Ihave do
summat wicked."
before this remonstrance be finish, Maggie be already out of
hearing, make her way toward the great attic that run under the old
high-pitch roof, shake the water from her black lock as she run,
like a skye terrier escape from his bath. this attic be Maggie's
favorite retreat on a wet day, when the weather be not too cold; here
she fret out all her ill humor, and talk aloud to the worm-eat
floor and the worm-eat shelf, and the dark rafter festoon with
cobwebs; and here she keep a Fetish which she punish for all her
misfortune. this be the trunk of a large wooden doll, which once
star with the roundest of eye above the reddest of cheek; but be
now entirely deface by a long career of vicarious suffering. three
nail drive into the head commemorate as many crisis in Maggie's
nine year of earthly struggle; that luxury of vengeance have be
suggest to she by the picture of Jael destroy Sisera in the old
Bible. the last nail have be drive in with a fiercer stroke than
usual, for the Fetish on that occasion represent aunt Glegg. but
immediately afterward Maggie have reflect that if she drive many
nail in she would not be so well able to fancy that the head be hurt
when she knock it against the wall, nor to comfort it, and make
believe to poultice it, when her fury be abate; for even aunt Glegg
would be pitiable when she have be hurt very much, and thoroughly
humiliate, so as to beg her niece's pardon. since then she have drive
no more nail in, but have soothe herself by alternately grind and
beat the wooden head against the rough brick of the great chimney
that make two square pillar support the roof. that be what she
do this morning on reach the attic, sob all the while with a
passion that expel every other form of consciousness,--even the
memory of the grievance that have cause it. as at last the sob be
get quieter, and the grind less fierce, a sudden beam of
sunshine, fall through the wire lattice across the worm-eat
shelf, make she throw away the Fetish and run to the window. the sun
be really break out; the sound of the mill seem cheerful again;
the granary door be open; and there be Yap, the queer
white-and-brown terrier, with one ear turn back, trot about and
sniff vaguely, as if he be in search of a companion. it be
irresistible. Maggie toss her hair back and run downstairs, seize
her bonnet without put it on, peep, and then dash along the
passage lest she should encounter her mother, and be quickly out in
the yard, whirl round like a Pythoness, and singing as she whirl,
"Yap, Yap, Tom's come home!" while Yap dance and bark round she,
as much as to say, if there be any noise want he be the dog for
it.
"Hegh, hegh, Miss! youwill make yourself giddy, an' tumble down I' the
dirt," say Luke, the head miller, a tall, broad-shoulder man of
forty, black-eyed and black-haired, subdue by a general mealiness,
like an auricula.
Maggie pause in her whirling and say, stagger a little, "oh no,
it donot make I giddy, Luke; may I go into the mill with you?"
Maggie love to linger in the great space of the mill, and often come
out with her black hair powder to a soft whiteness that make she
dark eye flash out with new fire. the resolute din, the unreste
motion of the great stone, give she a dim, delicious awe as at the
presence of an uncontrollable force; the meal forever pour,
pour; the fine white powder soften all surface, and make the
very spidernet look like a faery lace-work; the sweet, pure scent of
the meal,--all help to make Maggie feel that the mill be a little
world apart from she outside every-day life. the spider be
especially a subject of speculation with she. she wonder if they have
any relative outside the mill, for in that case there must be a
painful difficulty in their family intercourse,--a fat and floury
spider, accustom to take his fly well dust with meal, must suffer
a little at a cousin's table where the fly be _au naturel_, and the
lady spider must be mutually shock at each other's appearance. but
the part of the mill she like well be the topmost story,--the
corn-hutch, where there be the great heap of grain, which she could
sit on and slide down continually. she be in the habit of take this
recreation as she converse with Luke, to whom she be very
communicative, wish he to think well of her understanding, as she
father do.
perhaps she feel it necessary to recover her position with he on the
present occasion for, as she sit slide on the heap of grain near
which he be busy himself, she say, at that shrill pitch which be
requisite in mill-society,--
"I think you never read any book but the Bible, do you, Luke?"
"Nay, Miss, an' not much o' that," say Luke, with great frankness.
"Ibe no reader, I benot."
"but if I lend you one of my book, Luke? Ihave not get any _very_
pretty book that would be easy for you to read; but therebe 'Pug's
Tour of Europe,'--that would tell you all about the different sort of
people in the world, and if you donot understand the reading, the
picture would help you; they show the look and way of the people,
and what they do. there be the Dutchmen, very fat, and smoking, you
know, and one sit on a barrel."
"Nay, Miss, i'n no opinion o' Dutchmen. there ben't much good I'
knowin' about _they_."
"but theybe our fellow-creature, Luke; we ought to know about our
fellow-creature."
"not much o' fellow-creatur, I think, Miss; all I know--my old
master, as war a knowin' man, use to say, say he, 'if e'er I sow my
wheat wi'out brinin', Ibe a Dutchman,' say he; an' that war as much
as to say as a Dutchman war a fool, or next door. Nay, nay, I benot
go to bother mysen about Dutchmen. there's fool enoo, an' rogue
enoo, wi'out lookin' I' book for them."
"oh, well," say Maggie, rather foil by Luke's unexpectedly decide
view about Dutchmen, "perhaps you would like 'Animated nature'
well; thatbe not Dutchmen, you know, but elephant and kangaroo,
and the civet-cat, and the sunfish, and a bird sit on its tail,--i
forget its name. there be country full of those creature, instead
of horse and cow, you know. shouldnot you like to know about they,
Luke?"
"Nay, Miss, I'n get to keep count o' the flour an' corn; I cannot do
wi' knowin' so many thing besides my work. thatbe what bring folk
to the gallows,--knowin' everything but what they'n get to get their
bread by. an' theybe mostly lie, I think, whatbe print I' the
book: they print sheet be, anyhow, as the man cry I' the
street."
"why, yoube like my brother Tom, Luke," say Maggie, wish to turn
the conversation agreeably; "Tombe not fond of reading. I love Tom so
dearly, luke,--better than anybody else in the world. when he grow up
I shall keep his house, and we shall always live together. I can tell
he everything he donot know. but I think Tom's clever, for all he
donot like book; he make beautiful whipcord and rabbit-pen."
"ah," say Luke, "but hewill be fine an' vex, as the rabbit be all
dead."
"dead!" scream Maggie, jump up from her slide seat on the corn.
"oh dear, Luke! what! the lop-eared one, and the spot doe that Tom
spend all his money to buy?"
"as dead as mole," say Luke, fetch his comparison from the
unmistakable corps nail to the stable wall.
"oh dear, Luke," say Maggie, in a piteous tone, while the big tear
roll down her cheek; "Tom tell I to take care of them, and I forget.
what _shall_ I do?"
"well, you see, Miss, they be in that far tool-house, an' it be
nobody's business to see to them. I reckon Master Tom tell Harry to
feed them, but therebe no countin' on Harry; _hebe_ an offal creatur as
iver come about the primise, he be. he remember nothing but his own
inside--an' I wish it'ud gripe he."
"oh, Luke, Tom tell I to be sure and remember the rabbit every day;
but how could I, when they donot come into my head, you know? oh, he
will be so angry with I, I know he will, and so sorry about his
rabbit, and so be I sorry. oh, what _shall_ I do?"
"donot you fret, Miss," say Luke, soothingly; "theybe nash thing,
they lop-eared rabbit; theywould happen ha' die, if theyhave be feed.
thing out o' natur niver thrive: God A'mighty donot like them. he
make the rabbit' ear to lie back, an' itbe nothing but contrairiness
to make them he down like a mastiff dog's. Master Tom 'ull know
well nor buy such thing another time. donot you fret, Miss. will
you come along home wi' I, and see my wife? Ibe a-go this minute."
the invitation offer an agreeable distraction to Maggie's grief, and
her tear gradually subside as she trot along by Luke's side to
his pleasant cottage, which stand with its apple and pear tree, and
with the add dignity of a lean-to pigsty, at the other end of the
Mill field. Mrs. Moggs, Luke's wife, be a decidely agreeable
acquaintance. she exhibit her hospitality in bread and treacle, and
possess various work of art. Maggie actually forget that she have
any special cause of sadness this morning, as she stand on a chair to
look at a remarkable series of picture represent the Prodigal Son
in the costume of Sir Charles Grandison, except that, as might have
be expect from his defective moral character, he have not, like
that accomplished hero, the taste and strength of mind to dispense
with a wig. but the indefinable weight the dead rabbit have leave on
her mind cause she to feel more than usual pity for the career of
this weak young man, particularly when she look at the picture where
he lean against a tree with a flaccid appearance, his knee-breech
unbuttoned and his wig awry, while the swine apparently of some
foreign breed, seem to insult he by their good spirit over their
feast of husk.
"Ibe very glad his father take he back again, benot you, Luke?" she
say. "for he be very sorry, you know, and wouldnot do wrong again."
"eh, Miss," say Luke, "hewould be no great shake, I doubt, letus
feyther do what he would for he."
that be a painful thought to Maggie, and she wish much that the
subsequent history of the young man have not be leave a blank.
chapter V
Tom come home
Tom be to arrive early in the afternoon, and there be another

strong feeling, it be fondness for her boy. at last the sound
came,--that quick light bowling of the gig-wheels,--and in spite of

all the grief of the morning.
"there he be, my sweet lad! but, Lord ha' mercy! hebe get never a
collar on; itbe be lose on the road, Iwill be bind, and spoilt the
set."
Mrs. Tulliver stand with her arm open; Maggie jump first on one leg
and then on the other; while Tom descend from the gig, and say,
with masculine reticence as to the tender emotion, "Hallo! Yap--what!
be you there?"
nevertheless he submit to be kiss willingly enough, though Maggie
hang on his neck in rather a strangle fashion, while his blue-gray

promise himself that he would begin to fish the first thing to-morrow
morning. he be one of those lad that grow everywhere in England, and
at twelve or thirteen year of age look as much alike as goslings,--a
lad with light-brown hair, cheek of cream and rose, full lip,
indeterminate nose and eyebrows,--a physiognomy in which it seem
impossible to discern anything but the generic character to boyhood;
as different as possible from poor Maggie's phiz, which Nature seem
to have mould and color with the most decide intention. but that
same nature have the deep cunning which hide itself under the
appearance of openness, so that simple people think they can see
through her quite well, and all the while she be secretly prepare a
refutation of their confident prophecy. under these average boyish
physiognomy that she seem to turn off by the gross, she conceal
some of her most rigid, inflexible purpose, some of her most
unmodifiable character; and the dark-eye, demonstrative, rebellious
girl may after all turn out to be a passive being compare with this
pink-and-white bit of masculinity with the indeterminate feature.
"Maggie," say Tom, confidentially, take she into a corner, as soon

take off the chill he have feel from the long drive, "you donot know
what Ihave get in _my_ pocket," nod his head up and down as a
mean of rouse her sense of mystery.
"no," say Maggie. "how stodgy they look, Tom! be it marl (marble)
or cobnut?" Maggie's heart sink a little, because Tom always say it
be "no good" play with _she_ at those game, she play so badly.
"Marls! no; Ihave swap all my marl with the little fellow, and

here!" he draw something half out of his right-hand pocket.
"what be it?" say Maggie, in a whisper. "I can see nothing but a bit
of yellow."
"why, it's--a--new--guess, Maggie!"
"oh, I _cannot_ guess, Tom," say Maggie, impatiently.
"donot be a spitfire, else I willnot tell you," say Tom, thrust his
hand back into his pocket and look determine.

hold stiffly in the pocket. "Ibe not cross, Tom; it be only because I
cannot bear guess. _please_ be good to I."
Tom's arm slowly relax, and he say, "well, then, itbe a new
fish-line--two new uns,--one for you, Maggie, all to yourself. I

and here's hook; see here--I say, _willnot_ we go and fish to-morrow
down by the Round Pool? and you shall catch your own fish, Maggie and
put the worm on, and everything; willnot it be fun?"
Maggie's answer be to throw her arm round Tom's neck and hug he,

"benot I a good brother, now, to buy you a line all to yourself? you

"yes, very, very good--I _do_ love you, Tom."
Tom have put the line back in his pocket, and be look at the hook
one by one, before he speak again.
"and the fellow fight I, because I wouldnot give in about the
toffee."
"oh, dear! I wish they wouldnot fight at your school, Tom. donot it

"hurt I? no," say Tom, put up the hook again, take out a

"I give Spouncer a black eye, I know; thatbe what he get by want to

I."
"oh, how brave you be, Tom! I think yoube like Samson. if there come
a lion roar at I, I think youwould fight he, wouldnot you, Tom?"
"how can a lion come roar at you, you silly thing? therebe no
lion, only in the show."
"no; but if we be in the lion country--I mean in Africa, where
itbe very hot; the lion eat people there. I can show it you in the
book where I read it."
"well, I should get a gun and shoot he."
"but if you havenot get a gun,--we might have go out, you know, not
think, just as we go fish; and then a great lion might run
toward we roar, and we couldnot get away from he. what should you
do, Tom?"
Tom pause, and at last turn away contemptuously, say, "but the
lion _benot_ come. whatbe the use of talk?"
"but I like to fancy how it would be," say Maggie, follow he.
"just think what you would do, Tom."
"oh, donot bother, Maggie! yoube such a silly. I shall go and see my
rabbit."
Maggie's heart begin to flutter with fear. she dare not tell the sad
truth at once, but she walk after Tom in tremble silence as he

once his sorrow and his anger; for Maggie dread Tom's anger of all
thing; it be quite a different anger from her own.
"Tom," she say, timidly, when they be out of door, "how much money
do you give for your rabbit?"
"two half-crown and a sixpence," say Tom, promptly.
"I think Ihave get a great deal more than that in my steel purse
upstairs. Iwill ask mother to give it you."
"what for?" say Tom. "I donot want _your_ money, you silly thing.

only a girl."

and buy some more rabbit with it?"
"More rabbit? I donot want any more."
"oh, but, Tom, theybe all dead."

with this terrible conclusion, Tom run away from Maggie toward the

these bitter sorrow of childhood! when sorrow be all new and strange,
when hope have not yet get wing to fly beyond the day and week, and
the space from summer to summer seem measureless.
Maggie soon think she have be hour in the attic, and it must be

but she know Tom's step, and her heart begin to beat violently with

we learn to restrain ourselves as we get old. we keep apart when we
have quarrel, express ourselves in well-breed phrase, and in this
way preserve a dignified alienation, show much firmness on one
side, and swallow much grief on the other. we no long approximate
in our behavior to the mere impulsiveness of the low animal, but
conduct ourselves in every respect like member of a highly civilized
society. Maggie and Tom be still very much like young animal, and
so she could rub her cheek against his, and kiss his ear in a random
sob way; and there be tender fibre in the lad that have be
use to answer to Maggie's fondling, so that he behave with a
weakness quite inconsistent with his resolution to punish she as much
as she deserve. he actually begin to kiss she in return, and say,--
"donot cry, then, Magsie; here, eat a bit o' cake."
Maggie's sob begin to subside, and she put out her mouth for the cake

eat together and rub each other's cheek and brow and nose
together, while they eat, with a humiliating resemblance to two
friendly pony.
"come along, Magsie, and have tea," say Tom at last, when there be
no more cake except what be down-stair.
so end the sorrow of this day, and the next morning Maggie be
trot with her own fishing-rod in one hand and a handle of the
basket in the other, step always, by a peculiar gift, in the

what be in the book; and she be rather in awe of Tom's superiority,
for he be the only person who call her knowledge "stuff," and do
not feel surprised at her cleverness. Tom, indeed, be of opinion that
Maggie be a silly little thing; all girl be silly,--they couldnot
throw a stone so as to hit anything, couldnot do anything with a
pocket-knife, and be frighten at frog. still, he be very fond of
his sister, and mean always to take care of she, make she his
housekeeper, and punish she when she do wrong.
they be on their way to the Round Pool,--that wonderful pool, which
the flood have make a long while ago. no one know how deep it be; and
it be mysterious, too, that it should be almost a perfect round,
frame in with willow and tall reed, so that the water be only to
be see when you get close to the brink. the sight of the old favorite
spot always heighten Tom's good humor, and he speak to Maggie in the
most amicable whisper, as he open the precious basket and prepare

hand. Maggie think it probable that the small fish would come to her
hook, and the large one to Tom's. but she have forget all about the

a loud whisper, "look, look, Maggie!" and come run to prevent she
from snatch her line away.
Maggie be frightened lest she have be do something wrong, as
usual, but presently Tom draw out her line and bring a large tench
bounce on the grass.
Tom be excited.
"O Magsie, you little duck! empty the basket."
Maggie be not conscious of unusual merit, but it be enough that Tom
call her Magsie, and be pleased with she. there be nothing to mar
her delight in the whisper and the dreamy silence, when she listen
to the light dripping sound of the rise fish, and the gentle
rustling, as if the willow and the reed and the water have their
happy whispering also. Maggie think it would make a very nice
heaven to sit by the pool in that way, and never be scold. she never
know she have a bite till Tom tell she; but she like fish very
much.
it be one of their happy morning. they trot along and sit down
together, with no thought that life would ever change much for they;
they would only get big and not go to school, and it would always
be like the holiday; they would always live together and be fond of
each other. and the mill with its booming; the great chestnut-tree
under which they play at house; their own little river, the Ripple,
where the bank seem like home, and Tom be always see the
water-rat, while Maggie gather the purple plumy top of the reed,
which she forget and drop afterward; above all, the great Floss,
along which they wander with a sense of travel, to see the rushing
spring-tide, the awful Eagle, come up like a hungry monster, or to see
the Great Ash which have once wail and groan like a man, these
thing would always be just the same to they. Tom think people be
at a disadvantage who live on any other spot of the globe; and
Maggie, when she read about Christiana pass "the river over which
there be no bridge," always see the Floss between the green pasture
by the Great Ash.
Life do change for Tom and Maggie; and yet they be not wrong in
believe that the thought and love of these first year would
always make part of their life. we could never have love the earth
so well if we have have no childhood in it,--if it be not the earth
where the same flower come up again every spring that we use to
gather with our tiny finger as we sit lisping to ourselves on the
grass; the same hip and haw on the autumn's hedgerow; the same
redbreast that we use to call "God's bird," because they do no
harm to the precious crop. what novelty be worth that sweet monotony
where everything be know, and _love_ because it be know?
the wood I walk in on this mild May day, with the young yellow-brown
foliage of the oak between I and the blue sky, the white
star-flower and the blue-eyed speedwell and the ground ivy at my
foot, what grove of tropic palm, what strange fern or splendid
broad-petalle blossom, could ever thrill such deep and delicate
fibre within I as this home scene? these familiar flower, these
well-remember bird-note, this sky, with its fitful brightness,
these furrowed and grassy field, each with a sort of personality
give to it by the capricious hedgerows,--such thing as these be the
mother-tongue of our imagination, the language that be laden with all
the subtle, inextricable association the fleeting hour of our
childhood leave behind they. our delight in the sunshine on the
deep-bladed grass to-day might be no more than the faint perception of
weary soul, if it be not for the sunshine and the grass in the
far-off year which still live in we, and transform our perception
into love.
Chapter VI
the Aunts and Uncles be come
it be Easter week, and Mrs. Tulliver's cheesecake be more
exquisitely light than usual. "a puff o' wind 'ud make them blow about
like feather," Kezia the housemaid say, feel proud to live under
a mistress who could make such pastry; so that no season or
circumstance could have be more propitious for a family party, even
if it have not be advisable to consult sister Glegg and sister Pullet
about Tom's go to school.
"Iwould as lief not invite sister Deane this time," say Mrs. Tulliver,
"for shebe as jealous and have as can be, and's allay try to
make the bad o' my poor child to their aunt and uncle."
"yes, yes," say Mr. Tulliver, "ask she to come. I never hardly get a
bit o' talk with Deane now; we havenot have he this six month. whatbe
it matter what she say? my child need be behold to nobody."
"thatbe what you allay say, Mr. Tulliver; but Ibe sure therebe nobody
o' your side, neither aunt nor uncle, to leave them so much as a
five-pound note for a leggicy. and therebe sister Glegg, and sister
Pullet too, save money unknown, for they put by all their own
interest and butter-money too; their husband buy them everything."
Mrs. Tulliver be a mild woman, but even a sheep will face about a
little when she have lamb.
"Tchuh!" say Mr. Tulliver. "it take a big loaf when therebe many to
breakfast. what signify your sister' bit o' money when theyhave get
half-a-dozen nevvie and niece to divide it among? and your sister
Deane willnot get them to leave all to one, I reckon, and make the
country cry shame on them when they be dead?"
"I donot know what she willnot get them to do," say Mrs. Tulliver, "for

time naughty when they come than she be other day, and Tom donot
like them, bless him!--though itbe more nat'ral in a boy than a gell.
and therebe Lucy Dean's such a good child,--you may set she on a

get off. I cannot help love the child as if she be my own; and Ibe
sure shebe more like _my_ child than sister Deane's, for shewould allay
a very poor color for one of our family, sister Deane have."
"well, well, if yoube fond o' the child, ask her father and mother to
bring she with them. and willnot you ask their aunt and uncle Moss too,
and some o' _their_ child?"
"oh, dear, Mr. Tulliver, why, therewould be eight people besides the
child, and I must put two more leaf I' the table, besides
reach down more o' the dinner-service; and you know as well as I do
as _my_ sister and _your_ sister donot suit well together."
"well, well, do as you like, Bessy," say Mr. Tulliver, take up his
hat and walk out to the mill. few wife be more submissive than
Mrs. Tulliver on all point unconnected with her family relation; but
she have be a Miss Dodson, and the Dodsons be a very respectable
family indeed,--as much look up to as any in their own parish, or
the next to it. the Miss Dodsons have always be think to hold up
their head very high, and no one be surprise the two eld have
marry so well,--not at an early age, for that be not the practice
of the Dodson family. there be particular way of do everything
in that family: particular way of bleach the linen, of make the
cowslip wine, cure the ham, and keep the bottled gooseberry;
so that no daughter of that house could be indifferent to the
privilege of have be bear a Dodson, rather than a Gibson or a
Watson. funeral be always conduct with peculiar propriety in the
Dodson family: the hat-band be never of a blue shade, the glove
never split at the thumb, everybody be a mourner who ought to be, and
there be always scarf for the bearer. when one of the family be
in trouble or sickness, all the rest go to visit the unfortunate
member, usually at the same time, and do not shrink from utter the
most disagreeable truth that correct family feel dictate; if the
illness or trouble be the sufferer's own fault, it be not in the
practice of the Dodson family to shrink from say so. in short,
there be in this family a peculiar tradition as to what be the right
thing in household management and social demeanor, and the only bitter
circumstance attend this superiority be a painful inability to
approve the condiment or the conduct of family ungoverne by the
Dodson tradition. a female Dodson, when in "strange house," always
eat dry bread with her tea, and decline any sort of preserve, have
no confidence in the butter, and think that the preserve have
probably begin to ferment from want of due sugar and boiling. there
be some dodson less like the family than other, that be admit;
but in so far as they be "kin," they be of necessity well than
those who be "no kin." and it be remarkable that while no individual
Dodson be satisfied with any other individual Dodson, each be
satisfied, not only with he or her self, but with the dodson
collectively. the feeble member of a family--the one who have the
least character--be often the mere epitome of the family habit and
tradition; and Mrs. Tulliver be a thorough Dodson, though a mild
one, as small-beer, so long as it be anything, be only describable as
very weak ale: and though she have groan a little in her youth under
the yoke of her eld sister, and still shed occasional tear at
their sisterly reproach, it be not in Mrs. Tulliver to be an
innovator on the family idea. she be thankful to have be a Dodson,
and to have one child who take after her own family, at least in his
feature and complexion, in like salt and in eat bean, which a
Tulliver never do.
in other respect the true Dodson be partly latent in Tom, and he be
as far from appreciate his "kin" on the mother's side as Maggie
herself, generally abscond for the day with a large supply of the
most portable food, when he receive timely warning that his aunt and
uncle be coming,--a moral symptom from which his aunt Glegg deduce
the gloomy view of his future. it be rather hard on Maggie that
Tom always abscond without let she into the secret, but the
weak sex be acknowledge to be serious _impedimenta_ in case of
flight.
on Wednesday, the day before the aunt and uncle be come, there
be such various and suggestive scent, as of plumcake in the oven
and jelly in the hot state, mingle with the aroma of gravy, that it
be impossible to feel altogether gloomy: there be hope in the air.
Tom and Maggie make several inroad into the kitchen, and, like other
marauder, be induce to keep aloof for a time only by be allow
to carry away a sufficient load of booty.
"Tom," say Maggie, as they sit on the bough of the elder-tree,
eat their jam-puff, "shall you run away to-morrow?"
"no," say Tom, slowly, when he have finish his puff, and be eye
the third, which be to be divide between them,--"no, I sha'n't."
"why, Tom? because Lucy's come?"
"no," say Tom, open his pocket-knife and hold it over the puff,
with his head on one side in a dubitative manner. (it be a difficult
problem to divide that very irregular polygon into two equal part.)
"what do _I_ care about Lucy? shebe only a girl,--_she_ cannot play at
bandy."
"be it the tipsy-cake, then?" say Maggie, exert her hypothetic
power, while she lean forward toward Tom with her eye fix on the
hover knife.
"no, you silly, thatwill be good the day after. itbe the pudden. I know
what the pudden's to be,--apricot roll-up--o my button!"
with this interjection, the knife descend on the puff, and it be in
two, but the result be not satisfactory to Tom, for he still eye the
half doubtfully. at last he said,--
"shut your eye, Maggie."
"what for?"
"you never mind what for. shut them when I tell you."
Maggie obey.
"now, which'll you have, maggie,--right hand or leave?"
"Iwill have that with the jam run out," say Maggie, keep her eye
shut to please Tom.
"why, you donot like that, you silly. you may have it if it come to
you fair, but I sha'n't give it you without. right or left,--you
choose, now. ha-a-a!" say Tom, in a tone of exasperation, as Maggie
peep. "you keep your eye shut, now, else you sha'n't have any."
Maggie's power of sacrifice do not extend so far; indeed, I fear she
care less that Tom should enjoy the utmost possible amount of puff,
than that he should be pleased with she for give he the good bit.
so she shut her eye quite close, till Tom tell she to "say which,"
and then she say, "left hand."
"youhave get it," say Tom, in rather a bitter tone.
"what! the bit with the jam run out?"
"no; here, take it," say Tom, firmly, handing, decidedly the good
piece to Maggie.
"oh, please, Tom, have it; I donot mind--I like the other; please take
this."
"no, I sha'n't," say Tom, almost crossly, begin on his own
inferior piece.
Maggie, think it be no use to contend far, begin too, and eat
up her half puff with considerable relish as well as rapidity. but Tom
have finish first, and have to look on while Maggie eat her last
morsel or two, feel in himself a capacity for more. Maggie donot
know Tom be look at she; she be seesaw on the elder-bough,
lose to almost everything but a vague sense of jam and idleness.
"oh, you greedy thing!" say Tom, when she have swallow the last
morsel. he be conscious of have act very fairly, and think she
ought to have consider this, and make up to he for it. he would
have refuse a bit of hers beforehand, but one be naturally at a
different point of view before and after one's own share of puff be
swallow.
Maggie turn quite pale. "oh, Tom, why donot you ask I?"
"I benot go to ask you for a bit, you greedy. you might have
thought of it without, when you know I give you the good bit."
"but I want you to have it; you know I do," say Maggie, in an
injure tone.
"yes, but I benot go to do what benot fair, like Spouncer. he
always take the good bit, if you donot punch he for it; and if you
choose the good with your eye shut, he change his hand. but if I go
half, Iwill go them fair; only I wouldnot be a greedy."
with this cutting innuendo, Tom jump down from his bough, and throw
a stone with a "hoigh!" as a friendly attention to Yap, who have also
be look on while the eatable vanish, with an agitation of his
ear and feeling which could hardly have be without bitterness. yet
the excellent dog accept Tom's attention with as much alacrity as if
he have be treat quite generously.
but Maggie, gift with that superior power of misery which
distinguish the human being, and place he at a proud distance from
the most melancholy chimpanzee, sit still on her bough, and give
herself up to the keen sense of unmerited reproach. she would have
give the world not to have eat all her puff, and to have save some
of it for Tom. not but that the puff be very nice, for Maggie's
palate be not at all obtuse, but she would have go without it many
time over, soon than Tom should call she greedy and be cross with
she. and he have say he wouldnot have it, and she eat it without
thinking; how could she help it? the tear flow so plentifully that
Maggie see nothing around she for the next ten minute; but by that
time resentment begin to give way to the desire of reconciliation, and
she jump from her bough to look for Tom. he be no long in the
paddock behind the rickyard; where be he likely to be go, and Yap
with he? Maggie run to the high bank against the great holly-tree,
where she could see far away toward the Floss. there be Tom; but her
heart sink again as she see how far off he be on his way to the great
river, and that he have another companion besides Yap,--naughty Bob
Jakin, whose official, if not natural, function of frighten the
bird be just now at a standstill. Maggie feel sure that Bob be
wicked, without very distinctly know why; unless it be because
Bob's mother be a dreadfully large fat woman, who live at a queer
