round house down the river; and once, when Maggie and Tom have wander
thither, there rush out a brindled dog that wouldnot stop bark;
and when Bob's mother come out after it, and scream above the
barking to tell they not to be frightened, Maggie think she be
scold they fiercely, and her heart beat with terror. Maggie think
it very likely that the round house have snake on the floor, and bat
in the bedroom; for she have see Bob take off his cap to show Tom a
little snake that be inside it, and another time he have a handful of
young bat: altogether, he be an irregular character, perhaps even
slightly diabolical, judge from his intimacy with snake and bat;
and to crown all, when Tom have Bob for a companion, he donot mind
about Maggie, and would never let she go with he.
it must be own that Tom be fond of Bob's company. how could it be
otherwise? Bob know, directly he see a bird's egg, whether it be a
swallow's, or a tomtit's, or a yellow-hammer's; he find out all the
wasp' nest, and could set all sort of trap; he could climb the
tree like a squirrel, and have quite a magical power of detect
hedgehog and stoat; and he have courage to do thing that be rather
naughty, such as make gap in the hedgerow, throw stone after
the sheep, and kill a cat that be wander _incognito_.
such quality in an inferior, who could always be treat with
authority in spite of his superior knowingness, have necessarily a
fatal fascination for Tom; and every holiday-time Maggie be sure to
have day of grief because he have go off with Bob.
well! there be no hope for it; he be go now, and Maggie could
think of no comfort but to sit down by the hollow, or wander by the
hedgerow, and fancy it be all different, refashion her little
world into just what she should like it to be.
Maggie's be a troublous life, and this be the form in which she take
her opium.
meanwhile Tom, forget all about Maggie and the sting of reproach
which he have leave in her heart, be hurry along with Bob, whom he
have meet accidentally, to the scene of a great rat-catch in a
neighboring barn. Bob know all about this particular affair, and speak
of the sport with an enthusiasm which no one who be not either
divest of all manly feeling, or pitiably ignorant of rat-catch,
can fail to imagine. for a person suspect of preternatural
wickedness, Bob be really not so very villanous-looking; there be
even something agreeable in his snub-nosed face, with its close-curl
border of red hair. but then his trouser be always roll up at the
knee, for the convenience of wad on the slight notice; and his
virtue, suppose it to exist, be undeniably "virtue in rag," which,
on the authority even of bilious philosopher, who think all
well-dress merit overpay, be notoriously likely to remain
unrecognized (perhaps because it be see so seldom).
"I know the chap as own the ferret," say Bob, in a hoarse treble
voice, as he shuffle along, keep his blue eye fix on the river,
like an amphibious animal who foresaw occasion for dart in. "he
live up the Kennel Yard at Sut Ogg's, he do. hebe the big
rot-catcher anywhere, he be. Iwould soon, be a rot-catcher nor
anything, I would. the mole be nothing to the rot. but lor! you mun
ha' ferret. Dogs be no good. why, therebe that dog, now!" Bob
continue, point with an air of disgust toward Yap, "hebe no more
good wi' a rot nor nothing. I see it myself, I do, at the
rot-catchin' I' your feyther's barn."
Yap, feel the wither influence of this scorn, tuck his tail in
and shrink close to Tom's leg, who feel a little hurt for he, but have
not the superhuman courage to seem behindhand with Bob in contempt for
a dog who make so poor a figure.
"no, no," he say, "Yap's no good at sport. Iwill have regular good
dog for rat and everything, when Ihave do school."
"Hev ferret, Measter Tom," say Bob, eagerly,--"them white ferret
wi' pink eye; lor, you might catch your own rot, an' you might put
a rot in a cage wi' a ferret, an' see them fight, you might. thatbe
what Iwould do, I know, an' it 'ud be well fun a'most nor seein' two
chap fight,--if it benot they chap as sell cake an' orange at the
Fair, as the thing fly out o' their basket, an' some o' the cake
be smash--but they taste just as good," add Bob, by way of note
or addendum, after a moment's pause.
"but, I say, Bob," say Tom, in a tone of deliberation, "ferret be
nasty bite things,--they'll bite a fellow without be set on."
"lor! why thatbe the beauty on them. if a chap lay hold o' your
ferret, he willnot be long before he hollow out a good un, _he_ willnot."
at this moment a striking incident make the boy pause suddenly in
their walk. it be the plunging of some small body in the water from
among the neighboring bulrush; if it be not a water-rat, Bob
intimate that he be ready to undergo the most unpleasant
consequence.
"Hoigh! yap,--hoigh! there he be," say Tom, clap his hand, as
the little black snout make its arrowy course to the opposite bank.
"seize he, lad! seize he!"
Yap agitate his ear and wrinkle his brow, but decline to plunge,
try whether barking would not answer the purpose just as well.
"Ugh! you coward!" say Tom, and kick he over, feel humiliate
as a sportsman to possess so poor-spirited an animal. Bob abstain
from remark and pass on, choose, however, to walk in the shallow
edge of the overflowing river by way of change.
"hebe none so full now, the Floss benot," say Bob, as he kick the
water up before he, with an agreeable sense of be insolent to it.
"why, last 'ear, the meadow be all one sheet o' water, they be."
"ay, but," say Tom, whose mind be prone to see an opposition between
statement that be really accordant,--"but there be a big flood
once, when the Round Pool be make. _I_ know there be, 'cause father
say so. and the sheep and cow all drown, and the boat go all
over the field ever such a way."
"_i_ donot care about a flood comin'," say Bob; "I donot mind the
water, no more nor the land. Iwould swim, _i_ would."
"ah, but if you get nothing to eat for ever so long?" say Tom, his
imagination become quite active under the stimulus of that dread.
"when Ibe a man, I shall make a boat with a wooden house on the top of
it, like Noah's ark, and keep plenty to eat in it,--rabbit and
things,--all ready. and then if the flood come, you know, Bob, I
shouldnot mind. and Iwould take you in, if I see you swim," he add,
in the tone of a benevolent patron.
"I benot fright," say Bob, to whom hunger do not appear so
appalling. "but Iwould get in an' knock the rabbit on th' head when you
want to eat them."
"ah, and I should have halfpence, and wewould play at head-and-tail,"
say Tom, not contemplate the possibility that this recreation might
have few charm for his mature age. "Iwould divide fair to begin with,
and then wewould see whowould win."
"Ihave get a halfpenny o' my own," say Bob, proudly, come out of the
water and toss his halfpenny in the air. "yead or tail?"
"tail," say Tom, instantly fire with the desire to win.
"itbe yead," say Bob, hastily, snatch up the halfpenny as it
fall.
"it benot," say Tom, loudly and peremptorily. "you give I the
halfpenny; Ihave win it fair."
"I sha'n't," say Bob, hold it tight in his pocket.
"then Iwill make you; see if I donot," say Tom.
"yes, I can."
"you cannot make I do nothing, you cannot," say Bob.
"no, you cannot."
"Ibe master."
"I donot care for you."
"but Iwill make you care, you cheat," say Tom, collar Bob and
shake he.
"you get out wi' you," say Bob, give Tom a kick.
Tom's blood be thoroughly up: he go at Bob with a lunge and throw
he down, but Bob seize hold and keep it like a cat, and pull Tom
down after he. they struggle fiercely on the ground for a moment or
two, till Tom, pin Bob down by the shoulder, think he have the
mastery.
"_you_, say youwill give I the halfpenny now," he say, with
difficulty, while he exert himself to keep the command of Bob's
arm.
but at this moment Yap, who have be run on before, return
bark to the scene of action, and see a favorable opportunity for
bite Bob's bare leg not only with inpunity but with honor. the pain
from Yap's tooth, instead of surprising Bob into a relaxation of his
hold, give it a fiercer tenacity, and with a new exertion of his force
he push Tom backward and get uppermost. but now Yap, who could get
no sufficient purchase before, set his tooth in a new place, so that
Bob, harass in this way, let go his hold of Tom, and, almost
throttle Yap, fling he into the river. by this time Tom be up
again, and before Bob have quite recover his balance after the act of
swing Yap, Tom fall upon he, throw he down, and get his knee
firmly on Bob's chest.
"you give I the halfpenny now," say Tom.
"take it," say Bob, sulkily.
"no, I sha'n't take it; you give it I."
Bob take the halfpenny out of his pocket, and throw it away from he
on the ground.
Tom loose his hold, and leave Bob to rise.
"there the halfpenny lie," he say. "I donot want your halfpenny; I
wouldnot have keep it. but you want to cheat; I hate a cheat. I
sha'n't go along with you any more," he add, turn round homeward,
not without cast a regret toward the rat-catching and other
pleasure which he must relinquish along with Bob's society.
"you may let it alone, then," Bob call out after he. "I shall cheat
if I like; therebe no fun I' play else; and I know where therebe a
goldfinch's nest, but Iwill take care _you_ donot. an' yoube a nasty
fightin' turkey-cock, you are----"
Tom walk on without look around, and Yap follow his example,
the cold bath have moderate his passion.
"go along wi' you, then, wi' your drowned dog; I wouldnot own such a
dog--_I_ wouldnot," say Bob, get louder, in a last effort to
sustain his defiance. but Tom be not to be provoke into turning
round, and Bob's voice begin to falter a little as he said,--
"An' i'n gi'en you everything, an' show you everything, an' niver
want nothing from you. an' therebe your horn-handed knife, then as
you gi'en I." here Bob fling the knife as far as he could after Tom's
retreating footstep. but it produce no effect, except the sense in
Bob's mind that there be a terrible void in his lot, now that knife
be go.
he stand still till Tom have pass through the gate and disappear
behind the hedge. the knife would do not good on the ground there; it
wouldnot vex Tom; and pride or resentment be a feeble passion in
Bob's mind compare with the love of a pocket-knife. his very finger
send entreating thrill that he would go and clutch that familiar
rough buck's-horn handle, which they have so often grasp for mere
affection, as it lie idle in his pocket. and there be two blade,
and they have just be sharpen! what be life without a pocket-knife
to he who have once taste a high existence? no; to throw the handle
after the hatchet be a comprehensible act of desperation, but to throw
one's pocket-knife after an implacable friend be clearly in every
sense a hyperbole, or throw beyond the mark. so Bob shuffle back
to the spot where the beloved knife lie in the dirt, and feel quite a
new pleasure in clutch it again after the temporary separation, in
open one blade after the other, and feel their edge with his
well-harden thumb. poor Bob! he be not sensitive on the point of
honor, not a chivalrous character. that fine moral aroma would not
have be think much of by the public opinion of Kennel Yard, which
be the very focus or heart of Bob's world, even if it could have make
itself perceptible there; yet, for all that, he be not utterly a
sneak and a thief as our friend Tom have hastily decide.
but Tom, you perceive, be rather a Rhadamanthine personage, have
more than the usual share of boy's justice in him,--the justice that
desire to hurt culprit as much as they deserve to be hurt, and be
trouble with no doubt concern the exact amount of their desert.
Maggie see a cloud on his brow when he come home, which check she
joy at his come so much soon than she have expect, and she dare
hardly speak to he as he stand silently throw the small
gravel-stone into the mill-dam. it be not pleasant to give up a
rat-catching when you have set your mind on it. but if Tom have tell
his strong feeling at that moment, he would have say, "Iwould do just
the same again." that be his usual mode of view his past action;
whereas Maggie be always wish she have do something different.
Chapter VII
enter the Aunts and Uncles
the Dodsons be certainly a handsome family, and Mrs. Glegg be not
the least handsome of the sister. as she sit in Mrs. Tulliver's
arm-chair, no impartial observer could have deny that for a woman of
fifty she have a very comely face and figure, though Tom and Maggie
consider their aunt Glegg as the type of ugliness. it be true she
despise the advantage of costume, for though, as she often observe,
no woman have well clothe, it be not her way to wear her new thing
out before her old one. other woman, if they like, might have their
good thread-lace in every wash; but when Mrs. Glegg die, it would be
find that she have well lace lay by in the right-hand drawer of her
wardrobe in the Spotted Chamber than ever Mrs. Wooll of St. Ogg's have
buy in her life, although Mrs. Wooll wear her lace before it be
pay for. so of her curl front: Mrs. Glegg have doubtless the
glossy and crispest brown curl in her drawer, as well as curl in
various degree of fuzzy laxness; but to look out on the week-day
world from under a crisp and glossy front would be to introduce a most
dreamlike and unpleasant confusion between the sacred and the secular.
occasionally, indeed, Mrs. Glegg wear one of her third-good front on
a week-day visit, but not at a sister's house; especially not at Mrs.
Tulliver's, who, since her marriage, have hurt her sister's feeling
greatly by wear her own hair, though, as Mrs. Glegg observe to
Mrs. Deane, a mother of a family, like Bessy, with a husband always
go to law, might have be expect to know well. but Bessy be
always weak!
so if Mrs. Glegg's front to-day be more fuzzy and lax than usual, she
have a design under it: she intend the most pointed and cut
allusion to Mrs. Tulliver's bunch of blond curl, separate from
each other by a due wave of smoothness on each side of the parting.
Mrs. Tulliver have shed tear several time at sister Glegg's
unkindness on the subject of these unmatronly curl, but the
consciousness of look the handsomer for they naturally administer
support. Mrs. Glegg choose to wear her bonnet in the house
to-day,--unite and tilt slightly, of course--a frequent practice of
her when she be on a visit, and happen to be in a severe humor:
she donot know what draught there might be in strange house. for
the same reason she wear a small sable tippet, which reach just to
her shoulder, and be very far from meet across her well-form
chest, while her long neck be protect by a _chevaux-de-frise_ of
miscellaneous frilling. one would need to be learn in the fashion
of those time to know how far in the rear of they Mrs. Glegg's
slate-color silk gown must have be; but from certain
constellation of small yellow spot upon it, and a mouldy odor about
it suggestive of a damp clothe-chest, it be probable that it
belong to a stratum of garment just old enough to have come
recently into wear.
Mrs. Glegg hold her large gold watch in her hand with the many-double
chain round her finger, and observe to Mrs. Tulliver, who have just
return from a visit to the kitchen, that whatever it might be by
other people's clock and watch, it be go half-past twelve by
her.
"I donot know what ail sister Pullet," she continue. "it use to be
the way in our family for one to be as early as another,--I'm sure it
be so in my poor father's time,--and not for one sister to sit half
an hour before the other come. but if the way o' the family be
alter, it sha'n't be _my_ fault; _Iwill_ never be the one to come
into a house when all the rest be go away. I wonder _at_ sister
Deane,--she use to be more like I. but if youwill take my advice,
Bessy, youwill put the dinner forrard a bit, soon than put it back,
because folk be late as ought to ha' know well."
"oh dear, therebe no fear but what theywill be all here in time,
sister," say Mrs. Tulliver, in her mild-peevish tone. "the dinner
willnot be ready till half-past one. but if itbe long for you to wait,
let I fetch you a cheesecake and a glass o' wine."
"well, Bessy!" say Mrs. Glegg, with a bitter smile and a scarcely
perceptible toss of her head, "I should ha' think youwould know your
own sister well. I never _do_ eat between meal, and Ibe not go
to begin. not but what I hate that nonsense of have your dinner at
half-past one, when you might have it at one. you be never bring up
in that way, Bessy."
"why, Jane, what can I do? Mr. Tulliver donot like his dinner before
two o'clock, but I put it half an hour early because o' you."
"yes, yes, I know how it be with husbands,--they're for put
everything off; theywill put the dinner off till after tea, if theyhave
get wife as be weak enough to give in to such work; but itbe a pity
for you, Bessy, as you havenot get more strength o' mind. itwill be
well if your child donot suffer for it. and I hope youhave not go
and get a great dinner for us,--going to expense for your sister, as
'ud soon eat a crust o' dry bread nor help to ruin you with
extravagance. I wonder you donot take pattern by your sister Deane;
shebe far more sensible. and here youhave get two child to provide
for, and your husband's spend your fortin I' go to law, andbe
likely to spend his own too. a boil joint, as you could make broth
of for the kitchen," Mrs. Glegg add, in a tone of emphatic protest,
"and a plain pudding, with a spoonful o' sugar, and no spice, 'ud be
far more become."
with sister Glegg in this humor, there be a cheerful prospect for the
day. Mrs. Tulliver never go the length of quarrel with she, any
more than a water-fowl that put out its leg in a deprecating manner
can be say to quarrel with a boy who throw stone. but this point of
the dinner be a tender one, and not at all new, so that Mrs. Tulliver
could make the same answer she have often make before.
"Mr. Tulliver say he always _will_ have a good dinner for his friend
while he can pay for it," she say; "and hebe a right to do as he
like in his own house, sister."
"well, Bessy, _i_ cannot leave your child enough out o' my saving
to keep them from ruin. and you mustnot look to have any o' Mr.
Glegg's money, for itbe well if I donot go first,--he come of a
long-live family; and if he be to die and leave I well for my life,
hewould tie all the money up to go back to his own kin."
the sound of wheel while Mrs. Glegg be speak be an interruption
highly welcome to Mrs. Tulliver, who hasten out to receive sister
Pullet; it must be sister Pullet, because the sound be that of a
four-wheel.
Mrs. Glegg toss her head and look rather sour about the mouth at
the thought of the "four-wheel." she have a strong opinion on that
subject.
Sister Pullet be in tear when the one-horse chaise stop before
Mrs. Tulliver's door, and it be apparently requisite that she should
shed a few more before get out; for though her husband and Mrs.
Tulliver stand ready to support she, she sit still and shake her head
sadly, as she look through her tear at the vague distance.
"why, whativer be the matter, sister?" say Mrs. Tulliver. she be not
an imaginative woman, but it occur to she that the large
toilet-glass in sister Pullet's good bedroom be possibly break for
the second time.
there be no reply but a further shake of the head, as Mrs. Pullet
slowly rise and get down from the chaise, not without cast a glance
at Mr. Pullet to see that he be guard her handsome silk dress from
injury. Mr. Pullet be a small man, with a high nose, small twinkling
eye, and thin lip, in a fresh-look suit of black and a white
cravat, that seem to have be tie very tight on some high
principle than that of mere personal ease. he bear about the same
relation to his tall, good-look wife, with her balloon sleeve,
abundant mantle, and a large befeathered and beribbone bonnet, as a
small fishing-smack bear to a brig with all its sail spread.
it be a pathetic sight and a striking example of the complexity
introduce into the emotion by a high state of civilization, the
sight of a fashionably dress female in grief. from the sorrow of a
Hottentot to that of a woman in large buckram sleeve, with several
bracelet on each arm, an architectural bonnet, and delicate ribbon
string, what a long series of gradation! in the enlightened child of
civilization the abandonment characteristic of grief be check and
varied in the subtle manner, so as to present an interesting problem
to the analytic mind. if, with a crushed heart and eye half blind
by the mist of tear, she be to walk with a too-devious step through
a door-place, she might crush her buckram sleeve too, and the deep
consciousness of this possibility produce a composition of force by
which she take a line that just clear the door-post. perceive that
the tear be hurry fast, she unpin her string and throw they
languidly backward, a touching gesture, indicative, even in the
deep gloom, of the hope in future dry moment when cap-string will
once more have a charm. as the tear subside a little, and with she
head lean backward at the angle that will not injure her bonnet,
she endure that terrible moment when grief, which have make all thing
else a weariness, have itself become weary; she look down pensively at
her bracelet, and adjust their clasp with that pretty studied
fortuity which would be gratify to her mind if it be once more in
a calm and healthy state.
Mrs. Pullet brush each door-post with great nicety, about the
latitude of her shoulder (at that period a woman be truly ridiculous
to an instructed eye if she do not measure a yard and a half across
the shoulder), and have do that send the muscle of her face in
quest of fresh tear as she advance into the parlor where Mrs. Glegg
be seat.
"well, sister, yoube late; whatbe the matter?" say Mrs. Glegg,
rather sharply, as they shake hand.
Mrs. Pullet sit down, lift up her mantle carefully behind, before
she answered,--
"shebe go," unconsciously use an impressive figure of rhetoric.
"it benot the glass this time, then," think Mrs. Tulliver.
"die the day before yesterday," continue Mrs. Pullet; "an' her leg
be as thick as my body,"' she add, with deep sadness, after a
pause. "theywould tap she no end o' time, and the water--they say you
might ha' swum in it, if youhave like."
"well, Sophy, itbe a mercy shebe go, then, whoever she may be," say
Mrs. Glegg, with the promptitude and emphasis of a mind naturally
clear and decide; "but I cannot think who yoube talk of, for my
part."
"but _I_ know," say Mrs. Pullet, sigh and shake her head; "and
there benot another such a dropsy in the parish. _I_ know as itbe old
Mrs. Sutton o' the Twentylands."
"well, shebe no kin o' your, nor much acquaintance as Ihave ever
heared of," say Mrs. Glegg, who always cry just as much as be
proper when anything happen to her own "kin," but not on other
occasion.
"shebe so much acquaintance as Ihave see her leg when they be like
bladder. and an old lady as have double her money over and over
again, and keep it all in her own management to the last, and have she
pocket with her key in under her pillow constant. there benot many
old _par_ish'ner like she, I doubt."
"and they say shewould take as much physic as 'ud fill a wagon," observe
Mr. Pullet.
"ah!" sigh Mrs. Pullet, "shehave another complaint ever so many year
before she have the dropsy, and the doctor couldnot make out what it
be. and she say to I, when I go to see she last Christmas, she
say, 'Mrs. Pullet, if ever you have the dropsy, youwill think o' I.'
she _do_ say so," add Mrs. Pullet, begin to cry bitterly again;
"those be her very word. and shebe to be bury o' Saturday, and
Pullet's bid to the funeral."
"Sophy," say Mrs. Glegg, unable any long to contain her spirit of
rational remonstrance,--"sophy, I wonder _at_ you, fret and
injure your health about people as donot belong to you. your poor
father never do so, nor your aunt Frances neither, nor any o' the
family as I ever hear of. you couldnot fret no more than this, if
wewould hear as our cousin Abbott have die sudden without make his
will."
Mrs. Pullet be silent, have to finish her crying, and rather
flatter than indignant at be upbraided for cry too much. it
be not everybody who could afford to cry so much about their
neighbor who have leave they nothing; but Mrs. Pullet have marry a
gentleman farmer, and have leisure and money to carry her crying and
everything else to the high pitch of respectability.
"Mrs. Sutton donot die without make she will, though," say Mr.
Pullet, with a confused sense that he be say something to sanction
his wife's tear; "ours be a rich parish, but they say therebe nobody
else to leave as many thousand behind them as Mrs. Sutton. and shebe
leave no leggicie to speak on,--left it all in a lump to her husband's
nevvy."
"there benot much good I' be so rich, then," say Mrs. Glegg, "if
shewould get none but husband's kin to leave it to. itbe poor work when
thatbe all youhave get to pinch yourself for. not as Ibe one o' those
as 'ud like to die without leave more money out at interest than
other folk have reckon; but itbe a poor tale when it must go out o'
your own family."
"Ibe sure, sister," say Mrs. Pullet, who have recover sufficiently
to take off her veil and fold it carefully, "itbe a nice sort o' man
as Mrs. Sutton have leave her money to, for hebe trouble with the
asthmy, and go to bed every night at eight o'clock. he tell I about
it himself--as free as could be--one Sunday when he come to our
church. he wear a hareskin on his chest, and have a trembling in his
talk,--quite a gentleman sort o' man. I tell he there benot many
month in the year as I benot under the doctor's hand. and he say,
'Mrs. Pullet, I can feel for you.' that be what he said,--the very
word. ah!" sigh Mrs. Pullet, shake her head at the idea that
there be but few who could enter fully into her experience in pink
mixture and white mixture, strong stuff in small bottle, and weak
stuff in large bottle, damp bolus at a shilling, and draught at
eighteenpence. "Sister, I may as well go and take my bonnet off now.
do you see as the cap-box be put out?" she add, turn to her
husband.
Mr. Pullet, by an unaccountable lapse of memory, have forget it, and
hasten out, with a stricken conscience, to remedy the omission.
"theywill bring it upstairs, sister," say Mrs. Tulliver, wish to go
at once, lest Mrs. Glegg should begin to explain her feeling about
Sophy's be the first Dodson who ever ruin her constitution with
doctor's stuff.
Mrs. Tulliver be fond of go upstairs with her sister Pullet, and
look thoroughly at her cap before she put it on her head, and
discuss millinery in general. this be part of Bessy's weakness
that stir Mrs. Glegg's sisterly compassion: Bessy go far too well
dress, consider; and she be too proud to dress her child in the
good clothing her sister Glegg give she from the primeval strata of
her wardrobe; it be a sin and a shame to buy anything to dress that
child, if it benot a pair of shoe. in this particular, however, Mrs.
Glegg do her sister bessy some injustice, for Mrs. Tulliver have
really make great effort to induce Maggie to wear a leghorn bonnet
and a dye silk frock make out of her aunt Glegg's, but the result
have be such that Mrs. Tulliver be oblige to bury they in her
maternal bosom; for Maggie, declare that the frock smell of nasty
dye, have take an opportunity of baste it together with the roast
beef the first Sunday she wear it, and find this scheme answer, she
have subsequently pump on the bonnet with its green ribbon, so as to
give it a general resemblance to a sage cheese garnish with withered
lettuce. I must urge in excuse for Maggie, that Tom have laugh at
she in the bonnet, and say she look like an old Judy. Aunt Pullet,
too, make present of clothe, but these be always pretty enough to
please Maggie as well as her mother. of all her sister, Mrs. Tulliver
certainly prefer her sister Pullet, not without a return of
preference; but Mrs. Pullet be sorry Bessy have those naughty, awkward
child; she would do the good she could by they, but it be a pity
they benot as good and as pretty as sister Deane's child. Maggie and
Tom, on their part, think their aunt Pullet tolerable, chiefly
because she be not their aunt glegg. Tom always decline to go more
than once during his holiday to see either of they. both his uncle
tip he that once, of course; but at his aunt Pullet's there be a
great many toad to pelt in the cellar-area, so that he prefer the
visit to she. Maggie shudder at the toad, and dream of they
horribly, but she like her uncle Pullet's musical snuff-box. still,
it be agree by the sister, in Mrs. Tulliver's absence, that the
Tulliver blood do not mix well with the Dodson blood; that, in fact,
poor Bessy's child be Tullivers, and that Tom, notwithstanding he
have the Dodson complexion, be likely to be as "contrairy" as his
father. as for Maggie, she be the picture of her aunt Moss, Mr.
Tulliver's sister,--a large-boned woman, who have marry as poorly as
could be; have no china, and have a husband who have much ado to pay his
rent. but when Mrs. Pullet be alone with Mrs. Tulliver upstairs, the
remark be naturally to the disadvantage of Mrs. Glegg, and they
agree, in confidence, that there be no know what sort of fright
sister Jane would come out next. but their _tete-a-tete_ be curtail
by the appearance of Mrs. Deane with little Lucy; and Mrs. Tulliver
have to look on with a silent pang while Lucy's blond curl be
adjust. it be quite unaccountable that Mrs. Deane, the thin and
sallowest of all the Miss Dodsons, should have have this child, who
might have be take for Mrs. Tulliverbe any day. and Maggie always
look twice as dark as usual when she be by the side of Lucy.
she do to-day, when she and Tom come in from the garden with their
father and their uncle Glegg. Maggie have throw her bonnet off very
carelessly, and come in with her hair rough as well as out of curl,
rush at once to Lucy, who be stand by her mother's knee.
certainly the contrast between the cousin be conspicuous, and to
superficial eye be very much to the disadvantage of Maggie though a
connoisseur might have see "point" in she which have a high promise
for maturity than Lucy's natty completeness. it be like the contrast
between a rough, dark, overgrown puppy and a white kitten. Lucy put up
the neat little rosebud mouth to be kiss; everything about she
be neat,--her little round neck, with the row of coral bead; her
little straight nose, not at all snubby; her little clear eyebrow,
rather dark than her curl, to match hazel eye, which look up
with shy pleasure at Maggie, tall by the head, though scarcely a
year old. Maggie always look at Lucy with delight.
she be fond of fancy a world where the people never get any large
than child of their own age, and she make the queen of it just like
Lucy, with a little crown on her head, and a little sceptre in her
hand--only the queen be Maggie herself in Lucy's form.
"oh, Lucy," she burst out, after kiss she, "youwill stay with Tom
and I, willnot you? oh, kiss she, Tom."
Tom, too, have come up to Lucy, but he be not go to kiss she--no;
he come up to she with Maggie, because it seem easy, on the whole,
than say, "how do you do?" to all those aunt and uncle. he stand
look at nothing in particular, with the blushing, awkward air and
semi-smile which be common to shy boy when in company,--very much as
if they have come into the world by mistake, and find it in a degree
of undress that be quite embarrassing.
"Heyday!" say aunt Glegg, with loud emphasis. "do little boy and
gell come into a room without take notice of their uncle and
aunt? that benot the way when _I_ be a little gell."
"go and speak to your aunt and uncle, my dear," say Mrs. Tulliver,
look anxious and melancholy. she want to whisper to Maggie a
command to go and have her hair brush.
"well, and how do you do? and I hope yoube good child, be you?"
say Aunt Glegg, in the same loud, emphatic way, as she take their
hand, hurt they with her large ring, and kiss their cheek
much against their desire. "look up, Tom, look up. boy as go to
boarding-school should hold their head up. look at I now." Tom
decline that pleasure apparently, for he try to draw his hand away.
"put your hair behind your ear, Maggie, and keep your frock on your
shoulder."
Aunt Glegg always speak to they in this loud, emphatic way, as if she
consider they deaf, or perhaps rather idiotic; it be a means, she
think, of make they feel that they be accountable creature, and
might be a salutary check on naughty tendency. Bessy's child be
so spoiled--they'd need have somebody to make they feel their duty.
"well, my dear," say aunt Pullet, in a compassionate voice, "you
grow wonderful fast. I doubt theywill outgrow their strength," she
add, look over their head, with a melancholy expression, at
their mother. "I think the gell have too much hair. Iwould have it thin
and cut short, sister, if I be you; it benot good for her health.
itbe that as make her skin so brown, I shouldnot wonder. donot you
think so, sister Deane?"
"I cannot say, Ibe sure, sister," say Mrs. Deane, shut her lip
close again, and look at Maggie with a critical eye.
"no, no," say Mr. Tulliver, "the childbe healthy enough; therebe
nothing ail she. there's red wheat as well as white, for that matter,
and some like the dark grain well. but it 'ud be as well if Bessy 'ud
have the child's hair cut, so as it 'ud lie smooth."
a dreadful resolve be gather in Maggie's breast, but it be
arrest by the desire to know from her aunt Deane whether she would
leave Lucy behind. Aunt Deane would hardly ever let Lucy come to see
they. after various reason for refusal, Mrs. Deane appeal to Lucy
herself.
"you wouldnot like to stay behind without mother, should you, Lucy?"
"yes, please, mother," say Lucy, timidly, blush very pink all over
her little neck.
"well do, Lucy! let she stay, Mrs. Deane, let she stay," say Mr.
Deane, a large but alert-look man, with a type of _physique_ to be
see in all rank of English society,--bald crown, red whisker, full
forehead, and general solidity without heaviness. you may see nobleman
like Mr. Deane, and you may see grocer or day-laborer like he; but
the keenness of his brown eye be less common than his contour.
he hold a silver snuff-box very tightly in his hand, and now and then
exchange a pinch with Mr. Tulliver, whose box be only
silver-mount, so that it be naturally a joke between they that Mr.
Tulliver want to exchange snuff-box also. Mr. Deane's box have be
give he by the superior partner in the firm to which he belong,
at the same time that they give he a share in the business, in
acknowledgment of his valuable service as manager. no man be think
more highly of in St. Ogg's than Mr. Deane; and some person be even
of opinion that Miss Susan Dodson, who be once hold to have make the
bad match of all the Dodson sister, might one day ride in a well
carriage, and live in a well house, even than her sister Pullet.
there be no knowing where a man would stop, who have get his foot into
a great mill-owning, shipowne business like that of Guest & Co.,
with a banking concern attach. and Mrs. Deane, as her intimate
female friend observe, be proud and "have" enough; _she_ wouldnot
let her husband stand still in the world for want of spur.
"Maggie," say Mrs. Tulliver, beckon Maggie to she, and whispering
in her ear, as soon as this point of Lucy's staying be settle, "go
and get your hair brush, do, for shame. I tell you not to come in
without go to Martha first, you know I do."
"Tom come out with I," whisper Maggie, pull his sleeve as she
pass he; and Tom follow willingly enough.
"come upstairs with I, Tom," she whisper, when they be outside
the door. "therebe something I want to do before dinner."
"therebe no time to play at anything before dinner," say Tom, whose
imagination be impatient of any intermediate prospect.
"oh yes, there be time for this; _do_ come, Tom."
Tom follow Maggie upstairs into her mother's room, and see she go at
once to a drawer, from which she take out a large pair of scissor.
"what be they for, Maggie?" say Tom, feel his curiosity awaken.
Maggie answer by seize her front lock and cut they straight
across the middle of her forehead.
"oh, my button! Maggie, youwill catch it!" exclaim Tom; "youwould
well not cut any more off."
Snip! go the great scissor again while Tom be speak, and he
couldnot help feel it be rather good fun; Maggie would look so
queer.
"here, Tom, cut it behind for I," say Maggie, excite by her own
daring, and anxious to finish the deed.
"youwill catch it, you know," say Tom, nod his head in an
admonitory manner, and hesitate a little as he take the scissor.
"never mind, make haste!" say Maggie, give a little stamp with her
foot. her cheek be quite flushed.
the black lock be so thick, nothing could be more tempting to a lad
who have already taste the forbidden pleasure of cut the pony's
mane. I speak to those who know the satisfaction of make a pair of
scissor meet through a duly resist mass of hair. one delicious
grind snip, and then another and another, and the hinder-lock fall
heavily on the floor, and Maggie stand crop in a jagged, uneven
manner, but with a sense of clearness and freedom, as if she have
emerge from a wood into the open plain.
"oh, Maggie," say Tom, jump round she, and slap his knee as
he laugh, "oh, my button! what a queer thing you look! look at
yourself in the glass; you look like the idiot we throw out nutshell
to at school."
Maggie feel an unexpected pang. she have think beforehand chiefly at
her own deliverance from her teasing hair and teasing remark about
it, and something also of the triumph she should have over her mother
and her aunt by this very decide course of action; she donot want
her hair to look pretty,--that be out of the question,--she only
want people to think she a clever little girl, and not to find fault
with she. but now, when Tom begin to laugh at she, and say she be
like an idiot, the affair have quite a new aspect. she look in the
glass, and still Tom laugh and clap his hand, and Maggie's
cheek begin to pale, and her lip to tremble a little.
"oh, Maggie, youwill have to go down to dinner directly," say Tom.
"oh, my!"
"donot laugh at I, Tom," say Maggie, in a passionate tone, with an
outburst of angry tear, stamping, and give he a push.
"now, then, spitfire!" say Tom. "what do you cut it off for, then? I
shall go down: I can smell the dinner go in."
he hurry downstairs and leave poor Maggie to that bitter sense of the
irrevocable which be almost an every-day experience of her small
soul. she could see clearly enough, now the thing be do, that it
be very foolish, and that she should have to hear and think more
about her hair than ever; for Maggie rush to her deed with
passionate impulse, and then see not only their consequence, but what
would have happen if they have not be do, with all the detail and
exaggerated circumstance of an active imagination. Tom never do the
same sort of foolish thing as Maggie, have a wonderful instinctive
discernment of what would turn to his advantage or disadvantage; and
so it happen, that though he be much more wilful and inflexible
than Maggie, his mother hardly ever call he naughty. but if Tom do
make a mistake of that sort, he espouse it, and stand by it: he
"donot mind." if he break the lash of his father's gigwhip by lash
the gate, he couldnot help it,--the whip shouldnot have get catch in
the hinge. if Tom Tulliver whip a gate, he be convince, not that
the whipping of gate by all boy be a justifiable act, but that he,
Tom Tulliver, be justifiable in whip that particular gate, and he
benot go to be sorry. but Maggie, as she stand cry before the
glass, feel it impossible that she should go down to dinner and endure
the severe eye and severe word of her aunt, while Tom and Lucy, and
Martha, who wait at table, and perhaps her father and her uncle,
would laugh at she; for if Tom have laugh at she, of course every one
else would; and if she have only let her hair alone, she could have sit
with Tom and Lucy, and have the apricot pudding and the custard! what
could she do but sob? she sit as helpless and despairing among her
black lock as Ajax among the slaughter sheep. very trivial,
perhaps, this anguish seem to weather-wear mortal who have to think
of Christmas bill, dead love, and broken friendship; but it be not
less bitter to Maggie--perhaps it be even more bitter--than what we
be fond of call antithetically the real trouble of mature life.
"ah, my child, you will have real trouble to fret about by and by,"
be the consolation we have almost all of we have administer to we in
our childhood, and have repeat to other child since we have be
grow up. we have all of we sob so piteously, stand with tiny
bare leg above our little sock, when we lose sight of our mother or
nurse in some strange place; but we can no long recall the poignancy
of that moment and weep over it, as we do over the remember
suffering of five or ten year ago. every one of those keen moment
have leave its trace, and live in we still, but such trace have blent
themselves irrecoverably with the firm texture of our youth and
manhood; and so it come that we can look on at the trouble of our
child with a smile disbelief in the reality of their pain. be
there any one who can recover the experience of his childhood, not
merely with a memory _of_ what he do and what happen to he, of
what he like and dislike when he be in frock and trouser, but with
an intimate penetration, a revive consciousness of what he feel then,
when it be so long from one Midsummer to another; what he feel when
his school fellow shut he out of their game because he would pitch
the ball wrong out of mere wilfulness; or on a rainy day in the
holiday, when he donot know how to amuse himself, and fall from
idleness into mischief, from mischief into defiance, and from defiance
into sulkiness; or when his mother absolutely refuse to let he have
a tail coat that "half," although every other boy of his age have
go into tail already? surely if we could recall that early
bitterness, and the dim guess, the strangely perspectiveless
conception of life, that give the bitterness its intensity, we should
not pooh-pooh the grief of our child.
"Miss Maggie, yoube to come down this minute," say Kezia, enter
the room hurriedly. "Lawks! what have you be a-doing? I never _see_
such a fright!"
"donot, Kezia," say Maggie, angrily. "go away!"
"but I tell you yoube to come down, Miss, this minute; your mother
say so," say Kezia, go up to Maggie and take she by the hand to
raise she from the floor.
"get away, Kezia; I donot want any dinner," say Maggie, resist
Kezia's arm. "I sha'n't come."
"oh, well, I cannot stay. Ihave get to wait at dinner," say Kezia,
go out again.
"Maggie, you little silly," say Tom, peep into the room ten
minute after, "why donot you come and have your dinner? therebe lot
o' goody, and mother say yoube to come. what be you cry for,
you little spooney?"
oh, it be dreadful! Tom be so hard and unconcerned; if _he_ have be
cry on the floor, Maggie would have cry too. and there be the
dinner, so nice; and she be _so_ hungry. it be very bitter.
but Tom be not altogether hard. he be not inclined to cry, and do
not feel that Maggie's grief spoil his prospect of the sweet; but
he go and put his head near she, and say in a low, comfort
tone,--
"willnot you come, then, Magsie? shall I bring you a bit o' pudding when
Ihave have mine, and a custard and thing?"
"ye-e-es," say Maggie, begin to feel life a little more
tolerable.
"very well," say Tom, go away. but he turn again at the door and
say, "but youwould well come, you know. therebe the dessert,--nuts,
you know, and cowslip wine."
Maggie's tear have cease, and she look reflective as Tom leave she.
his good nature have take off the keen edge of her suffering, and
nut with cowslip wine begin to assert their legitimate influence.
slowly she rise from amongst her scatter lock, and slowly she make
her way downstairs. then she stand lean with one shoulder against
the frame of the dining-parlour door, peep in when it be ajar. she
see Tom and Lucy with an empty chair between they, and there be the
custard on a side-table; it be too much. she slip in and go
toward the empty chair. but she have no soon sit down than she
repent and wish herself back again.
Mrs. Tulliver give a little scream as she see she, and feel such a
"turn" that she drop the large gravy-spoon into the dish, with the
most serious result to the table-cloth. for Kezia have not betray
the reason of Maggie's refusal to come down, not like to give she
mistress a shock in the moment of carving, and Mrs. Tulliver think
there be nothing bad in question than a fit of perverseness, which
be inflict its own punishment by deprive Maggie of half her
dinner.
Mrs. Tulliver's scream make all eye turn towards the same point as
her own, and Maggie's cheek and ear begin to burn, while uncle
Glegg, a kind-look, white-haired old gentleman, said,--
"Heyday! what little gell's this? why, I donot know she. be it some
little gell youhave pick up in the road, Kezia?"
"why, shebe go and cut her hair herself," say Mr. Tulliver in an
undertone to Mr. Deane, laugh with much enjoyment. "do you ever
know such a little hussy as it be?"
"why, little miss, youhave make yourself look very funny," say Uncle
Pullet, and perhaps he never in his life make an observation which be
feel to be so lacerate.
"Fie, for shame!" say aunt Glegg, in her loud, severest tone of
reproof. "little gell as cut their own hair should be whip and feed
on bread and water,--not come and sit down with their aunt and
uncle."
"ay, ay," say uncle Glegg, mean to give a playful turn to this
denunciation, "she must be send to jail, I think, and theywill cut the
rest of her hair off there, and make it all even."
"shebe more like a gypsy nor ever," say aunt Pullet, in a pitying
tone; "itbe very bad luck, sister, as the gell should be so brown; the
boybe fair enough. I doubt itwill stand in her way I' life to be so
brown."
"shebe a naughty child, as'll break her mother's heart," say Mrs.
Tulliver, with the tear in her eye.
Maggie seem to be listen to a chorus of reproach and derision.
her first flush come from anger, which give she a transient power of
defiance, and Tom think she be brave it out, support by the
recent appearance of the pudding and custard. under this impression,
he whisper, "oh, my! Maggie, I tell you youwould catch it." he mean to
be friendly, but Maggie feel convinced that Tom be rejoice in her
ignominy. her feeble power of defiance leave she in an instant, her
heart swell, and get up from her chair, she run to her father,
hide her face on his shoulder, and burst out into loud sobbing.
"come, come, my wench," say her father, soothingly, put his arm
round she, "never mind; you be I' the right to cut it off if it
plague you; give over crying; father'll take your part."
Delicious word of tenderness! Maggie never forget any of these
moment when her father "take her part"; she keep they in her heart,
and thought of they long year after, when every one else say that
her father have do very ill by his child.
"how your husband do spoil that child, Bessy!" say Mrs. Glegg, in a
loud "aside," to Mrs. Tulliver. "itwill be the ruin of she, if you
donot take care. _my_ father never bring his child up so, else we
should ha' be a different sort o' family to what we be."
Mrs. Tulliver's domestic sorrow seem at this moment to have reach
the point at which insensibility begin. she take no notice of her
sister's remark, but throw back her capstring and dispense the
pudding, in mute resignation.
with the dessert there come entire deliverance for Maggie, for the
child be tell they might have their nut and wine in the
summer-house, since the day be so mild; and they scamper out among
the bud bush of the garden with the alacrity of small animal
get from under a burn glass.
Mrs. Tulliver have her special reason for this permission: now the
dinner be despatch, and every one's mind disengage, it be the
right moment to communicate Mr. Tulliver's intention concern Tom,
and it would be as well for Tom himself to be absent. the child
be use to hear themselves talk of as freely as if they be
bird, and could understand nothing, however they might stretch their
neck and listen; but on this occasion Mrs. Tulliver manifest an
unusual discretion, because she have recently have evidence that the
go to school to a clergyman be a sore point with Tom, who look
at it as very much on a par with go to school to a constable. Mrs.
Tulliver have a sighing sense that her husband would do as he like,
whatever sister Glegg say, or sister Pullet either; but at least they
would not be able to say, if the thing turn out ill, that Bessy have
fall in with her husband's folly without let her own friend
know a word about it.
"Mr. Tulliver," she say, interrupt her husband in his talk with
Mr. Deane, "itbe time now to tell the child's aunt and uncle what
yoube think of do with Tom, benot it?"
"very well," say Mr. Tulliver, rather sharply, "Ihave no objection to
tell anybody what I mean to do with he. Ihave settle," he add,
look toward Mr. Glegg and Mr. Deane,--"I've settle to send he to
a Mr. Stelling, a parson, down at King's Lorton, there,--an uncommon
clever fellow, I understand, as'll put he up to most thing."
there be a rustling demonstration of surprise in the company, such as
you may have observe in a country congregation when they hear an
allusion to their week-day affair from the pulpit. it be equally
astonishing to the aunt and uncle to find a parson introduce into
Mr. Tulliver's family arrangement. as for uncle Pullet, he could
hardly have be more thoroughly obfuscate if Mr. Tulliver have say
that he be go to send Tom to the Lord Chancellor; for uncle Pullet
belong to that extinct class of british yeoman who, dress in good
broadcloth, pay high rate and taxis, go to church, and eat a
particularly good dinner on Sunday, without dream that the british
constitution in Church and State have a traceable origin any more than
the solar system and the fix star.
it be melancholy, but true, that Mr. Pullet have the most confused idea
of a bishop as a sort of a baronet, who might or might not be a
clergyman; and as the rector of his own parish be a man of high
family and fortune, the idea that a clergyman could be a schoolmaster
be too remote from Mr. Pullet's experience to be readily conceivable.
I know it be difficult for people in these instruct time to believe
in uncle Pullet's ignorance; but let they reflect on the remarkable
result of a great natural faculty under favor circumstance. and
uncle Pullet have a great natural faculty for ignorance. he be the
first to give utterance to his astonishment.
"why, what can you be go to send he to a parson for?" he say,
with an amazed twinkling in his eye, look at Mr. Glegg and Mr.
Deane, to see if they show any sign of comprehension.
"why, because the parson be the good schoolmaster, by what I can
make out," say poor Mr. Tulliver, who, in the maze of this puzzling
world, lay hold of any clue with great readiness and tenacity.
"Jacobs at th' academy's no parson, and hebe do very bad by the boy;
and I make up my mind, if I send he to school again, it should be to
somebody different to Jacobs. and this Mr. Stelling, by what I can
make out, be the sort o' man I want. and I mean my boy to go to he at
Midsummer," he conclude, in a tone of decision, tap his snuff-box
and take a pinch.
"youwill have to pay a swinging half-yearly bill, then, eh, Tulliver?
the clergyman have highish notion, in general," say Mr. Deane,
take snuff vigorously, as he always do when wish to maintain a
neutral position.
"what! do you think the parson'll teach he to know a good sample o'
wheat when he see it, neighbor Tulliver?" say Mr. Glegg, who be
fond of his jest, and have retire from business, feel that it be
not only allowable but become in he to take a playful view of
thing.
"why, you see, Ihave get a plan I' my head about Tom," say Mr.
Tulliver, pause after that statement and lift up his glass.
"well, if I may be allow to speak, and itbe seldom as I be," say
Mrs. Glegg, with a tone of bitter meaning, "I should like to know what
good be to come to the boy by bringin' he up above his fortin."
"why," say Mr. Tulliver, not look at Mrs. Glegg, but at the male
part of his audience, "you see, Ihave make up my mind not to bring Tom
up to my own business. Ihave have my thought about it all along, and I
make up my mind by what I see with Garnett and _his_ son. I mean to
put he to some business as he can go into without capital, and I want
to give he an eddication as hewill be even wi' the lawyer and folk,
and put I up to a notion now an' then."
Mrs. Glegg emit a long sort of guttural sound with closed lip,
that smile in mingled pity and scorn.
"it 'ud be a fine deal well for some people," she say, after that
introductory note, "if theywould let the lawyer alone."
"be he at the head of a grammar school, then, this clergyman, such as
that at Market Bewley?" say Mr. Deane.
"no, nothing of that," say Mr. Tulliver. "he willnot take more than two
or three pupil, and so hewill have the more time to attend to them, you
know."
"ah, and get his eddication do the soon; they cannot learn much at
a time when therebe so many of them," say uncle Pullet, feel that
he be get quite an insight into this difficult matter.
"but hewill want the more pay, I doubt," say Mr. Glegg.
"ay, ay, a cool hundred a year, thatbe all," say Mr. Tulliver, with
some pride at his own spirited course. "but then, you know, itbe an
investment; Tom's eddication 'ull be so much capital to he."
"ay, therebe something in that," say Mr. Glegg. "well well, neighbor
Tulliver, you may be right, you may be right:
'when land be go and money's spend,
then learn be most excellent.'
"I remember see those two line write on a window at Buxton. but we
that have get no learning have well keep our money, eh, neighbor
Pullet?" Mr. Glegg rub his knee, and look very pleasant.
"Mr. Glegg, I wonder _at_ you," say his wife. "itbe very unbecoming
in a man o' your age and belonging."
"whatbe unbecoming, Mrs. G.?" say Mr. Glegg, wink pleasantly at
the company. "my new blue coat as Ihave get on?"
"I pity your weakness, Mr. Glegg. I say itbe unbecoming to be make a
joke when you see your own kin go headlong to ruin."
"if you mean I by that," say Mr. Tulliver, considerably nettled,
"you neednot trouble yourself to fret about I. I can manage my own
affair without trouble other folk."
"bless I!" say Mr. Deane, judiciously introduce a new idea, "why,
now I come to think of it, somebody say Wakem be go to send _his_
son--the deform lad--to a clergyman, donot they, Susan?" (appeal
to his wife).
"I can give no account of it, Ibe sure," say Mrs. Deane, close her
lip very tightly again. Mrs. Deane be not a woman to take part in a
scene where missile be fly.
"well," say Mr. Tulliver, speak all the more cheerfully, that Mrs.
Glegg might see he donot mind she, "if Wakem think o' send his
son to a clergyman, depend on it I shall make no mistake I' send
Tom to one. Wakem's as big a scoundrel as Old Harry ever make, but he
know the length of every man's foot hebe get to deal with. ay, ay,
tell I whobe Wakem's butcher, and Iwill tell you where to get your
meat."
"but lawyer Wakem's son's get a hump-back," say Mrs. Pullet, who feel
as if the whole business have a funereal aspect; "itbe more nat'ral to
send _he_ to a clergyman."
"yes," say Mr. Glegg, interpret Mrs. Pullet's observation with
erroneous plausibility, "you must consider that, neighbor Tulliver;
Wakem's son benot likely to follow any business. Wakem 'ull make a
gentleman of he, poor fellow."
"Mr. Glegg," say Mrs. G., in a tone which imply that her
indignation would fizz and ooze a little, though she be determined to
keep it cork up, "youwould far well hold your tongue. Mr. Tulliver
donot want to know your opinion nor mine either. therebe folk in
the world as know well than everybody else."
"why, I should think thatbe you, if webe to trust your own tale,"
say Mr. Tulliver, begin to boil up again.
"oh, _i_ say nothing," say Mrs. Glegg, sarcastically. "my advice have
never be ask, and I donot give it."
"itwill be the first time, then," say Mr. Tulliver. "itbe the only
thing yoube over-ready at give."
"Ihave be over-ready at lending, then, if I havenot be over-ready
at give," say Mrs. Glegg. "therebe folk Ihave lend money to, as
perhaps I shall repent o' lend money to kin."
"come, come, come," say Mr. Glegg, soothingly. but Mr. Tulliver be
not to be hinder of his retort.
"youhave get a bond for it, I reckon," he say; "and youhave have your
five per cent, kin or no kin."
"Sister," say Mrs. Tulliver, pleadingly, "drink your wine, and let I
give you some almond and raisin."
"Bessy, Ibe sorry for you," say Mrs. Glegg, very much with the
feeling of a cur that seize the opportunity of divert his bark
toward the man who carry no stick. "itbe poor work talk o'
almond and raisin."
"lor, sister Glegg, donot be so quarrelsome," say Mrs. Pullet,
begin to cry a little. "you may be strike with a fit, get so
red in the face after dinner, and we be but just out o' mourning, all
of us,--and all wi' gown crap alike and just put by; itbe very bad
among sister."
"I should think it _be_ bad," say Mrs. Glegg. "thing be come to a
fine pass when one sister invite the other to her house o' purpose to
quarrel with she and abuse she."
"softly, softly, Jane; be reasonable, be reasonable," say Mr. Glegg.
but while he be speak, Mr. Tulliver, who have by no mean say
enough to satisfy his anger, burst out again.
"who want to quarrel with you?" he say. "itbe you as cannot let
people alone, but must be gnaw at them forever. _I_ should never
want to quarrel with any woman if she keep her place."
"my place, indeed!" say Mrs. Glegg, get rather more shrill.
"therebe your better, Mr. Tulliver, as be dead and in their grave,
treat I with a different sort o' respect to what you do; _though_
Ihave get a husband as'll sit by and see I abuse by they as 'ud never
ha' have the chance if there havenot be they in our family as marry
bad than they might ha' do."
"if you talk o' that," say Mr. Tulliver, "my family's as good as
your, and well, for it havenot get a damned ill-temper woman in
it!"
"well," say Mrs. Glegg, rise from her chair, "I donot know whether
you think itbe a fine thing to sit by and hear I swear at, Mr. Glegg;
but Ibe not go to stay a minute long in this house. you can stay
behind, and come home with the gig, and Iwill walk home."
"dear heart, dear heart!" say Mr. Glegg in a melancholy tone, as he
follow his wife out of the room.
"Mr. Tulliver, how could you talk so?" say Mrs. Tulliver, with the
tear in her eye.
"let she go," say Mr. Tulliver, too hot to be damp by any amount of
tear. "let she go, and the soon the well; she willnot be try to
domineer over _I_ again in a hurry."
"Sister Pullet," say Mrs. Tulliver, helplessly, "do you think it 'ud
be any use for you to go after she and try to pacify she?"
"well not, well not," say Mr. Deane. "youwill make it up another
day."
"then, sister, shall we go and look at the child?" say Mrs.
Tulliver, dry her eye.
no proposition could have be more seasonable. Mr. Tulliver feel very
much as if the air have be clear of obtrusive fly now the woman
be out of the room. there be few thing he like well than a
chat with Mr. Deane, whose close application to business allow the
pleasure very rarely. Mr. Deane, he consider, be the "knowing"
man of his acquaintance, and he have besides a ready causticity of
tongue that make an agreeable supplement to Mr. Tulliver's own
tendency that way, which have remain in rather an inarticulate
condition. and now the woman be go, they could carry on their
serious talk without frivolous interruption. they could exchange their
view concern the Duke of Wellington, whose conduct in the Catholic
Question have throw such an entirely new light on his character; and
speak slightingly of his conduct at the battle of Waterloo, which he
would never have win if there havenot be a great many englishman at
his back, not to speak of Blucher and the Prussians, who, as Mr.
Tulliver have hear from a person of particular knowledge in that
matter, have come up in the very nick of time; though here there be a
slight dissidence, Mr. Deane remark that he be not dispose to
give much credit to the prussians,--the build of their vessel,
together with the unsatisfactory character of transaction in Dantzic
beer, incline he to form rather a low view of prussian pluck
generally. rather beat on this ground, Mr. Tulliver proceed to
express his fear that the country could never again be what it use
to be; but Mr. Deane, attach to a firm of which the return be on
the increase, naturally take a more lively view of the present, and
have some detail to give concern the state of the import,
especially in hide and spelter, which soothe Mr. Tulliver's
imagination by throw into more distant perspective the period when
the country would become utterly the prey of Papists and Radicals, and
there would be no more chance for honest man.
Uncle Pullet sit by and listen with twinkle eye to these high
matter. he donot understand politic himself,--thought they be a
natural gift,--but by what he could make out, this Duke of Wellington
be no well than he should be.
Chapter VIII
Mr. Tulliver show his weak side
"suppose sister Glegg should call her money in; it 'ud be very awkward
for you to have to raise five hundred pound now," say Mrs. Tulliver
to her husband that evening, as she take a plaintive review of the
day.
Mrs. Tulliver have live thirteen year with her husband, yet she
retain in all the freshness of her early married life a facility of
say thing which drive he in the opposite direction to the one she
desire. some mind be wonderful for keep their bloom in this way,
as a patriarchal goldfish apparently retain to the last its youthful
illusion that it can swim in a straight line beyond the encircling
glass. Mrs. Tulliver be an amiable fish of this kind, and after
run her head against the same resist medium for thirteen year
would go at it again to-day with undulled alacrity.
this observation of hers tend directly to convince Mr. Tulliver that
it would not be at all awkward for he to raise five hundred pound;
and when Mrs. Tulliver become rather press to know _how_ he would
raise it without mortgage the mill and the house which he have say
he never _would_ mortgage, since nowadays people be none so ready to
lend money without security, Mr. Tulliver, get warm, declare that
Mrs. Glegg might do as she like about call in her money, he should
pay it in whether or not. he be not go to be beholden to his
wife's sister. when a man have marry into a family where there be a
whole litter of woman, he might have plenty to put up with if he
choose. but Mr. Tulliver do _not_ choose.
Mrs. Tulliver cry a little in a trickling, quiet way as she put on
her nightcap; but presently sink into a comfortable sleep, lull by
the thought that she would talk everything over with her sister Pullet
to-morrow, when she be to take the child to Garum Firs to tea. not
that she look forward to any distinct issue from that talk; but it
seem impossible that past event should be so obstinate as to remain
unmodified when they be complain against.
her husband lie awake rather long, for he too be think of a
visit he would pay on the morrow; and his idea on the subject be
not of so vague and soothe a kind as those of his amiable partner.
Mr. Tulliver, when under the influence of a strong feeling, have a
promptitude in action that may seem inconsistent with that painful
sense of the complicated, puzzle nature of human affair under which
his more dispassionate deliberation be conduct; but it be really
not improbable that there be a direct relation between these
apparently contradictory phenomenon, since I have observe that for
get a strong impression that a skein be tangle there be nothing
like snatch hastily at a single thread. it be owe to this
promptitude that Mr. Tulliver be on horseback soon after dinner the
next day (he be not dyspeptic) on his way to basset to see his sister
Moss and her husband. for have make up his mind irrevocably that he
would pay Mrs. Glegg her loan of five hundred pound, it naturally
occur to he that he have a promissory note for three hundred pound
lend to his brother-in-law Moss; and if the say brother-in-law could
manage to pay in the money within a give time, it would go far to
lessen the fallacious air of inconvenience which Mr. Tulliver's
spirited step might have wear in the eye of weak people who require
to know precisely _how_ a thing be to be do before they be strongly
confident that it will be easy.
for Mr. Tulliver be in a position neither new nor striking, but, like
other every-day thing, sure to have a cumulative effect that will be
feel in the long run: he be hold to be a much more substantial man
than he really be. and as we be all apt to believe what the world
believe about we, it be his habit to think of failure and ruin with
the same sort of remote pity with which a spare, long-neck man hear
that his plethoric short-neck neighbor be stricken with apoplexy. he
have be always use to hear pleasant joke about his advantage as a
man who work his own mill, and own a pretty bit of land; and these
joke naturally keep up his sense that he be a man of considerable
substance. they give a pleasant flavor to his glass on a market-day,
and if it have not be for the recurrence of half-yearly payment, Mr.
Tulliver would really have forget that there be a mortgage of two
thousand pound on his very desirable freehold. that be not
altogether his own fault, since one of the thousand pound be his
sister's fortune, which he have to pay on her marriage; and a man who
have neighbor that _will_ go to law with he be not likely to pay off
his mortgage, especially if he enjoy the good opinion of
acquaintance who want to borrow a hundred pound on security too
lofty to be represent by parchment. our friend Mr. Tulliver have a
good-natured fibre in he, and do not like to give harsh refusal
even to his sister, who have not only come in to the world in that
superfluous way characteristic of sister, create a necessity for
mortgage, but have quite throw herself away in marriage, and have
crown her mistake by have an eighth baby. on this point Mr.
Tulliver be conscious of be a little weak; but he apologize to
himself by say that poor Gritty have be a good-look wench
before she marry Moss; he would sometimes say this even with a
slight tremulousness in his voice. but this morning he be in a mood
more become a man of business, and in the course of his ride along
the Basset lane, with their deep ruts,--lying so far away from a
market-town that the labor of draw produce and manure be enough to
take away the good part of the profit on such poor land as that
parish be make of,--he get up a due amount of irritation against Moss
as a man without capital, who, if murrain and blight be abroad, be
sure to have his share of they, and who, the more you try to help
he out of the mud, would sink the far in. it would do he good
rather than harm, now, if he be oblige to raise this three hundred
pound; it would make he look about he well, and not act so
foolishly about his wool this year as he do the last; in fact, Mr.
Tulliver have be too easy with his brother-in-law, and because he have
let the interest run on for two year, Moss be likely enough to think
that he should never be trouble about the principal. but Mr. Tulliver
be determine not to encourage such shuffle people any long; and
a ride along the Basset lane be not likely to enervate a man's
resolution by soften his temper. the deep-trodden hoof-mark, make
in the muddy day of winter, give he a shake now and then which
suggest a rash but stimulate snarl at the father of lawyer, who,
whether by mean of his hoof or otherwise, have doubtless something to
do with this state of the road; and the abundance of foul land and
neglect fence that meet his eye, though they make no part of his
brother Moss's farm, strongly contribute to his dissatisfaction with
that unlucky agriculturist. if this benot Moss's fallow, it might
have be; Basset be all alike; it be a beggarly parish, in Mr.
Tulliver's opinion, and his opinion be certainly not groundless.
Basset have a poor soil, poor road, a poor non-resident landlord, a
poor non-resident vicar, and rather less than half a curate, also
poor. if any one strongly impress with the power of the human mind
to triumph over circumstance will contend that the parishioner of
Basset might nevertheless have be a very superior class of people, I
have nothing to urge against that abstract proposition; I only know
that, in point of fact, the Basset mind be in strict keeping with its
circumstance. the muddy lane, green or clayey, that seem to the
unaccustomed eye to lead nowhere but into each other, do really lead,
with patience, to a distant high-road; but there be many foot in
basset which they lead more frequently to a centre of dissipation,
speak of formerly as the "Markis o' Granby," but among intimate as
"Dickison's." a large low room with a sanded floor; a cold scent of
tobacco, modify by undetected beer-dreg; Mr. Dickison lean
against the door-post with a melancholy pimple face, look as
irrelevant to the daylight as a last night's guttered candle,--all
this may not seem a very seductive form of temptation; but the
majority of man in Basset find it fatally alluring when encounter
on their road toward four o'clock on a wintry afternoon; and if any
wife in Basset wish to indicate that her husband be not a
pleasure-seek man, she could hardly do it more emphatically than by
say that he donot spend a shilling at Dickison's from one
Whitsuntide to another. Mrs. Moss have say so of _she_ husband more
than once, when her brother be in a mood to find fault with he, as
he certainly be to-day. and nothing could be less pacifying to Mr.
Tulliver than the behavior of the farmyard gate, which he no soon
attempt to push open with his riding-stick than it act as gate
without the upper hinge be know to do, to the peril of shin,
whether equine or human. he be about to get down and lead his horse
through the damp dirt of the hollow farmyard, shadow drearily by the
large half-timbere building, up to the long line of tumble-down
dwelling-house stand on a raise causeway; but the timely
appearance of a cowboy save he that frustration of a plan he have
determine on,--namely, not to get down from his horse during this
visit. if a man mean to be hard, let he keep in his saddle and speak
from that height, above the level of plead eye, and with the
command of a distant horizon. Mrs. Moss hear the sound of the horse's
foot, and, when her brother ride up, be already outside the kitchen
door, with a half-weary smile on her face, and a black-eyed baby in
her arm. Mrs. Moss's face bear a fade resemblance to her brother's;
baby's little fat hand, press against her cheek, seem to show more
strikingly that the cheek be fade.
"Brother, Ibe glad to see you," she say, in an affectionate tone. "I
donot look for you to-day. how do you do?"
"oh, pretty well, Mrs. Moss, pretty well," answer the brother, with
cool deliberation, as if it be rather too forward of she to ask that
question. she know at once that her brother be not in a good humor;
he never call she Mrs. Moss except when he be angry, and when they
be in company. but she think it be in the order of nature that
people who be poorly off should be snub. Mrs. Moss do not take
her stand on the equality of the human race; she be a patient,
prolific, love-hearted woman.
"your husband benot in the house, I suppose?" add Mr. Tulliver after
a grave pause, during which four child have run out, like chicken
whose mother have be suddenly in eclipse behind the hen-coop.
"no," say Mrs. Moss, "but hebe only in the potato-field yonder.
georgy, run to the Far Close in a minute, and tell father your uncle's
come. youwill get down, brother, willnot you, and take something?"
"no, no; I cannot get down. I must be go home again directly," say
Mr. Tulliver, look at the distance.
"and howbe Mrs. Tulliver and the child?" say Mrs. Moss, humbly,
not dare to press her invitation.
"oh, pretty well. Tombe go to a new school at Midsummer,--a deal of
expense to I. itbe bad work for I, lie out o' my money."
"I wish youwould be so good as let the child come and see their
cousin some day. my little uns want to see their cousin Maggie so as
never be. and I her godmother, and so fond of she; therebe nobody
'ud make a big fuss with she, accord to what theyhave get. and I
know she like to come, for shebe a love child, and how quick and
clever she be, to be sure!"
if Mrs. Moss have be one of the most astute woman in the world,
instead of be one of the simple, she could have think of
nothing more likely to propitiate her brother than this praise of
Maggie. he seldom find any one volunteering praise of "the little
wench"; it be usually leave entirely to himself to insist on her
merit. but Maggie always appear in the most amiable light at her
aunt Moss's; it be her Alsatia, where she be out of the reach of
law,--if she upset anything, dirty her shoe, or tear her frock,
these thing be matter of course at her aunt Moss's. in spite of
himself, Mr. Tulliver's eye get milder, and he do not look away from
his sister as he said,--
"ay; shebe fonder o' you than o' the other aunt, I think. she take
after our family: not a bit of her mother's in she."
"Moss say shebe just like what I use to be," say Mrs. Moss, "though
I be never so quick and fond o' the book. but I think my Lizzy's
like she; _shebe_ sharp. come here, Lizzy, my dear, and let your uncle
see you; he hardly know you, you grow so fast."
Lizzy, a black-eyed child of seven, look very shy when her mother
draw she forward, for the small Mosses be much in awe of their uncle
from Dorlcote Mill. she be inferior enough to Maggie in fire and
strength of expression to make the resemblance between the two
entirely flattering to Mr. Tulliver's fatherly love.
"ay, theybe a bit alike," he say, look kindly at the little
figure in the soil pinafore. "they both take after our mother.
youhave get enough o' gell, Gritty," he add, in a tone half
compassionate, half reproachful.
"four of them, bless them!" say Mrs. Moss, with a sigh, stroke
Lizzy's hair on each side of her forehead; "as many as therebe boy.
theyhave get a brother apiece."
"ah, but they must turn out and fend for themselves," say Mr.
Tulliver, feel that his severity be relax and try to brace
it by throw out a wholesome hint "they mustnot look to hang on
their brother."
"no; but I hope their brother 'ull love the poor thing, and remember
they come o' one father and mother; the lad 'ull never be the poor
for that," say Mrs. Moss, flash out with hurried timidity, like a
half-smother fire.
Mr. Tulliver give his horse a little stroke on the flank, then check
it, and say angrily, "stand still with you!" much to the astonishment
of that innocent animal.
"and the more there be of them, the more they must love one another,"
Mrs. Moss go on, look at her child with a didactic purpose.
but she turn toward her brother again to say, "not but what I hope
your boy 'ull allay be good to his sister, though therebe but two of
them, like you and I, brother."
the arrow go straight to Mr. Tulliver's heart. he have not a rapid
imagination, but the thought of Maggie be very near to he, and he
be not long in see his relation to his own sister side by side
with Tom's relation to Maggie. would the little wench ever be poorly
off, and Tom rather hard upon she?
"ay, ay, Gritty," say the miller, with a new softness in his tone;
"but Ihave allay do what I could for you," he add, as if
vindicate himself from a reproach.
"Ibe not deny that, brother, and Ibe noway ungrateful," say poor
Mrs. Moss, too fag by toil and child to have strength leave for
any pride. "but herebe the father. what a while youhave be, Moss!"
"while, do you call it?" say Mr. Moss, feel out of breath and
injure. "Ihave be run all the way. willnot you 'light, Mr.
Tulliver?"
"well, Iwill just get down and have a bit o' talk with you in the
garden," say Mr. Tulliver, think that he should be more likely to
show a due spirit of resolve if his sister be not present.
he get down, and pass with Mr. Moss into the garden, toward an old
yew-tree arbor, while his sister stand tap her baby on the back
and look wistfully after they.
their entrance into the yew-tree arbor surprise several fowl that
be recreate themselves by scratch deep hole in the dusty
ground, and at once take flight with much pother and cackling. Mr.
Tulliver sit down on the bench, and tap the ground curiously here
and there with his stick, as if he suspect some hollowness, open
the conversation by observe, with something like a snarl in his
tone,--
"why, youhave get wheat again in that Corner Close, I see; and never a
bit o' dress on it. youwill do no good with it this year."
Mr. Moss, who, when he marry Miss Tulliver, have be regard as the
buck of Basset, now wear a beard nearly a week old, and have the
depressed, unexpectant air of a machine-horse. he answer in a
patient-grumble tone, "why, poor farmer like I must do as they
can; they must leave it to they as have get money to play with, to put
half as much into the ground as they mean to get out of it."
"I donot know who should have money to play with, if it benot they as
can borrow money without pay interest," say Mr. Tulliver, who
wish to get into a slight quarrel; it be the most natural and easy
introduction to call in money.
"I know Ibe behind with the interest," say Mr. Moss, "but I be so
unlucky wi' the wool last year; and what with the Missis be lay up
so, thing have go awk'arder nor usual."
"ay," snarl Mr. Tulliver, "therebe folk as thing 'ull allay go
awk'ard with; empty sack 'ull never stand upright."
"well, I donot know what fault youhave get to find wi' I, Mr.
Tulliver," say Mr. Moss, deprecatingly; "I know there benot a
day-laborer work hard."
"whatbe the use o' that," say Mr. Tulliver, sharply, "when a man
marrie, andbe get no capital to work his farm but his wife's bit o'
fortin? I be against it from the first; but youwould neither of you
listen to I. and I cannot lie out o' my money any long, for Ihave get
to pay five hundred o' Mrs. Glegg's, and therewill be Tom an expense to
I. I should find myself short, even say Iwould get back all as be my
own. you must look about and see how you can pay I the three hundred
pound."
"well, if thatbe what you mean," say Mr. Moss, look blankly before
he, "wewould well be sell up, and ha' do with it; I must part wi'
every head o' stock Ihave get, to pay you and the landlord too."
poor relation be undeniably irritating,--their existence be so
entirely uncalled for on our part, and they be almost always very
faulty people. Mr. Tulliver have succeed in get quite as much
irritated with Mr. Moss as he have desire, and he be able to say
angrily, rise from his seat,--
"well, you must do as you can. _I_ cannot find money for everybody else
as well as myself. I must look to my own business and my own family. I
cannot lie out o' my money any long. you must raise it as quick as
you can."
Mr. Tulliver walk abruptly out of the arbor as he utter the last
sentence, and, without look round at Mr. Moss, go on to the
kitchen door, where the eld boy be hold his horse, and his
sister be wait in a state of wonder alarm, which be not
without its alleviation, for baby be make pleasant gurgling
sound, and perform a great deal of finger practice on the faded
face. Mrs. Moss have eight child, but could never overcome she
