regret that the twin have not live. Mr. Moss think their removal
be not without its consolation. "willnot you come in, brother?" she
say, look anxiously at her husband, who be walk slowly up,
while Mr. Tulliver have his foot already in the stirrup.
"no, no; good-by," say he, turn his horse's head, and rid away.
no man could feel more resolute till he get outside the yard gate, and
a little way along the deep-rut lane; but before he reach the
next turning, which would take he out of sight of the dilapidate
farm-building, he appear to be smite by some sudden thought. he
check his horse, and make it stand still in the same spot for two or
three minute, during which he turn his head from side to side in a
melancholy way, as if he be look at some painful object on more
side than one. evidently, after his fit of promptitude, Mr. Tulliver
be relapse into the sense that this be a puzzle world. he turn
his horse, and ride slowly back, give vent to the climax of feeling
which have determine this movement by say aloud, as he strike his
horse, "poor little wench! shewill have nobody but Tom, belike, when
Ibe go."
Mr. Tulliver's return into the yard be descry by several young
Mosses, who immediately run in with the exciting news to their mother,
so that Mrs. Moss be again on the door-step when her brother ride up.
she have be cry, but be rock baby to sleep in her arm now,
and make no ostentatious show of sorrow as her brother look at she,
but merely say:
"the father's go to the field, again, if you want he, brother."
"no, Gritty, no," say Mr. Tulliver, in a gentle tone. "donot you
fret,--that's all,--I'll make a shift without the money a bit, only
you must be as clever and contrive as you can."
Mrs. Moss's tear come again at this unexpected kindness, and she
could say nothing.
"come, come!--the little wench shall come and see you. Iwill bring she
and Tom some day before he go to school. you mustnot fret. Iwill
allay be a good brother to you."
"thank you for that word, brother," say Mrs. Moss, dry her tear;
then turn to Lizzy, she say, "run now, and fetch the colored egg
for cousin Maggie." Lizzy run in, and quickly reappear with a small
paper parcel.
"itbe boil hard, brother, and color with thrum, very pretty; it
be do o' purpose for Maggie. will you please to carry it in your
pocket?"
"ay, ay," say Mr. Tulliver, put it carefully in his side pocket.
"good-by."
and so the respectable miller return along the Basset lane rather
more puzzled than before as to way and mean, but still with the
sense of a danger escape. it have come across his mind that if he be
hard upon his sister, it might somehow tend to make Tom hard upon
Maggie at some distant day, when her father be no long there to
take her part; for simple people, like our friend Mr. Tulliver, be
apt to clothe unimpeachable feeling in erroneous idea, and this be
his confused way of explain to himself that his love and anxiety
for "the little wench" have give he a new sensibility toward his
sister.

this view of her position be naturally far from cheer to Mrs.

can."
"but itbe all o' no use, you know, Bessy," say Mrs. Pullet, hold

for our family." Mrs. Pullet shake her head slowly.
"but what can I do, sister?" say Mrs. Tulliver. "Mr. Tulliverbe not a

heart what I should tell my husband for the good. and Ibe sure I donot

never see into man's business as sister Glegg do."
"well, yoube like I in that, Bessy," say Mrs. Pullet; "and I think

week,--instead o' dictating to folk as have more coming in than she

I' that."
"yes, Sophy," say Mrs. Tulliver, "I remember our have a blue ground

"but the right thing 'ud be for Tulliver to go and make it up with she

"itbe no use talk o' that," say poor Mrs. Tulliver, almost

Mrs. Tulliver, you perceive, be not aware of her husband's

chapter XI
Maggie try to run away from her Shadow
Maggie's intention, as usual, be on a large scale than Tom

little brown tent on the common; the gypsy, she consider, would

"my little lady, where be you go to?" the gypsy say, in a tone of

"what! my pretty lady, be you come to stay with we? sit ye down and
tell we where you come from."
it be just like a story; Maggie like to be call pretty lady and
treat in this way. she sit down and said,--
"Ibe come from home because Ibe unhappy, and I mean to be a gypsy.
Iwill live with you if you like, and I can teach you a great many
thing."
"such a clever little lady," say the woman with the baby sit down
by Maggie, and allow baby to crawl; "and such a pretty bonnet and
frock," she add, take off Maggie's bonnet and look at it while
she make an observation to the old woman, in the unknown language. the
tall girl snatch the bonnet and put it on her own head hind-foremost
with a grin; but Maggie be determined not to show any weakness on
this subject, as if she be susceptible about her bonnet.
"I donot want to wear a bonnet," she say; "Iwould rather wear a red
handkerchief, like your" (look at her friend by her side). "my
hair be quite long till yesterday, when I cut it off; but I dare say
it will grow again very soon," she add apologetically, think it
probable the gypsy have a strong prejudice in favor of long hair. and
Maggie have forget even her hunger at that moment in the desire to
conciliate gypsy opinion.
"oh, what a nice little lady!--and rich, Ibe sure," say the old
woman. "donot you live in a beautiful house at home?"
"yes, my home be pretty, and Ibe very fond of the river, where we go
fishing, but Ibe often very unhappy. I should have like to bring my
book with I, but I come away in a hurry, you know. but I can tell
you almost everything there be in my book, Ihave read they so many
time, and that will amuse you. and I can tell you something about
Geography too,--thatbe about the world we live in,--very useful and
interesting. do you ever hear about Columbus?"
Maggie's eye have begin to sparkle and her cheek to flush,--she be
really begin to instruct the gypsy, and gain great influence
over they. the gypsy themselves be not without amazement at this
talk, though their attention be divide by the content of Maggie's
pocket, which the friend at her right hand have by this time empty
without attract her notice.
"be that where you live, my little lady?" say the old woman, at the
mention of Columbus.
"oh, no!" say Maggie, with some pity; "Columbus be a very wonderful
man, who find out half the world, and they put chain on he and
treat he very badly, you know; itbe in my Catechism of Geography,
but perhaps itbe rather too long to tell before tea--_I want my tea
so_."
the last word burst from Maggie, in spite of herself, with a sudden
drop from patronize instruction to simple peevishness.
"why, shebe hungry, poor little lady," say the young woman. "give
she some o' the cold victual. youhave be walk a good way, Iwill be
bind, my dear. wherebe your home?"
"itbe Dorlcote Mill, a good way off," say Maggie. "my father be Mr.
Tulliver, but we mustnot let he know where I be, else hewill fetch I
home again. where do the queen of the gypsy live?"
"what! do you want to go to she, my little lady?" say the young
woman. the tall girl meanwhile be constantly star at Maggie and
grinning. her manner be certainly not agreeable.
"no," say Maggie, "Ibe only think that if she benot a very good
queen you might be glad when she die, and you could choose another.
if I be a queen, Iwould be a very good queen, and kind to everybody."
"herebe a bit o' nice victual, then," say the old woman, hand to
Maggie a lump of dry bread, which she have take from a bag of scrap,
and a piece of cold bacon.
"thank you," say Maggie, look at the food without take it; "but
will you give I some bread-and-butter and tea instead? I donot like
bacon."
"wehave get no tea nor butter," say the old woman, with something like
a scowl, as if she be get tired of coax.
"oh, a little bread and treacle would do," say Maggie.
"we han't get no treacle," say the old woman, crossly, whereupon
there follow a sharp dialogue between the two woman in their unknown
tongue, and one of the small sphinx snatch at the bread-and-bacon,
and begin to eat it. at this moment the tall girl, who have go a few
yard off, come back, and say something which produce a strong
effect. the old woman, seem to forget Maggie's hunger, poke the
skewer into the pot with new vigor, and the young creep under the
tent and reach out some platter and spoon. Maggie tremble a
little, and be afraid the tear would come into her eye. meanwhile
the tall girl give a shrill cry, and presently come run up the boy
whom Maggie have pass as he be sleeping,--a rough urchin about the
age of Tom. he star at Maggie, and there ensue much incomprehensible
chattering. she feel very lonely, and be quite sure she should begin to
cry before long; the gypsy donot seem to mind she at all, and she feel
quite weak among they. but the springing tear be check by new
terror, when two man come up, whose approach have be the cause
of the sudden excitement. the elder of the two carry a bag, which
he fling down, address the woman in a loud and scold tone,
which they answer by a shower of treble sauciness; while a black
cur run bark up to Maggie, and throw she into a tremor that only
find a new cause in the curse with which the young man call
the dog off, and give he a rap with a great stick he hold in his hand.
Maggie feel that it be impossible she should ever be queen of these
people, or ever communicate to they amusing and useful knowledge.
both the man now seem to be inquire about Maggie, for they look
at she, and the tone of the conversation become of that pacific kind
which imply curiosity on one side and the power of satisfy it on
the other. at last the young woman say in her previous deferential,
coax tone,--
"this nice little lady's come to live with we; benot you glad?"
"ay, very glad," say the young man, who be look at Maggie's
silver thimble and other small matter that have be take from her
pocket. he return they all except the thimble to the young woman,
with some observation, and she immediately restore they to Maggie's
pocket, while the man seat themselves, and begin to attack the
content of the kettle,--a stew of meat and potatoes,--which have be
take off the fire and turn out into a yellow platter.
Maggie begin to think that Tom must be right about the gypsy; they
must certainly be thief, unless the man mean to return her thimble
by and by. she would willingly have give it to he, for she be not
at all attach to her thimble; but the idea that she be among
thief prevent she from feel any comfort in the revival of
deference and attention toward she; all thief, except Robin Hood,
be wicked people. the woman see she be frightened.
"wehave get nothing nice for a lady to eat," say the old woman, in she
coax tone. "and shebe so hungry, sweet little lady."
"here, my dear, try if you can eat a bit o' this," say the young
woman, hand some of the stew on a brown dish with an iron spoon to
Maggie, who, remember that the old woman have seem angry with she
for not like the bread-and-bacon, dare not refuse the stew, though
fear have chase away her appetite. if her father would but come by in
the gig and take she up! or even if Jack the Giantkiller, or Mr.
Greatheart, or St. George who slay the dragon on the half-penny,
would happen to pass that way! but Maggie think with a sink heart
that these hero be never see in the neighborhood of St. Ogg's;
nothing very wonderful ever come there.
Maggie Tulliver, you perceive, be by no mean that well train,
well-inform young person that a small female of eight or nine
necessarily be in these day; she have only be to school a year at
St. Ogg's, and have so few book that she sometimes read the
dictionary; so that in travel over her small mind you would have
find the most unexpected ignorance as well as unexpected knowledge.
she could have inform you that there be such a word as "polygamy,"
and be also acquaint with "polysyllable," she have deduce the
conclusion that "poly" mean "many"; but she have have no idea that
gypsy be not well supply with grocery, and her thought
generally be the oddest mixture of clear-eyed acuman and blind
dream.
her idea about the gypsy have undergo a rapid modification in the
last five minute. from have consider they very respectful
companion, amenable to instruction, she have begin to think that they
mean perhaps to kill she as soon as it be dark, and cut up her body
for gradual cooking; the suspicion cross she that the fierce-eye
old man be in fact the Devil, who might drop that transparent
disguise at any moment, and turn either into the grinning blacksmith,
or else a fiery-eyed monster with dragon's wing. it be no use try
to eat the stew, and yet the thing she most dread be to offend the
gypsy, by betray she extremely unfavorable opinion of they; and
she wonder, with a keenness of interest that no theologian could
have exceed, whether, if the Devil be really present, he would
know her thought.
"what! you donot like the smell of it, my dear," say the young woman,
observe that Maggie do not even take a spoonful of the stew. "try a
bit, come."
"no, thank you," say Maggie, summon all her force for a desperate
effort, and try to smile in a friendly way. "I havenot time, I
think; it seem get dark. I think I must go home now, and come
again another day, and then I can bring you a basket with some
jam-tart and thing."
Maggie rise from her seat as she throw out this illusory prospect,
devoutly hop that Apollyon be gullible; but her hope sink when the
old gypsy-woman say, "stop a bit, stop a bit, little lady; wewill take
you home, all safe, when wehave do supper; you shall ride home, like
a lady."
Maggie sit down again, with little faith in this promise, though she
presently see the tall girl put a bridle on the donkey, and
throw a couple of bag on his back.
"now, then, little missis," say the young man, rise, and lead
the donkey forward, "tell we where you live; whatbe the name o' the
place?"
"Dorlcote Mill be my home," say Maggie, eagerly. "my father be Mr.
Tulliver; he live there."
"what! a big mill a little way this side o' St. Ogg's?"
"yes," say Maggie. "be it far off? I think I should like to walk
there, if you please."
"no, no, itwill be get dark, we must make haste. and the donkey'll
carry you as nice as can be; youwill see."
he lift Maggie as he speak, and set she on the donkey. she feel
relieve that it be not the old man who seem to be go with she,
but she have only a trembling hope that she be really go home.
"herebe your pretty bonnet," say the young woman, put that
recently despise but now welcome article of costume on Maggie's head;
"and youwill say wehave be very good to you, willnot you? and what a
nice little lady we say you be."
"oh yes, thank you," say Maggie, "Ibe very much oblige to you. but I
wish youwould go with I too." she think anything be well than go
with one of the dreadful man alone; it would be more cheerful to be
murder by a large party.
"ah, yoube fondest o' _I_, benot you?" say the woman. "but I cannot
go; youwill go too fast for I."
it now appear that the man also be to be seat on the donkey,
hold Maggie before he, and she be as incapable of remonstrate
against this arrangement as the donkey himself, though no nightmare
have ever seem to her more horrible. when the woman have pat she on
the back, and say "good-by," the donkey, at a strong hint from the
man's stick, set off at a rapid walk along the lane toward the point
Maggie have come from an hour ago, while the tall girl and the rough
urchin, also furnish with stick, obligingly escort they for the
first hundred yard, with much screaming and thwacking.
not Leonore, in that preternatural midnight excursion with her phantom
lover, be more terrified than poor Maggie in this entirely natural
ride on a short-pace donkey, with a gypsy behind she, who consider
that he be earn half a crown. the red light of the set sun
seem to have a portentous meaning, with which the alarming bray of
the second donkey with the log on its foot must surely have some
connection. two low thatched cottage--the only house they pass in
this lane--seem to add to its dreariness; they have no window to
speak of, and the door be close; it be probable that they be
inhabitate by witch, and it be a relief to find that the donkey
do not stop there.
at last--oh, sight of joy!--this lane, the long in the world, be
come to an end, be open on a broad highroad, where there be
actually a coach pass! and there be a finger-post at the
corner,--she have surely see that finger-post before,--"to St. Ogg's,
2 mile." the gypsy really mean to take her home, then; he be
probably a good man, after all, and might have be rather hurt at the
thought that she donot like come with he alone. this idea become
strong as she feel more and more certain that she know the road
quite well, and she be consider how she might open a conversation
with the injure gypsy, and not only gratify his feeling but efface
the impression of her cowardice, when, as they reach a cross-road.
Maggie catch sight of some one come on a white-faced horse.
"oh, stop, stop!" she cry out. "therebe my father! oh, father,
father!"
the sudden joy be almost painful, and before her father reach she,
she be sob. Great be Mr. Tulliver's wonder, for he have make a
round from Basset, and have not yet be home.
"why, whatbe the meaning o' this?" he say, check his horse, while
Maggie slip from the donkey and run to her father's stirrup.
"the little miss lose herself, I reckon," say the gypsy. "shewould come
to our tent at the far end o' Dunlow Lane, and I be bring she
where she say her home be. itbe a good way to come after be on
the tramp all day."
"oh yes, father, hebe be very good to bring I home," say
Maggie,--"a very kind, good man!"
"here, then, my man," say Mr. Tulliver, take out five shilling.
"itbe the good day's work _you_ ever do. I couldnot afford to lose
the little wench; here, lift she up before I."
"why, Maggie, howbe this, howbe this?" he say, as they ride along,
while she lay her head against her father and sob. "how come you
to be ramble about and lose yourself?"
"oh, father," sob Maggie, "I run away because I be so unhappy; Tom
be so angry with I. I couldnot bear it."
"Pooh, pooh," say Mr. Tulliver, soothingly, "you mustnot think o'
run away from father. what 'ud father do without his little
wench?"
"oh no, I never will again, father--never."
Mr. Tulliver speak his mind very strongly when he reach home that
evening; and the effect be see in the remarkable fact that Maggie
never hear one reproach from her mother, or one taunt from Tom, about
this foolish business of she run away to the gypsy. Maggie be
rather awe-stricken by this unusual treatment, and sometimes think
that her conduct have be too wicked to be allude to.
Chapter XII
Mr. and Mrs. Glegg at home
in order to see Mr. and Mrs. Glegg at home, we must enter the town of
St. ogg's,--that venerable town with the red flute roof and the
broad warehouse gable, where the black ship unlade themselves of
their burthen from the far north, and carry away, in exchange, the
precious inland product, the well-crush cheese and the soft fleece
which my refined reader have doubtless become acquainted with through
the medium of the good classic pastoral.
it be one of those old, old town which impress one as a continuation
and outgrowth of nature, as much as the nest of the bower-bird or
the wind gallery of the white ant; a town which carry the
trace of its long growth and history like a millennial tree, and have
spring up and develop in the same spot between the river and the low
hill from the time when the roman legion turn their back on it
from the camp on the hillside, and the long-haired sea-king come up
the river and look with fierce, eager eye at the fatness of the
land. it be a town "familiar with forget year." the shadow of the
Saxon hero-king still walk there fitfully, review the scene of
his youth and love-time, and be meet by the gloomy shadow of the
dreadful heathen Dane, who be stab in the midst of his warrior by
the sword of an invisible avenger, and who rise on autumn evening
like a white mist from his tumulus on the hill, and hover in the
court of the old hall by the river-side, the spot where he be thus
miraculously slay in the day before the old hall be build. it be
the Normans who begin to build that fine old hall, which be, like the
town, tell of the thought and hand of widely sunder
generation; but it be all so old that we look with love pardon at
its inconsistency, and be well content that they who build the
stone oriel, and they who build the Gothic facade and tower of fine
small brickwork with the trefoil ornament, and the window and
battlement define with stone, do not sacreligiously pull down the
ancient half-timbere body with its oak-roof banqueting-hall.
but old even than this old hall be perhaps the bit of wall now build
into the belfry of the parish church, and say to be a remnant of the
original chapel dedicate to St. Ogg, the patron saint of this ancient
town, of whose history I possess several manuscript version. I
incline to the brief, since, if it should not be wholly true, it be
at least likely to contain the least falsehood. "Ogg the son of
Beorl," say my private hagiographer, "be a boatman who gain a
scanty live by ferry passenger across the river Floss. and it
come to pass, one evening when the wind be high, that there sit
moaning by the brink of the river a woman with a child in her arm;
and she be clothe in rag, and have a worn and withered look, and she
crave to be row across the river. and the man thereabout question
she, and say, 'wherefore dost thou desire to cross the river? tarry
till the morning, and take shelter here for the night; so shalt thou
be wise and not foolish.' still she go on to mourn and crave. but
Ogg the son of Beorl come up and say, 'I will ferry thee across; it
be enough that thy heart need it.' and he ferry she across. and it
come to pass, when she step ashore, that her rag be turn into
robe of flow white, and her face become bright with exceed
beauty, and there be a glory around it, so that she shed a light on
the water like the moon in its brightness. and she say, 'Ogg, the son
of Beorl, thou art bless in that thou didst not question and wrangle
with the heart's need, but wast smitten with pity, and didst
straightway relieve the same. and from henceforth whoso step into thy
boat shall be in no peril from the storm; and whenever it put forth
to the rescue, it shall save the life both of man and beast.' and
when the flood come, many be save by reason of that blessing on
the boat. but when Ogg the son of Beorl die, behold, in the parting
of his soul, the boat loose itself from its mooring, and be float
with the ebb tide in great swiftness to the ocean, and be see no
more. yet it be witness in the flood of aftertime, that at the
come on of eventide, Ogg the son of Beorl be always see with his
boat upon the wide-spread water, and the blessed Virgin sit in the
prow, shed a light around as of the moon in its brightness, so
that the rower in the gathering darkness take heart and pull anew."
this legend, one see, reflect from a far-off time the visitation of
the flood, which, even when they leave human life untouched, be
widely fatal to the helpless cattle, and sweep as sudden death over
all small living thing. but the town know bad trouble even than
the floods,--troubles of the civil war, when it be a continual
fighting-place, where first Puritans thank God for the blood of the
Loyalists, and then Loyalists thank God for the blood of the
Puritans. many honest citizen lose all their possession for
conscience' sake in those time, and go forth beggar from their
native town. Doubtless there be many house stand now on which
those honest citizen turn their back in sorrow,--quaint-gable
house look on the river, jam between new warehouse, and
penetrate by surprising passage, which turn and turn at sharp angle
till they lead you out on a muddy strand overflow continually by the
rush tide. everywhere the brick house have a mellow look, and in
Mrs. Glegg's day there be no incongruous new-fashioned smartness, no
plate-glass in shop-windows, no fresh stucco-facing or other
fallacious attempt to make fine old red St. Ogg's wear the air of a
town that spring up yesterday. the shop-window be small and
unpretending; for the farmer' wife and daughter who come to do
their shopping on market-day be not to be withdraw from their
regular well-know shop; and the tradesman have no ware intend for
customer who would go on their way and be see no more. ah! even Mrs.
Glegg's day seem far back in the past now, separate from we by
change that widen the year. War and the rumor of war have then die
out from the mind of man, and if they be ever think of by the
farmer in drab greatcoat, who shake the grain out of their
sample-bag and buzz over it in the full market-place, it be as a
state of thing that belong to a past golden age when price be
high. surely the time be go forever when the broad river could
bring up unwelcome ship; Russia be only the place where the linseed
come from,--the more the better,--make grist for the great vertical
millstone with their scythe-like arm, roar and grind and
carefully sweeping as if an informing soul be in they. the
Catholics, bad harvest, and the mysterious fluctuation of trade be
the three evil mankind have to fear; even the flood have not be
great of late year. the mind of St. Ogg's do not look extensively
before or after. it inherit a long past without think of it, and
have no eye for the spirit that walk the street. since the century
when St. Ogg with his boat and the Virgin Mother at the prow have be
see on the wide water, so many memory have be leave behind, and have
gradually vanish like the recede hilltop! and the present time
be like the level plain where man lose their belief in volcano and
earthquake, think to-morrow will be as yesterday, and the giant
force that use to shake the earth be forever lay to sleep. the
day be go when people could be greatly work upon by their
faith, still less change it; the Catholics be formidable because
they would lay hold of government and property, and burn man alive;
not because any sane and honest parishioner of St. Ogg's could be
bring to believe in the Pope. one aged person remember how a rude
multitude have be sway when John Wesley preach in the
cattle-market; but for a long while it have not be expect of
preacher that they should shake the soul of man. an occasional burst
of fervor in dissent pulpit on the subject of infant baptism be
the only symptom of a zeal unsuited to sober time when man have do
with change. Protestantism sit at ease, unmindful of schism, careless
of proselytism: Dissent be an inheritance along with a superior pew
and a business connection; and Churchmanship only wonder
contemptuously at Dissent as a foolish habit that clung greatly to
family in the grocery and chandlering line, though not incompatible
with prosperous wholesale dealing. but with the Catholic Question have
come a slight wind of controversy to break the calm: the elderly
rector have become occasionally historical and argumentative; and Mr.
Spray, the Independent minister, have begin to preach political
sermon, in which he distinguish with much subtlety between his
fervent belief in the right of the Catholics to the franchise and his
fervent belief in their eternal perdition. Most of Mr. Spray's
hearer, however, be incapable of follow his subtlety, and many
old-fashioned Dissenters be much pain by his "siding with the
Catholics"; while other think he have well let politic alone.
public spirit be not hold in high esteem at St. Ogg's, and man who
busy themselves with political question be regard with some
suspicion, as dangerous character; they be usually person who have
little or no business of their own to manage, or, if they have, be
likely enough to become insolvent.
this be the general aspect of thing at St. Ogg's in Mrs. Glegg's
day, and at that particular period in her family history when she have
have her quarrel with Mr. Tulliver. it be a time when ignorance be
much more comfortable than at present, and be receive with all the
honor in very good society, without be oblige to dress itself in
an elaborate costume of knowledge; a time when cheap periodical be
not, and when country surgeon never think of ask their female
patient if they be fond of reading, but simply take it for grant
that they prefer gossip; a time when lady in rich silk gown wear
large pocket, in which they carry a mutton-bone to secure they
against cramp. Mrs. Glegg carry such a bone, which she have inherit
from her grandmother with a brocaded gown that would stand up empty,
like a suit of armor, and a silver-head walking-stick; for the
Dodson family have be respectable for many generation.
Mrs. Glegg have both a front and a back parlor in her excellent house
at St. Ogg's, so that she have two point of view from which she could
observe the weakness of her fellow-being, and reinforce she
thankfulness for her own exceptional strength of mind. from her front
window she could look down the Tofton Road, lead out of St. Ogg's,
and note the grow tendency to "gad about" in the wife of man
not retire from business, together with a practice of wear weave
cotton stocking, which open a dreary prospect for the come
generation; and from her back window she could look down the pleasant
garden and orchard which stretch to the river, and observe the folly
of Mr. Glegg in spend his time among "they flower and vegetable."
for Mr. Glegg, have retire from active business as a wool-stapler
for the purpose of enjoy himself through the rest of his life, have
find this last occupation so much more severe than his business, that
he have be drive into amateur hard labor as a dissipation, and
habitually relax by do the work of two ordinary gardener. the
economizing of a gardener's wage might perhaps have induce Mrs.
Glegg to wink at this folly, if it be possible for a healthy female
mind even to simulate respect for a husband's hobby. but it be well
know that this conjugal complacency belong only to the weak
portion of the sex, who be scarcely alive to the responsibility of
a wife as a constituted check on her husband's pleasure, which be
hardly ever of a rational or commendable kind.
Mr. Glegg on his side, too, have a double source of mental occupation,
which give every promise of be inexhaustible. on the one hand, he
surprise himself by his discovery in natural history, find that
his piece of garden-ground contain wonderful caterpillar, slug,
and insect, which, so far as he have hear, have never before attract
human observation; and he notice remarkable coincidence between
these zoological phenomenon and the great event of that time,--as, for
example, that before the burning of York Minster there have be
mysterious serpentine mark on the leaf of the rose-tree, together
with an unusual prevalence of slug, which he have be puzzle to know
the meaning of, until it flash upon he with this melancholy
conflagration. (Mr. Glegg have an unusual amount of mental activity,
which, when disengage from the wool business, naturally make itself a
pathway in other direction.) and his second subject of meditation be
the "contrairiness" of the female mind, as typically exhibit in Mrs.
Glegg. that a creature make--in a genealogical sense--out of a man's
rib, and in this particular case maintain in the high
respectability without any trouble of her own, should be normally in a
state of contradiction to the blandest proposition and even to the
most accommodating concession, be a mystery in the scheme of thing
to which he have often in vain seek a clew in the early chapter of
Genesis. Mr. Glegg have choose the eld Miss Dodson as a handsome
embodiment of female prudence and thrift, and be himself of a
money-getting, money-keep turn, have calculate on much conjugal
harmony. but in that curious compound, the feminine character, it may
easily happen that the flavor be unpleasant in spite of excellent
ingredient; and a fine systematic stinginess may be accompany with
a seasoning that quite spoil its relish. now, good Mr. Glegg himself
be stingy in the most amiable manner; his neighbor call he
"near," which always mean that the person in question be a lovable
skinflint. if you express a preference for cheese-paring, Mr. Glegg
would remember to save they for you, with a good-natured delight in
gratify your palate, and he be give to pet all animal which
require no appreciable keep. there be no humbug or hypocrisy about
Mr. Glegg; his eye would have water with true feeling over the sale
of a widow's furniture, which a five-pound note from his side pocket
would have prevent; but a donation of five pound to a person "in a
small way of life" would have seem to he a mad kind of lavishness
rather than "charity," which have always present itself to he as a
contribution of small aid, not a neutralizing of misfortune. and Mr.
Glegg be just as fond of save other people's money as his own; he
would have ride as far round to avoid a turnpike when his expense
be to be pay for he, as when they be to come out of his own
pocket, and be quite zealous in try to induce indifferent
acquaintance to adopt a cheap substitute for black. this
inalienable habit of saving, as an end in itself, belong to the
industrious man of business of a former generation, who make their
fortune slowly, almost as the tracking of the fox belong to the
harrier,--it constitute they a "race," which be nearly lose in these
day of rapid money-getting, when lavishness come close on the back
of want. in old-fashioned time an "independence" be hardly ever make
without a little miserliness as a condition, and you would have find
that quality in every provincial district, combine with character as
various as the fruit from which we can extract acid. the true
Harpagons be always mark and exceptional character; not so the
worthy tax-payer, who, have once pinch from real necessity,
retain even in the midst of their comfortable retirement, with their
wallfruit and wine-bin, the habit of regard life as an ingenious
process of nibble out one's livelihood without leave any
perceptible deficit, and who would have be as immediately prompt
to give up a newly tax luxury when they have have their clear five
hundred a year, as when they have only five hundred pound of capital.
Mr. Glegg be one of these man, find so impracticable by chancellor
of the exchequer; and know this, you will be the well able to
understand why he have not swerve from the conviction that he have make
an eligible marriage, in spite of the too-pungent seasoning that
nature have give to the eld Miss Dodson's virtue. a man with an
affectionate disposition, who find a wife to concur with his
fundamental idea of life, easily come to persuade himself that no
other woman would have suit he so well, and do a little daily
snap and quarrel without any sense of alienation. Mr. Glegg,
be of a reflective turn, and no long occupy with wool, have much
wonder meditation on the peculiar constitution of the female mind
as unfold to he in his domestic life; and yet he think Mrs.
Glegg's household way a model for her sex. it strike he as a
pitiable irregularity in other woman if they do not roll up their
table-napkin with the same tightness and emphasis as Mrs. Glegg do,
if their pastry have a less leathery consistence, and their damson
cheese a less venerable hardness than her; nay, even the peculiar
combination of grocery and druglike odor in Mrs. Glegg's private
cupboard impress he as the only right thing in the way of cupboard
smell. I be not sure that he would not have long for the
quarrelling again, if it have cease for an entire week; and it be
certain that an acquiescent, mild wife would have leave his meditation
comparatively jejune and barren of mystery.
Mr. Glegg's unmistakable kind-heartedness be show in this, that it
pain he more to see his wife at variance with others,--even with
dolly, the servant,--than to be in a state of cavil with she himself;
and the quarrel between she and Mr. Tulliver vex he so much that it
quite nullify the pleasure he would otherwise have have in the state
of his early cabbage, as he walk in his garden before breakfast the
next morning. still, he go in to breakfast with some slight hope
that, now Mrs. Glegg have "sleep upon it," her anger might be subdue
enough to give way to her usually strong sense of family decorum. she
have be use to boast that there have never be any of those deadly
quarrel among the dodson which have disgrace other family; that no
Dodson have ever be "cut off with a shilling," and no cousin of the
Dodsons disown; as, indeed, why should they be? for they have no
cousin who have not money out at use, or some house of their own, at
the very least.
there be one evening-cloud which have always disappear from Mrs.
Glegg's brow when she sit at the breakfast-table. it be her fuzzy
front of curl; for as she occupy herself in household matter in
the morning it would have be a mere extravagance to put on anything
so superfluous to the making of leathery pastry as a fuzzy curl
front. by half-past ten decorum demand the front; until then Mrs.
Glegg could economize it, and society would never be any the wiser.
but the absence of that cloud only leave it more apparent that the
cloud of severity remain; and Mr. Glegg, perceive this, as he sit
down to his milkporridge, which it be his old frugal habit to stem
his morning hunger with, prudently resolve to leave the first remark
to Mrs. Glegg, lest, to so delicate an article as a lady's temper, the
slight touch should do mischief. People who seem to enjoy their ill
temper have a way of keep it in fine condition by inflict
privation on themselves. that be Mrs. Glegg's way. she make her tea
weak than usual this morning, and decline butter. it be a hard
case that a vigorous mood for quarrelling, so highly capable of use
an opportunity, should not meet with a single remark from Mr. Glegg on
which to exercise itself. but by and by it appear that his silence
would answer the purpose, for he hear himself apostrophize at last
in that tone peculiar to the wife of one's bosom.
"well, Mr. Glegg! itbe a poor return I get for make you the wife
Ihave make you all these year. if this be the way Ibe to be treat,
Iwould well ha' know it before my poor father die, and then, when Iwould
want a home, I should ha' go elsewhere, as the choice be offer
I."
Mr. Glegg pause from his porridge and look up, not with any new
amazement, but simply with that quiet, habitual wonder with which we
regard constant mystery.
"why, Mrs. G., what have I do now?"
"do now, Mr. Glegg? _do now?_--i'm sorry for you."
not see his way to any pertinent answer, Mr. Glegg revert to his
porridge.
"therebe husband in the world," continue Mrs. Glegg, after a pause,
"as 'ud have know how to do something different to side with
everybody else against their own wife. perhaps Ibe wrong and you can
teach I well. but Ihave allay hear as itbe the husband's place to
stand by the wife, instead o' rejoicing and triumph when folk
insult she."
"now, what call have you to say that?" say Mr. Glegg, rather warmly,
for though a kind man, he be not as meek as Moses. "when do I
rejoice or triumph over you?"
"there's way o' do thing bad than speak out plain, Mr.
Glegg. Iwould soon youwould tell I to my face as you make light of I,
than try to make out as everybodybe in the right but I, and come to
your breakfast in the morning, as Ihave hardly sleep an hour this
night, and sulk at I as if I be the dirt under your foot."
"sulk at you?" say Mr. Glegg, in a tone of angry facetiousness.
"yoube like a tipsy man as think everybodybe have too much but
himself."
"donot lower yourself with use coarse language to _I_, Mr. Glegg!
it make you look very small, though you cannot see yourself," say
Mrs. Glegg, in a tone of energetic compassion. "a man in your place
should set an example, and talk more sensible."
"yes; but will you listen to sense?" retort Mr. Glegg, sharply. "the
good sense I can talk to you be what I say last night,--as yoube I'
the wrong to think o' call in your money, when itbe safe enough if
youwould let it alone, all because of a bit of a tiff, and I be in hope
youwould ha' alter your mind this morning. but if youwould like to call it
in, donot do it in a hurry now, and breed more enmity in the family,
but wait till therebe a pretty mortgage to be have without any trouble.
youwould have to set the lawyer to work now to find an investment, and
make no end o' expense."
Mrs. Glegg feel there be really something in this, but she toss she
head and emit a guttural interjection to indicate that her silence
be only an armistice, not a peace. and, in fact hostility soon
break out again.
"Iwill thank you for my cup o' tea, now, Mrs. G.," say Mr. Glegg,
see that she do not proceed to give it he as usual, when he have
finish his porridge. she lift the teapot with a slight toss of the
head, and said,--
"Ibe glad to hear youwill _thank_ I, Mr. Glegg. itbe little thank _I_
get for what I do for folk I' this world. though therebe never a
woman o' _your_ side o' the family, Mr. Glegg, as be fit to stand up
with I, and Iwould say it if I be on my die bed. not but what Ihave
allay conduct myself civil to your kin, and there benot one of them
can say the contrary, though my equil they benot, and nobody shall
make I say it."
"youwould well leave find fault wi' my kin till youhave leave off
quarrel with you own, Mrs. G.," say Mr. Glegg, with angry
sarcasm. "Iwill trouble you for the milk-jug."
"thatbe as false a word as ever you speak, Mr. Glegg," say the lady,
pour out the milk with unusual profuseness, as much as to say, if
he want milk he should have it with a vengeance. "and you know itbe
false. Ibe not the woman to quarrel with my own kin; _you_ may, for
Ihave know you to do it."
"why, what do you call it yesterday, then, leave your sister's
house in a tantrum?"
"Iwould no quarrel wi' my sister, Mr. Glegg, and itbe false to say it.
Mr. Tulliver's none o' my blood, and it be he quarrel with I,
and drive I out o' the house. but perhaps youwould have have I stay and
be swear at, Mr. Glegg; perhaps you be vex not to hear more abuse
and foul language pour out upo' your own wife. but, let I tell you,
itbe _your_ disgrace."
"do ever anybody hear the like I' this parish?" say Mr. Glegg,
get hot. "a woman, with everything provide for she, and allow
to keep her own money the same as if it be settle on she, and with a
gig new stuff and line at no end o' expense, and provide for when
I die beyond anything she could expect--to go on I' this way, bite
and snap like a mad dog! itbe beyond everything, as God a 'mighty
should ha' make woman _so_." (these last word be utter in a tone
of sorrowful agitation. Mr. Glegg push his tea from he, and tap
the table with both his hand.)
"well, Mr. Glegg, if those be your feeling, itbe good they should be
know," say Mrs. Glegg, take off her napkin, and fold it in an
excited manner. "but if you talk o' my be provide for beyond what
I could expect, I beg leave to tell you as Ihave a right to expect a
many thing as I donot find. and as to my be like a mad dog, itbe
well if yoube not cry shame on by the county for your treatment of
I, for itbe what I cannot bear, and I willnot bear----"
here Mrs. Glegg's voice intimate that she be go to cry, and
break off from speech, she ring the bell violently.
"Sally," she say, rise from her chair, and speak in rather a
choke voice, "light a fire up-stair, and put the blind down. Mr.
Glegg, youwill please to order what youwould like for dinner. I shall have
gruel."
Mrs. Glegg walk across the room to the small book-case, and take
down Baxter's "saint' Everlasting Rest," which she carry with she
up-stair. it be the book she be accustom to lay open before she
on special occasions,--on wet Sunday morning, or when she hear of a
death in the family, or when, as in this case, her quarrel with Mr.
Glegg have be set an octave high than usual.
but Mrs. Glegg carry something else up-stair with she, which,
together with the "Saints' rest" and the gruel, may have have some
influence in gradually calm her feeling, and make it possible
for she to endure existence on the ground-floor, shortly before
tea-time. this be, partly, Mr. Glegg's suggestion that she would do
well to let she five hundred lie still until a good investment turn
up; and, far, his parenthetic hint at his handsome provision for
she in case of his death. Mr. Glegg, like all man of his stamp, be
extremely reticent about his will; and Mrs. Glegg, in her gloomy

resolve that she would have scarcely any weeper on her bonnet, and

really show she any testamentary tenderness, it would be affect to
think of he, poor man, when he be go; and even his foolish fuss
about the flower and garden-stuff, and his insistence on the subject
of snail, would be touch when it be once fairly at an end. to
survive Mr. Glegg, and talk eulogistically of he as a man who might

not-withstand his numerous poor relation; to have sum of interest

strong-box would have nullify the pleasure of property; she might

of the future. so that when good Mr. Glegg, restore to good humor by
much hoeing, and move by the sight of his wife's empty chair, with

to."
Baxter have be open at least eight hour by this time, for it be
nearly five o'clock; and if people be to quarrel often, it follow as
a corollary that their quarrel cannot be protract beyond certain
limit.
Mr. and Mrs. Glegg talk quite amicably about the Tullivers that

sad man for get into hot water, and be like enough to run through

declare that it be beneath she to take notice of such a man's
conduct, and that, for her sister's sake, she would let he keep the
five hundred a while long, for when she put it out on a mortgage she
should only get four per cent.
Chapter XIII
Mr. Tulliver Further Entangles the Skein of Life
owe to this new adjustment of Mrs. Glegg's thought, Mrs. Pullet
find her task of mediation the next day surprisingly easy. Mrs.
Glegg, indeed check she rather sharply for think it would be
necessary to tell her eld sister what be the right mode of behavior
in family matter. Mrs. Pullet's argument, that it would look ill in
the neighborhood if people should have it in their power to say that
there be a quarrel in the family, be particularly offensive. if the
family name never suffer except through Mrs. Glegg, Mrs. Pullet
might lay her head on her pillow in perfect confidence.
"itbe not to be expect, I suppose," observe Mrs. Glegg, by way of
wind up the subject, "as I shall go to the mill again before Bessy
come to see I, or as I shall go and fall down o' my knee to Mr.
Tulliver, and ask his pardon for show he favor; but I shall bear
no malice, and when Mr. Tulliver speak civil to I, Iwill speak civil
to he. nobody have any call to tell I whatbe become."
find it unnecessary to plead for the Tullivers, it be natural that

apparently ill-fat house. Mrs. Glegg hear a circumstantial

child, and express a half-form project of pay for Maggie's
be send to a distant boarding-school, which would not prevent she
be so brown, but might tend to subdue some other vice in she, aunt

"yes, you may, Sophy," say Mrs. Glegg; "you may tell Mr. Tulliver,

Mrs. Glegg be in this state of satisfaction in her own lofty
magnanimity, I leave you to judge what effect be produce on she by
the reception of a short letter from Mr. Tulliver that very evening,
after Mrs. Pullet's departure, inform she that she neednot trouble
her mind about her five hundred pound, for it should be pay back to
she in the course of the next month at farth, together with the
interest due thereon until the time of payment. and furthermore, that
Mr. Tulliver have no wish to behave uncivilly to Mrs. Glegg, and she
be welcome to his house whenever she like to come, but he desire no
favor from she, either for himself or his child.
it be poor Mrs. Tulliver who have hasten this catastrophe, entirely
through that irrepressible hopefulness of hers which lead she to expect
that similar cause may at any time produce different result. it have
very often occur in her experience that Mr. Tulliver have do
something because other people have say he be not able to do it, or
have pity he for his suppose inability, or in any other way pique
his pride; still, she think to-day, if she tell he when he come in
to tea that sister Pullet be go to try and make everything up with
sister Glegg, so that he neednot think about pay in the money, it
would give a cheerful effect to the meal. Mr. Tulliver have never
slacken in his resolve to raise the money, but now he at once
determine to write a letter to Mrs. Glegg, which should cut off all
possibility of mistake. Mrs. Pullet go to beg and pray for _he_
indeed! Mr. Tulliver do not willingly write a letter, and find the
relation between speak and write language, briefly know as
spelling, one of the most puzzling thing in this puzzle world.
nevertheless, like all fervid writing, the task be do in less time
than usual, and if the spelling differ from Mrs. Glegg's,--why, she
belong, like himself, to a generation with whom spelling be a
matter of private judgment.
Mrs. Glegg do not alter she will in consequence of this letter, and
cut off the Tulliver child from their sixth and seventh share in
her thousand pound; for she have her principle. no one must be able
to say of she when she be dead that she have not divide her money
with perfect fairness among her own kin. in the matter of will,
personal quality be subordinate to the great fundamental fact of
blood; and to be determine in the distribution of your property by
caprice, and not make your legacy bear a direct ratio to degree of
kinship, be a prospective disgrace that would have embitter her
life. this have always be a principle in the Dodson family; it be
one form if that sense of honor and rectitude which be a proud
tradition in such families,--a tradition which have be the salt of
our provincial society.
but though the letter could not shake Mrs. Glegg's principle, it make
the family breach much more difficult to mend; and as to the effect it
produce on Mrs. Glegg's opinion of Mr. Tulliver, she beg to be
understand from that time forth that she have nothing whatever to say
about he; his state of mind, apparently, be too corrupt for she to
contemplate it for a moment. it be not until the evening before Tom
go to school, at the beginning of August, that Mrs. Glegg pay a
visit to her sister Tulliver, sit in her gig all the while, and
show her displeasure by markedly abstain from all advice and
criticism; for, as she observe to her sister Deane, "Bessy must bear
the consequence o' have such a husband, though Ibe sorry for she,"
and Mrs. Deane agree that Bessy be pitiable.
that evening Tom observe to Maggie: "oh my! Maggie, aunt Glegg's
begin to come again; Ibe glad Ibe go to school. _youwill_ catch
it all now!"
Maggie be already so full of sorrow at the thought of Tom's go
away from she, that this playful exultation of his seem very unkind,
and she cry herself to sleep that night.
Mr. Tulliver's prompt procedure entail on he further promptitude in
find the convenient person who be desirous of lend five hundred
pound on bond. "it must be no client of Wakem's," he say to himself;
and yet at the end of a fortnight it turn out to the contrary; not
because Mr. Tulliver's will be feeble, but because external fact be
strong. Wakem's client be the only convenient person to be find.
Mr. Tulliver have a destiny as well as Oedipus, and in this case
he might plead, like Oedipus, that his deed be inflict on he
rather than commit by he.
Book II
_school-Time_
chapter I
Tom's "First Half"
Tom Tulliver's suffering during the first quarter he be at King's
Lorton, under the distinguished care of the Rev. Walter Stelling, be
rather severe. at Mr. Jacob's academy life have not present itself to
he as a difficult problem; there be plenty of fellow to play with,
and Tom be good at all active games,--fighting especially,--had
that precedence among they which appear to he inseparable from the
personality of Tom Tulliver. Mr. Jacobs himself, familiarly know as
Old Goggles, from his habit of wear spectacle, impose no painful
awe; and if it be the property of snuffy old hypocrite like he to
write like copperplate and surround their signature with arabesque,
to spell without forethought, and to spout "my name be Norval" without
bungling, Tom, for his part, be glad he be not in danger of those
mean accomplishment. he be not go to be a snuffy schoolmaster,
he, but a substantial man, like his father, who use to go hunting
when he be young, and ride a capital black mare,--as pretty a bit
of horse-flesh as ever you see; Tom have hear what her point be a
hundred time. _he_ mean to go hunt too, and to be generally
respect. when people be grow up, he consider, nobody inquire
about their writing and spelling; when he be a man, he should be
master of everything, and do just as he like. it have be very
difficult for he to reconcile himself to the idea that his
school-time be to be prolong and that he be not to be bring up
to his father's business, which he have always think extremely
pleasant; for it be nothing but rid about, give order, and
go to market; and he think that a clergyman would give he a
great many scripture lesson, and probably make he learn the Gospel
and Epistle on a Sunday, as well as the Collect. but in the absence of
specific information, it be impossible for he to imagine that school
and a schoolmaster would be something entirely different from the
academy of Mr. Jacobs. so, not to be at a deficiency, in case of his
finding genial companion, he have take care to carry with he a small
box of percussion-cap; not that there be anything particular to be
do with they, but they would serve to impress strange boy with a
sense of his familiarity with gun. thus poor Tom, though he see very
clearly through Maggie's illusion, be not without illusion of his
own, which be to be cruelly dissipate by his enlarged experience at
King's Lorton.
he have not be there a fortnight before it be evident to he that
life, complicated not only with the Latin grammar but with a new
standard of english pronunciation, be a very difficult business, make
all the more obscure by a thick mist of bash fulness. Tom, as you have
observe, be never an exception among boy for ease of address; but
the difficulty of enunciate a monosyllable in reply to Mr. or Mrs.
Stelling be so great, that he even dread to be ask at table
whether he would have more pudding. as to the percussion-cap, he have
almost resolve, in the bitterness of his heart, that he would throw
they into a neighboring pond; for not only be he the solitary pupil,
but he begin even to have a certain scepticism about gun, and a
general sense that his theory of life be undermine. for Mr. Stelling
think nothing of gun, or horse either, apparently; and yet it be
impossible for Tom to despise Mr. Stelling as he have despise Old
Goggles. if there be anything that be not thoroughly genuine about
Mr. Stelling, it lie quite beyond Tom's power to detect it; it be only
by a wide comparison of fact that the wise full-grow man can
distinguish well-roll barrel from mere supernal thunder.
Mr. Stelling be a well-size, broad-cheste man, not yet thirty, with
flaxen hair standing erect, and large lightish-gray eye, which be
always very wide open; he have a sonorous bass voice, and an air of
defiant self-confidence incline to brazenness. he have enter on his
career with great vigor, and intend to make a considerable
impression on his fellowman. the Rev. Walter Stelling be not a man
who would remain among the "inferior clergy" all his life. he have a
true british determination to push his way in the world,--as a
schoolmaster, in the first place, for there be capital mastership
of grammar-school to be have, and Mr. Stelling mean to have one of
they; but as a preacher also, for he mean always to preach in a
strike manner, so as to have his congregation swell by admirer
from neighboring parish, and to produce a great sensation whenever
he take occasional duty for a brother clergyman of minor gift. the
style of preach he have choose be the extemporaneous, which be
hold little short of the miraculous in rural parish like King's
Lorton. some passage of Massillon and Bourdaloue, which he know by
heart, be really very effective when roll out in Mr. Stelling's
deep tone; but as comparatively feeble appeal of his own be
deliver in the same loud and impressive manner, they be often
think quite as strike by his hearer. Mr. Stelling's doctrine be
of no particular school; if anything, it have a tinge of
evangelicalism, for that be "the tell thing" just then in the
diocese to which King's Lorton belong. in short, Mr. Stelling be a
man who mean to rise in his profession, and to rise by merit,
clearly, since he have no interest beyond what might be promise by a
problematic relationship to a great lawyer who have not yet become Lord
Chancellor. a clergyman who have such vigorous intention naturally
get a little into debt at start; it be not to be expect that he
will live in the meagre style of a man who mean to be a poor curate
all his life; and if the few hundred Mr. Timpson advance toward his
daughter's fortune do not suffice for the purchase of handsome
furniture, together with a stock of wine, a grand piano, and the
lay out of a superior flower-garden, it follow in the most
rigorous manner, either that these thing must be procure by some
other mean, or else that the Rev. Mr. Stelling must go without they,
which last alternative would be an absurd procrastination of the
fruit of success, where success be certain. Mr. Stelling be so
broad-cheste and resolute that he feel equal to anything; he would
become celebrate by shake the conscience of his hearer, and he
would by and by edit a greek play, and invent several new reading. he
have not yet select the play, for have be marry little more
than two year, his leisure time have be much occupy with
attention to Mrs. Stelling; but he have tell that fine woman what he
mean to do some day, and she feel great confidence in her husband, as
a man who understand everything of that sort.
but the immediate step to future success be to bring on Tom Tulliver
during this first half-year; for, by a singular coincidence, there have
be some negotiation concern another pupil from the same
neighborhood and it might further a decision in Mr. Stelling's favor,
if it be understand that young Tulliver, who, Mr. Stelling observe
in conjugal privacy, be rather a rough cub, have make prodigious
progress in a short time. it be on this ground that he be severe
with Tom about his lesson; he be clearly a boy whose power would
never be develop through the medium of the latin grammar, without
the application of some sternness. not that Mr. Stelling be a
harsh-temper or unkind man; quite the contrary. he be jocose with
Tom at table, and correct his provincialism and his deportment in
the most playful manner; but poor Tom be only the more cowed and
confuse by this double novelty, for he have never be use to joke
at all like Mr. Stelling's; and for the first time in his life he have
a painful sense that he be all wrong somehow. when Mr. Stelling say,
as the roast-beef be be uncover, "now, Tulliver! which would you
rather decline, roast-beef or the Latin for it?" Tom, to whom in his
cool moment a pun would have be a hard nut, be throw into a
state of embarrassed alarm that make everything dim to he except the
feeling that he would rather not have anything to do with Latin; of
course he answer, "roast-beef," whereupon there follow much
laughter and some practical joking with the plate, from which Tom
gather that he have in some mysterious way refuse beef, and, in
fact, make himself appear "a silly." if he could have see a
fellow-pupil undergo these painful operation and survive they in good
spirit, he might soon have take they as a matter of course. but
there be two expensive form of education, either of which a parent
may procure for his son by send he as solitary pupil to a
clergyman: one be the enjoyment of the reverend gentleman's undivided
neglect; the other be the endurance of the reverend gentleman's
undivided attention. it be the latter privilege for which Mr.
Tulliver pay a high price in Tom's initiatory month at King's
Lorton.
that respectable miller and maltster have leave Tom behind, and drive
