fact; he have the same way of stick his thumb in the armhole of
his waistcoat. Mr. Tulliver be not by any mean an exception in
mistaking brazenness for shrewdness; most layman think stelling
shrewd, and a man of remarkable power generally; it be chiefly by
his clerical brother that he be consider rather a full fellow. but
he tell Mr. Tulliver several story about "swing" and incendiarism,
and ask his advice about feed pig in so thoroughly secular and
judicious a manner, with so much polished glibness of tongue, that the
miller thought, here be the very thing he want for Tom. he have no
doubt this first-rate man be acquaint with every branch of
information, and know exactly what Tom must learn in order to become a
match for the lawyer, which poor Mr. Tulliver himself do _not_ know,
and so be necessarily throw for self-direction on this wide kind of
inference. it be hardly fair to laugh at he, for I have know much
more highly instruct person than he make inference quite as wide,
and not at all wise.
as for Mrs. Tulliver, find that Mrs. Stelling's view as to the
airing of linen and the frequent recurrence of hunger in a grow boy
entirely coincide with her own; moreover, that Mrs. Stelling, though
so young a woman, and only anticipate her second confinement, have
go through very nearly the same experience as herself with regard to
the behavior and fundamental character of the monthly nurse,--she
express great contentment to her husband, when they drive away, at
leave Tom with a woman who, in spite of her youth, seem quite
sensible and motherly, and ask advice as prettily as could be.
"they must be very well off, though," say Mrs. Tulliver, "for
everythingbe as nice as can be all over the house, and that water
silk she have on cost a pretty penny. Sister Pullet have get one like
it."
"ah," say Mr. Tulliver, "hebe get some income besides the curacy, I
reckon. perhaps her father allow them something. therebe Tom 'ull be
another hundred to he, and not much trouble either, by his own
account; he say teaching come natural to he. thatbe wonderful,
now," add Mr. Tulliver, turn his head on one side, and give his
horse a meditative tickling on the flank.
perhaps it be because teaching come naturally to Mr. Stelling, that
he set about it with that uniformity of method and independence of
circumstance which distinguish the action of animal understand to
be under the immediate teaching of nature. Mr. Broderip's amiable
beaver, as that charming naturalist tell we, busy himself as
earnestly in construct a dam, in a room up three pair of stair in
London, as if he have be lay his foundation in a stream or lake in
Upper Canada. it be "Binny's" function to build; the absence of water
or of possible progeny be an accident for which he be not
accountable. with the same unerring instinct Mr. Stelling set to work
at his natural method of instill the Eton Grammar and Euclid into
the mind of Tom Tulliver. this, he consider, be the only basis of
solid instruction; all other mean of education be mere
charlatanism, and could produce nothing well than smatterer. fix
on this firm basis, a man might observe the display of various or
special knowledge make by irregularly educate people with a pitying
smile; all that sort of thing be very well, but it be impossible
these people could form sound opinion. in hold this conviction Mr.
Stelling be not biassed, as some tutor have be, by the excessive
accuracy or extent of his own scholarship; and as to his view about
Euclid, no opinion could have be free from personal partiality. Mr.
Stelling be very far from be lead astray by enthusiasm, either
religious or intellectual; on the other hand, he have no secret belief
that everything be humbug. he think religion be a very excellent
thing, and Aristotle a great authority, and deanery and prebend
useful institution, and Great Britain the providential bulwark of
Protestantism, and faith in the unseen a great support to afflict
mind; he believe in all these thing, as a swiss hotel-keeper
believe in the beauty of the scenery around he, and in the pleasure
it give to artistic visitor. and in the same way Mr. Stelling
believe in his method of education; he have no doubt that he be do
the very good thing for Mr. Tulliver's boy. of course, when the miller
talk of "mapping" and "sum" in a vague and diffident manner, Mr
Stelling have set his mind at rest by an assurance that he understand
what be want; for how be it possible the good man could form any
reasonable judgment about the matter? Mr Stelling's duty be to teach
the lad in the only right way,--indeed he know no other; he have not
waste his time in the acquirement of anything abnormal.
he very soon set down poor Tom as a thoroughly stupid lad; for though
by hard labor he could get particular declension into his brain,
anything so abstract as the relation between case and termination
could by no mean get such a lodgment there as to enable he to
recognize a chance genitive or dative. this strike Mr. Stelling as
something more than natural stupidity; he suspect obstinacy, or at
any rate indifference, and lecture Tom severely on his want of
thorough application. "you feel no interest in what yoube do,
sir," Mr. Stelling would say, and the reproach be painfully true. Tom
have never find any difficulty in discern a pointer from a setter,
when once he have be tell the distinction, and his perceptive power
be not at all deficient. I fancy they be quite as strong as those
of the Rev. Mr. Stelling; for Tom could predict with accuracy what
number of horse be canter behind he, he could throw a stone
right into the centre of a give ripple, he could guess to a fraction
how many length of his stick it would take to reach across the
playground, and could draw almost perfect square on his slate without
any measurement. but Mr. Stelling take no note of these thing; he
only observe that Tom's faculty fail he before the abstraction
hideously symbolize to he in the page of the Eton Grammar, and that
he be in a state border on idiocy with regard to the demonstration
that two give triangle must be equal, though he could discern with
great promptitude and certainty the fact that they _be_ equal.
whence Mr. Stelling conclude that Tom's brain, be peculiarly
impervious to etymology and demonstration, be peculiarly in need of
be plough and harrow by these patent implement; it be his
favorite metaphor, that the classic and geometry constitute that
culture of the mind which prepare it for the reception of any
subsequent crop. I say nothing against Mr. Stelling's theory; if we
be to have one regimen for all mind, his seem to I as good as any
other. I only know it turn out as uncomfortably for Tom Tulliver as
if he have be ply with cheese in order to remedy a gastric weakness
which prevent he from digest it. it be astonish what a
different result one get by change the metaphor! once call the
brain an intellectual stomach, and one's ingenious conception of the
classic and geometry as plough and harrow seem to settle nothing.
but then it be open to some one else to follow great authority, and
call the mind a sheet of white paper or a mirror, in which case one's
knowledge of the digestive process become quite irrelevant. it be
doubtless an ingenious idea to call the camel the ship of the desert,
but it would hardly lead one far in training that useful beast. O
Aristotle! if you have have the advantage of be "the fresh modern"
instead of the great ancient, would you not have mingle your
praise of metaphorical speech, as a sign of high intelligence, with a
lamentation that intelligence so rarely show itself in speech without
metaphor,--that we can so seldom declare what a thing be, except by
say it be something else?
Tom Tulliver, be abundant in no form of speech, do not use any
metaphor to declare his view as to the nature of Latin; he never
call it an instrument of torture; and it be not until he have get on
some way in the next half-year, and in the Delectus, that he be
advanced enough to call it a "bore" and "beastly stuff." at present,
in relation to this demand that he should learn Latin declension and
conjugation, Tom be in a state of as blank unimaginativeness
concern the cause and tendency of his suffering, as if he have be
an innocent shrewmouse imprison in the split trunk of an ash-tree in
order to cure lameness in cattle. it be doubtless almost incredible to
instruct mind of the present day that a boy of twelve, not
belong strictly to "the mass," who be now understand to have the
monopoly of mental darkness, should have have no distinct idea how
there come to be such a thing as Latin on this earth; yet so it be
with Tom. it would have take a long while to make conceivable to he
that there ever exist a people who buy and sell sheep and oxen,
and transact the every-day affair of life, through the medium of
this language; and still long to make he understand why he should
be call upon to learn it, when its connection with those affair have
become entirely latent. so far as Tom have gain any acquaintance with
the Romans at Mr. Jacob's academy, his knowledge be strictly correct,
but it go no far than the fact that they be "in the New
Testament"; and Mr. Stelling be not the man to enfeeble and
emasculate his pupil's mind by simplify and explain, or to
reduce the tonic effect of etymology by mix it with smatter,
extraneous information, such as be give to girl.
yet, strange to say, under this vigorous treatment Tom become more
like a girl than he have ever be in his life before. he have a large
share of pride, which have hitherto find itself very comfortable in
the world, despise Old Goggles, and repose in the sense of
unquestione right; but now this same pride meet with nothing but
bruise and crushing. Tom be too clear-sight not to be aware that
Mr. Stelling's standard of thing be quite different, be certainly
something high in the eye of the world than that of the people he
have be live amongst, and that, bring in contact with it, he, Tom
Tulliver, appear uncouth and stupid; he be by no mean indifferent
to this, and his pride get into an uneasy condition which quite
nullify his boyish self-satisfaction, and give he something of the
girl's susceptibility. he be a very firm, not to say obstinate,
disposition, but there be no brute-like rebellion and recklessness in
his nature; the human sensibility predominate, and if it have
occur to he that he could enable himself to show some quickness at
his lesson, and so acquire Mr. Stelling's approbation, by stand on
one leg for an inconvenient length of time, or rap his head
moderately against the wall, or any voluntary action of that sort, he
would certainly have try it. but no; Tom have never hear that these
measure would brighten the understanding, or strengthen the verbal
memory; and he be not give to hypothesis and experiment. it do
occur to he that he could perhaps get some help by pray for it;
but as the prayer he say every evening be form learn by heart,
he rather shrink from the novelty and irregularity of introduce an
extempore passage on a topic of petition for which he be not aware of
any precedent. but one day, when he have break down, for the fifth
time, in the supine of the third conjugation, and Mr. Stelling,
convince that this must be carelessness, since it transcend the
bound of possible stupidity, have lecture he very seriously,
point out that if he fail to seize the present golden opportunity
of learn supine, he would have to regret it when he become a
man,--tom, more miserable than usual, determine to try his sole
resource; and that evening, after his usual form of prayer for his
parent and "little sister" (he have begin to pray for Maggie when she
be a baby), and that he might be able always to keep God's
commandment, he add, in the same low whisper, "and please to make
I always remember my Latin." he pause a little to consider how he
should pray about Euclid--whether he should ask to see what it mean,
or whether there be any other mental state which would be more
applicable to the case. but at last he add: "and make Mr. Stelling
say I sha'n't do Euclid any more. amen."
the fact that he get through his supine without mistake the next day,
encourage he to persevere in this appendix to his prayer, and
neutralize any scepticism that might have arise from Mr. Stelling's
continue demand for Euclid. but his faith break down under the
apparent absence of all help when he get into the irregular verb. it
seem clear that Tom's despair under the caprice of the present
tense do not constitute a _nodus_ worthy of interference, and since
this be the climax of his difficulty, where be the use of pray
for help any long? he make up his mind to this conclusion in one of
his dull, lonely evening, which he spend in the study, prepare his
lesson for the morrow. his eye be apt to get dim over the page,
though he hat cry, and be ashamed of it; he couldnot help
think with some affection even of Spouncer, whom he use to fight
and quarrel with; he would have feel at home with Spouncer, and in a
condition of superiority. and then the mill, and the river, and Yap
prick up his ear, ready to obey the least sign when Tom say,
"Hoigh!" would all come before he in a sort of calenture, when his
finger play absently in his pocket with his great knife and his
coil of whipcord, and other relic of the past.
Tom, as I say, have never be so much like a girl in his life before,
and at that epoch of irregular verb his spirit be far depress
by a new mean of mental development which have be think of for he
out of school hour. Mrs. Stelling have lately have her second baby, and
as nothing could be more salutary for a boy than to feel himself
useful, Mrs. Stelling consider she be do Tom a service by
set he to watch the little cherub Laura while the nurse be
occupy with the sickly baby. it be quite a pretty employment for
Tom to take little Laura out in the sunniest hour of the autumn day;
it would help to make he feel that Lorton Parsonage be a home for
he, and that he be one of the family. the little cherub Laura, not
be an accomplished walker at present, have a ribbon fasten round
her waist, by which Tom hold she as if she have be a little dog
during the minute in which she choose to walk; but as these be rare,
he be for the most part carry this fine child round and round the
garden, within sight of Mrs. Stelling's window, accord to order.
if any one consider this unfair and even oppressive toward Tom, I beg
he to consider that there be feminine virtue which be with
difficulty combine, even if they be not incompatible. when the wife
of a poor curate contrive, under all her disadvantage, to dress
extremely well, and to have a style of coiffure which require that
her nurse shall occasionally officiate as lady's-maid; when, moreover,
her dinner-party and her drawing-room show that effort at elegance
and completeness of appointment to which ordinary woman might imagine
a large income necessary, it would be unreasonable to expect of she
that she should employ a second nurse, or even act as a nurse herself.
Mr. Stelling know well; he see that his wife do wonder already,
and be proud of she. it be certainly not the good thing in the world
for young Tulliver's gait to carry a heavy child, but he have plenty of
exercise in long walk with himself, and next half-year Mr. Stelling
would see about have a drilling-master. among the many mean whereby
Mr. Stelling intend to be more fortunate than the bulk of his
fellow-man, he have entirely give up that of have his own way in his
own house. what then? he have marry "as kind a little soul as ever
breathe," accord to Mr. Riley, who have be acquaint with Mrs.
Stelling's blond ringlet and smile demeanor throughout her maiden
life, and on the strength of that knowledge would have be ready any
day to pronounce that whatever domestic difference might arise in her
married life must be entirely Mr. Stelling's fault.
if Tom have have a bad disposition, he would certainly have hat the
little cherub Laura, but he be too kind-hearte a lad for that; there
be too much in he of the fibre that turn to true manliness, and to
protect pity for the weak. I be afraid he hat Mrs. Stelling, and
contract a last dislike to pale blond ringlet and broad plait,
as directly associate with haughtiness of manner, and a frequent
reference to other people's "duty." but he couldnot help play with
little Laura, and like to amuse she; he even sacrifice his
percussion-cap for her sake, in despair of their ever serve a
great purpose,--thinking the small flash and bang would delight she,
and thereby draw down on himself a rebuke from Mrs. Stelling for
teach her child to play with fire. Laura be a sort of
playfellow--and oh, how Tom long for playfellow! in his secret
heart he yearn to have Maggie with he, and be almost ready to dote
on her exasperating act of forgetfulness; though, when he be at
home, he always represent it as a great favor on his part to let
Maggie trot by his side on his pleasure excursion.
and before this dreary half-year be end, Maggie actually come. Mrs.
Stelling have give a general invitation for the little girl to come
and stay with her brother; so when Mr. Tulliver drive over to King's
Lorton late in October, Maggie come too, with the sense that she be
take a great journey, and begin to see the world. it be Mr.
Tulliver's first visit to see Tom, for the lad must learn not to think
too much about home.
"well, my lad," he say to Tom, when Mr. Stelling have leave the room to
announce the arrival to his wife, and Maggie have begin to kiss Tom
freely, "you look rarely! school agree with you."
Tom wish he have look rather ill.
"I donot think I _be_ well, father," say Tom; "I wish youwould ask Mr.
Stelling not to let I do Euclid; it bring on the toothache, I
think."
(the toothache be the only malady to which Tom have ever be
subject.)
"Euclid, my lad,--why, whatbe that?" say Mr. Tulliver.
"oh, I donot know; itbe definition, and axiom, and triangle, and
thing. itbe a book Ihave get to learn in--therebe no sense in it."
"go, go!" say Mr. Tulliver, reprovingly; "you mustnot say so. you
must learn what your master tell you. he know what itbe right for
you to learn."
"_Iwill_ help you now, Tom," say Maggie, with a little air of
patronize consolation. "Ibe come to stay ever so long, if Mrs.
Stelling ask I. Ihave bring my box and my pinafore, havenot I,
father?"
"_you_ help I, you silly little thing!" say Tom, in such high
spirit at this announcement that he quite enjoy the idea of
confound Maggie by show she a page of Euclid. "I should like to
see you do one of _my_ lesson! why, I learn Latin too! girl never
learn such thing. theybe too silly."
"I know what Latin be very well," say Maggie, confidently, "Latin's a
language. there be latin word in the Dictionary. there's bonus, a
gift."
"now, yoube just wrong there, Miss Maggie!" say Tom, secretly
astonish. "you think yoube very wise! but 'bonus' mean 'good,' as
it happens,--bonus, bona, bonum."
"well, thatbe no reason why it shouldnot mean 'gift,'" say Maggie,
stoutly. "it may mean several thing; almost every word do. therebe
'lawn,'--it mean the grass-plot, as well as the stuff pocket-
handkerchief be make of."
"well do, little 'un," say Mr. Tulliver, laugh, while Tom feel
rather disgusted with Maggie's knowingness, though beyond measure
cheerful at the thought that she be go to stay with he. her
conceit would soon be overawe by the actual inspection of his book.
Mrs. Stelling, in her press invitation, do not mention a long
time than a week for Maggie's stay; but Mr. Stelling, who take she
between his knee, and ask she where she steal her dark eye from,
insist that she must stay a fortnight. Maggie think Mr. Stelling
be a charming man, and Mr. Tulliver be quite proud to leave his
little wench where she would have an opportunity of show she
cleverness to appreciate stranger. so it be agree that she should
not be fetched home till the end of the fortnight.
"now, then, come with I into the study, Maggie," say Tom, as their
father drive away. "what do you shake and toss your head now for, you
silly?" he continue; for though her hair be now under a new
dispensation, and be brush smoothly behind her ear, she seem
still in imagination to be toss it out of her eye. "it make you
look as if you be crazy."
"oh, I cannot help it," say Maggie, impatiently. "donot tease I, Tom.
oh, what book!" she exclaim, as she see the bookcase in the study.
"how I should like to have as many book as that!"
"why, you couldnot read one of them," say Tom, triumphantly. "theybe
all Latin."
"no, they benot," say Maggie. "I can read the back of
this,--'history of the Decline and Fall of the Roman Empire.'"
"well, what do that mean? _you_ donot know," say Tom, wag his
head.
"but I could soon find out," say Maggie, scornfully.
"why, how?"
"I should look inside, and see what it be about."
"youwould well not, Miss Maggie," say Tom, see her hand on the
volume. "Mr. Stelling let nobody touch his book without leave, and
_I_ shall catch it, if you take it out."
"oh, very well. let I see all _your_ book, then," say Maggie,
turn to throw her arm round Tom's neck, and rub his cheek with her
small round nose.
Tom, in the gladness of his heart at have dear old Maggie to dispute
with and crow over again, seize her round the waist, and begin to
jump with her round the large library table. away they jump with
more and more vigor, till Maggie's hair fly from behind her ear, and
twirl about like an animate mop. but the revolution round the
table become more and more irregular in their sweep, till at last
reach Mr. Stelling's reading stand, they send it thunder down
with its heavy lexicon to the floor. happily it be the ground-floor,
and the study be a one-story wing to the house, so that the
downfall make no alarming resonance, though Tom stand dizzy and aghast
for a few minute, dread the appearance of Mr. or Mrs. Stelling.
"oh, I say, Maggie," say Tom at last, lift up the stand, "we must
keep quiet here, you know. if we break anything Mrs. Stelling'll make
we cry peccavi."
"whatbe that?" say Maggie.
"oh, itbe the Latin for a good scolding," say Tom, not without some
pride in his knowledge.
"be she a cross woman?" say Maggie.
"I believe you!" say Tom, with an emphatic nod.
"I think all woman be cross than man," say Maggie. "Aunt Glegg's a
great deal crosser than uncle Glegg, and mother scold I more than
father do."
"well, _youwill_ be a woman some day," say Tom, "so _you_ neednot
talk."
"but I shall be a _clever_ woman," say Maggie, with a toss.
"oh, I dare say, and a nasty conceited thing. Everybody'll hate you."
"but you oughtnot to hate I, Tom; itwill be very wicked of you, for I
shall be your sister."
"yes, but if yoube a nasty disagreeable thing I _shall_ hate you."
"oh, but, Tom, you willnot! I sha'n't be disagreeable. I shall be very
good to you, and I shall be good to everybody. you willnot hate I
really, will you, Tom?"
"oh, bother! never mind! come, itbe time for I to learn my lesson.
see here! what Ihave get to do," say Tom, draw Maggie toward he
and show she his theorem, while she push her hair behind her
ear, and prepare herself to prove her capability of help he in
Euclid. she begin to read with full confidence in her own power, but
presently, become quite bewilder, her face flush with
irritation. it be unavoidable; she must confess her incompetency, and
she be not fond of humiliation.
"itbe nonsense!" she say, "and very ugly stuff; nobody need want to
make it out."
"ah, there, now, Miss Maggie!" say Tom, draw the book away, and
wag his head at she, "you see yoube not so clever as you think
you be."
"oh," say Maggie, pouting, "I dare say I could make it out, if Ihave
learn what go before, as you have."
"but thatbe what you just couldnot, Miss Wisdom," say Tom. "for itbe
all the hard when you know what go before; for then youhave get to
say what definition 3 be, and what axiom V. be. but get along with you
now; I must go on with this. herebe the Latin Grammar. see what you
can make of that."
Maggie find the Latin Grammar quite soothing after her mathematical
mortification; for she delight in new word, and quickly find that
there be an English Key at the end, which would make she very wise
about Latin, at slight expense. she presently make up her mind to skip
the rule in the Syntax, the example become so absorbing. these
mysterious sentence, snatch from an unknown context,--like strange
horn of beast, and leaf of unknown plant, bring from some
far-off region,--gave boundless scope to her imagination, and be all
the more fascinating because they be in a peculiar tongue of their
own, which she could learn to interpret. it be really very
interesting, the Latin Grammar that Tom have say no girl could learn;
and she be proud because she find it interesting. the most
fragmentary example be her favourite. _mor omnibus est communi_
would have be jejune, only she like to know the Latin; but the
fortunate gentleman whom every one congratulate because he have a son
"endow with _such_ a disposition" afford she a great deal of
pleasant conjecture, and she be quite lose in the "thick grove
penetrable by no star," when Tom call out,--
"now, then, Magsie, give we the Grammar!"
"oh, Tom, itbe such a pretty book!" she say, as she jump out of the
large arm-chair to give it he; "itbe much pretty than the
Dictionary. I could learn Latin very soon. I donot think itbe at all
hard."
"oh, I know what youhave be do," say Tom; "youhave be read
the English at the end. any donkey can do that."
Tom seize the book and open it with a determined and business-like
air, as much as to say that he have a lesson to learn which no donkey
would find themselves equal to. Maggie, rather pique, turn to the
bookcase to amuse herself with puzzle out the title.
presently Tom call to she: "here, Magsie, come and hear if I can say
this. stand at that end of the table, where Mr. Stelling sit when he
hear I."
Maggie obey, and take the open book.
"where do you begin, Tom?"
"oh, I begin at _'Appellativa arborum,'_ because I say all over again
what Ihave be learn this week."
Tom sail along pretty well for three line; and Maggie be begin
to forget her office of prompter in speculate as to what _mas_ could
mean, which come twice over, when he stick fast at _Sunt etiam
volucrum_.
"donot tell I, Maggie; _Sunt etiam volucrum_--_Sunt etiam
volucrum_--_ut ostrea, cetus_----"
"no," say Maggie, open her mouth and shake her head.
"_Sunt etiam volucrum_," say Tom, very slowly, as if the next word
might be expect to come soon when he give they this strong hint
that they be wait for.
"C, e, u," say Maggie, get impatient.
"oh, I know--hold your tongue," say Tom. "_Ceu passer, hirundo;
ferarum_--_ferarum_----" Tom take his pencil and make several hard
dot with it on his book-cover--"_ferarum_----"
"oh dear, oh dear, Tom," say Maggie, "what a time you be! _ut_----"
"_Ut ostrea_----"
"no, no," say Maggie, "_ut tigris_----"
"oh yes, now I can do," say Tom; "it be _tigris, vulpe_, Iwould
forget: _ut tigris, volupe; et Piscium_."
with some further stammering and repetition, Tom get through the next
few line.
"now, then," he say, "the next be what Ihave just learn for
to-morrow. give I hold of the book a minute."
after some whisper gabbling, assist by the beating of his fist on
the table, Tom return the book.
"_Mascula nomina in a_," he begin.
"no, Tom," say Maggie, "that donot come next. itbe _noman non
cresken genittivo_----"
"_cresken genittivo!_" exclaim Tom, with a derisive laugh, for Tom
have learn this omit passage for his yesterday's lesson, and a
young gentleman do not require an intimate or extensive acquaintance
with Latin before he can feel the pitiable absurdity of a false
quantity. "_cresken genittivo!_ what a little silly you be, Maggie!"
"well, you neednot laugh, Tom, for you donot remember it at all. Ibe
sure itbe spell so; how be I to know?"
"phee-e-e-h! I tell you girl couldnot learn Latin. itbe _noman non
crescen genitivo_."
"very well, then," say Maggie, pouting. I can say that as well as you
can. and you donot mind your stop. for you ought to stop twice as
long at a semicolon as you do at a comma, and you make the long
stop where there ought to be no stop at all."
"oh, well, donot chatter. let I go on."
they be presently fetch to spend the rest of the evening in the
drawing-room, and Maggie become so animate with Mr. Stelling, who,
she feel sure, admire her cleverness, that Tom be rather amazed and
alarm at her audacity. but she be suddenly subdue by Mr.
Stelling's allude to a little girl of whom he have hear that she
once run away to the gypsy.
"what a very odd little girl that must be!" say Mrs. Stelling,
meaning to be playful; but a playfulness that turn on her suppose
oddity be not at all to Maggie's taste. she fear that Mr. Stelling,
after all, do not think much of she, and go to bed in rather low
spirit. Mrs. Stelling, she feel, look at she as if she think she
hair be very ugly because it hang down straight behind.
nevertheless it be a very happy fortnight to Maggie, this visit to
Tom. she be allow to be in the study while he have his lesson, and
in her various reading get very deep into the example in the Latin
Grammar. the astronomer who hat woman generally cause she so much
puzzle speculation that she one day ask Mr. Stelling if all
astronomer hat woman, or whether it be only this particular
astronomer. but forestall his answer, she said,--
"I suppose itbe all astronomer; because, you know, they live up in
high tower, and if the woman come there they might talk and hinder
they from look at the star."
Mr. Stelling like she prattle immensely, and they be on the good
term. she tell Tom she should like to go to school to Mr. Stelling,
as he do, and learn just the same thing. she know she could do
Euclid, for she have look into it again, and she see what a b C
meant; they be the name of the line.
"Ibe sure you couldnot do it, now," say Tom; "and Iwill just ask Mr.
Stelling if you could."
"I donot mind," say the little conceited minx, "Iwill ask he myself."
"Mr. Stelling," she say, that same evening when they be in the
drawing-room, "couldnot I do Euclid, and all Tom's lesson, if you
be to teach I instead of he?"
"no, you couldnot," say Tom, indignantly. "girl cannot do Euclid; can
they, sir?"
"they can pick up a little of everything, I dare say," say Mr.
Stelling. "theyhave a great deal of superficial cleverness; but they
couldnot go far into anything. theybe quick and shallow."
Tom, delight with this verdict, telegraph his triumph by wag
his head at Maggie, behind Mr. Stelling's chair. as for Maggie, she
have hardly ever be so mortified. she have be so proud to be call
"quick" all her little life, and now it appear that this quickness
be the brand of inferiority. it would have be well to be slow,
like Tom.
"ha, ha! Miss Maggie!" say Tom, when they be alone; "you see itbe
not such a fine thing to be quick. youwill never go far into anything,
you know."
and Maggie be so oppress by this dreadful destiny that she have no
spirit for a retort.
but when this small apparatus of shallow quickness be fetch away in
the gig by Luke, and the study be once more quite lonely for Tom, he
miss she grievously. he have really be bright, and have get
through his lesson well, since she have be there; and she have
ask Mr. Stelling so many question about the Roman Empire, and
whether there really ever be a man who say, in Latin, "I would not
buy it for a farthing or a rotten nut," or whether that have only be
turn into Latin, that Tom have actually come to a dim understanding
of the fact that there have once be people upon the earth who be so
fortunate as to know Latin without learn it through the medium of
the Eton Grammar. this luminous idea be a great addition to his
historical acquirement during this half-year, which be otherwise
confine to an epitomize history of the Jews.
but the dreary half-year _do_ come to an end. how glad Tom be to see
the last yellow leaf flutter before the cold wind! the dark
afternoon and the first December snow seem to he far lively than
the August sunshine; and that he might make himself the surer about
the flight of the day that be carry he homeward, he stick
twenty-one stick deep in a corner of the garden, when he be three
week from the holiday, and pull one up every day with a great
wrench, throw it to a distance with a vigor of will which would
have carry it to limbo, if it have be in the nature of stick to
travel so far.
but it be worth purchasing, even at the heavy price of the Latin
Grammar, the happiness of see the bright light in the parlor at
home, as the gig pass noiselessly over the snow-cover bridge; the
happiness of pass from the cold air to the warmth and the kiss
and the smile of that familiar hearth, where the pattern of the rug
and the grate and the fire-iron be "first idea" that it be no
more possible to criticise than the solidity and extension of matter.
there be no sense of ease like the ease we feel in those scene where
we be bear, where object become dear to we before we have know the
labor of choice, and where the outer world seem only an extension of
our own personality; we accept and love it as we accept our own
sense of existence and our own limb. very commonplace, even ugly,
that furniture of our early home might look if it be put up to
auction; an improved taste in upholstery scorn it; and be not the
strive after something well and well in our surrounding the
grand characteristic that distinguish man from the brute, or, to
satisfy a scrupulous accuracy of definition, that distinguish the
british man from the foreign brute? but heaven know where that
strive might lead we, if our affection have not a trick of twining
round those old inferior thing; if the love and sanctity of our
life have no deep immovable root in memory. one's delight in an
elderberry bush overhang the confused leafage of a hedgerow bank,
as a more gladden sight than the fine cistus or fuchsia spread
itself on the soft undulating turf, be an entirely unjustifiable
preference to a nursery-gardener, or to any of those regulate mind
who be free from the weakness of any attachment that do not rest on
a demonstrable superiority of quality. and there be no well reason
for prefer this elderberry bush than that it stir an early
memory; that it be no novelty in my life, speak to I merely
through my present sensibility to form and color, but the long
companion of my existence, that weave itself into my joy when joy
be vivid.
Chapter II
the Christmas Holidays
fine old Christmas, with the snowy hair and ruddy face, have do his
duty that year in the noblest fashion, and have set off his rich gift
of warmth and color with all the heighten contrast of frost and
snow.
Snow lie on the croft and river-bank in undulation soft than the
limb of infancy; it lie with the neatli finished border on every
slop roof, make the dark-red gable stand out with a new depth of
color; it weigh heavily on the laurel and fir-tree, till it fall
from they with a shudder sound; it clothe the rough turnip-field
with whiteness, and make the sheep look like dark blotch; the gate
be all block up with the slop drift, and here and there a
disregarded four-footed beast stand as if petrify "in unrecumbent
sadness"; there be no gleam, no shadow, for the heaven, too, be
one still, pale cloud; no sound or motion in anything but the dark
river that flow and moan like an unreste sorrow. but old
Christmas smile as he lay this cruel-seem spell on the outdoor
world, for he mean to light up home with new brightness, to deepen
all the richness of indoor color, and give a keener edge of delight to
the warm fragrance of food; he mean to prepare a sweet imprisonment
that would strengthen the primitive fellowship of kindred, and make
the sunshine of familiar human face as welcome as the hide
day-star. his kindness fall but hardly on the homeless,--fell but
hardly on the home where the hearth be not very warm, and where the
food have little fragrance; where the human face have have no sunshine
in they, but rather the leaden, blank-eyed gaze of unexpectant want.
but the fine old season mean well; and if he have not learn the
secret how to bless man impartially, it be because his father Time,
with ever-unrelenting unrelenting purpose, still hide that secret in
his own mighty, slow-beat heart.
and yet this Christmas day, in spite of Tom's fresh delight in home,
be not, he think, somehow or other, quite so happy as it have always
be before. the red berry be just as abundant on the holly, and
he and Maggie have dress all the window and mantlepiece and
picture-frame on Christmas eve with as much taste as ever, wedding
the thick-set scarlet cluster with branch of the black-berry ivy.
there have be sing under the window after midnight,--supernatural
singing, Maggie always feel, in spite of Tom's contemptuous insistence
that the singer be old Patch, the parish clerk, and the rest of the
church choir; she tremble with awe when their carolling break in upon
her dream, and the image of man in fustian clothe be always thrust
away by the vision of angel rest on the part cloud. the midnight
chant have help as usual to lift the morning above the level of
common day; and then there be the smell of hot toast and ale from
the kitchen, at the breakfast hour; the favorite anthem, the green
bough, and the short sermon give the appropriate festal character to
the church-going; and aunt and uncle Moss, with all their seven
child, be look like so many reflector of the bright
parlor-fire, when the church-goer come back, stamp the snow from
their foot. the plum-pudding be of the same handsome roundness as
ever, and come in with the symbolic blue flame around it, as if it
have be heroically snatch from the nether fire, into which it have
be throw by dyspeptic Puritans; the dessert be as splendid as
ever, with its golden orange, brown nut, and the crystalline light
and dark of apple-jelly and damson cheese; in all these thing
Christmas be as it have always be since Tom could remember; it be
only distinguish, it by anything, by superior sliding and snowball.
Christmas be cheery, but not so Mr. Tulliver. he be irate and
defiant; and Tom, though he espouse his father's quarrel and share
his father's sense of injury, be not without some of the feeling that
oppress Maggie when Mr. Tulliver get loud and more angry in
narration and assertion with the increase leisure of dessert. the
attention that Tom might have concentrate on his nut and wine be
distract by a sense that there be rascally enemy in the world,
and that the business of grow-up life could hardly be conduct
without a good deal of quarrel. now, Tom be not fond of
quarrelling, unless it could soon be put an end to by a fair stand-up
fight with an adversary whom he have every chance of thrashing; and his
father's irritable talk make he uncomfortable, though he never
account to himself for the feeling, or conceive the notion that his
father be faulty in this respect.
the particular embodiment of the evil principle now excite Mr.
Tulliver's determined resistance be Mr. Pivart, who, have land
high up the Ripple, be take measure for their irrigation, which
either be, or would be, or be bind to be (on the principle that
water be water), an infringement on Mr. Tulliver's legitimate share
of water-power. Dix, who have a mill on the stream, be a feeble
auxiliary of Old Harry compare with Pivart. Dix have be bring to
his sens by arbitration, and Wakem's advice have not carry _he_
far. no; Dix, Mr. Tulliver consider, have be as good as nowhere in
point of law; and in the intensity of his indignation against Pivart,
his contempt for a baffled adversary like Dix begin to wear the air of
a friendly attachment. he have no male audience to-day except Mr. Moss,
who know nothing, as he say, of the "natur' o' mill," and could only
assent to Mr. Tulliver's argument on the _a priori_ ground of family
relationship and monetary obligation; but Mr. Tulliver do not talk
with the futile intention of convince his audience, he talk to
relieve himself; while good Mr. Moss make strong effort to keep his
eye wide open, in spite of the sleepiness which an unusually good
dinner produce in his hard-work frame. Mrs. Moss, more alive to the
subject, and interested in everything that affect her brother,
listen and put in a word as often as maternal preoccupation
allow.
"why, Pivart's a new name hereabout, brother, benot it?" she say; "he
donot own the land in father's time, nor yours either, before I be
married."
"new name? yes, I should think it _be_ a new name," say Mr. Tulliver,
with angry emphasis. "Dorlcote Mill's be in our family a hundred
year and well, and nobody ever hear of a Pivart meddling with the
river, till this fellow come and buy Bincome's farm out of hand,
before anybody else could so much as say 'snap.' but Iwill _pivart_
he!" add Mr. Tulliver, lift his glass with a sense that he have
define his resolution in an unmistakable manner.
"you willnot be force to go to law with he, I hope, brother?" say
Mrs. Moss, with some anxiety.
"I donot know what I shall be force to; but I know what I shall force
_he_ to, with his dike and erigation, if therebe any law to be
bring to bear o' the right side. I know well enough whobe at the
bottom of it; hebe get Wakem to back he and egg he on. I know Wakem
tell he the law cannot touch he for it, but therebe folk can handle
the law besides Wakem. it take a big raskil to beat he; but therebe
big to be find, as know more o' th' in and out o' the law, else
how come Wakem to lose Brumley's suit for he?"
Mr. Tulliver be a strictly honest man, and proud of be honest, but
he consider that in law the end of justice could only be achieve
by employ a strong knave to frustrate a weak. Law be a sort of
cock-fight, in which it be the business of injure honesty to get a
game bird with the good pluck and the strong spur.
"Gore's no fool; you neednot tell I that," he observe presently, in
a pugnacious tone, as if poor Gritty have be urge that lawyer's
capability; "but, you see, he benot up to the law as Wakem be. and
water's a very particular thing; you cannot pick it up with a
pitchfork. thatbe why itbe be nuts to Old Harry and the lawyer.
itbe plain enough whatbe the right and the wrong of water, if you
look at it straight-forrard; for a river's a river, and if youhave get
a mill, you must have water to turn it; and itbe no use tell I
Pivart's erigation and nonsense willnot stop my wheel; I know what
belong to water well than that. talk to I o' what th' engineer
say! I say itbe common sense, as Pivart's dike must do I an injury.
but if thatbe their engineering, Iwill put Tom to it by-and-by, and he
shall see if he cannot find a bit more sense in th' engineering
business than what _that_ come to."
Tom, look round with some anxiety at this announcement of his
prospect, unthinkingly withdraw a small rattle he be amusing baby
Moss with, whereupon she, be a baby that know her own mind with
remarkable clearness, instantaneously express her sentiment in a
pierce yell, and be not to be appease even by the restoration of
the rattle, feel apparently that the original wrong of have it
take from she remain in all its force. Mrs. Moss hurry away with
she into another room, and express to Mrs. Tulliver, who accompany
she, the conviction that the dear child have good reason for cry;
imply that if it be suppose to be the rattle that baby clamor
for, she be a misunderstood baby. the thoroughly justifiable yell
be quiet, Mrs. Moss look at her sister-in-law and said,--
"Ibe sorry to see brother so put out about this water work."
"itbe your brother's way, Mrs. Moss; Iwould never anything o' that sort
before I be marry," say Mrs. Tulliver, with a half-imply
reproach. she always speak of her husband as "your brother" to Mrs.
Moss in any case when his line of conduct be not matter of pure
admiration. amiable Mrs. Tulliver, who be never angry in her life,
have yet her mild share of that spirit without which she could hardly
have be at once a Dodson and a woman. be always on the defensive
toward her own sister, it be natural that she should be keenly
conscious of her superiority, even as the weak Dodson, over a
husband's sister, who, besides be poorly off, and incline to "hang
on" her brother, have the good-natured submissiveness of a large,
easy-temper, untidy, prolific woman, with affection enough in she
not only for her own husband and abundant child, but for any number
of collateral relation.
"I hope and pray he willnot go to law," say Mrs. Moss, "for therebe
never any knowing where thatwill end. and the right donot allay win.
this Mr. Pivart's a rich man, by what I can make out, and the rich
mostly get thing their own way."
"as to that," say Mrs. Tulliver, stroke her dress down, "Ihave see
what rich be in my own family; for my sister have get husband as
can afford to do pretty much what they like. but I think sometimes I
shall be drive off my head with the talk about this law and erigation;
and my sister lie all the fault to I, for they donot know what it be
to marry a man like your brother; how should they? Sister Pullet have
her own way from morning till night."
"well," say Mrs. Moss, "I donot think I should like my husband if he
havenot get any wit of his own, and I have to find head-piece for he.
itbe a deal easy to do what please one's husband, than to be
puzzle what else one should do."
"if people come to talk o' do what please their husband," say
Mrs. Tulliver, with a faint imitation of her sister Glegg, "Ibe sure
your brother might have wait a long while before hewould have find a
wife that 'ud have let he have his say in everything, as I do. itbe
nothing but law and erigation now, from when we first get up in the
morning till we go to bed at night; and I never contradict he; I only
say, 'well, Mr. Tulliver, do as you like; but whativer you do, donot
go to law."
Mrs. Tulliver, as we have see, be not without influence over her
husband. no woman be; she can always incline he to do either what she
wish, or the reverse; and on the composite impulse that be
threaten to hurry Mr. Tulliver into "law," Mrs. Tulliver's
monotonous pleading have doubtless its share of force; it might even be
comparable to that proverbial feather which have the credit or
discredit of break the camel's back; though, on a strictly
impartial view, the blame ought rather to lie with the previous weight
of feather which have already place the back in such imminent peril
that an otherwise innocent feather could not settle on it without
mischief. not that Mrs. Tulliver's feeble beseeching could have have
this feather's weight in virtue of her single personality; but
whenever she depart from entire assent to her husband, he see in her
the representative of the Dodson family; and it be a guide
principle with Mr. Tulliver to let the dodson know that they be not
to domineer over _he_, or--more specifically--that a male Tulliver
be far more than equal to four female dodson, even though one of
they be Mrs. Glegg.
but not even a direct argument from that typical Dodson female herself
against his go to law could have heighten his disposition toward
it so much as the mere thought of Wakem, continually freshen by the
sight of the too able attorney on market-day. Wakem, to his certain
knowledge, be (metaphorically speak) at the bottom of Pivart's
irrigation; Wakem have try to make Dix stand out, and go to law about
the dam; it be unquestionably Wakem who have cause Mr. Tulliver to
lose the suit about the right of road and the bridge that make a
thoroughfare of his land for every vagabond who prefer an
opportunity of damage private property to walk like an honest man
along the highroad; all lawyer be more or less rascal, but Wakem's
rascality be of that peculiarly aggravate kind which place itself
in opposition to that form of right embody in Mr. Tulliver's
interest and opinion. and as an extra touch of bitterness, the
injure miller have recently, in borrow the five hundred pound,
be oblige to carry a little business to Wakem's office on his own
account. a hook-nosed glib fellow! as cool as a cucumber,--always
look so sure of his game! and it be vexatious that Lawyer Gore be
not more like he, but be a bald, round-feature man, with bland
manner and fat hand; a game-cock that you would be rash to bet upon
against Wakem. Gore be a sly fellow. his weakness do not lie on the
side of scrupulosity; but the large amount of winking, however
significant, be not equivalent to see through a stone wall; and
confident as Mr. Tulliver be in his principle that water be water,
and in the direct inference that Pivart have not a leg to stand on in
this affair of irrigation, he have an uncomfortable suspicion that
Wakem have more law to show against this (rationally) irrefragable
inference than Gore could show for it. but then, if they go to law,
there be a chance for Mr. Tulliver to employ Counsellor Wylde on his
side, instead of have that admirable bully against he; and the
prospect of see a witness of Wakem's make to perspire and become
confound, as Mr. Tulliver's witness have once be, be alluring to
the love of retributive justice.
much rumination have Mr. Tulliver on these puzzle subject during his
ride on the gray horse; much turning of the head from side to side,
as the scale dip alternately; but the probable result be still
out of sight, only to be reach through much hot argument and
iteration in domestic and social life. that initial stage of the
dispute which consist in the narration of the case and the
enforcement of Mr. Tulliver's view concern it throughout the
entire circle of his connection would necessarily take time; and at
the beginning of February, when Tom be go to school again, there
be scarcely any new item to be detect in his father's statement
of the case against Pivart, or any more specific indication of the
measure he be bent on take against that rash contravener of the
principle that water be water. Iteration, like friction, be likely to
generate heat instead of progress, and Mr. Tulliver's heat be
certainly more and more palpable. if there have be no new evidence on
any other point, there have be new evidence that Pivart be as "thick
as mud" with Wakem.
"Father," say Tom, one evening near the end of the holiday, "uncle
Glegg say Lawyer Wakem _be_ go to send his son to Mr. Stelling. it
benot true, what they say about his go to be send to France. you
willnot like I to go to school with Wakem's son, shall you?"
"itbe no matter for that, my boy," say Mr. Tulliver; "donot you learn
anything bad of he, thatbe all. the lad's a poor deform creatur,
and take after his mother in the face; I think there benot much of
his father in he. itbe a sign Wakem think high o' Mr. Sterling, as
he send his son to he, and Wakem know meal from bran."
Mr. Tulliver in his heart be rather proud of the fact that his son
be to have the same advantage as Wakem's; but Tom be not at all
easy on the point. it would have be much clear if the lawyer's son
have not be deform, for then Tom would have have the prospect of
pitch into he with all that freedom which be derive from a high
moral sanction.
Chapter III
the New Schoolfellow
it be a cold, wet January day on which Tom go back to school; a day
quite in keep with this severe phase of his destiny. if he have not
carry in his pocket a parcel of sugar-candy and a small dutch doll
for little Laura, there would have be no ray of expected pleasure to
enliven the general gloom. but he like to think how Laura would put
out her lip and her tiny hand for the bit of sugarcandy; and to
give the great keenness to these pleasure of imagination, he take
out the parcel, make a small hole in the paper, and bite off a crystal
or two, which have so solace an effect under the confine prospect
and damp odor of the gig-umbrella, that he repeat the process more
than once on his way.
"well, Tulliver, webe glad to see you again," say Mr. Stelling,
heartily. "take off your wrapping and come into the study till
dinner. youwill find a bright fire there, and a new companion."
Tom feel in an uncomfortable flutter as he take off his woollen
comforter and other wrapping. he have see Philip Wakem at St. Ogg's,
but have always turn his eye away from he as quickly as possible.
he would have dislike have a deformed boy for his companion, even
if Philip have not be the son of a bad man. and Tom do not see how a
bad man's son could be very good. his own father be a good man, and
he would readily have fight any one who say the contrary. he be in
a state of mingled embarrassment and defiance as he follow Mr.
Stelling to the study.
"here be a new companion for you to shake hand with, Tulliver," say
that gentleman on enter the study,--"master Philip Wakem. I shall
leave you to make acquaintance by yourself. you already know
something of each other, I imagine; for you be neighbor at home."
Tom look confused and awkward, while Philip rise and glance at he
timidly. Tom do not like to go up and put out his hand, and he be
not prepared to say, "how do you do?" on so short a notice.
Mr. Stelling wisely turn away, and close the door behind he; boy'
shyness only wear off in the absence of their elder.
Philip be at once too proud and too timid to walk toward Tom. he
think, or rather feel, that Tom have an aversion to look at he;
every one, almost, dislike look at he; and his deformity be more
conspicuous when he walk. so they remain without shake hand or
even speak, while Tom go to the fire and warm himself, every
now and then cast furtive glance at Philip, who seem to be
draw absently first one object and then another on a piece of paper
he have before he. he have seat himself again, and as he draw, be
think what he could say to Tom, and try to overcome his own
repugnance to make the first advance.
Tom begin to look oftener and long at Philip's face, for he could
see it without notice the hump, and it be really not a disagreeable
face,--very old-look, Tom think. he wonder how much old
Philip be than himself. an anatomist--even a mere physiognomist--
would have see that the deformity of Philip's spine be not a
congenital hump, but the result of an accident in infancy; but you
do not expect from Tom any acquaintance with such distinction;
to he, Philip be simply a humpback. he have a vague notion
that the deformity of Wakem's son have some relation to the lawyer's
rascality, of which he have so often hear his father talk with hot
emphasis; and he feel, too, a half-admit fear of he as probably
a spiteful fellow, who, not be able to fight you, have cunning
way of do you a mischief by the sly. there be a humpbacked
tailor in the neighborhood of Mr. Jacobs's academy, who be consider
a very unamiable character, and be much hoot after by public-spirit
boy solely on the ground of his unsatisfactory moral quality; so
that Tom be not without a basis of fact to go upon. still, no face
could be more unlike that ugly tailor's than this melancholy boy's
face,--the brown hair round it wave and curl at the end like a
girl's; Tom think that truly pitiable. this Wakem be a pale,
puny fellow, and it be quite clear he would not be able to play at
anything worth speak of; but he handle his pencil in an enviable
manner, and be apparently make one thing after another without
any trouble. what be he draw? Tom be quite warm now, and want
something new to be go forward. it be certainly more agreeable
to have an ill-natured humpback as a companion than to stand look
out of the study window at the rain, and kick his foot against
the washboard in solitude; something would happen every day,--
"a quarrel or something"; and Tom think he should rather like to
show Philip that he have well not try his spiteful trick on _he_.
he suddenly walk across the hearth and look over Philip's paper.
"why, thatbe a donkey with pannier, and a spaniel, and partridge in
the corn!" he exclaim, his tongue be completely loose by
surprise and admiration. "oh my button! I wish I could draw like
that. Ibe to learn draw this half; I wonder if I shall learn to
make dog and donkey!"
"oh, you can do they without learn," say Philip; "I never learn
drawing."
"never learn?" say Tom, in amazement. "why, when I make dog and
horse, and those thing, the head and the leg willnot come right;
though I can see how they ought to be very well. I can make house,
and all sort of chimneys,--chimneys go all down the wall,--and
window in the roof, and all that. but I dare say I could do dog and
horse if I be to try more," he add, reflect that Philip might
falsely suppose that he be go to "knock under," if he be too
frank about the imperfection of his accomplishment.
"oh, yes," say Philip, "itbe very easy. youhave only to look well at
thing, and draw they over and over again. what you do wrong once, you
can alter the next time."
"but havenot you be teach _any_the?" say Tom, begin to have
a puzzled suspicion that Philip's crooked back might be the source of
remarkable faculty. "I think youwould be to school a long while."
"yes," say Philip, smile; "Ihave be teach Latin and greek and
mathematic, and writing and such thing."
"oh, but I say, you donot like Latin, though, do you?" say Tom,
lower his voice confidentially.
"pretty well; I donot care much about it," say Philip.
"ah, but perhaps you havenot get into the _Propria quae maribus_," say
Tom, nod his head sideways, as much as to say, "that be the test;
it be easy talk till you come to _that_."
Philip feel some bitter complacency in the promising stupidity of this
well-make, active-looking boy; but make polite by his own extreme
sensitiveness, as well as by his desire to conciliate, he check his
inclination to laugh, and say quietly,--
"Ihave do with the grammar; I donot learn that any more."
"then you willnot have the same lesson as I shall?" say Tom, with a
sense of disappointment.
"no; but I dare say I can help you. I shall be very glad to help you
if I can."
Tom do not say "thank you," for he be quite absorb in the thought
that Wakem's son do not seem so spiteful a fellow as might have be
expect.
"I say," he say presently, "do you love your father?"
"yes," say Philip, color deeply; "donot you love your?"
"oh yes--I only want to know," say Tom, rather ashamed of himself,
now he see Philip color and look uncomfortable. he find much
difficulty in adjust his attitude of mind toward the son of Lawyer
Wakem, and it have occur to he that if Philip dislike his father,
that fact might go some way toward clear up his perplexity.
"shall you learn draw now?" he say, by way of change the
subject.
"no," say Philip. "my father wish I to give all my time to other
thing now."
"what! latin and Euclid, and those thing?" say Tom.
"yes," say Philip, who have leave off use his pencil, and be rest
his head on one hand, while Tom be learn forward on both elbow,
and look with increase admiration at the dog and the donkey.
"and you donot mind that?" say Tom, with strong curiosity.
"no; I like to know what everybody else know. I can study what I like
by-and-by."
"I cannot think why anybody should learn Latin," say Tom. "itbe no
good."
"itbe part of the education of a gentleman," say Philip. "all
gentleman learn the same thing."
"what! do you think Sir John Crake, the master of the harrier, know
Latin?" say Tom, who have often think he should like to resemble Sir
John Crake.
"he learn it when he be a boy, of course," say Philip. "but I dare
say hebe forget it."
"oh, well, I can do that, then," say Tom, not with any epigrammatic
intention, but with serious satisfaction at the idea that, as far as
Latin be concern, there be no hindrance to his resembling Sir John
Crake. "only yoube oblige to remember it while yoube at school,
else youhave get to learn ever so many line of 'Speaker.' Mr.
Stelling's very particular--do you know? hewill have you up ten time
if you say 'nam' for 'jam,'--he willnot let you go a letter wrong, _i_
can tell you."
"oh, I donot mind," say Philip, unable to choke a laugh; "I can
remember thing easily. and there be some lesson Ibe very fond of.
Ibe very fond of greek history, and everything about the Greeks. I
should like to have be a Greek and fight the Persians, and then
have come home and have write tragedy, or else have be listen
to by everybody for my wisdom, like Socrates, and have die a grand
death." (Philip, you perceive, be not without a wish to impress the
well-make barbarian with a sense of his mental superiority.)
"why, be the Greeks great fighter?" say Tom, who see a vista in
this direction. "be there anything like David and Goliath and Samson
in the greek history? those be the only bit I like in the history of
the Jews."
"oh, there be very fine story of that sort about the greeks,--about
the hero of early time who kill the wild beast, as Samson do.
and in the Odyssey--thatbe a beautiful poem--therebe a more wonderful
giant than Goliath,--Polypheme, who have only one eye in the middle of
his forehead; and Ulysses, a little fellow, but very wise and cunning,
get a red-hot pine-tree and stick it into this one eye, and make he
roar like a thousand bull."
"oh, what fun!" say Tom, jump away from the table, and stamp
first with one leg and then the other. "I say, can you tell I all
about those story? because I sha'n't learn Greek, you know. shall
I?" he add, pause in his stamp with a sudden alarm, lest the
contrary might be possible. "do every gentleman learn Greek? will
Mr. Stelling make I begin with it, do you think?"
"no, I should think not, very likely not," say Philip. "but you may
read those story without know Greek. Ihave get they in English."
"oh, but I donot like read; Iwould soon have you tell they I. but
only the fight one, you know. my sister Maggie be always want
to tell I story, but theybe stupid thing. girl' story always
be. can you tell a good many fighting story?"
"oh yes," say Philip; "lot of they, besides the greek story. I can
tell you about Richard Coeur-de-Lion and Saladin, and about William
Wallace and Robert Bruce and James Douglas,--I know no end."
"yoube old than I be, benot you?" say Tom.
"why, how old be _you?_ Ibe fifteen."
"Ibe only go in fourteen," say Tom. "but I thrash all the
fellow at jacob'--thatbe where I be before I come here. and I beat
them all at bandy and climbing. and I wish Mr. Stelling would let we go
fishing. _I_ could show you how to fish. you _could_ fish, couldnot
you? itbe only stand, and sit still, you know."
Tom, in his turn, wish to make the balance dip in his favor. this
hunchback must not suppose that his acquaintance with fighting story
put he on a par with an actual fighting hero, like Tom Tulliver.
Philip wince under this allusion to his unfitness for active sport,
and he answer almost peevishly,--
"I cannot bear fish. I think people look like fool sit watch
a line hour after hour, or else throw and throwing, and catch
nothing."
"ah, but you wouldnot say they look like fool when they land a
big pike, I can tell you," say Tom, who have never catch anything
that be "big" in his life, but whose imagination be on the stretch
with indignant zeal for the honor of sport. Wakem's son, it be plain,
have his disagreeable point, and must be keep in due check. happily
for the harmony of this first interview, they be now call to
dinner, and Philip be not allow to develop far his unsound
view on the subject of fishing. but Tom say to himself, that be
just what he should have expect from a hunchback.
Chapter IV
"the Young Idea"
the alteration of feeling in that first dialogue between Tom and
Philip continue to make their intercourse even after many week of
schoolboy intimacy. Tom never quite lose the feeling that Philip,
be the son of a "rascal," be his natural enemy; never thoroughly
overcome his repulsion to Philip's deformity. he be a boy who adhere
tenaciously to impression once receive; as with all mind in which
mere perception predominate over thought and emotion, the external
remain to he rigidly what it be in the first instance. but then it
be impossible not to like Philip's company when he be in a good

regard as a kind of puzzle that could only be find out by a lucky
chance; and he could tell such wonderful fighting story about Hal of
the Wynd, for example, and other hero who be especial favorite
with Tom, because they lay about they with heavy stroke. he have
small opinion of Saladin, whose cimeter could cut a cushion in two in
an instant; who want to cut cushion? that be a stupid story, and
he donot care to hear it again. but when Robert Bruce, on the black
pony, rise in his stirrup, and lift his good battle-axe, crack
at once the helmet and the skull of the too hasty knight at

have have a cocoanut at hand, he would have crack it at once with the
poker. Philip in his happy mood indulge Tom to the top of his

the artillery of epithet and simile at his command. but he be not
always in a good humor or happy mood. the slight spurt of peevish

northern spring. poor Tom's blunder patronage when they be out of

quite savagely; and his eye, usually sad and quiet, would flash with
anything but playful lightning. no wonder Tom retain his suspicion
of the humpback.
but Philip's self-teach skill in drawing be another link between
they; for Tom find, to his disgust, that his new drawing-master give

ruin, all with a general softness of black-lead surface, indicate
that nature, if anything, be rather satiny; and as Tom's feeling for
the picturesque in landscape be at present quite latent, it be not
surprising that Mr. Goodrich's production seem to he an
uninteresting form of art. Mr. Tulliver, have a vague intention that
Tom should be put to some business which include the drawing out of
plan and map, have complain to Mr. Riley, when he see he at
Mudport, that Tom seem to be learn nothing of that sort;
whereupon that oblige adviser have suggest that Tom should have
drawing-lesson. Mr. Tulliver must not mind pay extra for drawing;
let Tom be make a good draughtsman, and he would be able to turn his
pencil to any purpose. so it be order that Tom should have
drawing-lesson; and whom should Mr. Stelling have select as a
master if not Mr. Goodrich, who be consider quite at the head of
his profession within a circuit of twelve mile round King's Lorton?
by which mean Tom learn to make an extremely fine point to his
pencil, and to represent landscape with a "broad generality," which,
doubtless from a narrow tendency in his mind to detail, he think
extremely dull.
all this, you remember, happen in those dark age when there be no
school of design; before schoolmaster be invariably man of
scrupulous integrity, and before the clergy be all man of enlarged
mind and varied culture. in those less favored day, it be no fable
that there be other clergyman besides Mr. Stelling who have narrow
intellect and large want, and whose income, by a logical confusion
to which Fortune, be a female as well as blindfold, be peculiarly
liable, be proportion not to their want but to their intellect,
with which income have clearly no inherent relation. the problem these
gentleman have to solve be to readjust the proportion between their
want and their income; and since want be not easily starve to
death, the simple method appear to be to raise their income. there
be but one way of do this; any of those low calling in which man
be oblige to do good work at a low price be forbid to
clergyman; be it their fault if their only resource be to turn out
very poor work at a high price? besides, how should Mr. Stelling be
expect to know that education be a delicate and difficult business,
any more than an animal endow with a power of bore a hole through
a rock should be expect to have wide view of excavation? Mr.
Stelling's faculty have be early train to bore in a straight
line, and he have no faculty to spare. but among Tom's contemporary,
whose father cast their son on clerical instruction to find they
ignorant after many day, there be many far less lucky than Tom
Tulliver. education be almost entirely a matter of luck--usually of
ill-luck--in those distant day. the state of mind in which you take a
billiard-cue or a dice-box in your hand be one of sober certainty
compare with that of old-fashioned father, like Mr. Tulliver, when
they select a school or a tutor for their son. excellent man, who
have be force all their life to spell on an impromptu-phonetic
system, and have carry on a successful business in spite of this
disadvantage, have acquire money enough to give their son a well
start in life than they have have themselves, must necessarily take
their chance as to the conscience and the competence of the
schoolmaster whose circular fall in their way, and appear to promise
so much more than they would ever have think of ask for,
include the return of linen, fork, and spoon. it be happy for they
if some ambitious draper of their acquaintance have not bring up his
son to the Church, and if that young gentleman, at the age of
four-and-twenty, have not close his college dissipation by an
imprudent marriage; otherwise, these innocent father, desirous of
do the good for their offspring, could only escape the draper's son
by happen to be on the foundation of a grammar-school as yet
unvisite by commissioner, where two or three boy could have, all to
themselves, the advantage of a large and lofty building, together
with a head-master, toothless, dim-eyed and deaf, whose erudite
indistinctness and inattention be engross by they at the rate of
three hundred pound a-head,--a ripe scholar, doubtless, when first
appoint; but all ripeness beneath the sun have a further stage less
esteem in the market.
Tom Tulliver, then, compare with many other british youth of his
time who have since have to scramble through life with some fragment
of more or less relevant knowledge, and a great deal of strictly
relevant ignorance, be not so very unlucky. Mr. Stelling be a
broad-cheste, healthy man, with the bearing of a gentleman, a
conviction that a grow boy require a sufficiency of beef, and a

well and enjoy his dinner; not a man of refined conscience, or with

shorter than the other. a method of education sanction by the long
practice of our venerable ancestor be not to give way before the
exceptional dulness of a boy who be merely live at the time then
present. and Mr. Stelling be convince that a boy so stupid at sign
and abstraction must be stupid at everything else, even if that
reverend gentleman could have teach he everything else. it be the
practice of our venerable ancestor to apply that ingenious instrument
the thumb-screw, and to tighten and tighten it in order to elicit
non-existent fact; they have a fix opinion to begin with, that the
fact be existent, and what have they to do but to tighten the
thumb-screw? in like manner, Mr. Stelling have a fix opinion that all
boy with any capacity could learn what it be the only regular thing
to teach; if they be slow, the thumb-screw must be tightened,--the
exercise must be insist on with increase severity, and a page of

languid inclination to latin verse.
the thumb-screw be a little relaxed, however, during this second
half-year. Philip be so advanced in his study, and so apt, that Mr.
Stelling could obtain credit by his facility, which require little

nevertheless, there be a visible improvement in Tom under this

Mr. Poulter, it appear, have be a conspicuous figure at Talavera,

"and General Wolfe, Mr. Poulter,--wasn't he a wonderful fighter?" say

"not at all!" say Mr. Poulter, contemptuously. "nothing o' the sort!
head up!" he add, in a tone of stern command, which delight Tom,

"no, no!" Mr. Poulter would continue, on come to a pause in his

nothing but die of his wound; thatbe a poor haction, I consider. any
other man 'ud have die o' the wound Ihave have. one of my sword-cut
'ud ha' kill a fellow like General Wolfe."
"Mr. Poulter," Tom would say, at any allusion to the sword, "I wish

for a long while Mr. Poulter only shake his head in a significant

Tom run in to Philip, who be enjoy his afternoon's holiday at the

Philip be not her favorite of the two pupil; he be less obliging

he say, in answer to her question, "my toothache come on, and make I

this have be the fact once, and Philip be glad of the recollection;

to accept eau-de-cologne and to refuse creosote in consequence; but

meanwhile Tom, who have for the first time send a poison arrow into

"Mr. Poulter," say Tom, when the sword be be finally sheathe, "I

"no no, young gentleman," say Mr. Poulter, shake his head

"no, Ibe sure I wouldnot; Ibe sure Iwould take care and not hurt myself.
I shouldnot take it out of the sheath much, but I could ground arm
with it, and all that."
"no, no, it willnot do, I tell you; it willnot do," say Mr. Poulter,

"oh, I say, do, Mr. Poulter! Iwould give you my five-shilling piece if

the attractively large round of silver. the young dog calculate the
effect as well as if he have be a philosopher.
"well," say Mr. Poulter, with still deep gravity, "you must keep it

"oh yes, Iwill keep it under the bed," say Tom, eagerly, "or else at

"and let I see, now, whether you can draw it out of the sheath

"oh no, indeed, Mr. Poulter," say Tom, delightedly hand he the

"but if Mr. Stelling catch you carry it in?" say Mr. Poulter,

soldier. there be nobody but Maggie who would be silly enough to
believe he, or whom he dare allow to know he have a sword; and Maggie
be really come next week to see Tom, before she go to a
boarding-school with Lucy.
if you think a lad of thirteen would have be so childish, you must
be an exceptionally wise man, who, although you be devoted to a civil
calling, require you to look bland rather than formidable, yet
never, since you have a beard, throw yourself into a martial attitude,
and frown before the look-glass. it be doubtful whether our
soldier would be maintain if there be not pacific people at home
who like to fancy themselves soldier. War, like other dramatic
spectacle, might possibly cease for want of a "public."
Chapter V
Maggie's Second Visit
this last breach between the two lad be not readily mend, and for
some time they speak to each other no more than be necessary. their
natural antipathy of temperament make resentment an easy passage to
hatred, and in Philip the transition seem to have begin; there be
no malignity in his disposition, but there be a susceptibility that
make he peculiarly liable to a strong sense of repulsion. the ox--we
may venture to assert it on the authority of a great classic--be not
give to use his tooth as an instrument of attack, and Tom be an
excellent bovine lad, who run at questionable object in a truly
ingenious bovine manner; but he have blunder on Philip's tender
point, and have cause he as much acute pain as if he have study the
mean with the nice precision and the most envenomed spite. Tom see
no reason why they should not make up this quarrel as they have do
many other, by behave as if nothing have happen; for though he have

so habitually make part of his feeling as to the relation between
himself and his dubious schoolfellow, who he could neither like nor
dislike, that the mere utterance do not make such an epoch to he as
it do to Philip. and he have a right to say so when Philip hector
over _him_, and call he name. but perceive that his first
advance toward amity be not meet, he relapse into his least
favorable disposition toward Philip, and resolve never to appeal to
he either about draw or exercise again. they be only so far
civil to each other as be necessary to prevent their state of feud
from be observe by Mr. Stelling, who would have "put down" such
nonsense with great vigor.
when Maggie come, however, she could not help look with grow

A Love-Scene

the Golden Gates be pass

on she like a startling sound.

be at the turnpike at ten to meet the coach." she say this with hasty
decision, rub her eye, and rise to seize her bonnet.
Tom at once feel the same impulse, and rise too. "wait a minute,
Maggie," he say. "I must speak to Mr. Stelling, and then wewill go."
he think he must go to the study where the pupil be; but on his
way he meet Mr. Stelling, who have hear from his wife that Maggie
appear to be in trouble when she ask for her brother, and now that
he think the brother and sister have be alone long enough, be
come to inquire and offer his sympathy.
"please, sir, I must go home," Tom say abruptly, as he meet Mr.
Stelling in the passage. "I must go back with my sister directly. my
father's lose his lawsuit--hebe lose all his property--and hebe very
ill."
Mr. Stelling feel like a kind-hearted man; he foresee a probable money
loss for himself, but this have no appreciable share in his feeling,
while he look with grave pity at the brother and sister for whom
youth and sorrow have begin together. when he know how Maggie have come,
and how eager she be to get home again, he hurry their departure,
only whisper something to Mrs. Stelling, who have follow he, and
who immediately leave the room.
Tom and Maggie be stand on the door-step, ready to set out, when
Mrs. Stelling come with a little basket, which she hang on Maggie's
arm, say: "do remember to eat something on the way, dear." Maggie's
heart go out toward this woman whom she have never like, and she
kiss she silently. it be the first sign within the poor child of
that new sense which be the gift of sorrow,--that susceptibility to
the bare office of humanity which raise they into a bond of love
fellowship, as to haggard man among the ice-berg the mere presence of
an ordinary comrade stir the deep fountain of affection.
Mr. Stelling put his hand on Tom's shoulder and say: "God bless you,
my boy; let I know how you get on." then he press Maggie's hand;
but there be no audible good-bye. Tom have so often think how
joyful he should be the day he leave school "for good"! and now his
school year seem like a holiday that have come to an end.
the two slight youthful figure soon grow indistinct on the distant
road,--were soon lose behind the project hedgerow.
they have go forth together into their life of sorrow, and they would
never more see the sunshine undimmed by remember care. they have
enter the thorny wilderness, and the golden gate of their childhood
have forever close behind they.

when Mr. Tulliver first know the fact that the law-suit be decide

hot-temper a man, he bear the blow remarkably well. he think so

consider he crush, they would find themselves mistaken. he could
not refuse to see that the cost of this protract suit would take
more than he possess to pay they; but he appear to himself to be
full of expedient by which he could ward off any result but such as
be tolerable, and could avoid the appearance of break down in the
world. all the obstinacy and defiance of his nature, drive out of
their old channel, find a vent for themselves in the immediate
formation of plan by which he would meet his difficulty, and remain

of project in his brain, that it be no wonder his face be flush
when he come away from his talk with his attorney, Mr. Gore, and
