the mortgage on the land,--a reasonable fellow, who would see his own
interest, Mr. Tulliver be convince, and who would be glad not only
to purchase the whole estate, include the mill and homestead, but
would accept Mr. Tulliver as tenant, and be willing to advance money
to be repay with high interest out of the profit of the business,
which would be make over to he, Mr. Tulliver only take enough
barely to maintain himself and his family. who would neglect such a
profitable investment? certainly not Furley, for Mr. Tulliver have
determine that Furley should meet his plan with the utmost alacrity;
and there be man whose brain have not yet be dangerously heat
by the loss of a lawsuit, who be apt to see in their own interest or
desire a motive for other man's action. there be no doubt (in the
miller's mind) that Furley would do just what be desirable; and if he
do--why, thing would not be so very much bad. Mr. Tulliver and his
family must live more meagrely and humbly, but it would only be till
the profit of the business have pay off Furley's advance, and that

pleasant reading than a man might desire toward Christmas. well! he
have never be one of those poor-spirited sneak who would refuse to

have become uneasy about his money (set on by Wakem, of course), and

find it eminently inconvenient to raise the say sum until that
desirable issue have take place, have rashly accede to the demand that

other effect, as security in lieu of the bond. it be all one, he have
say to himself; he should soon pay off the money, and there be no
harm in give that security any more than another. but now the

it be precisely the proud and most obstinate man who be the most

fact that they have be thoroughly defeat, and must begin life

be a very lofty personage, in whom such disposition might be a

stage in regal robe, and make the dull chronicl sublime. the
pride and obstinacy of miller and other insignificant people, whom
you pass unnoticingly on the road every day, have their tragedy too;
but it be of that unwept, hidden sort that go on from generation to
generation, and leave no record,--such tragedy, perhaps, as lie in
the conflict of young soul, hungry for joy, under a lot make
suddenly hard to they, under the dreariness of a home where the
morning bring no promise with it, and where the unexpectant

follow on a bruise passion, though it may be a death that find only
a parish funeral. there be certain animal to which tenacity of
position be a law of life,--they can never flourish again, after a
single wrench: and there be certain human being to whom predominance
be a law of life,--they can only sustain humiliation so long as they
can refuse to believe in it, and, in their own conception, predominate
still.
Mr. Tulliver be still predominate, in his own imagination, as he

Chapter III
the Family Council
it be at eleven o'clock the next morning that the aunt and uncle
come to hold their consultation. the fire be light in the large
parlor, and poor Mrs. Tulliver, with a confused impression that it be
a great occasion, like a funeral, unbagge the bell-rope tassel, and
unpin the curtain, adjust they in proper fold, look round
and shake her head sadly at the polished top and leg of the
table, which sister Pullet herself could not accuse of insufficient
brightness.
Mr. Deane be not come, he be away on business; but Mrs. Deane
appear punctually in that handsome new gig with the head to it, and
the livery-servant drive it, which have throw so clear a light on
several trait in her character to some of her female friend in St.
Ogg's. Mr. Deane have be advance in the world as rapidly as Mr.
Tulliver have be go down in it; and in Mrs. Deane's house the
Dodson linen and plate be begin to hold quite a subordinate
position, as a mere supplement to the handsomer article of the same
kind, purchase in recent years,--a change which have cause an
occasional coolness in the sisterly intercourse between her and Mrs.
Glegg, who feel that Susan be get "like the rest," and there
would soon be little of the true Dodson spirit survive except in
herself, and, it might be hop, in those nephew who support the
Dodson name on the family land, far away in the Wolds.
People who live at a distance be naturally less faulty than those
immediately under our own eye; and it seem superfluous, when we
consider the remote geographical position of the Ethiopians, and how
very little the Greeks have to do with they, to inquire far why
Homer call they "blameless."
Mrs. Deane be the first to arrive; and when she have take her seat in
the large parlor, Mrs. Tulliver come down to she with she comely face
a little distorted, nearly as it would have be if she have be
cry. she be not a woman who could shed abundant tear, except in
moment when the prospect of lose her furniture become unusually
vivid, but she feel how unfit it be to be quite calm under
present circumstance.
"oh, sister, what a world this be!" she exclaim as she enter;
"what trouble, oh dear!"
Mrs. Deane be a thin-lipped woman, who make small well-consider
speech on peculiar occasion, repeat they afterward to her
husband, and ask he if she have not speak very properly.
"yes, sister," she say deliberately, "this be a change world, and
we donot know to-day what may happen tomorrow. but itbe right to be
prepare for all thing, and if trouble's send, to remember as it
benot send without a cause. Ibe very sorry for you as a sister, and if
the doctor order jelly for Mr. Tulliver, I hope youwill let I know.
Iwill send it willingly; for it be but right he should have proper
attendance while hebe ill."
"thank you, Susan," say Mrs. Tulliver, rather faintly, withdraw
her fat hand from her sister's thin one. "but therebe be no talk o'
jelly yet." then after a moment's pause she add, "therebe a dozen o'
cut jelly-glass upstairs--I shall never put jelly into them no more."
her voice be rather agitated as she utter the last word, but the
sound of wheel divert her thought. Mr. and Mrs. Glegg be come,
and be almost immediately follow by Mr. and Mrs. Pullet.
Mrs. Pullet enter crying, as a compendious mode, at all time, of
express what be her view of life in general, and what, in brief,
be the opinion she hold concern the particular case before she.
Mrs. Glegg have on her fuzziest front, and garment which appear to
have have a recent resurrection from rather a creasy form of burial; a
costume select with the high moral purpose of instill perfect
humility into Bessy and her child.
"Mrs. G., willnot you come near the fire?" say her husband, unwilling
to take the more comfortable seat without offer it to she.
"you see Ihave seat myself here, Mr. Glegg," return this superior
woman; "_you_ can roast yourself, if you like."
"well," say Mr. Glegg, seat himself good-humoredly, "and howbe the
poor man upstairs?"
"Dr. Turnbull think he a deal well this morning," say Mrs.
Tulliver; "he take more notice, and speak to I; but hebe never know
Tom yet,--looks at the poor lad as if he be a stranger, though he
say something once about Tom and the pony. the doctor say his
memory's go a long way back, and he donot know Tom because hebe
thinking of he when he be little. eh dear, eh dear!"
"I doubt itbe the water get on his brain," say aunt Pullet, turn
round from adjust her cap in a melancholy way at the pier-glass.
"itbe much if he ever get up again; and if he do, hewill most like
be childish, as Mr. Carr be, poor man! they feed he with a spoon as
if hehave be a babby for three year. hewould quite lose the use of his
limb; but then hewould got a bath chair, and somebody to draw he; and
thatbe what you willnot have, I doubt, Bessy."
"Sister Pullet," say Mrs. Glegg, severely, "if I understand right,
wehave come together this morning to advise and consult about whatbe to
be do in this disgrace as have fall upon the family, and not to
talk o' people as donot belong to we. Mr. Carr be none of our blood,
nor noway connect with we, as Ihave ever hear."
"Sister Glegg," say Mrs. Pullet, in a plead tone, draw on her
glove again, and stroke the finger in an agitated manner, "if
youhave get anything disrespectful to say o' Mr. Carr, I do beg of you
as you willnot say it to I. _I_ know what he be," she add, with a
sigh; "his breath be short to that degree as you could hear he two
room off."
"Sophy!" say Mrs. Glegg, with indignant disgust, "you _do_ talk o'
people's complaint till itbe quite undecent. but I say again, as I
say before, I donot come away from home to talk about acquaintance,
whether theywould short breath or long. if we benot come together for
one to hear what the other 'ull do to save a sister and her child
from the parish, _i_ shall go back. _one_ cannot act without the other,
I suppose; it benot to be expect as _i_ should do everything."
"well, Jane," say Mrs. Pullet, "I donot see as youhave be so very
forrard at do. so far as I know, this be the first time as here
youhave be, since itbe be know as the bailiff's in the house; and
I be here yesterday, and look at all Bessy's linen and thing, and
I tell she Iwould buy in the spotted tablecloth. I couldnot speak
fairer; for as for the teapot as she donot want to go out o' the
family, it stand to sense I cannot do with two silver teapot, not if
it _havenot_ a straight spout, but the spot damask I be allay fond
on."
"I wish it could be manage so as my teapot and chany and the good
castor neednot be put up for sale," say poor Mrs. Tulliver,
beseechingly, "and the sugar-tong the first thing ever I buy."
"but that cannot be help, you know," say Mr. Glegg. "if one o' the
family choose to buy them in, they can, but one thing must be bid for
as well as another."
"and it benot to be look for," say uncle Pullet, with unwonted
independence of idea, "as your own family should pay more for thing
nor theywill fetch. they may go for an old song by auction."
"oh dear, oh dear," say Mrs. Tulliver, "to think o' my chany be
sell I' that way, and I buy it when I be married, just as you do
your, Jane and Sophy; and I know you donot like mine, because o' the
sprig, but I be fond of it; and therebe never be a bit broke, for
Ihave wash it myself; and therebe the tulip on the cup, and the
rose, as anybody might go and look at them for pleasure. you wouldnot
like _your_ chany to go for an old song and be broke to piece, though
yours have get no color in it, Jane,--it's all white and flute, and
donot cost so much as mine. and therebe the castor, sister Deane, I
cannot think but youwould like to have the castor, for Ihave hear you say
theybe pretty."
"well, Ihave no objection to buy some of the good thing," say Mrs.
Deane, rather loftily; "we can do with extra thing in our house."
"best thing!" exclaim Mrs. Glegg, with severity, which have gather
intensity from her long silence. "it drive I past patience to hear
you all talk o' good thing, and buy in this, that, and the
other, such as silver and chany. you must bring your mind to your
circumstance, Bessy, and not be think o' silver and chany; but
whether you shall get so much as a flock-bed to lie on, and a blanket
to cover you, and a stool to sit on. you must remember, if you get
them, itwill be because your friend have buy them for you, for yoube
dependent upon _they_ for everything; for your husband lie there
helpless, and havenot get a penny I' the world to call his own. and
itbe for your own good I say this, for itbe right you should feel what
your state be, and what disgrace your husband's bring on your own
family, as youhave get to look to for everything, and be humble in your
mind."
Mrs. Glegg pause, for speak with much energy for the good of
other be naturally exhausting.
Mrs. Tulliver, always bear down by the family predominance of sister
Jane, who have make she wear the yoke of a young sister in very
tender year, say pleadingly:
"Ibe sure, sister, Ihave never ask anybody to do anything, only buy
thing as it 'ud be a pleasure to them to have, so as they mightnot go
and be spoil I' strange house. I never ask anybody to buy the
thing in for I and my child; though therebe the linen I spin, and
I think when Tom be born,--I think one o' the first thing when
he be lie I' the cradle, as all the thing Iwould buy wi' my own
money, and be so careful of, 'ud go to he. but Ihave say nothing as
I want my sister to pay their money for I. what my husband have
do for _his_ sister's unknown, and we should ha' be well off
this day if it havenot be as hebe lend money and never ask for it
again."
"come, come," say Mr. Glegg, kindly, "donot let we make thing too
dark. whatbe do cannot be undo. we shall make a shift among we to
buy whatbe sufficient for you; though, as Mrs. G. say, they must be
useful, plain thing. we mustnot be think o' whatbe unnecessary. a
table, and a chair or two, and kitchen thing, and a good bed, and
such-like. why, Ihave see the day when I shouldnot ha' know myself if
Iwould lain on sack i'stead o' the floor. we get a deal o' useless
thing about we, only because wehave get the money to spend."
"Mr. Glegg," say Mrs. G., "if youwill be kind enough to let I speak,
i'stead o' take the word out o' my mouth,--I be go to say,
Bessy, as itbe fine talk for you to say as youhave never ask we to
buy anything for you; let I tell you, you _ought_ to have ask we.
pray, how be you to be purvide for, if your own family donot help
you? you must go to the parish, if they donot. and you ought to know
that, and keep it in mind, and ask we humble to do what we can for
you, i'stead o' say, and make a boast, as youhave never ask we
for anything."
"you talk o' the Mosses, and what Mr. Tulliverbe do for them," say
uncle Pullet, who become unusually suggestive where advance of money
be concern. "havenot _they_ be anear you? they ought to do
something as well as other folk; and if hebe lend them money, they
ought to be make to pay it back."
"yes, to be sure," say Mrs. Deane; "Ihave be think so. how be it
Mr. and Mrs. Moss benot here to meet we? it be but right they should
do their share."
"oh, dear!" say Mrs. Tulliver, "I never send them word about Mr.
Tulliver, and they live so back'ard among the lane at Basset, they
niver hear anything only when Mr. Moss come to market. but I niver
give them a thought. I wonder Maggie donot, though, for she be allay
so fond of her aunt Moss."
"why donot your child come in, Bessy?" say Mrs. Pullet, at the
mention of Maggie. "they should hear what their aunt and uncle have
get to say; and maggie,--when itbe I as have pay for half her
schooling, she ought to think more of her aunt Pullet than of aunt
Moss. I may go off sudden when I get home to-day; therebe no telling."
"if Ihave have _my_ way," say Mrs. Glegg, "the child 'ud ha' be in
the room from the first. itbe time they know who theyhave to look to,
and itbe right as _somebody_ should talk to them, and let them know
their condition I' life, and what theybe come down to, and make them
feel as theyhave get to suffer for their father's fault."
"well, Iwill go and fetch them, sister," say Mrs. Tulliver, resignedly.
she be quite crush now, and thought of the treasure in the
storeroom with no other feeling than blank despair.
she go upstairs to fetch Tom and Maggie, who be both in their
father's room, and be on her way down again, when the sight of the
storeroom door suggest a new thought to she. she go toward it, and
leave the child to go down by themselves.
the aunt and uncle appear to have be in warm discussion when the
brother and sister entered,--both with shrink reluctance; for
though Tom, with a practical sagacity which have be rouse into
activity by the strong stimulus of the new emotion he have undergo
since yesterday, have be turn over in his mind a plan which he
mean to propose to one of his aunt or uncle, he feel by no mean
amicably toward they, and dread meet they all at once as he would
have dread a large dose of concentrated physic, which be but just
endurable in small draught. as for Maggie, she be peculiarly
depressed this morning; she have be call up, after brief rest, at
three o'clock, and have that strange dreamy weariness which come from
watch in a sick-room through the chill hour of early twilight and
breaking day,--in which the outside day-light life seem to have no
importance, and to be a mere margin to the hour in the darken
chamber. their entrance interrupt the conversation. the shaking of
hand be a melancholy and silent ceremony, till uncle Pullet
observe, as Tom approach he:
"well, young sir, wehave be talk as we should want your pen and
ink; you can write rarely now, after all your schooling, I should
think."
"ay, ay," say uncle Glegg, with admonition which he mean to be kind,
"we must look to see the good of all this schooling, as your father's
sink so much money in, now,--
'when land be go and money's spend,
then learn be most excellent.'
nowbe the time, Tom, to let we see the good o' your learning. let we
see whether you can do well than I can, as have make my fortin
without it. but I begin wi' do with little, you see; I could live
on a basin o' porridge and a crust o' bread-and-cheese. but I doubt
high living and high learning 'ull make it hard for you, young man,
nor it be for I."
"but he must do it," interpose aunt Glegg, energetically, "whether
itbe hard or no. he havenot get to consider whatbe hard; he must
consider as he benot to trusten to his friend to keep he in idleness
and luxury; hebe get to bear the fruit of his father's misconduct,
and bring his mind to fare hard and to work hard. and he must be
humble and grateful to his aunt and uncle for what theybe do for
his mother and father, as must be turn out into the street and go
to the workhouse if they donot help them. and his sister, too,"
continue Mrs. Glegg, look severely at Maggie, who have sit down on
the sofa by her aunt Deane, draw to she by the sense that she be
Lucy's mother, "she must make up her mind to be humble and work; for
therewill be no servant to wait on she any more,--she must remember
that. she must do the work o' the house, and she must respect and love
her aunt as have do so much for she, and save their money to leave
to their nephey and niece."
Tom be still stand before the table in the centre of the group.
there be a heighten color in his face, and he be very far from
look humble, but he be prepare to say, in a respectful tone,
something he have previously meditate, when the door open and his
mother re-enter.
Poor Mrs. Tulliver have in her hand a small tray, on which she have
place her silver teapot, a speciman teacup and saucer, the castor,
and sugar-tong.
"see here, sister," she say, look at Mrs. Deane, as she set the
tray on the table, "I think, perhaps, if you look at the teapot
again,--it's a good while since you see it,--you might like the
pattern well; it make beautiful tea, and therebe a stand and
everything; you might use it for every day, or else lie it by for Lucy
when she go to housekeeping. I should be so loath for them to buy it
at the Golden Lion," say the poor woman, her heart swell, and the
tear coming,--"my teapot as I buy when I be marry, and to think
of its be scratch, and set before the traveller and folk, and
my letter on it,--see here, E. D.,--and everybody to see them."
"ah, dear, dear!" say aunt Pullet, shake her head with deep
sadness, "itbe very bad,--to think o' the family initial go about
everywhere--it niver be so before; yoube a very unlucky sister,
Bessy. but whatbe the use o' buy the teapot, when therebe the linen
and spoon and everything to go, and some of them with your full
name,--and when itbe get that straight spout, too."
"as to disgrace o' the family," say Mrs. Glegg, "that cannot be help
wi' buying teapot. the disgrace be, for one o' the family to ha'
marry a man as have bring she to beggary. the disgrace be, as
theybe to be sell up. we cannot hinder the country from know that."
Maggie have start up from the sofa at the allusion to her father, but
Tom see her action and flushed face in time to prevent she from
speak. "be quiet, Maggie," he say authoritatively, push she
aside. it be a remarkable manifestation of self-command and practical
judgment in a lad of fifteen, that when his aunt Glegg cease, he
begin to speak in a quiet and respectful manner, though with a good
deal of tremble in his voice; for his mother's word have cut he to
the quick.
"then, aunt," he say, look straight at Mrs. Glegg, "if you think
itbe a disgrace to the family that we should be sell up, wouldnot it
be well to prevent it altogether? and if you and aunt Pullet," he
continue, look at the latter, "think of leave any money to I
and Maggie, wouldnot it be well to give it now, and pay the debt
webe go to be sell up for, and save my mother from part with
her furniture?"
there be silence for a few moment, for every one, include Maggie,
be astonish at Tom's sudden manliness of tone. Uncle Glegg be the
first to speak.
"ay, ay, young man, come now! you show some notion o' thing. but
therebe the interest, you must remember; your aunt get five per cent
on their money, and theywould lose that if they advance it; you havenot
think o' that."
"I could work and pay that every year," say Tom, promptly. "Iwould do
anything to save my mother from part with her thing."
"well do!" say uncle Glegg, admiringly. he have be draw Tom
out, rather than reflect on the practicability of his proposal. but
he have produce the unfortunate result of irritate his wife."
"yes, Mr. Glegg!" say that lady, with angry sarcasm. "itbe pleasant
work for you to be give my money away, as youhave pretend to leave
at my own disposal. and my money, as be my own father's gift, and not
your, Mr. Glegg; and Ihave save it, and add to it myself, and have
more to put out almost every year, and itbe to go and be sink in other
folk' furniture, and encourage them in luxury and extravagance as
theyhave no mean of support; and Ibe to alter my will, or have a
codicil make, and leave two or three hundred less behind I when I
die,--me as have allay do right and be careful, and the eld o'
the family; and my moneybe to go and be squander on they as have have
the same chance as I, only theyhave be wicked and wasteful. Sister
Pullet, _you_ may do as you like, and you may let your husband rob you
back again o' the money hebe give you, but that benot _my_ sperrit."
"La, Jane, how fiery you be!" say Mrs. Pullet. "Ibe sure youwill have
the blood in your head, and have to be cup. Ibe sorry for Bessy and
her children,--i'm sure I think of them o' night dreadful, for I sleep
very bad wi' this new medicine,--but itbe no use for I to think o'
do anything, if you willnot meet I half-way."
"why, therebe this to be consider," say Mr. Glegg. "itbe no use to
pay off this debt and save the furniture, when therebe all the law
debt behind, as 'ud take every shilling, and more than could be make
out o' land and stock, for Ihave make that out from Lawyer Gore. wewould
need save our money to keep the poor man with, instead o' spend it
on furniture as he can neither eat nor drink. you _will_ be so hasty,
Jane, as if I donot know what be reasonable."
"then speak accordingly, Mr. Glegg!" say his wife, with slow, loud
emphasis, bend her head toward he significantly.
Tom's countenance have fall during this conversation, and his lip
quiver; but he be determine not to give way. he would behave like
a man. Maggie, on the contrary, after her momentary delight in Tom's
speech, have relapse into her state of tremble indignation. her
mother have be stand close by Tom's side, and have be cling to
his arm ever since he have last speak; Maggie suddenly start up and
stand in front of they, her eye flash like the eye of a young
lioness.
"why do you come, then," she burst out, "talk and interfere with
we and scold we, if you donot mean to do anything to help my poor
mother--your own sister,--if youhave no feeling for she when shebe in
trouble, and willnot part with anything, though you would never miss it,
to save she from pain? keep away from we then, and donot come to find
fault with my father,--he be well than any of you; he be kind,--he
would have help _you_, if you have be in trouble. Tom and I donot
ever want to have any of your money, if you willnot help my mother. wewould
rather not have it! wewill do without you."
Maggie, have hurl her defiance at aunt and uncle in this way,
stand still, with her large dark eye glare at they, as if she be
ready to await all consequence.
Mrs. Tulliver be frightened; there be something portentous in this
mad outbreak; she do not see how life could go on after it. Tom be
vex; it be no _use_ to talk so. the aunt be silent with surprise
for some moment. at length, in a case of aberration such as this,
comment present itself as more expedient than any answer.
"you havenot see the end o' your trouble wi' that child, Bessy," say
Mrs. Pullet; "shebe beyond everything for boldness and unthankfulness.
itbe dreadful. I might ha' let alone pay for her schooling, for
shebe bad nor ever."
"itbe no more than what Ihave allay say," follow Mrs. Glegg. "other
folk may be surprised, but Ibe not. Ihave say over and over
again,--year ago Ihave said,--'Mark my word; that child 'ull come to
no good; there benot a bit of our family in she.' and as for she
have so much schooling, I never think well o' that. Ihave my reason
when I say _I_ wouldnot pay anything toward it."
"come, come," say Mr. Glegg, "letus waste no more time in
talking,--let's go to business. Tom, now, get the pen and ink----"
while Mr. Glegg be speak, a tall dark figure be see hurrying
past the window.
"why, therebe Mrs. Moss," say Mrs. Tulliver. "the bad news must ha'
reach she, then"; and she go out to open the door, Maggie eagerly
follow she.
"thatbe fortunate," say Mrs. Glegg. "she can agree to the list o'
thing to be buy in. itbe but right she should do her share when
itbe her own brother."
Mrs. Moss be in too much agitation to resist Mrs. Tulliver's
movement, as she draw she into the parlor automatically, without
reflect that it be hardly kind to take she among so many person
in the first painful moment of arrival. the tall, worn, dark-haire
woman be a strong contrast to the Dodson sister as she enter in
her shabby dress, with her shawl and bonnet look as if they have
be hastily huddle on, and with that entire absence of
self-consciousness which belong to keenly feel trouble. Maggie be
cling to her arm; and Mrs. Moss seem to notice no one else except
Tom, whom she go straight up to and take by the hand.
"oh, my dear child," she burst out, "youhave no call to think well
o' I; Ibe a poor aunt to you, for Ibe one o' they as take all and
give nothing. howbe my poor brother?"
"Mr. Turnbull think hewill get well," say Maggie. "sit down, aunt
Gritty. donot fret."
"oh, my sweet child, I feel torn I' two," say Mrs. Moss, allow
Maggie to lead she to the sofa, but still not seem to notice the
presence of the rest. "wehave three hundred pound o' my brother's
money, and now he want it, and you all want it, poor things!--and yet
we must be sell up to pay it, and therebe my poor children,--eight of
them, and the little un of all cannot speak plain. and I feel as if I
be a robber. but Ibe sure Iwould no thought as my brother----"
the poor woman be interrupt by a rise sob.
"three hundred pound! oh dear, dear," say Mrs. Tulliver, who, when
she have say that her husband have do "unknown" thing for his
sister, have not have any particular sum in her mind, and feel a wife's
irritation at have be keep in the dark.
"what madness, to be sure!" say Mrs. Glegg. "a man with a family!
hehave no right to lend his money I' that way; and without security,
Iwill be bind, if the truth be know."
Mrs. Glegg's voice have arrest Mrs. Moss's attention, and look up,
she say:
"yes, there _be_ security; my husband give a note for it. webe not
that sort o' people, neither of we, as 'ud rob my brother's child;
and we look to pay back the money, when the time get a bit
well."
"well, but now," say Mr. Glegg, gently, "havenot your husband no way
o' raise this money? because it 'ud be a little fortin, like, for
these folk, if we can do without Tulliver's be make a bankrupt.
your husband's get stock; it be but right he should raise the money,
as it seem to me,--not but what Ibe sorry for you, Mrs. Moss."
"oh, sir, you donot know what bad luck my husband's have with his
stock. the farm's suffering so as never be for want o' stock; and
wehave sell all the wheat, and webe behind with our rent,--not but
what wewould like to do whatbe right, and Iwould sit up and work half the
night, if it 'ud be any good; but therebe they poor children,--four of
them such little uns----"
"donot cry so, aunt; donot fret," whisper Maggie, who have keep hold
of Mrs. Moss's hand.
"do Mr. Tulliver let you have the money all at once?" say Mrs.
Tulliver, still lose in the conception of thing which have be "go
on" without her knowledge.
"no; at twice," say Mrs. Moss, rub her eye and make an effort
to restrain her tear. "the last be after my bad illness four year
ago, as everything go wrong, and there be a new note make then.
what with illness and bad luck, Ihave be nothing but cumber all my
life."
"yes, Mrs. Moss," say Mrs. Glegg, with decision, "yours be a very
unlucky family; the morebe the pity for _my_ sister."
"I set off in the cart as soon as ever I hear o' what have happen,"
say Mrs. Moss, look at Mrs. Tulliver. "I should never ha' stay
away all this while, if youhave think well to let I know. and it
benot as Ibe think all about ourselves, and nothing about my
brother, only the money be so on my mind, I couldnot help speak
about it. and my husband and I desire to do the right thing, sir,"
she add, look at Mr. Glegg, "and wewill make shift and pay the
money, come what will, if thatbe all my brother's get to trust to.
wehave be use to trouble, and donot look for much else. itbe only
the thought o' my poor child pull I I' two."
"why, therebe this to be think on, Mrs. Moss," say Mr. Glegg, "and
itbe right to warn you,--if Tulliver's make a bankrupt, and hebe get a
note-of-hand of your husband's for three hundred pound, youwill be
oblige to pay it; th' assignee 'ull come on you for it."
"oh dear, oh dear!" say Mrs. Tulliver, think of the bankruptcy,
and not of Mrs. Moss's concern in it. poor Mrs. Moss herself listen
in tremble submission, while Maggie look with bewildered distress
at Tom to see if _he_ show any sign of understand this trouble,
and care about poor aunt Moss. Tom be only look thoughtful, with
his eye on the tablecloth.
"and if he benot make bankrupt," continue Mr. Glegg, "as I say
before, three hundred pound 'ud be a little fortin for he, poor man.
we donot know but what he may be partly helpless, if he ever get up
again. Ibe very sorry if it go hard with you, Mrs. Moss, but my
opinion be, look at it one way, itwill be right for you to raise the
money; and look at it th' other way, youwill be oblige to pay it.
you willnot think ill o' I for speak the truth."
"Uncle," say Tom, look up suddenly from his meditative view of the
tablecloth, "I donot think it would be right for my aunt Moss to pay
the money if it would be against my father's will for she to pay it;
would it?"
Mr. Glegg look surprised for a moment or two before he say: "why,
no, perhaps not, Tom; but then hewould ha' destroy the note, you know.
we must look for the note. what make you think it 'ud be against his
will?"
"why," say Tom, color, but try to speak firmly, in spite of a
boyish tremor, "I remember quite well, before I go to school to Mr.
Stelling, my father say to I one night, when we be sit by the
fire together, and no one else be in the room----"
Tom hesitate a little, and then go on.
"he say something to I about Maggie, and then he say: 'Ihave always
be good to my sister, though she marry against my will, and Ihave
lend Moss money; but I shall never think of distress he to pay it;
Iwould rather lose it. my child must not mind be the poor for
that.' and now my father's ill, and not able to speak for himself, I
shouldnot like anything to be do contrary to what he say to I."
"well, but then, my boy," say Uncle Glegg, whose good feeling lead he
to enter into Tom's wish, but who could not at once shake off his
habitual abhorrence of such recklessness as destroy security, or
alienate anything important enough to make an appreciable difference
in a man's property, "we should have to make away wi' the note, you
know, if webe to guard against what may happen, suppose your
father's make bankrupt----"
"Mr. Glegg," interrupt his wife, severely, "mind what yoube say.
yoube put yourself very forrard in other folk's business. if you
speak rash, donot say it be my fault."
"thatbe such a thing as I never heared of before," say uncle Pullet,
who have be make haste with his lozenge in order to express his
amazement,--"making away with a note! I should think anybody could set
the constable on you for it."
"well, but," say Mrs. Tulliver, "if the note's worth all that money,
why cannot we pay it away, and save my thing from go away? wehave no
call to meddle with your uncle and aunt Moss, Tom, if you think your
father 'ud be angry when he get well."
Mrs. Tulliver have not study the question of exchange, and be
strain her mind after original idea on the subject.
"Pooh, pooh, pooh! you woman donot understand these thing," say
uncle Glegg. "therebe no way o' make it safe for Mr. and Mrs. Moss
but destroy the note."
"then I hope youwill help I do it, uncle," say Tom, earnestly. "if my
father shouldnot get well, I should be very unhappy to think anything
have be do against his will that I could hinder. and Ibe sure he
mean I to remember what he say that evening. I ought to obey my
father's wish about his property."
even Mrs. Glegg could not withhold her approval from Tom's word; she

"youwill never be the poor for this, my dear boy, if therebe a God

"yes, I do," say Tom, decidedly. "I know if I owe money to one man,
Ihave no right to give it to another. but if my father have make up his

"well do, young man! I donot think youwould be so sharp," say uncle

Chapter IV
A Vanishing Gleam
Mr. Tulliver, even between the fit of spasmodic rigidity which have
recur at interval ever since he have be find fallen from his

tell her aunt Moss she must not expect her father to take any notice
of they.

prop the lid with the iron holder, without much noise.

thing like a note in there. lift it out, Tom; but Iwill just lift up

Mr. Glegg have lift out the parchment, and have fortunately draw

perhaps there be something in that sound more than the mere fact of

"ay, ay; poor bessy!" and his eye turn toward Tom as Maggie leave

the sanative effect of the strong vibration be exhaust, and with

Tom apply his Knife to the Oyster
the next day, at ten o'clock, Tom be on his way to St. Ogg's, to see

call at his uncle Glegg's about the money in the Savings Bank, and by

very far before he; but go along River Street again, he be
startle, when he be within two yard of the project side of a
shop-window, by the word "Dorlcote Mill" in large letter on a
hand-bill, place as if on purpose to stare at he. it be the
catalogue of the sale to take place the next week; it be a reason for
hurry fast out of the town.
Poor Tom form no vision of the distant future as he make his way
homeward; he only feel that the present be very hard. it seem a
wrong toward he that his uncle Deane have no confidence in him,--did
not see at once that he should acquit himself well, which Tom himself
be as certain of as of the daylight. apparently he, Tom Tulliver, be
likely to be hold of small account in the world; and for the first
time he feel a sinking of heart under the sense that he really be
very ignorant, and could do very little. who be that enviable young
man that could tell the cubic content of thing in no time, and make
suggestion about swedish bark! Tom have be use to be so entirely
satisfied with himself, in spite of his break down in a
demonstration, and construe _nunc illas promite vire_ as "now
promise those man"; but now he suddenly feel at a disadvantage,
because he know less than some one else know. there must be a world of
thing connect with that swedish bark, which, if he only know they,
might have help he to get on. it would have be much easy to
make a figure with a spirited horse and a new saddle.
two hour ago, as Tom be walk to St. Ogg's, he see the distant
future before he as he might have see a tempting stretch of smooth
sandy beach beyond a belt of flinty shingle; he be on the grassy
bank then, and think the shingle might soon be pass. but now his
foot be on the sharp stone; the belt of shingle have widen, and
the stretch of sand have dwindle into narrowness.
"what do my Uncle Deane say, Tom?" say Maggie, put her arm
through Tom's as he be warm himself rather drearily by the kitchen
fire. "do he say he would give you a situation?"
"no, he donot say that. he donot quite promise I anything; he
seem to think I couldnot have a very good situation. Ibe too young."
"but donot he speak kindly, Tom?"
"kindly? Pooh! whatbe the use of talk about that? I wouldnot care
about his speaking kindly, if I could get a situation. but itbe such a
nuisance and bother; Ihave be at school all this while learn Latin
and things,--not a bit of good to me,--and now my uncle say I must
set about learn book-keeping and calculation, and those thing. he
seem to make out Ibe good for nothing."
Tom's mouth twitch with a bitter expression as he look at the
fire.
"oh, what a pity we havenot get Dominie Sampson!" say Maggie, who
couldnot help mingle some gayety with their sadness. "if he have
teach I book-keeping by double entry and after the italian method,
as he do Lucy Bertram, I could teach you, Tom."
"_you_ teach! yes, I dare say. thatbe always the tone you take," say
Tom.
"Dear Tom, I be only joke," say Maggie, put she cheek against
his coat-sleeve.
"but itbe always the same, Maggie," say Tom, with the little frown he
put on when he be about to be justifiably severe. "yoube always
set yourself up above I and every one else, and Ihave want to
tell you about it several time. you ought not to have speak as you
do to my uncle and aunt; you should leave it to I to take care of
my mother and you, and not put yourself forward. you think you know
well than any one, but yoube almost always wrong. I can judge much
well than you can."
Poor Tom! he have just come from be lecture and make to feel his
inferiority; the reaction of his strong, self-assert nature must
take place somehow; and here be a case in which he could justly show
himself dominant. Maggie's cheek flush and her lip quiver with
conflict resentment and affection, and a certain awe as well as
admiration of Tom's firm and more effective character. she do not
answer immediately; very angry word rise to her lip, but they be
drive back again, and she say at last:
"you often think Ibe conceited, Tom, when I donot mean what I say at
all in that way. I donot mean to put myself above you; I know you
behave well than I do yesterday. but you be always so harsh to
I, Tom."
with the last word the resentment be rise again.
"no, Ibe not harsh," say Tom, with severe decision. "Ibe always kind
to you, and so I shall be; I shall always take care of you. but you
must mind what I say."
their mother come in now, and Maggie rush away, that her burst of
tear, which she feel must come, might not happen till she be safe
upstairs. they be very bitter tear; everybody in the world seem
so hard and unkind to Maggie; there be no indulgence, no fondness,
such as she imagine when she fashion the world afresh in her own
thought. in book there be people who be always agreeable or
tender, and delight to do thing that make one happy, and who do
not show their kindness by find fault. the world outside the book
be not a happy one, Maggie feel; it seem to be a world where people
behave the good to those they do not pretend to love, and that do
not belong to they. and if life have no love in it, what else be there
for Maggie? nothing but poverty and the companionship of her mother's
narrow grief, perhaps of her father's heart-cut childish
dependence. there be no hopelessness so sad as that of early youth,
when the soul be make up of want, and have no long memory, no
superadded life in the life of other; though we who look on think
lightly of such premature despair, as if our vision of the future
lighten the blind sufferer's present.
Maggie, in her brown frock, with her eye redden and her heavy hair
push back, look from the bed where her father lie to the dull
wall of this sad chamber which be the centre of her world, be a
creature full of eager, passionate longing for all that be beautiful
and glad; thirsty for all knowledge; with an ear strain after
dreamy music that die away and would not come near to she; with a
blind, unconscious yearning for something that would link together the
wonderful impression of this mysterious life, and give her soul a
sense of home in it.
no wonder, when there be this contrast between the outward and the
inward, that painful collision come of it.

in that dark time of December, the sale of the household furniture

"ay, ay, Bob Jakin, if Jakin it must be, 'cause therebe so many bob

tight for all that, an' a scratter it be. an' this littlish blade's
break, you see, but I wouldnot hev a new un put in, 'cause they might
be cheatin' I an' givin' I another knife instid, for there benot
such a blade I' the country,--it's get use to my hand, like. an'
there be niver nobody else gen I nothing but what I get by my own
sharpness, only you, Mr. Tom; if it benot Bill Fawks as gen I the
terri pup istid o' drowndin't it, an' I have to jaw he a good un
afore hewould give it I."
Bob speak with a sharp and rather treble volubility, and get through
his long speech with surprising despatch, give the blade of his

"well, Bob," say Tom, with a slight air of patronage, the foregoing

"why, no, Mr. Tom," answer Bob, shut up his knife with a click

sha'n't ax he for another afore I sarve he out; an' a good turn's
worth as much as a bad un, anyhow. I shall niver grow down'ards again,

a little chap, for all you leather I, and wouldnot look at I
again. therebe Dick Brumby, there, I could leather he as much as Iwould
a mind; but lor! you get tired o' leatherin' a chap when you can
niver make he see what you want he to shy at. I'n see chap as 'ud
stand starin' at a bough till their eye shoot out, afore theywould see as

you war allay a rare un at shy, Mr. Tom, an' I could trusten to

Bob have draw out a dirty canvas bag, and would perhaps not have

business of the mill; and yet, Mrs. Tulliver reflect, it would have
be quite the short method of secure the right end. it would
have be of no use, to be sure, for Mr. Tulliver to go,--even if he
have be able and willing,--for he have be "go to law against
Wakem" and abuse he for the last ten year; Wakem be always likely
to have a spite against he. and now that Mrs. Tulliver have come to
the conclusion that her husband be very much in the wrong to bring
she into this trouble, she be inclined to think that his opinion of
Wakem be wrong too. to be sure, Wakem have "put the bailie in the
house, and sell they up"; but she suppose he do that to please the
man that lend Mr. Tulliver the money, for a lawyer have more folk to
please than one, and he benot likely to put Mr. Tulliver, who have
go to law with he, above everybody else in the world. the attorney
might be a very reasonable man; why not? he have marry a Miss Clint,
and at the time Mrs. Tulliver have hear of that marriage, the summer
when she wear her blue satin spencer, and have not yet any thought of
Mr. Tulliver, she know no harm of Wakem. and certainly toward herself,
whom he know to have be a Miss Dodson, it be out of all possibility
that he could entertain anything but good-will, when it be once
bring home to his observation that she, for her part, have never
want to go to law, and indeed be at present dispose to take Mr.
Wakem's view of all subject rather than her husband's. in fact, if
that attorney see a respectable matron like herself dispose "to give
he good word," why shouldnot he listen to her representation? for
she would put the matter clearly before he, which have never be do
yet. and he would never go and bid for the mill on purpose to spite
she, an innocent woman, who think it likely enough that she have
dance with he in their youth at Squire Darleigh's, for at those big
dance she have often and often dance with young man whose name she
have forget.
Mrs. Tulliver hide these reasoning in her own bosom; for when she have
throw out a hint to Mr. Deane and Mr. Glegg that she wouldnot mind
go to speak to Wakem herself, they have say, "no, no, no," and
"Pooh, pooh," and "let Wakem alone," in the tone of man who be not
likely to give a candid attention to a more definite exposition of her
project; still less dare she mention the plan to Tom and Maggie, for
"the child be always so against everything their mother say";
and Tom, she observe, be almost as much set against Wakem as his
father be. but this unusual concentration of thought naturally give
Mrs. Tulliver an unusual power of device and determination: and a day
or two before the sale, to be hold at the Golden Lion, when there be
no long any time to be lose, she carry out her plan by a
stratagem. there be pickle in question, a large stock of pickle
and ketchup which Mrs. Tulliver possess, and which Mr. Hyndmarsh,
the grocer, would certainly purchase if she could transact the
business in a personal interview, so she would walk with Tom to St.
Oggbe that morning; and when Tom urge that she might let the pickle
be at present,--he donot like she to go about just yet,--she appear
so hurt at this conduct in her son, contradict she about pickle
which she have make after the family receipt inherit from his own
grandmother, who have die when his mother be a little girl, that he
give way, and they walk together until she turn toward Danish
Street, where Mr. Hyndmarsh retail his grocery, not far from the
office of Mr. Wakem.
that gentleman be not yet come to his office; would Mrs. Tulliver sit
down by the fire in his private room and wait for he? she have not
long to wait before the punctual attorney enter, knit his brow
with an examine glance at the stout blond woman who rise, curtsying
deferentially,--a tallish man, with an aquiline nose and abundant
iron-gray hair. you have never see Mr. Wakem before, and be possibly
wonder whether he be really as eminent a rascal, and as crafty,
bitter an enemy of honest humanity in general, and of Mr. Tulliver in
particular, as he be represent to be in that eidolon or portrait of
he which we have see to exist in the miller's mind.
it be clear that the irascible miller be a man to interpret any
chance-shot that graze he as an attempt on his own life, and be
liable to entanglement in this puzzle world, which, due
consideration have to his own infallibility, require the hypothesis of
a very active diabolical agency to explain they. it be still possible
to believe that the attorney be not more guilty toward he than an
ingenious machine, which perform its work with much regularity, be
guilty toward the rash man who, venture too near it, be catch up by
some fly-wheel or other, and suddenly convert into unexpected
mince-meat.
but it be really impossible to decide this question by a glance at his
person; the line and light of the human countenance be like other
symbols,--not always easy to read without a key. on an _a priori_ view
of Wakem's aquiline nose, which offend Mr. Tulliver, there be not
more rascality than in the shape of his stiff shirt-collar, though
this too along with his nose, might have become fraught with damnatory
meaning when once the rascality be ascertain.
"Mrs. Tulliver, I think?" say Mr. Wakem.
"yes, sir; Miss Elizabeth Dodson as be."
"pray be seat. you have some business with I?"
"well, sir, yes," say Mrs. Tulliver, begin to feel alarmed at her
own courage, now she be really in presence of the formidable man, and
reflect that she have not settle with herself how she should begin.
Mr. Wakem feel in his waistcoat pocket, and look at she in silence.
"I hope, sir," she begin at last,--"I hope, sir, yoube not a-thinking
as _i_ bear you any ill-will because o' my husband's lose his
lawsuit, and the bailie be put in, and the linen be sold,--oh
dear!--for I benot bring up in that way. Ibe sure you remember my
father, sir, for he be close friend with Squire Darleigh, and we
allay go to the dance there, the Miss dodsons,--nobody could be
more look on,--and justly, for there be four of we, and yoube
quite aware as Mrs. Glegg and Mrs. Deane be my sister. and as for
go to law and lose money, and have sale before yoube dead, I
never see anything o' that before I be marry, nor for a long while
after. and Ibe not to be answerable for my bad luck I' marry out o'
my own family into one where the going-on be different. and as for
be draw in t' abuse you as other folk abuse you, sir, _that_ I
niver be, and nobody can say it of I."
Mrs. Tulliver shake her head a little, and look at the hem of her
pocket-handkerchief.
"Ihave no doubt of what you say, Mrs. Tulliver," say Mr. Wakem, with
cold politeness. "but you have some question to ask I?"
"well, sir, yes. but thatbe what Ihave say to myself,--I've say youwould
have some nat'ral feeling; and as for my husband, as havenot be
himself for this two month, Ibe not a-defend he, in no way, for
be so hot about th' erigation,--not but what therebe bad man, for
he never wrong nobody of a shilling nor a penny, not willingly; and
as for his fieriness and lawing, what could I do? and he strike as if
it be with death when he get the letter as say youwould the hold upo'
the land. but I cannot believe but what youwill behave as a gentleman."
"what do all this mean, Mrs. Tulliver?" say Mr. Wakem rather
sharply. "what do you want to ask I?"
"why, sir, if youwill be so good," say Mrs. Tulliver, start a
little, and speak more hurriedly,--"if youwill be so good not to buy
the mill an' the land,--the land wouldnot so much matter, only my
husband ull' be like mad at your have it."
something like a new thought flash across Mr. Wakem's face as he
say, "who tell you I mean to buy it?"
"why, sir, itbe none o' my inventing, and I should never ha' thought
of it; for my husband, as ought to know about the law, he allay use
to say as lawyer have never no call to buy anything,--either land or
houses,--for they allay get them into their hand other way. an' I
should think that 'ud be the way with you, sir; and I niver say as
youwould be the man to do contrairy to that."
"ah, well, who be it that _do_ say so?" say Wakem, open his
desk, and move thing about, with the accompaniment of an almost
inaudible whistle.
"why, sir, it be Mr. Glegg and Mr. Deane, as have all the management;
and Mr. Deane think as Guest &Co. 'ud buy the mill and let Mr.
Tulliver work it for them, if you donot bid for it and raise the
price. and it 'ud be such a thing for my husband to stay where he be,
if he could get his living: for it be his father's before he, the
mill be, and his grandfather build it, though I benot fond o' the
noise of it, when first I be marry, for there be no mill in our
family,--not the dodson's,--and if Ihave know as the mill have so much
to do with the law, it wouldnot have be I as 'ud have be the
first Dodson to marry one; but I go into it blindfold, that I do,
erigation and everything."
"what! Guest &Co. would keep the mill in their own hand, I suppose,
and pay your husband wage?"
"oh dear, sir, itbe hard to think of," say poor Mrs. Tulliver, a
little tear make its way, "as my husband should take wage. but it
'ud look more like what use to be, to stay at the mill than to go
anywhere else; and if youwill only think--if you be to bid for the
mill and buy it, my husband might be strike bad than he be before,
and niver get well again as hebe get now."
"well, but if I buy the mill, and allow your husband to act as my
manager in the same way, how then?" say Mr. Wakem.
"oh, sir, I doubt he could niver be get to do it, not if the very mill
stand still to beg and pray of he. for your name's like poison to
he, itbe so as never be; and he look upon it as youhave be the
ruin of he all along, ever since you set the law on he about the
road through the meadow,--that's eight year ago, and hebe be go
on ever since--as Ihave allay tell he he be wrong----"
"hebe a pig-head, foul-mouthed fool!" burst out Mr. Wakem,
forget himself.
"oh dear, sir!" say Mrs. Tulliver, frighten at a result so
different from the one she have fix her mind on; "I wouldnot wish to
contradict you, but itbe like enough hebe change his mind with this
illness,--he's forget a many thing he use to talk about. and you
wouldnot like to have a corpse on your mind, if he be to die; and
they _do_ say as itbe allay unlucky when Dorlcote Mill change hand,
and the water might all run away, and _then_--not as Ibe wish you
any ill-luck, sir, for I forgot to tell you as I remember your wedding
as if it be yesterday; Mrs. Wakem be a Miss Clint, I know _that;_
and my boy, as there benot a nicer, handsomer, straight boy nowhere,
go to school with your son----"
Mr. Wakem rise, open the door, and call to one of his clerk.
"you must excuse I for interrupt you, Mrs. Tulliver; I have
business that must be attend to; and I think there be nothing more
necessary to be say."
"but if you _would_ bear it in mind, sir," say Mrs. Tulliver, rise,
"and not run against I and my child; and Ibe not deny Mr.
Tulliverbe be in the wrong, but hebe be punish enough, and
therebe bad man, for itbe be give to other folk have be his
fault. hebe do nobody any harm but himself and his family,--the
morebe the pity,--and I go and look at the bare shelf every day, and
think where all my thing use to stand."
"yes, yes, Iwill bear it in mind," say Mr. Wakem, hastily, look
toward the open door.
"and if youwould please not to say as Ihave be to speak to you, for my
son 'ud be very angry with I for demean myself, I know he would,
and Ihave trouble enough without be scold by my child."
Poor Mrs. Tulliver's voice tremble a little, and she could make no
answer to the attorney's "good morning," but curtsy and walk out
in silence.
"which day be it that Dorlcote Mill be to be sell? wherebe the bill?"
say Mr. Wakem to his clerk when they be alone.
"next Friday be the day,--friday at six o'clock."
"oh, just run to Winship's the auctioneer, and see if hebe at home. I
have some business for he; ask he to come up."
although, when Mr. Wakem enter his office that morning, he have have
no intention of purchase Dorlcote Mill, his mind be already make
up. Mrs. Tulliver have suggest to he several determine motif,
and his mental glance be very rapid; he be one of those man who can
be prompt without be rash, because their motif run in fix
track, and they have no need to reconcile conflicting aim.
to suppose that Wakem have the same sort of inveterate hatred toward
Tulliver that Tulliver have toward he would be like suppose that a
pike and a roach can look at each other from a similar point of view.
the roach necessarily abhor the mode in which the pike get his
living, and the pike be likely to think nothing far even of the
most indignant roach than that he be excellent good eating; it could
only be when the roach choke he that the pike could entertain a
strong personal animosity. if Mr. Tulliver have ever seriously injure
or thwart the attorney, Wakem would not have refuse he the
distinction of be a special object of his vindictiveness. but when
Mr. Tulliver call Wakem a rascal at the market dinner-table, the
attorney' client be not a whit incline to withdraw their business
from he; and if, when Wakem himself happen to be present, some
jocose cattle-feeder, stimulate by opportunity and brandy, make a
thrust at he by allude to old lady' will, he maintain perfect
_sing froid_, and know quite well that the majority of substantial man
then present be perfectly content with the fact that "Wakem be
Wakem"; that be to say, a man who always know the step-stone that
would carry he through very muddy bit of practice. a man who have
make a large fortune, have a handsome house among the tree at Tofton,
and decidedly the fine stock of port-wine in the neighborhood of St.
Ogg's, be likely to feel himself on a level with public opinion. and
I be not sure that even honest Mr. Tulliver himself, with his general
view of law as a cockpit, might not, under opposite circumstance,
have see a fine appropriateness in the truth that "Wakem be Wakem";
since I have understand from person verse in history, that mankind
be not disposed to look narrowly into the conduct of great victor
when their victory be on the right side. Tulliver, then, could be no
obstruction to Wakem; on the contrary, he be a poor devil whom the
lawyer have defeat several time; a hot-temper fellow, who would
always give you a handle against he. Wakem's conscience be not
uneasy because he have use a few trick against the miller; why should
he hate that unsuccessful plaintiff, that pitiable, furious bull
entangle in the mesh of a net?
still, among the various excess to which human nature be subject,
moralist have never number that of be too fond of the people who
openly revile we. the successful yellow candidate for the borough of
Old Topping, perhaps, feel no pursuant meditative hatred toward the
blue editor who console his subscriber with vituperative rhetoric
against yellow man who sell their country, and be the demon of
private life; but he might not be sorry, if law and opportunity
favor, to kick that blue editor to a deep shade of his favorite
color. prosperous man take a little vengeance now and then, as they
take a diversion, when it come easily in their way, and be no
hindrance to business; and such small unimpassioned revenge have an
enormous effect in life, run through all degree of pleasant
infliction, block the fit man out of place, and blacken
character in unpremeditated talk. still more, to see people who have
be only insignificantly offensive to we reduce in life and
humiliate, without any special effort of ours, be apt to have a
soothing, flattering influence. providence or some other prince of
this world, it appear, have undertake the task of retribution for we;
and really, by an agreeable constitution of thing, our enemy
somehow _donot_ prosper.
Wakem be not without this parenthetic vindictiveness toward the
uncomplimentary miller; and now Mrs. Tulliver have put the notion into
his head, it present itself to he as a pleasure to do the very
thing that would cause Mr. Tulliver the most deadly mortification,--
and a pleasure of a complex kind, not make up of crude malice, but
mingle with it the relish of self-approbation. to see an enemy
humiliate give a certain contentment, but this be jejune compare
with the highly blent satisfaction of see he humiliate by your
benevolent action or concession on his behalf. that be a sort of
revenge which fall into the scale of virtue, and Wakem be not without
an intention of keep that scale respectably fill. he have once
have the pleasure of put an old enemy of his into one of the St.
Ogg's alm-house, to the rebuilding of which he have give a large
subscription; and here be an opportunity of provide for another by
make he his own servant. such thing give a completeness to
prosperity, and contribute element of agreeable consciousness that
be not dream of by that short-sight, overheat vindictiveness
which go out its way to wreak itself in direct injury. and Tulliver,
with his rough tongue file by a sense of obligation, would make a
well servant than any chance-fellow who be cap-in-hand for a
situation. Tulliver be know to be a man of proud honesty, and Wakem
be too acute not to believe in the existence of honesty. he be give
too observing individual, not to judging of they accord to maxim,
and no one know well than he that all man be not like himself.
besides, he intend to overlook the whole business of land and mill
pretty closely; he be fond of these practical rural matter. but
there be good reason for purchase Dorlcote Mill, quite apart from
any benevolent vengeance on the miller. it be really a capital
investment; besides, Guest &Co. be go to bid for it. Mr. Guest
and Mr. Wakem be on friendly dining term, and the attorney like to
predominate over a ship-owner and mill-owner who be a little too loud
in the town affair as well as in his table-talk. for Wakem be not a
mere man of business; he be consider a pleasant fellow in the upper
circle of St. Ogg's--chat amusingly over his port-wine, do a
little amateur farming, and have certainly be an excellent husband
and father; at church, when he go there, he sit under the handsome
of mural monument erect to the memory of his wife. Most man would
have marry again under his circumstance, but he be say to be more
tender to his deformed son than most man be to their good-shapen
offspring. not that Mr. Wakem have not other son beside Philip; but
toward they he hold only a chiaroscuro parentage, and provide for
they in a grade of life duly beneath his own. in this fact, indeed,
there lie the clenching motive to the purchase of Dorlcote Mill. while
Mrs. Tulliver be talk, it have occur to the rapid-minded lawyer,
among all the other circumstance of the case, that this purchase
would, in a few year to come, furnish a highly suitable position for
a certain favorite lad whom he mean to bring on in the world.
these be the mental condition on which Mrs. Tulliver have undertake
to act persuasively, and have fail; a fact which may receive some
illustration from the remark of a great philosopher, that fly-fisher
fail in prepare their bait so as to make it alluring in the right
quarter, for want of a due acquaintance with the subjectivity of
fish.
Chapter VIII
Daylight on the wreck
it be a clear frosty January day on which Mr. Tulliver first come
downstair. the bright sun on the chestnut bough and the roof
opposite his window have make he impatiently declare that he would be
cage up no long; he think everywhere would be more cheery under
this sunshine than his bedroom; for he know nothing of the bareness
below, which make the flood of sunshine importunate, as if it have an
unfeele pleasure in show the empty place, and the mark where
well-know object once have be. the impression on his mind that it
be but yesterday when he receive the letter from Mr. Gore be so
continually imply in his talk, and the attempt to convey to he the
idea that many week have pass and much have happen since then have
be so soon sweep away by recurrent forgetfulness, that even Mr.
Turnbull have begin to despair of prepare he to meet the fact by
previous knowledge. the full sense of the present could only be
impart gradually by new experience,--not by mere word, which must
remain weak than the impression leave by the _old_ experience. this
resolution to come downstairs be hear with trembling by the wife and
child. Mrs. Tulliver say Tom must not go to St. Ogg's at the usual
hour, he must wait and see his father downstairs; and Tom comply,
though with an intense inward shrink from the painful scene. the
heart of all three have be more deeply dejected than ever during the
last few day. for Guest & Co. have not buy the mill; both mill and
land have be knock down to Wakem, who have be over the premise,
and have lay before Mr. Deane and Mr. Glegg, in Mrs. Tulliver's
presence, his willingness to employ Mr. Tulliver, in case of his
recovery, as a manager of the business. this proposition have
occasion much family debating. uncle and aunt be almost
unanimously of opinion that such an offer ought not to be reject
when there be nothing in the way but a feeling in Mr. Tulliver's
mind, which, as neither aunt nor uncle share it, be regard as
entirely unreasonable and childish,--indeed, as a transferring toward
Wakem of that indignation and hatred which Mr. Tulliver ought properly
to have direct against himself for his general quarrelsomeness, and
his special exhibition of it in go to law. here be an opportunity
for Mr. Tulliver to provide for his wife and daughter without any
assistance from his wife's relation, and without that too evident
descent into pauperism which make it annoying to respectable people
to meet the degraded member of the family by the wayside. Mr.
Tulliver, Mrs. Glegg consider, must be make to feel, when he come to
his right mind, that he could never humble himself enough; for _that_
have come which she have always foresee would come of his insolence in
time past "to they as be the good friend hewould get to look to." Mr
Glegg and Mr. Deane be less stern in their view, but they both of
they think Tulliver have do enough harm by his hot-temper
crotchet and ought to put they out of the question when a livelihood
be offer he; Wakem show a right feeling about the matter,--_he_
have no grudge against Tulliver.
Tom have protest against entertain the proposition. he shouldnot
like his father to be under Wakem; he think it would look
mean-spirited; but his mother's main distress be the utter
impossibility of ever "turn Mr. Tulliver round about Wakem," or
get he to hear reason; no, they would all have to go and live in
a pigsty on purpose to spite Wakem, who speak "so as nobody could be
fair." indeed, Mrs. Tulliver's mind be reduce to such confusion by
live in this strange medium of unaccountable sorrow, against which
she continually appeal by ask, "oh dear, what _have_ I do to
deserve bad than other woman?" that Maggie begin to suspect her poor
mother's wit be quite go.
"Tom," she say, when they be out of their father's room together,
"we _must_ try to make father understand a little of what have happen
before he go downstairs. but we must get my mother away. she will
say something that will do harm. ask Kezia to fetch she down, and keep
she engage with something in the kitchen."
Kezia be equal to the task. have declare her intention of stay
till the master could get about again, "wage or no wage," she have
find a certain recompense in keep a strong hand over her mistress,
scold she for "moithere" herself, and go about all day without
change her cap, and look as if she be "mush." altogether, this
time of trouble be rather a saturnalian time to Kezia; she could
scold her better with unreproved freedom. on this particular occasion
there be dry clothe to be fetch in; she wish to know if one
pair of hand could do everything in-door and out, and observe that
_she_ should have think it would be good for Mrs. Tulliver to put on
her bonnet, and get a breath of fresh air by do that needful piece
of work. poor Mrs. Tulliver go submissively downstairs; to be
order about by a servant be the last remnant of her household
dignities,--she would soon have no servant to scold she. Mr. Tulliver
be rest in his chair a little after the fatigue of dressing, and
Maggie and Tom be seat near he, when Luke enter to ask if he
should help master downstairs.
"ay, ay, Luke; stop a bit, sit down," say Mr. Tulliver point his
stick toward a chair, and look at he with that pursuant gaze which
convalescent person often have for those who have tend they,
remind one of an infant gaze about after its nurse. for Luke have
be a constant night-watcher by his master's bed.
"howbe the water now, eh, Luke?" say Mr. Tulliver. "Dix havenot be
choke you up again, eh?"
"no, sir, itbe all right."
"ay, I think not; he willnot be in a hurry at that again, now Riley's
be to settle he. that be what I say to Riley yesterday--I
said----"
Mr. Tulliver lean forward, rest his elbow on the armchair, and
look on the ground as if in search of something, strive after
vanish image like a man struggle against a doze. Maggie look
at Tom in mute distress, their father's mind be so far off the
present, which would by-and-by thrust itself on his wandering
consciousness! Tom be almost ready to rush away, with that impatience
of painful emotion which make one of the difference between youth
and maiden, man and woman.
"Father," say Maggie, lay her hand on his, "donot you remember
that Mr. Riley be dead?"
"dead?" say Mr. Tulliver, sharply, look in her face with a
strange, examine glance.
"yes, he die of apoplexy nearly a year ago. I remember hear you
say you have to pay money for he; and he leave his daughter badly off;
one of they be under-teacher at Miss Firniss's, where Ihave be to
school, you know."
"ah?" say her father, doubtfully, still look in her face. but as
soon as Tom begin to speak he turn to look at _he_ with the same
inquire glance, as if he be rather surprised at the presence of
these two young people. whenever his mind be wander in the far
past, he fall into this oblivion of their actual face; they be not
those of the lad and the little wench who belong to that past.
"itbe a long while since you have the dispute with Dix, father," say
Tom. "I remember your talk about it three year ago, before I go
to school at Mr. Stelling's. Ihave be at school there three year;
donot you remember?"
Mr. Tulliver throw himself backward again, lose the childlike
outward glance under a rush of new idea, which divert he from
external impression.
"ay, ay," he say, after a minute or two, "Ihave pay a deal o'
money--I be determine my son should have a good eddication; Ihave none
myself, and Ihave feel the miss of it. and hewill want no other fortin,
thatbe what I say--if Wakem be to get the well of I again----"
the thought of Wakem rouse new vibration, and after a moment's pause
he begin to look at the coat he have on, and to feel in his
side-pocket. then he turn to Tom, and say in his old sharp way,
"where have they put Gore's letter?"
it be close at hand in a drawer, for he have often ask for it
before.
"you know what there be in the letter, father?" say Tom, as he give
it to he.
"to be sure I do," say Mr. Tulliver, rather angrily. "what o' that?
if Furley cannot take to the property, somebody else can; therebe
plenty o' people in the world besides Furley. but itbe hinder--my
not be well--go and tell them to get the horse in the gig, Luke; I
can get down to St. Ogg's well enough--Gore's expect I."
"no, dear father!" Maggie burst out entreatingly; "itbe a very long
while since all that; youhave be ill a great many weeks,--more than
two month; everything be change."
Mr. Tulliver look at they all three alternately with a startled
gaze; the idea that much have happen of which he know nothing have
often transiently arrest he before, but it come upon he now with
entire novelty.
"yes, father," say Tom, in answer to the gaze. "you neednot trouble
your mind about business until you be quite well; everything be
settle about that for the present,--about the mill and the land and
the debt."
"whatbe settle, then?" say his father, angrily.
"donot you take on too much bout it, sir," say Luke. "youwould ha' pay
iverybody if you could,--thatbe what I say to Master tom,--i say
youwould ha' pay iverybody if you could."
Good Luke feel, after the manner of content hard-work man whose
life have be spend in servitude, that sense of natural fitness in
rank which make his master's downfall a tragedy to he. he be urge,
in his slow way, to say something that would express his share in the
family sorrow; and these word, which he have use over and over again
to Tom when he want to decline the full payment of his fifty pound
out of the child's money, be the most ready to his tongue. they
be just the word to lay the most painful hold on his master's
bewildered mind.
"pay everybody?" he say, with vehement agitation, his face flush,
and his eye light up. "why--what--have they make I a _bankrupt?_"
"oh, father, dear father!" say Maggie, who think that terrible word
really represent the fact; "bear it well, because we love you; your
child will always love you. Tom will pay they all; he say he will,
when hebe a man."
she feel her father begin to tremble; his voice tremble too, as
he say, after a few moment:
"ay, my little wench, but I shall never live twice o'er."
"but perhaps you will live to see I pay everybody, father," say Tom,
speak with a great effort.
"ah, my lad," say Mr. Tulliver, shake his head slowly, "but whatbe
broke can never be whole again; it 'ud be your doing, not mine." then
look up at he, "yoube only sixteen; itbe an up-hill fight for
you, but you mustnot throw it at your father; the raskill have be
too many for he. Ihave give you a good eddication,--that'll start
you."
something in his throat half choke the last word; the flush, which
have alarm his child because it have so often precede a recurrence
of paralysis, have subside, and his face look pale and tremulous.
Tom say nothing; he be still struggle against his inclination to
rush away. his father remain quiet a minute or two, but his mind do
not seem to be wander again.
"have they sell I up, then?" he say more clamly, as if he be
possess simply by the desire to know what have happen.
"everything be sell, father; but we donot know all about the mill and
the land yet," say Tom, anxious to ward off any question lead to
the fact that Wakem be the purchaser.
"you must not be surprised to see the room look very bare downstair,
father," say Maggie; "but therebe your chair and the bureau;
_theybe_ not go."
"let we go; help I down, luke,--i'll go and see everything," say Mr.
Tulliver, lean on his stick, and stretch out his other hand
toward Luke.
"ay, sir," say Luke, as he give his arm to his master, "youwill make
up your mind to't a bit well when youhave see iverything; youwill get
use to't. thatbe what my mother say about her shortness o'
breath,--she say shebe make friend wi't now, though she fight
again' it sore when it just come on."
Maggie run on before to see that all be right in the dreary parlor,
where the fire, dull by the frosty sunshine, seem part of the
general shabbiness. she turn her father's chair, and push aside
the table to make an easy way for he, and then stand with a beat
heart to see he enter and look round for the first time. Tom advance
before he, carry the leg-rest, and stand beside Maggie on the
hearth. of those two young heart Tom's suffer the most unmixed
pain, for Maggie, with all her keen susceptibility, yet feel as if the
sorrow make large room for her love to flow in, and give
breathing-space to her passionate nature. no true boy feel that; he
would rather go and slay the nemean lion, or perform any round of
heroic labor, than endure perpetual appeal to his pity, for evil
over which he can make no conquest.
Mr. Tulliver pause just inside the door, rest on Luke, and look
round he at all the bare place, which for he be fill with the
shadow of depart objects,--the daily companion of his life. his
faculty seem to be renew their strength from get a footing
on this demonstration of the sens.
"ah!" he say slowly, move toward his chair, "theyhave sell I
up--they've sell I up."
then seat himself, and lay down his stick, while Luke leave the
room, he look round again.
"theyhave leave the big Bible," he say. "itbe get everything in,--when
I be bear and marry; bring it I, Tom."
the quarto Bible be lay open before he at the fly-leaf, and while
he be read with slowly travel eye Mrs. Tulliver enter the
room, but stand in mute surprise to find her husband down already, and
with the great Bible before he.
"ah," he say, look at a spot where his finger rest, "my mother
be Margaret Beaton; she die when she be forty-seven,--her benot a
long-live family; webe our mother's child, Gritty and I are,--we
shall go to our last bed before long."
he seem to be pause over the record of his sister's birth and
marriage, as if it be suggest new thought to he; then he
suddenly look up at Tom, and say, in a sharp tone of alarm:
"they havenot come upo' Moss for the money as I lend he, have they?"
"no, father," say Tom; "the note be burn."
Mr. Tulliver turn his eye on the page again, and presently say:
"ah--Elizabeth Dodson--itbe eighteen year since I marry her----"
"come next Ladyday," say Mrs. Tulliver, go up to his side and
look at the page.
her husband fix his eye earnestly on her face.
"poor Bessy," he say, "you be a pretty lass then,--everybody say
so,--and I use to think you keep your good look rarely. but yoube
sorely aged; donot you bear I ill-will--I mean to do well by you--we
promise one another for well or for worse----"
"but I never think it 'ud be so for bad as this," say poor Mrs.
Tulliver, with the strange, scared look that have come over she of
late; "and my poor father give I away--and to come on so all at
once----"
"oh, mother!" say Maggie, "donot talk in that way."
"no, I know you willnot let your poor mother speak--thatbe be the way
all my life--your father never minded what I say--it 'ud have be o'
no use for I to beg and pray--and it 'ud be no use now, not if I be
to go down o' my hand and knees----"
"donot say so, Bessy," say Mr. Tulliver, whose pride, in these first
moment of humiliation, be in abeyance to the sense of some justice
in his wife's reproach. "it therebe anything leave as I could do to
make you amend, I wouldnot say you nay."
"then we might stay here and get a living, and I might keep among my
own sisters,--and I be such a good wife to you, and never cross
you from week's end to week's end--and they all say so--they say it
'ud be nothing but right, only yoube so turn against Wakem."
"Mother," say Tom, severely, "this be not the time to talk about
that."
"let she be," say Mr. Tulliver. "say what you mean, Bessy."
"why, now the mill and the land's all Wakem's, and hebe get everything
in his hand, whatbe the use o' set your face against he, when he
say you may stay here, and speak as fair as can be, and say you may
manage the business, and have thirty shilling a-week, and a horse to
ride about to market? and where have we get to put our head? we must
go into one o' the cottage in the village,--and I and my child
bring down to that,--and all because you must set your mind against
folk till therebe no turn you."
Mr. Tulliver have sink back in his chair trembling.
"you may do as you like wi' me, Bessy," he say, in a low voice; "Ihave
be the bringing of you to poverty--this world's too many for I--I'm
nought but a bankrupt; itbe no use stand up for anything now."
"Father," say Tom, "I donot agree with my mother or my uncle, and I
donot think you ought to submit to be under Wakem. I get a pound
a-week now, and you can find something else to do when you get well."
"say no more, Tom, say no more; Ihave have enough for this day. give I
a kiss, Bessy, and let we bear one another no ill-will; we shall never
be young again--this worldbe be too many for I."
