An Item add to the Family Register
that first moment of renunciation and submission be follow by day
of violent struggle in the miller's mind, as the gradual access of
bodily strength bring with it increase ability to embrace in one
view all the conflict condition under which he find himself.
feeble limb easily resign themselves to be tether, and when we be
subdue by sickness it seem possible to we to fulfil pledge which
the old vigor come back and break. there be time when poor
Tulliver think the fulfilment of his promise to Bessy be something
quite too hard for human nature; he have promise she without know
what she be go to say,--she might as well have ask he to carry
a ton weight on his back. but again, there be many feeling argue
on her side, besides the sense that life have be make hard to she by
have marry he. he see a possibility, by much pinching, of save
money out of his salary toward pay a second dividend to his
creditor, and it would not be easy elsewhere to get a situation such
as he could fill.
he have lead an easy life, order much and work little, and have no
aptitude for any new business. he must perhaps take to day-labor, and
his wife must have help from her sisters,--a prospect doubly bitter to
he, now they have let all Bessy's precious thing be sell, probably
because they like to set she against he, by make she feel that he
have bring she to that pass. he listen to their admonitory talk,
when they come to urge on he what he be bind to do for poor Bessy's
sake, with avert eye, that every now and then flash on they
furtively when their back be turn. nothing but the dread of
need their help could have make it an easy alternative to take
their advice.
but the strong influence of all be the love of the old premise
where he have run about when he be a boy, just as Tom have do after
he. the Tullivers have live on this spot for generation, and he have
sit listen on a low stool on winter evening while his father
talk of the old half-timbere mill that have be there before the
last great flood which damage it so that his grandfather pull it
down and build the new one. it be when he get able to walk about and

affection for the old home as part of his life, part of himself. he
couldnot bear to think of himself live on any other spot than this,
where he know the sound of every gate door, and feel that the shape
and color of every roof and weather-stain and broken hillock be good,
because his grow sens have be feed on they. our instructed
vagrancy, which be hardly time to linger by the hedgerow, but run

theatre of its imagination to the Zambesi,--can hardly get a dim

where all his memory centre, and where life seem like a familiar
smooth-handle tool that the finger clutch with love ease. and just
now he be live in that freshen memory of the far-off time which
come to we in the passive hour of recovery from sickness.
"ay, Luke," he say one afternoon, as he stand look over the
orchard gate, "I remember the day they plant those apple-tree. my
father be a huge man for planting,--it be like a merry-making to he
to get a cart full o' young tree; and I use to stand I' the cold
with he, and follow he about like a dog."
then he turn round, and lean against the gate-post, look at the
opposite building.
"the old mill 'ud miss I, I think, Luke. therebe a story as when the
mill change hand, the river's angry; Ihave hear my father say it
many a time. therebe no telling whether there maynot be summat _in_
the story, for this be a puzzle world, and Old Harry's get a finger
in it--itbe be too many for I, I know."
"ay, sir," say Luke, with soothe sympathy, "what wi' the rust on
the wheat, an' the firin' o' the rick an' that, as Ihave see I' my
time,--thing often look comical; therebe the bacon fat wi' our last
pig run away like butter,--it leave nought but a scratchin'."
"itbe just as if it be yesterday, now," Mr. Tulliver go on, "when

plum-pudding that day and a bit of a feast, and I say to my
mother,--she be a fine dark-eyed woman, my mother was,--the little
wench 'ull be as like she as two pea." here Mr. Tulliver put his
stick between his leg, and take out his snuff-box, for the great
enjoyment of this anecdote, which drop from he in fragment, as if
he every other moment lose narration in vision. "I be a little chap
no higher much than my mother's knee,--she be sore fond of we
child, Gritty and me,--and so I say to she, 'mother,' I say,
'shall we have plum-pudding _every_ day because o' the malt-house? she
use to tell I o' that till her die day. she be but a young woman
when she die, my mother be. but itbe forty good year since they
finish the malt-house, and it benot many day out of them all as I
havenot look out into the yard there, the first thing in the
morning,--all weather, from year's end to year's end. I should go off
my head in a new place. I should be like as if Ihave lose my way. itbe
all hard, whichever way I look at it,--the harness 'ull gall I, but
it 'ud be summat to draw along the old road, instead of a new un."
"ay, sir," say Luke, "youwould be a deal well here nor in some new
place. I cannot abide new place mysen: thing be allays
awk'ard,--narrow-wheel waggin, belike, and the stile all another
sort, an' oat-cake I' some place, tow'rt th' head o' the Floss,
there. itbe poor work, change your country-side."
"but I doubt, Luke, theywill be for get rid o' Ben, and make you
do with a lad; and I must help a bit wi' the mill. youwill have a bad
place."
"ne'er mind, sir," say Luke, "I sha'n't plague mysen. I'n be wi'
you twenty year, an' you cannot get twenty year wi' whistlin' for them,
no more nor you can make the tree grow: you mun wait till God
A'mighty send them. I cannot abide new victual nor new face, _i_
can't,--you niver know but what theywill gripe you."
the walk be finish in silence after this, for Luke have disburthene
himself of thought to an extent that leave his conversational
resource quite barren, and Mr. Tulliver have relapse from his
recollection into a painful meditation on the choice of hardship
before he. Maggie notice that he be unusually absent that evening
at tea; and afterward he sit lean forward in his chair, look at
the ground, move his lip, and shake his head from time to time.
then he look hard at Mrs. Tulliver, who be knit opposite he,
then at Maggie, who, as she bent over her sewing, be intensely
conscious of some drama go forward in her father's mind. suddenly
he take up the poker and break the large coal fiercely.
"dear heart, Mr. Tulliver, what can you be think of?" say his
wife, look up in alarm; "itbe very wasteful, break the coal, and
wehave get hardly any large coal left, and I donot know where the rest
be to come from."
"I donot think yoube quite so well to-night, be you, father?" say

"why, how be it Tom donot come?" say Mr. Tulliver, impatiently.
"dear heart, be it time? I must go and get his supper," say Mrs.

"itbe nigh upon half-past eight," say Mr. Tulliver. "hewill be here
soon. go, go and get the big Bible, and open it at the beginning,
where everythingbe set down. and get the pen and ink."
Maggie obey, wonder; but her father give no further order, and
only sit listen for Tom's footfall on the gravel, apparently
irritate by the wind, which have rise, and be roar so as to drown
all other sound. there be a strange light in his eye that rather
frighten Maggie; _she_ begin to wish that Tom would come, too.
"there he be, then," say Mr. Tulliver, in an excited way, when the

Mrs. Tulliver have begin to be a little frightened at her boy, but she
be jealous of every office other do for he.
"your supperbe ready by the kitchen-fire, my boy," she say, as he
take off his hat and coat. "you shall have it by yourself, just as you
like, and I willnot speak to you."
"I think my father want Tom, mother," say Maggie; "he must come into
the parlor first."
Tom enter with his usual sadden evening face, but his eye fall
immediately on the open Bible and the inkstand, and he glance with a
look of anxious surprise at his father, who be saying,--
"come, come, yoube late; I want you."
"be there anything the matter, father?" say Tom.
"you sit down, all of you," say Mr. Tulliver, peremptorily.
"and, Tom, sit down here; Ihave get something for you to write I' the
Bible."
they all three sit down, look at he. he begin to speak slowly,
look first at his wife.
"Ihave make up my mind, Bessy, and Iwill be as good as my word to you.
therewill be the same grave make for we to lie down in, and we mustnot
be bear one another ill-will. Iwill stop in the old place, and Iwill
serve under Wakem, and Iwill serve he like an honest man; therebe no
Tulliver but whatbe honest, mind that, Tom,"--here his voice
rose,--"they'll have it to throw up against I as I pay a dividend,
but it benot my fault; it be because therebe raskill in the world.
theyhave be too many for I, and I must give in. Iwill put my neck in
harness,--for youhave a right to say as Ihave bring you into trouble,
Bessy,--and Iwill serve he as honest as if he be no raskill; Ibe an
honest man, though I shall never hold my head up no more. Ibe a tree
as be break--a tree as be break."
he pause and look on the ground. then suddenly raise his head, he
say, in a loud yet deep tone:
"but I willnot forgive he! I know what they say, he never mean I any
harm. thatbe the way Old Harry prop up the rascal. hebe be at the
bottom of everything; but hebe a fine gentleman,--I know, I know. I
shouldnot ha' go to law, they say. but who make it so as there be
no arbitratin', and no justice to be get? it signify nothing to he,
I know that; hebe one o' they fine gentleman as get money by do
business for poor folk, and when hebe make beggar of them hewill
give them charity. I willnot forgive he! I wish he might be punish
with shame till his own son 'ud like to forget he. I wish he may do
summat as theywould make he work at the treadmill! but he won't,--he's
too big a raskill to let the law lay hold on he. and you mind this,
tom,--you never forgive he neither, if you mean to be my son.
therewill maybe come a time when you may make he feel; itwill never
come to I; I'n get my head under the yoke. now write--write it I' the
Bible."
"oh, father, what?" say Maggie, sink down by his knee, pale and
trembling. "itbe wicked to curse and bear malice."
"it benot wicked, I tell you," say her father, fiercely. "itbe wicked
as the raskill should prosper; itbe the Devil's doing. do as I tell
you, Tom. Write."
"what be I to write?" say Tom, with gloomy submission.
"write as your father, Edward Tulliver, take service under John Wakem,
the man as have help to ruin he, because Iwould promise my wife to
make she what amend I could for her trouble, and because I want to
die in th' old place where I be bear and my father be bear. put that
I' the right word--you know how--and then write, as I donot forgive
Wakem for all that; and for all Iwill serve he honest, I wish evil may
befall he. write that."
there be a dead silence as Tom's pen move along the paper; Mrs.
Tulliver look scared, and Maggie tremble like a leaf.
"now let I hear what youhave write," say Mr. Tulliver, Tom read aloud
slowly.
"now write--write as youwill remember what Wakembe do to your father,
and youwill make he and his feel it, if ever the day come. and sign
your name Thomas Tulliver."
"oh no, father, dear father!" say Maggie, almost choke with fear.
"you shouldnot make Tom write that."
"be quiet, Maggie!" say Tom. "I _shall_ write it."
Book IV
_the Valley of Humiliation_
chapter I
a Variation of Protestantism Unknown to Bossuet
Journeying down the Rhone on a summer's day, you have perhaps feel the
sunshine make dreary by those ruin village which stud the bank in
certain part of its course, tell how the swift river once rise,
like an angry, destroy god, sweep down the feeble generation
whose breath be in their nostril, and make their dwelling a
desolation. strange contrast, you may have think, between the
effect produce on we by these dismal remnant of commonplace
house, which in their good day be but the sign of a sordid life,
belong in all its detail to our own vulgar era, and the effect
produce by those ruin on the castle Rhine, which have crumble and
mellow into such harmony with the green and rocky steep that they
seem to have a natural fitness, like the mountain-pine; nay, even in
the day when they be build they must have have this fitness, as if
they have be raise by an earth-bear race, who have inherit from
their mighty parent a sublime instinct of form. and that be a day of
romance; if those robber-baron be somewhat grim and drunken ogre,
they have a certain grandeur of the wild beast in them,--they be
forest boar with tusk, tear and rending, not the ordinary
domestic grunter; they represent the demon force forever in
collision with beauty, virtue, and the gentle us of life; they make
a fine contrast in the picture with the wandering minstrel, the
soft-lipped princess, the pious recluse, and the timid Israelite. that
be a time of color, when the sunlight fall on glance steel and
float banner; a time of adventure and fierce struggle,--nay, of
living, religious art and religious enthusiasm; for be not
cathedral build in those day, and do not great emperor leave their
western palace to die before the infidel stronghold in the sacred
East? therefore it be that these rhine castle thrill I with a sense
of poetry; they belong to the grand historic life of humanity, and
raise up for I the vision of an echo. but these dead-tint,
hollow-eye, angular skeleton of village on the Rhone oppress I
with the feeling that human life--very much of it--be a narrow, ugly,
grovel existence, which even calamity do not elevate, but rather
tend to exhibit in all its bare vulgarity of conception; and I have a
cruel conviction that the life these ruin be the trace of be
part of a gross sum of obscure vitality, that will be sweep into the
same oblivion with the generation of ant and beaver.
perhaps something akin to this oppressive feeling may have weigh
upon you in watch this old-fashioned family life on the bank of
the Floss, which even sorrow hardly suffice to lift above the level
of the tragi-comic. it be a sordid life, you say, this of the
Tullivers and dodson, irradiate by no sublime principle, no
romantic vision, no active, self-renounce faith; move by none of
those wild, uncontrollable passion which create the dark shadow of
misery and crime; without that primitive, rough simplicity of want,
that hard, submissive, ill-pay toil, that childlike spelling-out of
what nature have write, which give its poetry to peasant life. here
one have conventional worldly notion and habit without instruction
and without polish, surely the most prosaic form of human life; proud
respectability in a gig of unfashionable build; worldliness without
side-dish. observe these people narrowly, even when the iron hand
of misfortune have shake they from their unquestione hold on the
world, one see little trace of religion, still less of a
distinctively christian creed. their belief in the Unseen, so far as
it manifest itself at all, seem to be rather a pagan kind; their
moral notion, though hold with strong tenacity, seem to have no
standard beyond hereditary custom. you could not live among such
people; you be stifle for want of an outlet toward something
beautiful, great, or noble; you be irritated with these dull man and
woman, as a kind of population out of keep with the earth on which
they live,--with this rich plain where the great river flow forever
onward, and link the small pulse of the old english town with the
beating of the world's mighty heart. a vigorous superstition, that
lash its god or lash its own back, seem to be more congruous
with the mystery of the human lot, than the mental condition of these
emmet-like Dodsons and Tullivers.
I share with you this sense of oppressive narrowness; but it be
necessary that we should feel it, if we care to understand how it
act on the life of Tom and Maggie,--how it have act on young
nature in many generation, that in the onward tendency of human
thing have rise above the mental level of the generation before
they, to which they have be nevertheless tie by the strong
fibre of their heart. the suffering, whether of martyr or victim,
which belong to every historical advance of mankind, be represent
in this way in every town, and by hundred of obscure hearth; and we
need not shrink from this comparison of small thing with great; for
do not science tell we that its high strive be after the
ascertainment of a unity which shall bind the small thing with the
great? in natural science, I have understand, there be nothing
petty to the mind that have a large vision of relation, and to which
every single object suggest a vast sum of condition. it be surely
the same with the observation of human life.
certainly the religious and moral idea of the Dodsons and Tullivers
be of too specific a kind to be arrive at deductively, from the
statement that they be part of the protestant population of Great
Britain. their theory of life have its core of soundness, as all
theory must have on which decent and prosperous family have be
rear and have flourish; but it have the very slight tincture of
theology. if, in the maiden day of the Dodson sister, their bible
open more easily at some part than other, it be because of dried
tulip-petal, which have be distribute quite impartially, without
preference for the historical, devotional, or doctrinal. their
religion be of a simple, semi-pagan kind, but there be no heresy in
it,--if heresy properly mean choice,--for they donot know there be
any other religion, except that of chapel-goer, which appear to run
in family, like asthma. how _should_ they know? the vicar of their
pleasant rural parish be not a controversialist, but a good hand at
whist, and one who have a joke always ready for a bloom female
parishioner. the religion of the dodson consist in revering
whatever be customary and respectable; it be necessary to be
baptize, else one could not be bury in the church-yard, and to take
the sacrament before death, as a security against more dimly
understand peril; but it be of equal necessity to have the proper
pall-bearer and well-cure ham at one's funeral, and to leave an
unimpeachable will. a Dodson would not be tax with the omission of
anything that be become, or that belong to that eternal fitness
of thing which be plainly indicate in the practice of the most
substantial parishioner, and in the family traditions,--such as
obedience to parent, faithfulness to kindre, industry, rigid
honesty, thrift, the thorough scouring of wooden and copper utensil,
the hoarding of coin likely to disappear from the currency, the
production of first-rate commodity for the market, and the general
preference of whatever be home-make. the Dodsons be a very proud
race, and their pride lie in the utter frustration of all desire to
tax they with a breach of traditional duty or propriety. a wholesome
pride in many respect, since it identify honor with perfect
integrity, thoroughness of work, and faithfulness to admit rule;
and society owe some worthy quality in many of her member to
mother of the Dodson class, who make their butter and their fromenty
well, and would have feel disgrace to make it otherwise. to be honest
and poor be never a Dodson motto, still less to seem rich though
be poor; rather, the family badge be to be honest and rich, and
not only rich, but rich than be suppose. to live respect, and
have the proper bearer at your funeral, be an achievement of the
end of existence that would be entirely nullify if, on the reading
of your will, you sink in the opinion of your fellow-man, either by
turn out to be poor than they expect, or by leave your money
in a capricious manner, without strict regard to degree of kin. the
right thing must always be do toward kindre. the right thing be to
correct they severely, if they be other than a credit to the family,
but still not to alienate from they the small rightful share in the
family shoebuckle and other property. a conspicuous quality in the
Dodson character be its genuineness; its vice and virtue alike be
phase of a proud honest egoism, which have a hearty dislike to
whatever make against its own credit and interest, and would be
frankly hard of speech to inconvenient "kin," but would never forsake
or ignore them,--would not let they want bread, but only require they
to eat it with bitter herb.
the same sort of traditional belief run in the Tulliver vein, but it
be carry in rich blood, have element of generous imprudence,
warm affection, and hot-temper rashness. Mr. Tulliver's grandfather
have be hear to say that he be descend from one Ralph Tulliver, a
wonderfully clever fellow, who have ruin himself. it be likely enough
that the clever Ralph be a high liver, ride spirited horse, and be
very decidedly of his own opinion. on the other hand, nobody have ever
hear of a Dodson who have ruin himself; it be not the way of that
family.
if such be the view of life on which the Dodsons and Tullivers have
be rear in the praiseworthy past of Pitt and high price, you will
infer from what you already know concern the state of society in
St. Ogg's, that there have be no highly modifying influence to act on
they in their mature life. it be still possible, even in that later
time of anti-catholic preaching, for people to hold many pagan idea,
and believe themselves good church-people, notwithstanding; so we need
hardly feel any surprise at the fact that Mr. Tulliver, though a
regular church-goer, record his vindictiveness on the fly-leaf of
his Bible. it be not that any harm could be say concern the vicar
of that charming rural parish to which Dorlcote Mill belong; he be
a man of excellent family, an irreproachable bachelor, of elegant
pursuits,--had take honor, and hold a fellowship. Mr. Tulliver
regard he with dutiful respect, as he do everything else belong
to the church-service; but he consider that church be one thing and
common-sense another, and he want nobody to tell _he_ what
commonsense be. certain seed which be require to find a nidus for
themselves under unfavorable circumstance have be supply by
nature with an apparatus of hook, so that they will get a hold on
very unreceptive surface. the spiritual seed which have be scatter
over Mr. Tulliver have apparently be destitute of any corresponding
provision, and have slip off to the wind again, from a total
absence of hook.
Chapter II
the Torn Nest be pierce by the Thorns
there be something sustain in the very agitation that accompany
the first shock of trouble, just as an acute pain be often a
stimulus, and produce an excitement which be transient strength. it
be in the slow, change life that follow; in the time when sorrow have
become stale, and have no long an emotive intensity that counteract
its pain; in the time when day follow day in dull, unexpectant
sameness, and trial be a dreary routine,--it be then that despair
threaten; it be then that the peremptory hunger of the soul be feel,
and eye and ear be strain after some unlearned secret of our
existence, which shall give to endurance the nature of satisfaction.
this time of utmost need be come to Maggie, with her short span of
thirteen year. to the usual precocity of the girl, she add that
early experience of struggle, of conflict between the inward impulse
and outward fact, which be the lot of every imaginative and passionate
nature; and the year since she hammer the nail into her wooden
Fetish among the worm-eat shelf of the attic have be fill with
so eager a life in the triple world of reality, Books, and Waking
Dreams, that Maggie be strangely old for her year in everything
except in her entire want of that prudence and self-command which be
the quality that make Tom manly in the midst of his intellectual

monotony, which throw she more than ever on her inward self. her
father be able to attend to business again, his affair be settle,
and he be act as Wakem's manager on the old spot. Tom go to and
fro every morning and evening, and become more and more silent in the
short interval at home; what be there to say? one day be like
another; and Tom's interest in life, drive back and crush on every
other side, be concentrate itself into the one channel of ambitious
resistance to misfortune. the peculiarity of his father and mother
be very irksome to he, now they be lay bare of all the soften
accompaniment of an easy, prosperous home; for Tom have very clear,
prosaic eye, not apt to be dim by mist of feeling or imagination.
Poor Mrs. Tulliver, it seem, would never recover her old self, her
placid household activity; how could she? the object among which she
mind have move complacently be all gone,--all the little hope and

to she which have not happen to other woman remain an insoluble
question by which she express her perpetual ruminate comparison of
the past with the present. it be piteous to see the comely woman
get thin and more worn under a bodily as well as mental
restlessness, which make she often wander about the empty house after

seek she, and bring she down by tell she how it vex Tom that she
be injure her health by never sit down and rest herself. yet
amidst this helpless imbecility there be a touching trait of humble,
self-devote maternity, which make Maggie feel tenderly toward her
poor mother amidst all the little wear grief cause by her mental
feebleness. she would let Maggie do none of the work that be heavy
and most soil to the hand, and be quite peevish when Maggie
attempt to relieve she from her grate-brushing and scour: "let it
alone, my dear; your hand 'ull get as hard as hard," she would say;
"itbe your mother's place to do that. I cannot do the sewing--my eye
fail I." and she would still brush and carefully tend Maggie's hair,
which she have become reconcile to, in spite of its refusal to curl,
now it be so long and massy. Maggie be not her pet child, and, in
general, would have be much well if she have be quite different;
yet the womanly heart, so bruised in its small personal desire, find
a future to rest on in the life of this young thing, and the mother
please herself with wear out her own hand to save the hand that
have so much more life in they.
but the constant presence of her mother's regretful bewilderment be
less painful to Maggie than that of her father's sullen,
incommunicative depression. as long as the paralysis be upon he, and
it seem as if he might always be in a childlike condition of
dependence,--as long as he be still only half awaken to his
trouble,--maggie have feel the strong tide of pity love almost as an
inspiration, a new power, that would make the most difficult life easy
for his sake; but now, instead of childlike dependence, there have come
a taciturn, hard concentration of purpose, in strange contrast with
his old vehement communicativeness and high spirit; and this last
from day to day, and from week to week, the dull eye never brighten
with any eagerness or any joy. it be something cruelly incomprehensible
to youthful nature, this sombre sameness in middle-aged and elderly
people, whose life have result in disappointment and discontent, to
whose face a smile become so strange that the sad line all about
the lip and brow seem to take no notice of it, and it hurry away
again for want of a welcome. "why will they not kindle up and be
glad sometimes?" think young elasticity. "it would be so easy if they
only like to do it." and these leaden cloud that never part be apt
to create impatience even in the filial affection that stream forth in
nothing but tenderness and pity in the time of more obvious affliction.
Mr. Tulliver linger nowhere away from home; he hurry away from
market, he refuse all invitation to stay and chat, as in old time,
in the house where he call on business. he could not be reconcile
with his lot. there be no attitude in which his pride do not feel
its bruise; and in all behavior toward he, whether kind or cold, he
detect an allusion to the change in his circumstance. even the day
on which Wakem come to ride round the land and inquire into the

meet several creditor who have accept a composition from he. to save
something toward the repayment of those creditor be the object
toward which he be now bend all his thought and effort; and
under the influence of this all-compelling demand of his nature, the
somewhat profuse man, who hat to be stint or to stint any one else
in his own house, be gradually metamorphose into the keen-eye

satisfy he, in their food and firing; and he would eat nothing

of home, enter thoroughly into his father's feeling about pay
the creditor; and the poor lad bring his first quarter's money,
with a delicious sense of achievement, and give it to his father to
put into the tin box which hold the saving. the little store of
sovereign in the tin box seem to be the only sight that bring a
faint beam of pleasure into the miller's eyes,--faint and transient,
for it be soon dispel by the thought that the time would be
long--perhaps long than his life,--before the narrow saving could
remove the hateful incubus of debt. a deficit of more than five
hundred pound, with the accumulate interest, seem a deep pit to
fill with the saving from thirty shilling a-week, even when Tom's
probable saving be to be add. on this one point there be entire
community of feeling in the four widely differ being who sit round
the die fire of stick, which make a cheap warmth for they on the

Dodsons in her blood, and have be bring up to think that to wrong
people of their money, which be another phrase for debt, be a sort
of moral pillory; it would have be wickedness, to her mind, to have
run counter to her husband's desire to "do the right thing," and
retrieve his name. she have a confused, dreamy notion that, if the
creditor be all pay, her plate and linen ought to come back to
she; but she have an inbreed perception that while people owe money
they be unable to pay, they couldnot rightly call anything their
own. she murmur a little that Mr. Tulliver so peremptorily refuse
to receive anything in repayment from Mr. and Mrs. Moss; but to all
his requirement of household economy she be submissive to the point
of deny herself the cheap indulgence of mere flavor; her only
rebellion be to smuggle into the kitchen something that would make
rather a well supper than usual for Tom.
these narrow notion about debt, hold by the old fashioned Tullivers,
may perhaps excite a smile on the face of many reader in these day
of wide commercial view and wide philosophy, accord to which
everything right itself without any trouble of our. the fact that my
tradesman be out of pocket by I be to be look at through the serene
certainty that somebody else's tradesman be in pocket by somebody
else; and since there must be bad debt in the world, why, it be mere
egoism not to like that we in particular should make they instead of
our fellow-citizen. I be tell the history of very simple people,
who have never have any illuminate doubt as to personal integrity and
honor.
under all this grim melancholy and narrowing concentration of desire,
Mr. Tulliver retain the feeling toward his "little wench" which make
her presence a need to he, though it would not suffice to cheer he.
she be still the desire of his eye; but the sweet spring of fatherly
love be now mingle with bitterness, like everything else. when
Maggie lay down her work at night, it be her habit to get a low

she wish he would stroke her head, or give some sign that he be
soothe by the sense that he have a daughter who love he! but now she
get no answer to her little caress, either from her father or from

preoccupy with the thought that the girl be grow up, be
shoot up into a woman; and how be she to do well in life? she have
a poor chance for marry, down in the world as they be. and he
hat the thought of she marry poorly, as her aunt Gritty have do;
_that_ would be a thing to make he turn in his grave,--the little
wench so pull down by child and toil, as her aunt Moss be. when
uncultured mind, confine to a narrow range of personal experience,
be under the pressure of continued misfortune, their inward life be
apt to become a perpetually repeat round of sad and bitter thought;
the same word, the same scene, be revolve over and over again, the
same mood accompany they; the end of the year find they as much
what they be at the beginning as if they be machine set to a
recurrent series of movement.
the sameness of the day be break by few visitor. uncle and aunt
pay only short visit now; of course, they could not stay to meal,
and the constraint cause by Mr. Tulliver's savage silence, which
seem to add to the hollow resonance of the bare, uncarpeted room
when the aunt be talk, heighten the unpleasantness of these

subject on which to converse with they. at that distant day, there
be a dreary isolation in the civilized christian society of these
realm for family that have drop below their original level,
unless they belong to a sectarian church, which get some warmth of

Chapter III
A Voice from the Past
one afternoon, when the chestnut be come into flower, Maggie have
bring her chair outside the front door, and be seat there with a
book on her knee. her dark eye have wander from the book, but they
do not seem to be enjoy the sunshine which pierce the screen of
jasmine on the project porch at her right, and throw leafy shadow
on her pale round cheek; they seem rather to be search for
something that be not disclose by the sunshine. it have be a more
miserable day than usual; her father, after a visit of Wakem's have have
a paroxysm of rage, in which for some trifling fault he have beat the
boy who serve in the mill. once before, since his illness, he have have
a similar paroxysm, in which he have beat his horse, and the scene
have leave a last terror in Maggie's mind. the thought have rise,
that some time or other he might beat her mother if she happen to
speak in her feeble way at the wrong moment. the keenest of all dread
with she be lest her father should add to his present misfortune the
wretchedness of do something irretrievably disgraceful. the
batter school-book of Tom's which she hold on her knee could give
she no fortitude under the pressure of that dread; and again and again
her eye have fill with tear, as they wander vaguely, see
neither the chestnut-tree, nor the distant horizon, but only future
scene of home-sorrow.
suddenly she be rouse by the sound of the opening gate and of
footstep on the gravel. it be not Tom who be enter, but a man in
a sealskin cap and a blue plush waistcoat, carry a pack on his
back, and follow closely by a bullterrier of brindled coat and
defiant aspect.
"oh, Bob, itbe you!" say Maggie, start up with a smile of pleased
recognition, for there have be no abundance of kind act to efface
the recollection of Bob's generosity; "Ibe so glad to see you."
"thank you, Miss," say Bob, lift his cap and show a delighted
face, but immediately relieve himself of some accompany
embarrassment by look down at his dog, and say in a tone of
disgust, "get out wi' you, you thunderin' sawney!"
"my brother be not at home yet, Bob," say Maggie; "he be always at
St. Oggbe in the daytime."
"well, Miss," say Bob, "I should be glad to see Mr. Tom, but that
benot just what Ibe come for,--look here!"
Bob be in the act of deposit his pack on the door-step, and with
it a row of small book fasten together with string.
apparently, however, they be not the object to which he wish to
call Maggie's attention, but rather something which he have carry
under his arm, wrap in a red handkerchief.
"see here!" he say again, lay the red parcel on the other and
unfold it; "you willnot think Ibe a-makin' too free, Miss, I hope,
but I light on these book, and I think they might make up to you
a bit for they as youhave lose; for I hear you speak o' picturs,--an'
as for pictur, _look_ here!"
the opening of the red handkerchief have disclose a superannuated
"Keepsake" and six or seven number of a "Portrait Gallery," in royal
octavo; and the emphatic request to look refer to a portrait of
George the Fourth in all the majesty of his depressed cranium and
voluminous neckcloth.
"therebe all sort o' genelman here," Bob go on, turn over the
leaf with some excitement, "wi' all sort o' nones,--an' some bald
an' some wi' wigs,--parlament genelman, I reckon. an' here," he add,
open the "Keepsake,"--"_here's_ lady for you, some wi' curly hair
and some wi' smooth, an' some a-smile wi' their head o' one side,
an' some as if they be go to cry,--look here,--a-sittin' on the
ground out o' door, dress like the lady i'n see get out o' the
carriage at the ball in th' Old Hall there. my eye! I wonder what
the chap wear as go a-courtin' them! I sot up till the clock be go
twelve last night, a-lookin' at 'em,--i did,--till they star at I
out o' the pictur as if theywould know when I speak to them. but, lor! I
shouldnot know what to say to them. theywill be more fittin' company for
you, Miss; and the man at the book-stall, he say they bang
iverything for pictur; he say they be a fust-rate article."
"and youhave buy they for I, Bob?" say Maggie, deeply touch by
this simple kindness. "how very, very good of you! but Ibe afraid you
give a great deal of money for they."
"not I!" say Bob. "Iwould ha' gev three time the money if theywill make
up to you a bit for they as be sell away from you, Miss. for I'n
niver forget how you look when you fret about the book bein'
go; itbe stick by I as if it be a pictur hingin' before I. an'
when I see'd the book open upo' the stall, wi' the lady lookin' out of
it wi' eye a bit like your'n when you be frettin',--you'll excuse my
takin' the liberty, Miss,--I think Iwould make free to buy it for you,
an' then I buy the book full o' genelman to match; an' then"--here
Bob take up the small string packet of books--"i think you might
like a bit more print as well as the pictur, an' I get these for a
sayso,--they're cram-full o' print, an' I think theywould do no harm
comin' along wi' these bettermost book. an' I hope you willnot say I
nay, an' tell I as you willnot have them, like Mr. Tom do wi' the
suvreign."
"no, indeed, Bob," say Maggie, "Ibe very thankful to you for thinking
of I, and be so good to I and Tom. I donot think any one ever do
such a kind thing for I before. I havenot many friend who care for
I."
"Hev a dog, miss!--they're well friend nor any Christian," say
Bob, lay down his pack again, which he have take up with the
intention of hurry away; for he feel considerable shyness in
talk to a young lass like Maggie, though, as he usually say of
himself, "his tongue overrun he" when he begin to speak. "I cannot
give you Mumps, 'cause hewould break his heart to go away from I--eh,
Mumps, what do you say, you riff-raff?" (Mumps decline to express
himself more diffusely than by a single affirmative movement of his
tail.) "but Iwould get you a pup, Miss, an' welcome."
"no, thank you, Bob. we have a yard dog, and I maynot keep a dog of my
own."
"eh, thatbe a pity; else therebe a pup,--if you donot mind about it
not be thoroughbre; its mother act in the Punch show,--an
uncommon sensible bitch; she mean more sense wi' her bark nor half
the chap can put into their talk from breakfast to sundown. therebe
one chap carry pots,--a poor, low trade as any on the road,--he
say, 'why Toby's nought but a mongrel; therebe nought to look at in
she.' but I say to he, 'why, what be you yoursen but a mongrel?
there benot much pickin' o' _your_ feyther an' mother, to look at
you.' not but I like a bit o' breed myself, but I cannot abide to see
one cur grinnin' at another. I wish you good evenin', Miss," say Bob,
abruptly take up his pack again, under the consciousness that his
tongue be act in an undisciplined manner.
"willnot you come in the evening some time, and see my brother, Bob?"
say Maggie.
"yes, Miss, thank you--another time. youwill give my duty to he, if
you please. eh, hebe a fine grow chap, Mr. Tom be; he take to
growin' I' the leg, an' _i_ donot."
the pack be down again, now, the hook of the stick have somehow
go wrong.
"you donot call Mumps a cur, I suppose?" say Maggie, divine that
any interest she show in Mumps would be gratify to his master.
"no, Miss, a fine way off that," say Bob, with pity smile; "mump
be as fine a cross as youwill see anywhere along the Floss, an' i'n
be up it wi' the barge times enow. why, the gentry stop to look at
he; but you willnot catch Mumps a-look at the gentry much,--he minds
his own business, he do."
the expression of Mump's face, which seem to be tolerate the
superfluous existence of object in general, be strongly confirmatory
of this high praise.
"he look dreadfully surly," say Maggie. "would he let I pat he?"
"ay, that would he, and thank you. he know his company, Mumps do.
he benot a dog as 'ull be catch wi' gingerbread; hewould smell a thief a
good deal strong nor the gingerbread, he would. lor, I talk to he
by th' hour together, when Ibe walk I' lone place, and if i'n do
a bit o' mischief, I allay tell he. I'n get no secret but what
Mumps know them. he know about my big thumb, he do."
"your big thumb--whatbe that, Bob?" say Maggie.
"thatbe what it be, Miss," say Bob, quickly, exhibit a singularly
broad speciman of that difference between the man and the monkey. "it
tell I' measure out the flannel, you see. I carry flannel, 'cause
itbe light for my pack, an' itbe dear stuff, you see, so a big thumb
tell. I clap my thumb at the end o' the yard and cut o' the hither
side of it, and the old woman benot up to't."
"but Bob," say Maggie, look serious, "thatbe cheat; I donot
like to hear you say that."
"donot you, Miss?" say Bob regretfully. "then Ibe sorry I say it.
but Ibe so used to talk to Mumps, an' he donot mind a bit o'
cheating, when itbe they skinflint woman, as haggle an' haggle, an'
'ud like to get their flannel for nothing, an' 'ud niver ask
theirselve how I get my dinner out on't. I niver cheat anybody as
donot want to cheat I, miss,--lor, Ibe a honest chap, I be; only I
must hev a bit o' sport, an' now I donot go wi' th' ferret, I'n get
no varmint to come over but they haggle woman. I wish you good
evening, Miss."
"good-by, Bob. thank you very much for bring I the book. and come
again to see Tom."
"yes, Miss," say Bob, move on a few step; then turn half round
he say, "Iwill leave off that trick wi' my big thumb, if you donot
think well on I for it, Miss; but it 'ud be a pity, it would. I
couldnot find another trick so good,--an' what 'ud be the use o'
havin' a big thumb? it might as well ha' be narrow."
Maggie, thus exalt into Bob's exalt Madonna, laugh in spite of
herself; at which her worshipper's blue eye twinkle too, and under
these favor auspex he touch his cap and walk away.
the day of chivalry be not go, notwithstanding Burke's grand dirge
over they; they live still in that far-off worship pay by many a
youth and man to the woman of whom he never dream that he shall touch
so much as her little finger or the hem of her robe. Bob, with the
pack on his back, have as respectful an adoration for this dark-eye

as he prick on to the fight.
that gleam of merriment soon die away from Maggie's face, and perhaps
only make the return gloom deep by contrast. she be too
dispirit even to like answer question about Bob's present of
book, and she carry they away to her bedroom, lay they down
there and seat herself on her one stool, without care to look at

think that the light-hearted Bob have a lot much happy than her.
Maggie's sense of loneliness, and utter privation of joy, have deepen
with the brightness of advance spring. all the favorite outdoor
nook about home, which seem to have do their part with her
parent in nurture and cherish she, be now mix up with the

charm of school-emulation--Telemaque be mere bran; so be the hard,

great man know," she think she should have hold the secret of life;
if she have only book, that she might learn for herself what wise man

have be well thumbed,--the Latin Dictionary and Grammar, a Delectus,

and feel a gleam of triumph now and then that her understanding be

relation between Aldrich and this live world be extremely remote
for she. the discouragement deepen as the day go on, and the
eager heart gain fast and fast on the patient mind. somehow,
when she sit at the window with her book, her eye _would_ fix
themselves blankly on the outdoor sunshine; then they would fill with
tear, and sometimes, if her mother be not in the room, the study
would all end in sobbing. she rebel against her lot, she faint
under its loneliness, and fit even of anger and hatred toward her
father and mother, who be so unlike what she would have they to be;

some thwarting difference,--would flow out over her affection and

be not difficult for she to become a demon. then her brain would be
busy with wild romance of a flight from home in search of something
less sordid and dreary; she would go to some great man--Walter Scott,
perhaps--and tell he how wretched and how clever she be, and he
would surely do something for she. but, in the middle of her vision,
her father would perhaps enter the room for the evening, and,
surprise that she sit still without notice he, would say
complainingly, "come, be I to fetch my slipper myself?" the voice

her own, and she have be think of turn she back on it and
forsake it.
this afternoon, the sight of Bob's cheerful freckle face have give

at last Maggie's eye glance down on the book that lie on the

thyself doth hurt thee more than anything in the world.... if thou
seek this or that, and wouldst be here or there to enjoy thy own
will and pleasure, thou shalt never be quiet nor free from care; for
in everything somewhat will be want, and in every place there will
be some that will cross thee.... both above and below, which way
soever thou dost turn thee, everywhere thou shalt find the Cross; and
everywhere of necessity thou must have patience, if thou wilt have
inward peace, and enjoy an everlasting crown.... if thou desir to

inordinate inclination to thyself, and unto all private and earthly

dependeth, whatsoever be thoroughly to be overcome; which evil being
once overcome and subdue, there will presently ensue great peace and
tranquillity.... it be but little thou sufferest in comparison of they
that have suffer so much, be so strongly tempt, so grievously
afflict, so many way try and exercise. Thou ought therefore
to call to mind the more heavy suffering of other, that thou mayest
the easy bear thy little adversity. and if they seem not little
unto thee, beware lest thy impatience be the cause thereof.... bless
be those ear that receive the whisper of the divine voice, and
listen not to the whispering of the world. blessed be those ear
which hearken not unto the voice which soundeth outwardly, but unto
the Truth, which teacheth inwardly."
a strange thrill of awe pass through Maggie while she read, as if

of being whose soul have be astir while hers be in stupor. she

while a low voice say;
"why dost thou here gaze about, since this be not the place of thy

to be look on as they forward thy journey thither. all thing pass

lest thou be entangle and perish.... if a man should give all his
substance, yet it be as nothing. and if he should do great penance,
yet be they but little. and if he should attain to all knowledge, he
be yet far off. and if he should be of great virtue, and very fervent

necessary for he. what be that? that have leave all, he leave
himself, and go wholly out of himself, and retain nothing of
self-love.... I have often say unto thee, and now again I say the
same, Forsake thyself, resign thyself, and thou shalt enjoy much
inward peace.... then shall all vain imagination, evil perturbation,
and superfluous care fly away; then shall immoderate fear leave thee,
and inordinate love shall die."
Maggie draw a long breath and push her heavy hair back, as if to see
a sudden vision more clearly. here, then, be a secret of life that
would enable she to renounce all other secret; here be a sublime

it flash through she like the suddenly apprehend solution of a

renunciation remain sorrow, though a sorrow bear willingly. Maggie

be the direct communication of a human soul's belief and experience,
and come to Maggie as an unquestioned message.
I suppose that be the reason why the small old-fashioned book, for
which you need only pay sixpence at a book-stall, work miracle to
this day, turn bitter water into sweetness; while expensive
sermon and treatise, newly issue, leave all thing as they be
before. it be write down by a hand that wait for the heart's
prompt; it be the chronicle of a solitary, hidden anguish,
struggle, trust, and triumph, not write on velvet cushion to teach
endurance to those who be treading with bleed foot on the stone.
and so it remain to all time a lasting record of human need and
human consolation; the voice of a brother who, age ago, feel and
suffer and renounced,--in the cloister, perhaps, with serge gown and
tonsure head, with much chant and long fast, and with a fashion
of speech different from ours,--but under the same silent far-off
heaven, and with the same passionate desire, the same striving, the
same failure, the same weariness.
in write the history of unfashionable family, one be apt to fall
into a tone of emphasis which be very far from be the tone of good
society, where principle and belief be not only of an extremely
moderate kind, but be always presuppose, no subject be eligible
but such as can be touch with a light and graceful irony. but then
good society have its claret and its velvet carpet, its
dinner-engagement six week deep, its opera and its faery ball-room;
rid off its _ennui_ on thoroughbred horse; lounge at the club; have
to keep clear of crinoline vortex; get its science do by Faraday,
and its religion by the superior clergy who be to be meet in the good
houses,--how should it have time or need for belief and emphasis? but
good society, float on gossamer wing of light irony, be of very
expensive production; require nothing less than a wide and arduous
national life condense in unfragrant deafen factory, cramp
itself in mine, sweat at furnace, grind, hammer, weave
under more or less oppression of carbonic acid, or else, spread over
sheepwalk, and scatter in lonely house and hut on the clayey or
chalky corn-land, where the rainy day look dreary. this wide
national life be base entirely on emphasis,--the emphasis of want,
which urge it into all the activity necessary for the maintenance
of good society and light irony; it spend its heavy year often in a
chill, uncarpeted fashion, amidst family discord unsoftene by long
corridor. under such circumstance, there be many among its myriad
of soul who have absolutely need an emphatic belief, life in this
unpleasurable shape demand some solution even to unspeculative
minds,--just as you inquire into the stuffing of your couch when
anything gall you there, whereas eider-down and perfect french
spring excite no question. some have an emphatic belief in alcohol,
and seek their _ekstasis_ or outside standing-ground in gin; but the
rest require something that good society call "enthusiasm," something
that will present motif in an entire absence of high prize;
something that will give patience and feed human love when the limb
ache with weariness, and human look be hard upon we; something,
clearly, that lie outside personal desire, that include resignation

and so it come to pass that she often lose the spirit of humility by

long night-watchings,--to she who have always love he so; and then
she strove to be content with that hardness, and to require nothing.
that be the path we all like when we set out on our abandonment of
egoism,--the path of martyrdom and endurance, where the palm-branch
grow, rather than the steep highway of tolerance, just allowance, and
self-blame, where there be no leafy honor to be gather and wear.
the old book, Virgil, Euclid, and Aldrich--that wrinkle fruit of the

no mean plain to Maggie, since wristband and sleeve and the like have

hang diligently over her sewing, Maggie be a sight any one might

"let your mother have that bit o' pleasure, my dear," say Mrs.
Tulliver; "Iwould trouble enough with your hair once."

and Maggie's grace of mind and body feed his gloom. he sit patiently

in a mind charge with an eager purpose and an unsatisfied

not want spiritual consolation--he want to shake off the degradation
of debt, and to have his revenge.

in the Red Deeps
the family sitting-room be a long room with a window at each end; one
look toward the croft and along the Ripple to the bank of the
Floss, the other into the mill-yard. Maggie be sit with her work
against the latter window when she see Mr. Wakem enter the yard, as
usual, on his fine black horse; but not alone, as usual. some one be
with him,--a figure in a cloak, on a handsome pony. Maggie have hardly
time to feel that it be Philip come back, before they be in front
of the window, and he be raise his hat to she; while his father,
catch the movement by a side-glance, look sharply round at they
both.
Maggie hurry away from the window and carry her work upstairs; for
Mr. Wakem sometimes come in and inspect the book, and Maggie feel
that the meeting with Philip would be rob of all pleasure in the
presence of the two father. some day, perhaps, she could see he when
they could just shake hand, and she could tell he that she
remember his goodness to Tom, and the thing he have say to she in
the old day, though they could never be friend any more. it be not
at all agitate to Maggie to see Philip again; she retain she
childish gratitude and pity toward he, and remember his cleverness;
and in the early week of her loneliness she have continually recall

fancy it might have be, in their talk together. but that sort of

seek her own will; and she think, besides, that Philip might be
alter by his life abroad,--he might have become worldly, and really

wonder if he remember how he use to like her eye; with that
think Maggie glance toward the square looking-glass which be

from her seat to reach it down; but she check herself and snatch
up her work, try to repress the rise wish by force her memory
to recall snatch of hymn, until she see Philip and his father
return along the road, and she could go down again.
it be far on in June now, and Maggie be incline to lengthen the
daily walk which be her one indulgence; but this day and the
following she be so busy with work which must be finish that she
never go beyond the gate, and satisfy her need of the open air by
sit out of door. one of her frequent walk, when she be not
oblige to go to St. Ogg's, be to a spot that lie beyond what be
call the "hill,"--an insignificant rise of ground crown by tree,
lie along the side of the road which run by the gate of Dorlcote
Mill. Insignificant I call it, because in height it be hardly more
than a bank; but there may come moment when Nature make a mere bank
a means toward a fateful result; and that be why I ask you to imagine
this high bank crown with tree, make an uneven wall for some
quarter of a mile along the left side of Dorlcote Mill and the
pleasant field behind it, bound by the murmur Ripple. just where
this line of bank slop down again to the level, a by-road turn off
and lead to the other side of the rise, where it be break into very
capricious hollow and mound by the working of an exhausted
stone-quarry, so long exhaust that both mound and hollow be now
clothe with bramble and tree, and here and there by a stretch of
grass which a few sheep keep close-nibble. in her childish day
Maggie hold this place, call the Red Deeps, in very great awe, and
need all her confidence in Tom's bravery to reconcile she to an
excursion thither,--visions of robber and fierce animal haunt
every hollow. but now it have the charm for she which any break
ground, any mimic rock and ravine, have for the eye that rest
habitually on the level; especially in summer, when she could sit on a
grassy hollow under the shadow of a branch ash, stoop aslant
from the steep above she, and listen to the hum of insect, like
tiny bell on the garment of Silence, or see the sunlight pierce
the distant bough, as if to chase and drive home the truant heavenly
blue of the wild hyacinth. in this June time, too, the dog-rose be
in their glory, and that be an additional reason why Maggie should
direct her walk to the Red Deeps, rather than to any other spot, on
the first day she be free to wander at her will,--a pleasure she
love so well, that sometimes, in her ardor of renunciation, she
think she ought to deny herself the frequent indulgence in it.
you may see she now, as she walk down the favorite turning and enter
the deep by a narrow path through a group of Scotch fir, her tall
figure and old lavender gown visible through an hereditary black silk
shawl of some wide-mesh net-like material; and now she be sure of
be unseen she take off her bonnet and tie it over her arm. one
would certainly suppose she to be far on in life than she
seventeenth year--perhaps because of the slow resign sadness of the
glance from which all search and unrest seem to have depart; perhaps
because her broad-cheste figure have the mould of early womanhood.
Youth and health have withstand well the involuntary and voluntary
hardship of her lot, and the night in which she have lie on the hard

the brown cheek be firm and round, the full lip be red. with her
dark coloring and jet crown surmount her tall figure, she seem to

such as one often see in old face under borderless cap, out of
keep with the resistant youth, which one expect to flash out in a
sudden, passionate glance, that will dissipate all the quietude, like
a damp fire leap out again when all seem safe.
but Maggie herself be not uneasy at this moment. she be clamly
enjoy the free air, while she look up at the old fir-tree, and
think that those broken end of branch be the record of past
storm, which have only make the red stem soar higher. but while her
eye be still turn upward, she become conscious of a move shadow

and then, blush deeply, come forward to she and put out his hand.
Maggie, too, color with surprise, which soon give way to pleasure.
she put out her hand and look down at the deformed figure before she

her child's feelings,--a memory that be always strong in she. she be

"you startle I," she say, smile faintly; "I never meet any one
here. how come you to be walk here? do you come to meet _I?_"
it be impossible not to perceive that Maggie feel herself a child
again.
"yes, I do," say Philip, still embarrassed; "I wish to see you
very much. I watch a long while yesterday on the bank near your
house to see if you would come out, but you never come. then I watch
again to-day, and when I see the way you take, I keep you in sight and
come down the bank, behind there. I hope you will not be displease
with I."
"no," say Maggie, with simple seriousness, walk on as if she mean
Philip to accompany she, "Ibe very glad you come, for I wish very
much to have an opportunity of speak to you. Ihave never forget
how good you be long ago to Tom, and I too; but I be not sure that
you would remember we so well. Tom and I have have a great deal of
trouble since then, and I think _that_ make one think more of what
happen before the trouble come."
"I cannot believe that you have think of I so much as I have think
of you," say Philip, timidly. "do you know, when I be away, I make a
picture of you as you look that morning in the study when you say
you would not forget I."
Philip draw a large miniature-case from his pocket, and open it.
Maggie see her old self lean on a table, with her black lock
hang down behind her ear, look into space, with strange, dreamy
eye. it be a water-color sketch, of real merit as a portrait.
"oh dear," say Maggie, smiling, and flush with pleasure, "what a
queer little girl I be! I remember myself with my hair in that way,
in that pink frock. I really _be_ like a gypsy. I dare say I be now,"
she add, after a little pause; "be I like what you expect I to
be?"
the word might have be those of a coquette, but the full, bright
glance Maggie turn on Philip be not that of a coquette. she really
do hope he like her face as it be now, but it be simply the rise
again of her innate delight in admiration and love. Philip meet her

quietly, "no, Maggie."
the light die out a little from Maggie's face, and there be a slight
trembling of the lip. her eyelid fall lower, but she do not turn
away her head, and Philip continue to look at she. then he say

"you be very much more beautiful than I think you would be."
"be I?" say Maggie, the pleasure return in a deep flush. she

she stand still when they be in the hollow, and look at Philip

"I wish we could have be friends,--i mean, if it would have be

the old book go; and Tom be different, and my father. it be like

Maggie speak with more and more sorrowful gentleness as she go on,

be an evil state of mind. Ibe quite sure that whatever I might do, I
should wish in the end that I have go without anything for myself,

"but would it make his life hard if we be to see each other

"oh, Ibe sure he wouldnot like it. donot ask I why, or anything about

about some thing. he be not at all happy."
"no more be I," say Philip, impetuously; "I be not happy."
"why?" say Maggie, gently. "at least--I ought not to ask--but Ibe
very, very sorry."
Philip turn to walk on, as if he have not patience to stand still any

bear to insist immediately on their parting.
"Ihave be a great deal happy," she say at last, timidly, "since I
have give up think about what be easy and pleasant, and be

we _must_ hunger after they. how can we ever be satisfied without they
until our feeling be deaden? I delight in fine picture; I long to
be able to paint such. I strive and strive, and cannot produce what I
want. that be pain to I, and always _will_ be pain, until my
