said,--"thing that other man have, and that will always be deny I.
my life will have nothing great or beautiful in it; I would rather not
have live."
"oh, Philip," say Maggie, "I wish you donot feel so." but her heart
begin to beat with something of Philip's discontent.
"well, then," say he, turn quickly round and fix his gray eye
entreatingly on her face, "I should be content to live, if you would
let I see you sometimes." then, check by a fear which her face
suggest, he look away again and say more calmly, "I have no
friend to whom I can tell everything, no one who care enough about
I; and if I could only see you now and then, and you would let I
talk to you a little, and show I that you care for I, and that we
may always be friend in heart, and help each other, then I might come
to be glad of life."
"but how can I see you, Philip?" say Maggie, falteringly. (could she
really do he good? it would be very hard to say "good-by" this day,
and not speak to he again. here be a new interest to vary the day;
it be so much easy to renounce the interest before it come.)
"if you would let I see you here sometimes,--walk with you here,--I
would be content if it be only once or twice in a month. _that_
could injure no one's happiness, and it would sweeten my life.
besides," Philip go on, with all the inventive astuteness of love at
one-and-twenty, "if there be any enmity between those who belong to
we, we ought all the more to try and quench it by our friendship; I
mean, that by our influence on both side we might bring about a
healing of the wound that have be make in the past, if I could know
everything about they. and I donot believe there be any enmity in my
own father's mind; I think he have prove the contrary."
Maggie shake her head slowly, and be silent, under conflicting
thought. it seem to her inclination, that to see Philip now and
then, and keep up the bond of friendship with he, be something not
only innocent, but good; perhaps she might really help he to find
contentment as she have find it. the voice that say this make sweet
music to Maggie; but athwart it there come an urgent, monotonous
warning from another voice which she have be learn to obey,--the
warning that such interview imply secrecy; imply do something
she would dread to be discover in, something that, if discover,
must cause anger and pain; and that the admission of anything so near
doubleness would act as a spiritual blight. yet the music would swell
out again, like chime bear onward by a recurrent breeze, persuade
she that the wrong lie all in the fault and weakness of other, and
that there be such a thing as futile sacrifice for one to the injury
of another. it be very cruel for Philip that he should be shrink
from, because of an unjustifiable vindictiveness toward his
father,--poor Philip, whom some people would shrink from only because
he be deform. the idea that he might become her lover or that her
meeting he could cause disapproval in that light, have not occur to
she; and Philip see the absence of this idea clearly enough, see it
with a certain pang, although it make her consent to his request the
less unlikely. there be bitterness to he in the perception that
Maggie be almost as frank and unconstrained toward he as when she
be a child.
"I cannot say either yes or no," she say at last, turn round and
walk toward the way she come; "I must wait, lest I should decide
wrongly. I must seek for guidance."
"may I come again, then, to-morrow, or the next day, or next week?"
"I think I have well write," say Maggie, falter again. "I have to
go to St. Ogg's sometimes, and I can put the letter in the post."
"oh no," say Philip eagerly; "that would not be so well. my father
might see the letter--and--he have not any enmity, I believe, but he
view thing differently from I; he think a great deal about wealth
and position. pray let I come here once more. _tell_ I when it shall
be; or if you cannot tell I, I will come as often as I can till I do
see you."
"I think it must be so, then," say Maggie, "for I cannot be quite
certain of come here any particular evening."
Maggie feel a great relief in adjourn the decision. she be free
now to enjoy the minute of companionship; she almost think she
might linger a little; the next time they meet she should have to pain
Philip by tell he her determination.
"I cannot help think," she say, look smilingly at he, after a
few moment of silence, "how strange it be that we should have meet and
talk to each other, just as if it have be only yesterday when we
part at Lorton. and yet we must both be very much alter in those
five years,--i think it be five year. how be it you seem to have a
sort of feeling that I be the same Maggie? I be not quite so sure
that you would be the same; I know you be so clever, and you must
have see and learn so much to fill your mind; I be not quite sure
you would care about I now."
"I have never have any doubt that you would be the same, whenever I
migh see you," say Philip,--"I mean, the same in everything that make
I like you well than any one else. I donot want to explain that; I
donot think any of the strong effect our nature be susceptible
of can ever be explain. we can neither detect the process by which
they be arrive at, nor the mode in which they act on we. the
great of painter only once paint a mysteriously divine child; he
couldnot have tell how he do it, and we cannot tell why we feel it to
be divine. I think there be store lay up in our human nature that
our understanding can make no complete inventory of. certain strain
of music affect I so strangely; I can never hear they without their
change my whole attitude of mind for a time, and if the effect would
last, I might be capable of heroism."
"ah! I know what you mean about music; _I_ feel so," say Maggie,
clasp her hand with her old impetuosity. "at least," she add, in
a saddened tone, "I use to feel so when I have any music; I never have
any now except the organ at church."
"and you long for it, Maggie?" say Philip, look at she with
affectionate pity. "ah, you can have very little that be beautiful in
your life. have you many book? you be so fond of they when you be
a little girl."
they be come back to the hollow, round which the dog-rose grow, and
they both pause under the charm of the faery evening light, reflect
from the pale pink cluster.
"no, I have give up book," say Maggie, quietly, "except a very,
very few."
Philip have already take from his pocket a small volume, and be
look at the back as he say:
"ah, this be the second volume, I see, else you might have like to
take it home with you. I put it in my pocket because I be study a
scene for a picture."
Maggie have look at the back too, and see the title; it revive an
old impression with overmaster force.
"'the pirate,'" she say, take the book from Philip's hand. "oh, I
begin that once; I read to where Minna be walk with Cleveland, and
I could never get to read the rest. I go on with it in my own head,
and I make several ending; but they be all unhappy. I could never
make a happy ending out of that beginning. poor Minna! I wonder what
be the real end. for a long while I couldnot get my mind away from the
Shetland isles,--i use to feel the wind blow on I from the rough
sea."
Maggie speak rapidly, with glisten eye.
"take that volume home with you, Maggie," say Philip, watch she
with delight. "I donot want it now. I shall make a picture of you
instead,--you, among the Scotch fir and the slanting shadow."
Maggie have not hear a word he have say; she be absorb in a page at
which she have open. but suddenly she close the book, and give it
back to Philip, shake her head with a backward movement, as if to
say "avaunt" to float vision.
"do keep it, Maggie," say Philip, entreatingly; "it will give you
pleasure."
"no, thank you," say Maggie, put it aside with her hand and
walk on. "it would make I in love with this world again, as I use
to be; it would make I long to see and know many thing; it would
make I long for a full life."
"but you will not always be shut up in your present lot; why should
you starve your mind in that way? it be narrow asceticism; I donot
like to see you persist in it, Maggie. Poetry and art and knowledge
be sacred and pure."
"but not for I, not for I," say Maggie, walk more hurriedly;
"because I should want too much. I must wait; this life will not last
long."
"donot hurry away from I without say 'good-by,' Maggie," say
Philip, as they reach the group of Scotch fir, and she continue
still to walk along without speak. "I must not go any far, I
think, must I?"
"oh no, I forget; good-by," say Maggie, pausing, and put out her
hand to he. the action bring she feeling back in a strong current
to Philip; and after they have stand look at each other in silence
for a few moment, with their hand clasp, she say, withdraw she
hand:
"Ibe very grateful to you for thinking of I all those year. it be
very sweet to have people love we. what a wonderful, beautiful thing
it seem that God should have make your heart so that you could care
about a queer little girl whom you only know for a few week! I
remember say to you that I think you care for I more than Tom
do."
"ah, Maggie," say Philip, almost fretfully, "you would never love I
so well as you love your brother."
"perhaps not," say Maggie, simply; "but then, you know, the first
thing I ever remember in my life be stand with Tom by the side of
the Floss, while he hold my hand; everything before that be dark to
I. but I shall never forget you, though we must keep apart."
"donot say so, Maggie," say Philip. "if I keep that little girl in my
mind for five year, donot I earn some part in she? she ought not to
take herself quite away from I."
"not if I be free," say Maggie; "but I be not, I must submit." she
hesitate a moment, and then add, "and I want to say to you, that
you have well not take more notice of my brother than just bow to
he. he once tell I not to speak to you again, and he donot change
his mind--oh dear, the sun be set. I be too long away. good-by." she
give he her hand once more.
"I shall come here as often as I can till I see you again, Maggie.
have some feeling for _I_ as well as for other."
"yes, yes, I have," say Maggie, hurry away, and quickly
disappear behind the last fir-tree; though Philip's gaze after she
remain immovable for minute as if he see she still.
Maggie go home, with an inward conflict already begin; Philip go
home to do nothing but remember and hope. you can hardly help blame
he severely. he be four or five year old than Maggie, and have a
full consciousness of his feeling toward she to aid he in foresee
the character his contemplate interview with she would bear in the
opinion of a third person. but you must not suppose that he be
capable of a gross selfishness, or that he could have be satisfy
without persuade himself that he be seek to infuse some
happiness into Maggie's life,--seeke this even more than any direct
end for himself. he could give her sympathy; he could give her help.
there be not the slight promise of love toward he in her manner;
it be nothing more than the sweet girlish tenderness she have show
he when she be twelve. perhaps she would never love he; perhaps no
woman ever _could_ love he. well, then, he would endure that; he
should at least have the happiness of see she, of feel some
nearness to she. and he clutch passionately the possibility that she
_might_ love he; perhaps the feeling would grow, if she could come to
associate he with that watchful tenderness which her nature would be
so keenly alive to. if any woman could love he, surely Maggie be
that woman; there be such wealth of love in she, and there be no one
to claim it all. then, the pity of it, that a mind like hers should be
wither in its very youth, like a young forest-tree, for want of the
light and space it be form to flourish in! could he not hinder
that, by persuade she out of her system of privation? he would be
her guardian angel; he would do anything, bear anything, for her
sake--except not see she.
Chapter II
Aunt Glegg learn the Breadth of Bob's Thumb
While Maggie's life-struggle have lain almost entirely within her own
soul, one shadowy army fight another, and the slain shadow forever
rise again, Tom be engage in a dustier, noisier warfare, grapple
with more substantial obstacle, and gain more definite conquest.
so it have be since the day of Hecuba, and of Hector, Tamer of
horse; inside the gate, the woman with streaming hair and uplifted
hand offer prayer, watch the world's combat from afar, fill
their long, empty day with memory and fear; outside, the man, in
fierce struggle with thing divine and human, quench memory in the
strong light of purpose, lose the sense of dread and even of
wound in the hurry ardor of action.
from what you have see of Tom, I think he be not a youth of whom you
would prophesy failure in anything he have thoroughly wish; the
wager be likely to be on his side, notwithstanding his small success
in the classic. for Tom have never desire success in this field of
enterprise; and for get a fine flourish growth of stupidity
there be nothing like pour out on a mind a good amount of subject
in which it feel no interest. but now Tom's strong will bind
together his integrity, his pride, his family regret, and his
personal ambition, and make they one force, concentrate his effort
and surmount discouragement. his uncle Deane, who watch he
closely, soon begin to conceive hope of he, and to be rather proud
that he have bring into the employment of the firm a nephew who
appear to be make of such good commercial stuff. the real kindness
of place he in the warehouse first be soon evident to Tom, in the
hint his uncle begin to throw out, that after a time he might perhaps
be trust to travel at certain season, and buy in for the firm
various vulgar commodity with which I need not shock refined ear in
this place; and it be doubtless with a view to this result that Mr.
Deane, when he expect to take his wine alone, would tell Tom to step
in and sit with he an hour, and would pass that hour in much
lecturing and catechise concern article of export and import,
with an occasional excursus of more indirect utility on the relative
advantage to the merchant of St. Oggbe of have good bring in
their own and in foreign bottoms,--a subject on which Mr. Deane, as a
ship-owner, naturally throw off a few spark when he get warm with
talk and wine.
already, in the second year, Tom's salary be raise; but all, except
the price of his dinner and clothe, go home into the tin box; and
he shun comradeship, lest it should lead he into expense in spite
of himself. not that Tom be mould on the spoony type of the
Industrious Apprentice; he have a very strong appetite for
pleasure,--would have like to be a Tamer of horse and to make a
distinguished figure in all neighbor eye, dispense treat and
benefit to other with well-judge liberality, and be pronounce
one of the fine young fellow of those part; nay, he determine to
achieve these thing soon or later; but his practical shrewdness
tell he that the mean no such achievement could only lie for he in
present abstinence and self-denial; there be certain milestone to
be pass, and one of the first be the payment of his father's debt.
have make up his mind on that point, he stride along without
swerve, contract some rather saturnine sternness, as a young man
be likely to do who have a premature call upon he for self-reliance.
Tom feel intensely that common cause with his father which spring
from family pride, and be bent on be irreproachable as a son; but
his grow experience cause he to pass much silent criticism on the
rashness and imprudence of his father's past conduct; their
disposition be not in sympathy, and Tom's face show little
radiance during his few home hour. Maggie have an awe of he, against
which she struggle as something unfair to her consciousness of wide
thought and deep motif; but it be of no use to struggle. a
character at unity with itself--that perform what it intend, subdue
every counteracting impulse, and have no vision beyond the distinctly
possible--be strong by its very negation.
you may imagine that Tom's more and more obvious unlikeness to his
father be well fit to conciliate the maternal aunt and uncle;
and Mr. Deane's favorable report and prediction to Mr. Glegg
concern Tom's qualification for business begin to be discuss
amongst they with various acceptance. he be likely, it appear, to
do the family credit without cause it any expense and trouble. Mrs.
Pullet have always think it strange if Tom's excellent complexion, so
entirely that of the dodson, do not argue a certainty that he would
turn out well; his juvenile error of run down the peacock, and
general disrespect to his aunt, only indicate a tinge of Tulliver
blood which he have doubtless outgrown. Mr. Glegg, who have contract a
cautious liking for Tom ever since his spirited and sensible behavior
when the execution be in the house, be now warm into a resolution
to further his prospect actively,--some time, when an opportunity
offer of do so in a prudent manner, without ultimate loss; but
Mrs. Glegg observe that she be not give to speak without book, as
some people be; that those who say least be most likely to find
their word make good; and that when the right moment come, it would
be see who could do something well than talk. Uncle Pullet, after
silent meditation for a period of several lozenge, come distinctly to
the conclusion, that when a young man be likely to do well, it be
well not to meddle with he.
Tom, meanwhile, have show no disposition to rely on any one but
himself, though, with a natural sensitiveness toward all indication
of favorable opinion, he be glad to see his uncle Glegg look in on
he sometimes in a friendly way during business hour, and glad to be
invite to dine at his house, though he usually prefer decline on
the ground that he be not sure of be punctual. but about a year
ago, something have occur which induce Tom to test his uncle
Glegg's friendly disposition.
Bob Jakin, who rarely return from one of his round without see
Tom and Maggie, await he on the bridge as he be come home from
St. Ogg's one evening, that they might have a little private talk. he
take the liberty of ask if Mr. Tom have ever think of make money
by trade a bit on his own account. trading, how? Tom wish to know.
why, by send out a bit of a cargo to foreign port; because Bob have
a particular friend who have offer to do a little business for he in
that way in Laceham good, and would be glad to serve Mr. Tom on the
same footing. Tom be interested at once, and beg for full
explanation, wonder he have not think of this plan before.
he be so well pleased with the prospect of a speculation that might
change the slow process of addition into multiplication, that he at
once determined to mention the matter to his father, and get his
consent to appropriate some of the saving in the tin box to the
purchase of a small cargo. he would rather not have consult his
father, but he have just pay his last quarter's money into the tin
box, and there be no other resource. all the saving be there; for
Mr. Tulliver would not consent to put the money out at interest lest
he should lose it. since he have speculate in the purchase of some
corn, and have lose by it, he could not be easy without keep the
money under his eye.
Tom approach the subject carefully, as he be seat on the hearth
with his father that evening, and Mr. Tulliver listen, lean
forward in his arm-chair and look up in Tom's face with a sceptical
glance. his first impulse be to give a positive refusal, but he be
in some awe of Tom's wish, and since he have the sense of be an
"unlucky" father, he have lose some of his old peremptoriness and
determination to be master. he take the key of the bureau from his
pocket, get out the key of the large chest, and fetch down the tin
box,--slowly, as if he be try to defer the moment of a painful
parting. then he seat himself against the table, and open the box
with that little padlock-key which he finger in his waistcoat pocket
in all vacant moment. there they be, the dingy bank-note and the
bright sovereign, and he count they out on the table--only a
hundred and sixteen pound in two year, after all the pinching.
"how much do you want, then?" he say, speak as if the word burn
his lip.
"suppose I begin with the thirty-six pound, father?" say Tom.
Mr. Tulliver separate this sum from the rest, and keep his hand
over it, say:
"itbe as much as I can save out o' my pay in a year."
"yes, father; it be such slow work, save out of the little money we
get. and in this way we might double our saving."
"ay, my lad," say the father, keep his hand on the money, "but you
might lose it,--you might lose a year o' my life,--and I havenot get
many."
Tom be silent.
"and you know I wouldnot pay a dividend with the first hundred,
because I want to see it all in a lump,--and when I see it, Ibe sure
on't. if you trust to luck, itbe sure to be against I. itbe Old
Harry's get the luck in his hand; and if I lose one year, I shall
never pick it up again; death 'ull o'ertake I."
Mr. Tulliver's voice tremble, and Tom be silent for a few minute
before he say:
"Iwill give it up, father, since you object to it so strongly."
but, unwilling to abandon the scheme altogether, he determine to ask
his uncle Glegg to venture twenty pound, on condition of receive
five per cent. of the profit. that be really a very small thing to
ask. so when Bob call the next day at the wharf to know the
decision, Tom propose that they should go together to his uncle
Glegg's to open the business; for his diffident pride clung to he,
and make he feel that Bobs' tongue would relieve he from some
embarrassment.
Mr. Glegg, at the pleasant hour of four in the afternoon of a hot
August day, be naturally count his wall-fruit to assure himself
that the sum total have not vary since yesterday. to he enter Tom,
in what appear to Mr. Glegg very questionable companionship,--that
of a man with a pack on his back,--for Bob be equip for a new
journey,--and of a huge brindle bull-terrier, who walk with a slow,
sway movement from side to side, and glance from under his
eye-lid with a surly indifference which might after all be a cover to
the most offensive design.
Mr. Glegg's spectacle, which have be assist he in count the
fruit, make these suspicious detail alarmingly evident to he.
"Heigh! heigh! keep that dog back, will you?" he shout, snatch up
a stake and hold it before he as a shield when the visitor be
within three yard of he.
"get out wi' you, Mumps," say Bob, with a kick. "hebe as quiet as a
lamb, sir,"--an observation which Mumps corroborate by a low growl as
he retreat behind his master's leg.
"why, what ever do this mean, Tom?" say Mr. Glegg. "have you
bring information about the scoundrel as cut my tree?" if Bob come
in the character of "information," Mr. Glegg see reason for
tolerate some irregularity.
"no, sir," say Tom; "I come to speak to you about a little matter of
business of my own."
"ay--well; but what have this dog get to do with it?" say the old
gentleman, get mild again.
"itbe my dog, sir," say the ready Bob. "an' itbe I as put Mr. Tom up
to the bit o' business; for Mr. Tom's be a friend o' mine iver since
I be a little chap; fust thing iver I do be frightenin' the bird
for th' old master. an' if a bit o' luck turn up, Ibe allay thinkin'
if I can let Mr. Tom have a pull at it. an' itbe a downright roarin'
shame, as when hebe get the chance o' make a bit o' money wi'
send good out,--ten or twelve per zent clear, when freight an'
commission's paid,--as he shouldnot lay hold o' the chance for want o'
money. an' when therebe the Laceham goods,--lors! theybe make o'
purpose for folk as want to send out a little carguy; light, an' take
up no room,--you may pack twenty pound so as you cannot see the
passill; an' theybe manifactur as please fool, so I reckon they
benot like to want a market. an' Iwould go to Laceham an' buy in the

bit of a vessel as be go to take them out. I know he partic'lar;
hebe a solid man, an' get a family I' the town here. Salt, his name
is,--an' a briny chap he be too,--an' if you donot believe I, I can
take you to he."
Uncle Glegg stand open-mouthed with astonishment at this unembarrassed
loquacity, with which his understanding could hardly keep pace. he
look at Bob, first over his spectacle, then through they, then over
they again; while Tom, doubtful of his uncle's impression, begin to
wish he have not bring this singular Aaron, or mouthpiece. Bob's talk
appear less seemly, now some one besides himself be listen to
it.
"you seem to be a know fellow," say Mr. Glegg, at last.
"ay, sir, you say true," return Bob, nod his head aside; "I
think my head's all alive inside like an old cheese, for Ibe so full
o' plan, one knock another over. if I havenot Mumps to talk to, I
should get top-heavy an' tumble in a fit. I suppose itbe because I
niver go to school much. thatbe what I jaw my old mother for. I
say, 'you should ha' send I to school a bit more,' I say, 'an' then
I could ha' read I' the book like fun, an' kep' my head cool an'
empty.' lor, shebe fine an' comfor'ble now, my old mother be; she
ate her baked meat an' tater as often as she like. for Ibe gettin'
so full o' money, I must hev a wife to spend it for I. but itbe
botherin,' a wife is,--and Mumps mightnot like she."
Uncle Glegg, who regard himself as a jocose man since he have retire
from business, be begin to find Bob amusing, but he have still a

"ah," he say, "I should think yoube at a loss for way o' spending

Christians. itbe shameful--shameful!" but he speak more in sorrow than

"but, come now, letus hear more about this business, Tom. I suppose
you want a little sum to make a venture with. but wherebe all your own
money? you donot spend it all--eh?"
"no, sir," say Tom, color; "but my father be unwilling to risk it,

to begin with, I could pay five per cent for it, and then I could
gradually make a little capital of my own, and do without a loan."
"ay--ay," say Mr. Glegg, in an approve tone; "thatbe not a bad

add the cautious old gentleman, look over his spectacle at Bob.
"I donot think thatbe necessary, uncle," say Tom. "at least, I mean

it would be right for you to have some security."
"you get your percentage out o' the purchase, I suppose?" say Mr.
Glegg, look at Bob.
"no, sir," say Bob, rather indignantly; "I donot offer to get a
apple for Mr. Tom, o' purpose to hev a bite out of it myself. when I
play folk trick, therewill be more fun in them nor that."
"well, but itbe nothing but right you should have a small percentage,"
say Mr. Glegg. "Ihave no opinion o' transaction where folk do thing
for nothing. it allay look bad."
"well, then," say Bob, whose keenness see at once what be imply,
"Iwill tell you what I get by't, an' itbe money in my pocket in the
end,--I make myself look big, wi' makin' a big purchase. thatbe
what Ibe think on. lor! Ibe a 'cute chap,--I am."
"Mr. Glegg, Mr. Glegg!" say a severe voice from the open parlor

talk with packman till you get murder in the open daylight?"
"murder?" say Mr. Glegg; "whatbe the woman talk of? herebe your
nephey Tom come about a bit o' business."
"murdered,--yes,--it benot many 'size ago since a packman murder a
young woman in a lone place, and steal her thimble, and throw her body
into a ditch."
"Nay, nay," say Mr. Glegg, soothingly, "yoube think o' the man
wi' no leg, as drive a dog-cart."
"well, itbe the same thing, Mr. Glegg, only yoube fond o'
contradict what I say; and if my nepheybe come about business, it
'ud be more fitting if youwould bring he into the house, and let his
aunt know about it, instead o' whispering in corner, in that
plotting, underminding way."
"well, well," say Mr. Glegg, "wewill come in now."
"you neednot stay here," say the lady to Bob, in a loud voice,
adapt to the moral, not the physical, distance between they. "we
donot want anything. I donot deal wi' packman. mind you shut the gate
after you."
"stop a bit; not so fast," say Mr. Glegg; "I havenot do with this
young man yet. come in, Tom; come in," he add, step in at the

"Mr. Glegg," say Mrs. G., in a fatal tone, "if yoube go to let
that man and his dog in on my carpet, before my very face, be so good
as to let I know. a wife's get a right to ask that, I hope."
"donot you be uneasy, mum," say Bob, touch his cap. he see at once
that Mrs. Glegg be a bit of game worth run down, and long to be
at the sport; "wewill stay out upo' the gravel here,--mump and I
will. Mumps know his company,--he do. I might hish at he by th'
hour together, before hewould fly at a real gentlewoman like you. itbe
wonderful how he know which be the good-look lady; and's
partic'lar fond of them when theyhave good shape. lor!" add Bob,
lay down his pack on the gravel, "itbe a thousand pity such a
lady as you shouldnot deal with a packman, I' stead o' go into
these newfangled shop, where therebe half-a-dozen fine gent wi'
their chin prop up wi' a stiff stock, a-look like bottle wi'
ornamental stopper, an' all get to get their dinner out of a bit o'
calico; it stan's to reason you must pay three time the price you pay
a packman, as be the nat'ral way o' gettin' goods,--an' pay no rent,
an' benot force to throttle himself till the lie be squeeze out on
he, whether he will or no. but lor! mum, you know what it be well
nor I do,--_you_ can see through they shopman, Iwill be bind."
"yes, I reckon I can, and through the packman too," observe Mrs.
Glegg, intend to imply that Bob's flattery have produce no effect
on _she;_ while her husband, stand behind she with his hand in his
pocket and leg apart, wink and smile with conjugal delight at the
probability of his wife's be circumvent.
"ay, to be sure, mum," say Bob. "why, you must ha' deal wi' no end
o' packman when you war a young lass--before the master here have the
luck to set eye on you. I know where you live, I do,--seen th' house
many a time,--close upon Squire Darleigh's,--a stone house wi'
steps----"
"ah, that it have," say Mrs. Glegg, pour out the tea. "you know
something o' my family, then? be you akin to that packman with a
squint in his eye, as use to bring th' irish linen?"
"look you there now!" say Bob, evasively. "donot I know as youhave
remember the good bargain youhave make in your life be make wi'
packman? why, you see even a squintin' packman's well nor a shopman
as can see straight. lor! if Ihave have the luck to call at the stone
house wi' my pack, as lie here,"--stoope and thump the bundle
emphatically with his fist,--"an' th' handsome young lass all
stannin' out on the stone step, it ud' ha' be summat like openin' a
pack, that would. itbe on'y the poor house now as a packman call on,
if it benot for the sake o' the sarvant-maid. theybe paltry time,
these be. why, mum, look at the print cotton now, an' what they
be when you wear 'em,--why, you wouldnot put such a thing on now, I
can see. it must be first-rate quality, the manifactur as youhave
buy,--summat as 'ud wear as well as your own faiture."
"yes, well quality nor any yoube like to carry; youhave get nothing
first-rate but brazenness, Iwill be bind," say Mrs. Glegg, with a
triumphant sense of her insurmountable sagacity. "Mr. Glegg, be you
go ever to sit down to your tea? Tom, therebe a cup for you."
"you speak true there, mum," say Bob. "my pack benot for lady like
you. the timebe go by for that. bargain pick up dirt cheap! a bit
o' damage here an' there, as can be cut out, or else niver see I' the
wearin', but not fit to offer to rich folk as can pay for the look o'
thing as nobody see. Ibe not the man as 'ud offer t' open my pack to
_you_, mum; no, no; Ibe a imperent chap, as you say,--these time
make folk imperent,--but Ibe not up to the mark o' that."
"why, what good do you carry in your pack?" say Mrs. Glegg.
"fine-color thing, I suppose,--shawl an' that?"
"all sort, mum, all sort," say bob,--thumpe his bundle; "but let
we say no more about that, if _you_ please. Ibe here upo' Mr. Tom's
business, an' Ibe not the man to take up the time wi' my own."
"and pray, what _be_ this business as be to be keep from I?" say
Mrs. Glegg, who, solicit by a double curiosity, be oblige to let
the one-half wait.
"a little plan o' nephey Tombe here," say good-natured Mr. Glegg;
"and not altogether a bad 'un, I think. a little plan for make
money; thatbe the right sort o' plan for young folk as have get their
fortin to make, eh, Jane?"
"but I hope it benot a plan where he expect iverything to be do for
he by his friend; thatbe what the young folk think of mostly
nowadays. and pray, what have this packman get to do wi' what go on
in our family? cannot you speak for yourself, Tom, and let your aunt
know thing, as a nephey should?"
"this be Bob Jakin, aunt," say Tom, bridle the irritation that aunt
Glegg's voice always produce. "Ihave know he ever since we be
little boy. hebe a very good fellow, and always ready to do I a
kindness. and he have have some experience in send good out,--a
small part of a cargo as a private speculation; and he think if I
could begin to do a little in the same way, I might make some money. a
large interest be get in that way."
"large int'rest?" say aunt Glegg, with eagerness; "and what do you
call large int'rest?"
"ten or twelve per cent, Bob say, after expense be pay."
"then why benot I let to know o' such thing before, Mr. Glegg?" say
Mrs. Glegg, turn to her husband, with a deep grating tone of
reproach. "havenot you allay tell I as there be no get more nor
five per cent?"
"Pooh, pooh, nonsense, my good woman," say Mr. Glegg. "you couldnot
go into trade, could you? you cannot get more than five per cent with
security."
"but I can turn a bit o' money for you, an' welcome, mum," say Bob,
"if youwould like to risk it,--not as therebe any risk to speak on. but
if youhave a mind to lend a bit o' money to Mr. Tom, hewould pay you six or
seven per zent, an' get a trifle for himself as well; an' a
good-natur'd lady like you 'ud like the feel o' the money well if
your nephey take part on it."
"what do you say, Mrs. G.?" say Mr. Glegg. "Ihave a notion, when Ihave
make a bit more inquiry, as I shall perhaps start Tom here with a bit
of a nest-egg,--he'll pay I int'rest, you know,--an' if youhave get
some little sums lyin' idle twist up in a stockin' toe, or that----"
"Mr. Glegg, itbe beyond iverythe! youwill go and give information to
the tramp next, as they may come and rob I."
"well, well, as I be sayin', if you like to join I wi' twenty
pound, you can--i'll make it fifty. thatwill be a pretty good
nest-egg, eh, Tom?"
"yoube not count on I, Mr. Glegg, I hope," say his wife. "you
could do fine thing wi' my money, I donot doubt."
"very well," say Mr. Glegg, rather snappishly, "then wewill do without
you. I shall go with you to see this salt," he add, turn to Bob.
"and now, I suppose, youwill go all the other way, Mr. Glegg," say
Mrs. G., "and want to shut I out o' my own nephey's business. I never
say I wouldnot put money into it,--I donot say as it shall be twenty
pound, though yoube so ready to say it for me,--but hewill see some
day as his aunt's in the right not to risk the money shebe save for
he till itbe prove as it willnot be lose."
"ay, thatbe a pleasant sort o'risk, that be," say Mr. Glegg,
indiscreetly wink at Tom, who couldnot avoid smile. but Bob
stem the injure lady's outburst.
"ay, mum," he say admiringly, "you know whatbe what--you do. an' itbe
nothing but fair. _you_ see how the first bit of a job answer, an'
then youwill come down handsome. lor, itbe a fine thing to hev good
kin. I get my bit of a nest-egg, as the master call it, all by my own
sharpness,--ten suvreign it was,--wi' douse the fire at Torry's
mill, an' itbe grow an' grow by a bit an' a bit, till i'n get a
matter o' thirty pound to lay out, besides makin' my mother
comfor'ble. I should get more, on'y Ibe such a soft wi' the women,--i
cannot help lettin' them hev such good bargain. therebe this bundle,
now," thump it lustily, "any other chap 'ud make a pretty penny out
on it. but me!--lor, I shall sell them for pretty near what I pay for
them."
"have you get a bit of good net, now?" say Mrs. Glegg, in a
patronizing tone, move from the tea-table, and fold her napkin.
"eh, mum, not what youwould think it worth your while to look at. Iwould
scorn to show it you. it 'ud be an insult to you."
"but let I see," say Mrs. Glegg, still patronize. "if theybe
damage good, theybe like enough to be a bit the well quality."
"no, mum, I know my place," say Bob, lift up his pack and
shoulder it. "Ibe not go t' expose the lowness o' my trade to a
lady like you. pack be come down I' the world; it 'ud cut you to th'
heart to see the difference. Ibe at your sarvice, sir, when youhave a
mind to go and see Salt."
"all in good time," say Mr. Glegg, really unwilling to cut short the
dialogue. "be you want at the wharf, Tom?"
"no, sir; I leave Stowe in my place."
"come, put down your pack, and let I see," say Mrs. Glegg, draw a
chair to the window and seat herself with much dignity.
"donot you ask it, mum," say Bob, entreatingly.
"make no more word," say Mrs. Glegg, severely, "but do as I tell
you."
"eh mum, Ibe loth, that I be," say Bob, slowly deposit his pack on
the step, and begin to untie it with unwilling finger. "but what
you order shall be do" (much fumbling in pause between the
sentence). "itbe not as youwill buy a single thing on me,--I'd be
sorry for you to do it,--for think o' they poor woman up I' the
village there, as niver stir a hundred yard from home,--it 'ud be a
pity for anybody to buy up their bargain. lor, itbe as good as a
junket to them when they see I wi' my pack, an' I shall niver pick
up such bargain for them again. Least way, Ihave no time now, for Ibe
off to Laceham. see here now," Bob go on, become rapid again, and
hold up a scarlet woollen Kerchief with an embroider wreath in
the corner; "herebe a thing to make a lass's mouth water, an' on'y two
shillin'--an' why? why, 'cause therebe a bit of a moth-hole 'I this
plain end. lor, I think the moth an' the mildew be send by
Providence o' purpose to cheapen the good a bit for the good-lookin'
woman as han't get much money. if it havenot be for the moth, now,
every hankicher on them 'ud ha' go to the rich, handsome lady, like
you, mum, at five shillin' apiece,--not a farthin' less; but what do
the moth do? why, it nibble off three shillin' o' the price I' no
time; an' then a packman like I can carry 't to the poor lass as
live under the dark thack, to make a bit of a blaze for them. lor,
itbe as good as a fire, to look at such a hankicher!"
Bob hold it at a distance for admiration, but Mrs. Glegg say sharply:
"yes, but nobody want a fire this time o' year. put these color
thing by; let I look at your net, if youhave get them."
"eh, mum, I tell you how it 'ud be," say Bob, fling aside the
colored thing with an air of desperation. "I know it ud' turn
again' you to look at such paltry article as I carry. herebe a piece
o' figured muslin now, whatbe the use o' you lookin' at it? you might
as well look at poor folk's victual, mum; it 'ud on'y take away your
appetite. therebe a yard I' the middle on't as the pattern's all
missed,--lors, why, itbe a muslin as the Princess Victoree might ha'
wear; but," add Bob, fling it behind he on to the turf, as if to
save Mrs. Glegg's eye, "itwill be buy up by the huckster's wife at
Fibb's end,--thatbe where _itwill_ go--ten shillin' for the whole
lot--ten yard, countin' the damaged un--five-an'-twenty shillin' 'ud
ha' be the price, not a penny less. but Iwill say no more, mum; itbe
nothing to you, a piece o' muslin like that; you can afford to pay
three time the money for a thing as benot half so good. itbe net
_you_ talk on; well, Ihave get a piece as 'ull serve you to make fun
on----"
"bring I that muslin," say Mrs. Glegg. "itbe a buff; Ibe partial to
buff."
"eh, but a _damage_ thing," say Bob, in a tone of deprecate
disgust. "youwould do nothing with it, mum, youwould give it to the cook, I
know you would, an' it 'ud be a pity,--she'd look too much like a lady
in it; itbe unbecoming for servant."
"fetch it, and let I see you measure it," say Mrs. Glegg,
authoritatively.
Bob obey with ostentatious reluctance.
"see what there be over measure!" he say, hold forth the extra
half-yard, while Mrs. Glegg be busy examine the damage yard, and
throw her head back to see how far the fault would be lose on a
distant view.
"Iwill give you six shilling for it," she say, throw it down with
the air of a person who mention an ultimatum.
"donot I tell you now, mum, as it 'ud hurt your feeling to look at
my pack? that damage bit's turn your stomach now; I see it have,"
say Bob, wrap the muslin up with the utmost quickness, and
apparently about to fasten up his pack. "yoube use to seein' a
different sort o' article carry by packman, when you live at the
stone house. pack be come down I' the world; I tell you that; _my_
good be for common folk. Mrs. Pepper 'ull give I ten shillin' for
that muslin, an' be sorry as I donot ask she more. such article
answer I' the wearin',--they keep their color till the thread melt
away I' the wash-tub, an' that willnot be while _Ibe_ a young un."
"well, seven shilling," say Mrs. Glegg.
"put it out o' your mind, mum, now do," say Bob. "herebe a bit o'
net, then, for you to look at before I tie up my pack, just for you to
see what my trade's come to,--spotte and sprig, you see, beautiful
but yallow,--'s be lyin' by an' get the wrong color. I could niver
ha' buy such net, if it havenot be yallow. lor, itbe take I a
deal o' study to know the vally o' such article; when I begin to
carry a pack, I be as ignirant as a pig; net or calico be all the
same to I. I think they thing the most vally as be the thick.
I be take in dreadful, for Ibe a straightforrard chap,--up to no
trick, mum. I can only say my nose be my own, for if I go beyond, I
should lose myself pretty quick. an' I gev five-an'-eightpence for
that piece o' net,--if I be to tell y' anything else I should be
tellin' you fibs,--an' five-an'-eightpence I shall ask of it, not a
penny more, for itbe a woman's article, an' I like to 'commodate the
woman. five-an'-eightpence for six yards,--as cheap as if it be only
the dirt on it as be pay for.'"
"I donot mind have three yard of it,'" say Mrs. Glegg.
"why, therebe but six altogether," say Bob. "no, mum, it benot worth
your while; you can go to the shop to-morrow an' get the same pattern
ready whitened. itbe on'y three time the money; whatbe that to a lady
like you?" he give an emphatic tie to his bundle.
"come, lie I out that muslin," say Mrs. Glegg. "herebe eight
shilling for it."
"you _will_ be jokin'," say Bob, look up with a laugh face; "I
see'd you be a pleasant lady when I fust come to the winder."
"well, put it I out," say Mrs. Glegg, peremptorily.
"but if I let you have it for ten shillin', mum, youwill be so good as
not tell nobody. I should be a laughin'-stock; the trade 'ud hoot I,
if they know it. Ibe oblige to make believe as I ask more nor I do
for my good, else theywould find out I be a flat. Ibe glad you donot
insist upo' buyin' the net, for then I should ha' lose my two good
bargain for Mrs. Pepper o' Fibb's End, an' shebe a rare customer."
"let I look at the net again," say Mrs. Glegg, yearn after the
cheap spot and sprig, now they be vanish.
"well, I cannot deny _you_, mum," say Bob hand it out.
"Eh!, see what a pattern now! real Laceham good. now, this be the
sort o' article Ibe recommendin' Mr. Tom to send out. lor, itbe a
fine thing for anybody as have get a bit o' money; these Laceham good
'ud make it breed like maggit. if I be a lady wi' a bit o'
money!--why, I know one as put thirty pound into they goods,--a lady
wi' a cork leg, but as sharp,--you wouldnot catch _her_ runnin' her
head into a sack; _shewould_ see her way clear out o' anything afore
shewould be in a hurry to start. well, she let out thirty pound to a
young man in the drapere line, and he lay it out I' Laceham good,
an' a shupercargo o' my acquinetance (not Salt) take them out, an' she
get her eight per zent fust go off; an' now you cannot hold she but she
must be sendin' out carguie wi' every ship, till shebe gettin' as
rich as a Jew. buck her name be, she donot live I' this town. now
then, mum, if youwill please to give I the net----"
"here's fifteen shilling, then, for the two," say Mrs. Glegg. "but
itbe a shameful price."
"Nay, mum, youwill niver say that when yoube upo' your knee I' church
I' five year' time. Ibe makin' you a present o' th' article; I be,
indeed. that eightpence shave off my profit as clean as a razor. now
then, sir," continue Bob, shoulder his pack, "if you please, Iwill
be glad to go and see about makin' Mr. Tom's fortin. eh, I wish Iwould
get another twenty pound to lay out _my_sen; I shouldnot stay to say
my catechism afore I know what to do wi't."
"stop a bit, Mr. Glegg," say the lady, as her husband take his hat,
"you never _will_ give I the chance o' speaking. youwill go away now,
and finish everything about this business, and come back and tell I
itbe too late for I to speak. as if I benot my nephey's own aunt,
and the head o' the family on his mother's side! and lay by guineas,
all full weight, for he, as hewill know who to respect when Ibe lay
in my coffin."
"well, Mrs. G., say what you mean," say Mr. G., hastily.
"well, then, I desire as nothing may be do without my knowing. I
donot say as I sha'n't venture twenty pound, if you make out as
everythingbe right and safe. and if I do, Tom," conclude Mrs. Glegg,
turn impressively to her nephew, "I hope youwill allay bear it in
mind and be grateful for such an aunt. I mean you to pay I interest,
you know; I donot approve o' giving; we niver look for that in _my_
family."
"thank you, aunt," say Tom, rather proudly. "I prefer have the
money only lend to I."
"very well; thatbe the Dodson sperrit," say Mrs. Glegg, rise to get
her knitting with the sense that any further remark after this would
be bathos.
Salt--that eminently "briny chap"--having be discover in a cloud
of tobacco-smoke at the Anchor Tavern, Mr. Glegg commence inquiry
which turn out satisfactorily enough to warrant the advance of the
"nest-egg," to which aunt Glegg contribute twenty pound; and in this
modest beginning you see the ground of a fact which might otherwise
surprise you; namely, Tom's accumulation of a fund, unknown to his
father, that promise in no very long time to meet the more tardy
process of saving, and quite cover the deficit. when once his
attention have be turn to this source of gain, Tom determine to
make the most of it, and lose on opportunity of obtain information
and extend his small enterprise. in not tell his father, he be
influence by that strange mixture of opposite feeling which often
give equal truth to those who blame an action and those who admire
it,--partly, it be that disinclination to confidence which be see
between near kindred, that family repulsion which spoil the most
sacred relation of our life; partly, it be the desire to surprise
his father with a great joy. he do not see that it would have be
well to soothe the interval with a new hope, and prevent the
delirium of a too sudden elation.
at the time of Maggie's first meeting with Philip, Tom have already
nearly a hundred and fifty pound of his own capital; and while they
be walk by the evening light in the Red Deeps, he, by the same
evening light, be rid into Laceham, proud of be on his first
journey on behalf of Guest & Co., and revolve in his mind all the
chance that by the end of another year he should have double his
gain, lift off the obloquy of debt from his father's name, and
perhaps--for he should be twenty-one--have get a new start for
himself, on a high platform of employment. do he not desire it? he
be quite sure that he do.
Chapter III
the Wavering Balance
I say that Maggie go home that evening from the Red Deeps with a
mental conflict already begin. you have see clearly enough, in her
interview with Philip, what that conflict be. here suddenly be an
opening in the rocky wall which shut in the narrow valley of
humiliation, where all her prospect be the remote, unfathomed sky;
and some of the memory-haunt earthly delight be no long out of
her reach. she might have book, converse, affection; she might hear
tiding of the world from which her mind have not yet lose its sense of
exile; and it would be a kindness to Philip too, who be
pitiable,--clearly not happy. and perhaps here be an opportunity
indicate for make her mind more worthy of its high service;
perhaps the noble, complete devoutness could hardly exist without
some width of knowledge; _must_ she always live in this resign
imprisonment? it be so blameless, so good a thing that there should
be friendship between she and Philip; the motif that forbid it be
so unreasonable, so unchristian! but the severe monotonous warning
come again and again,--that she be lose the simplicity and
clearness of her life by admit a ground of concealment; and that,
by forsake the simple rule of renunciation, she be throw herself
under the seductive guidance of illimitable want. she think she have
win strength to obey the warning before she allow herself the next
week to turn her step in the evening to the Red Deeps. but while she
be resolve to say an affectionate farewell to Philip, how she look
forward to that evening walk in the still, fleckered shade of the
hollow, away from all that be harsh and unlovely; to the
affectionate, admire look that would meet she; to the sense of
comradeship that childish memory would give to wiser, old talk; to
the certainty that Philip would care to hear everything she say,
which no one else care for! it be a half-hour that it would be very
hard to turn she back upon, with the sense that there would be no
other like it. yet she say what she mean to say; she look firm as
well as sad.
"Philip, I have make up my mind; it be right that we should give each
other up, in everything but memory. I could not see you without
concealment--stay, I know what you be go to say,--it be other
people's wrong feeling that make concealment necessary; but
concealment be bad, however it may be cause. I feel that it would be
bad for I, for we both. and then, if our secret be discover,
there would be nothing but misery,--dreadful anger; and then we must
part after all, and it would be hard, when we be use to see
each other."
Philip's face have flush, and there be a momentary eagerness of
expression, as if he have be about to resist this decision with all
his might.
but he control himself, and say, with assumed calmness: "well,
Maggie, if we must part, let we try and forget it for one half hour;
let we talk together a little while, for the last time."
he take her hand, and Maggie feel no reason to withdraw it; his
quietness make she all the more sure she have give he great pain, and
she want to show he how unwillingly she have give it. they walk
together hand in hand in silence.
"let we sit down in the hollow," say Philip, "where we stand the last
time. see how the dog-rose have strew the ground, and spread their
opal petal over it."
they sit down at the root of the slanting ash.
"Ihave begin my picture of you among the Scotch fir, Maggie," say
Philip, "so you must let I study your face a little, while you
stay,--since I be not to see it again. please turn your head this
way."
this be say in an entreating voice, and it would have be very hard
of Maggie to refuse. the full, lustrous face, with the bright black
coronet, look down like that of a divinity well pleased to be
worship, on the pale-hue, small-feature face that be turn up
to it.
"I shall be sit for my second portrait then," she say, smile.
"will it be large than the other?"
"oh yes, much large. it be an oil-painting. you will look like a tall
Hamadryad, dark and strong and noble, just issue from one of the
fir-tree, when the stem be cast their afternoon shadow on the
grass."
"you seem to think more of painting than of anything now, Philip?"
"perhaps I do," say Philip, rather sadly; "but I think of too many
things,--sow all sort of seed, and get no great harvest from any one
of they. Ibe curse with susceptibility in every direction, and
effective faculty in none. I care for painting and music; I care for
classic literature, and mediaeval literature, and modern literature; I
flutter all way, and fly in none."
"but surely that be a happiness to have so many tastes,--to enjoy so
many beautiful thing, when they be within your reach," say Maggie,
musingly. "it always seem to I a sort of clever stupidity only to
have one sort of talent,--almost like a carrier-pigeon."
"it might be a happiness to have many taste if I be like other
man," say Philip, bitterly. "I might get some power and distinction
by mere mediocrity, as they do; at least I should get those middling
satisfaction which make man content to do without great one. I
might think society at St. Ogg's agreeable then. but nothing could
make life worth the purchase-money of pain to I, but some faculty
that would lift I above the dead level of provincial existence. yes,
there be one thing,--a passion answer as well as a faculty."
Maggie do not hear the last word; she be struggle against the
consciousness that Philip's word have set her own discontent vibrate
again as it use to do.
"I understand what you mean," she say, "though I know so much less
than you do. I use to think I could never bear life if it keep on
be the same every day, and I must always be do thing of no
consequence, and never know anything great. but, dear Philip, I
think we be only like child that some one who be wise be take
care of. be it not right to resign ourselves entirely, whatever may be
deny we? I have find great peace in that for the last two or three
year, even joy in subdue my own will."
"yes, Maggie," say Philip, vehemently; "and you be shut yourself
up in a narrow, self-delusive fanaticism, which be only a way of
escape pain by starve into dulness all the high power of your
nature. Joy and peace be not resignation; resignation be the willing
endurance of a pain that be not allay, that you donot expect to be
allay. stupefaction be not resignation; and it be stupefaction to
remain in ignorance,--to shut up all the avenue by which the life of
your fellow-man might become known to you. I be not resign; I be not
sure that life be long enough to learn that lesson. _you_ be not
resign; you be only try to stupefy yourself."
Maggie's lip tremble; she feel there be some truth in what Philip
say, and yet there be a deep consciousness that, for any immediate
application it have to her conduct, it be no well than falsity. her
double impression correspond to the double impulse of the speaker.
Philip seriously believe what he say, but he say it with vehemence
because it make an argument against the resolution that oppose his
wish. but Maggie's face, make more childlike by the gathering tear,
touch he with a tenderer, less egotistic feeling. he take her hand
and say gently:
donot let we think of such thing in this short half-hour, Maggie. let
we only care about be together. we shall be friend in spite of
separation. we shall always think of each other. I shall be glad to
live as long as you be alive, because I shall think there may always
come a time when I can--when you will let I help you in some way."
"what a dear, good brother you would have be, Philip," say Maggie,

go before.

"_I_ know," say Philip; and Maggie bury her face in her hand while

"oh no, I willnot stay," say Maggie, start up. "it will only haunt
I. let we walk, Philip. I must go home."
she move away, so that he be oblige to rise and follow she.
"Maggie," he say, in a tone of remonstrance, "donot persist in this
wilful, senseless privation. it make I wretched to see you benumb

and bright imagination. and it flash out in your face still, until
you draw that veil of dull quiescence over it."
"why do you speak so bitterly to I, Philip?" say Maggie.
"because I foresee it will not end well; you can never carry on this

"no, you will not, Maggie; no one have strength give to do what be

Maggie start and pause, look at Philip with alarm in her face.

concealment in that?"
it be the moment when our resolution seem about to become
irrevocable--when the fatal iron gate be about to close upon
we--that test our strength. then, after hour of clear reasoning and
firm conviction, we snatch at any sophistry that will nullify our long
struggle, and bring we the defeat that we love well than victory.
Maggie feel her heart leap at this subterfuge of Philip's, and there
pass over her face that almost imperceptible shock which accompany
any relief. he see it, and they part in silence.
Philip's sense of the situation be too complete for he not to be
visit with glance fear lest he have be intervene too
presumptuously in the action of Maggie's conscience, perhaps for a
selfish end. but no!--he persuade himself his end be not selfish. he
have little hope that Maggie would ever return the strong feeling he
have for she; and it must be well for Maggie's future life, when
these petty family obstacle to her freedom have disappear, that the
present should not be entirely sacrifice, and that she should have

vulgar level of those she be now condemn to live with. if we only

can be justify; by adopt the point of view of a providence who
arrange result, or of a philosopher who trace they, we shall find
it possible to obtain perfect complacency in choose to do what be
most agreeable to we in the present moment. and it be in this way
that Philip justify his subtle effort to overcome Maggie's true
prompt against a concealment that would introduce doubleness into

natural claim on she. but there be a surplus of passion in he that
make he half independent of justify motif. his longing to see

the mental and bodily constitution have make pain predominate. he have
not his full share in the common good of man; he could not even pass
muster with the insignificant, but must be single out for pity, and
except from what be a matter of course with other. even to Maggie
he be an exception; it be clear that the thought of his be her
lover have never enter her mind.
do not think too hardly of Philip. ugly and deform people have great
need of unusual virtue, because they be likely to be extremely
uncomfortable without they; but the theory that unusual virtue spring
by a direct consequence out of personal disadvantage, as animal get
thick wool in severe climate, be perhaps a little overstrained. the
temptation of beauty be much dwelt upon, but I fancy they only bear
the same relation to those of ugliness, as the temptation to excess at
a feast, where the delight be varied for eye and ear as well as
palate, bear to the temptation that assail the desperation of
hunger. do not the Hunger Tower stand as the type of the utmost
trial to what be human in we?
Philip have never be soothe by that mother's love which flow out to
we in the great abundance because our need be great, which cling
to we the more tenderly because we be the less likely to be winner

indulgence toward he be mar by the keener perception of his
father's fault. keep aloof from all practical life as Philip have
be, and by nature half feminine in sensitiveness, he have some of the
woman's intolerant repulsion toward worldliness and the deliberate
pursuit of sensual enjoyment; and this one strong natural tie in his
life,--his relation as a son,--was like an aching limb to he. perhaps
there be inevitably something morbid in a human being who be in any
way unfavorably except from ordinary condition, until the good
force have have time to triumph; and it have rarely have time for that at
two-and-twenty. that force be present in Philip in much strength, but
the sun himself look feeble through the morning mist.

Deeps?"

fund of defiance, and keep her sense of error in abeyance. "you need
ask I no more question. we have be friendly a year. we have meet
and walk together often. he have lend I book."
"be that all?" say Tom, look straight at she with his frown.
Maggie pause a moment; then, determine to make an end of Tom's right
to accuse she of deceit, she say haughtily:
"no, not quite all. on Saturday he tell I that he love I. I donot
think of it before then; I have only thought of he as an old friend."
"and you _encourage_ he?" say Tom, with an expression of disgust.
"I tell he that I love he too."
Tom be silent a few moment, look on the ground and frown, with
his hand in his pocket. at last he look up and say coldly,--
"now, then, Maggie, there be but two course for you to take,--either
you vow solemnly to I, with your hand on my father's Bible, that you
will never have another meeting or speak another word in private with
Philip Wakem, or you refuse, and I tell my father everything; and this
month, when by my exertion he might be make happy once more, you will
cause he the blow of know that you be a disobedient, deceitful
daughter, who throw away her own respectability by clandestine
meeting with the son of a man that have help to ruin her father.
choose!" Tom end with cold decision, go up to the large Bible,
draw it forward, and open it at the fly-leaf, where the writing
be.
it be a crush alternative to Maggie.
"Tom," she say, urge out of pride into pleading, "donot ask I that.
I will promise you to give up all intercourse with Philip, if you will
let I see he once, or even only write to he and explain
everything,--to give it up as long as it would ever cause any pain to
my father. I feel something for Philip too. _he_ be not happy."
"I donot wish to hear anything of your feeling; I have say exactly
what I mean. choose, and quickly, lest my mother should come in."
"if I give you my word, that will be as strong a bond to I as if I
lay my hand on the Bible. I donot require that to bind I."
"do what _I_ require," say Tom. "I cannot trust you, Maggie. there be
no consistency in you. put your hand on this Bible, and say, 'I
renounce all private speech and intercourse with Philip Wakem from
this time forth.' else you will bring shame on we all, and grief on my
father; and what be the use of my exert myself and give up
everything else for the sake of pay my father's debt, if you be
to bring madness and vexation on he, just when he might be easy and
hold up his head once more?"
"oh, Tom, _will_ the debt be pay soon?" say Maggie, clasp her
hand, with a sudden flash of joy across her wretchedness.
"if thing turn out as I expect," say Tom. "but," he add, his voice
tremble with indignation, "while I have be contrive and work
that my father may have some peace of mind before he dies,--worke
for the respectability of our family,--you have do all you can to
destroy both."
Maggie feel a deep movement of compunction; for the moment, her mind
cease to contend against what she feel to be cruel and unreasonable,
and in her self-blame she justify her brother.
"Tom," she say in a low voice, "it be wrong of I; but I be so
lonely, and I be sorry for Philip. and I think enmity and hatred be
wicked."
"nonsense!" say Tom. "your duty be clear enough. say no more; but
promise, in the word I tell you."
"I _must_ speak to Philip once more."
"you will go with I now and speak to he."
"I give you my word not to meet he or write to he again without your
knowledge. that be the only thing I will say. I will put my hand on
the Bible if you like."
"say it, then."
Maggie lay her hand on the page of manuscript and repeat the
promise. Tom close the book, and say, "now let we go."
not a word be speak as they walk along. Maggie be suffer in
anticipation of what Philip be about to suffer, and dread the
galling word that would fall on he from Tom's lip; but she feel it
be in vain to attempt anything but submission. Tom have his terrible
clutch on her conscience and her deep dread; she writhe under the
demonstrable truth of the character he have give to her conduct, and
yet her whole soul rebel against it as unfair from its
incompleteness. he, meanwhile, feel the impetus of his indignation
divert toward Philip. he do not know how much of an old boyish
repulsion and of mere personal pride and animosity be concern in
the bitter severity of the word by which he mean to do the duty of a
son and a brother. Tom be not give to inquire subtly into his own
motif any more than into other matter of an intangible kind; he be
quite sure that his own motif as well as action be good, else he
would have have nothing to do with they.
Maggie's only hope be that something might, for the first time, have
prevent Philip from come. then there would be delay,--then she
might get Tom's permission to write to he. her heart beat with double
violence when they get under the Scotch fir. it be the last moment
of suspense, she think; Philip always meet she soon after she get
beyond they. but they pass across the more open green space, and
enter the narrow bushy path by the mound. another turning, and they
come so close upon he that both Tom and Philip stop suddenly
within a yard of each other. there be a moment's silence, in which
Philip dart a look of inquiry at Maggie's face. he see an answer
there, in the pale, part lip, and the terrified tension of the
large eye. her imagination, always rush extravagantly beyond an
immediate impression, see she tall, strong brother grasp the feeble
Philip bodily, crush he and trample on he.
"do you call this act the part of a man and a gentleman, sir?" Tom
say, in a voice of harsh scorn, as soon as Philip's eye be turn
on he again.
"what do you mean?" answer Philip, haughtily.
"mean? stand far from I, lest I should lay hand on you, and Iwill
tell you what I mean. I mean, take advantage of a young girl's
foolishness and ignorance to get she to have secret meeting with you.
I mean, dare to trifle with the respectability of a family that have
a good and honest name to support."
"I deny that," interrupt Philip, impetuously. "I could never trifle
with anything that affect your sister's happiness. she be dearer to
I than she be to you; I honor she more than you can ever honor she; I
would give up my life to she."
"donot talk high-flown nonsense to I, sir! do you mean to pretend
that you donot know it would be injurious to she to meet you here
week after week? do you pretend you have any right to make profession
of love to she, even if you have be a fit husband for she, when
neither her father nor your father would ever consent to a marriage
between you? and _you_,--_you_ to try and worm yourself into the
affection of a handsome girl who be not eighteen, and have be shut
out from the world by her father's misfortune! thatbe your crooked
notion of honor, be it? I call it base treachery; I call it take
advantage of circumstance to win whatbe too good for you,--what youwould
never get by fair mean."
"it be manly of you to talk in this way to _I_," say Philip,
bitterly, his whole frame shake by violent emotion. "giant have an
immemorial right to stupidity and insolent abuse. you be incapable
even of understand what I feel for your sister. I feel so much for
she that I could even desire to be at friendship with _you_."
"I should be very sorry to understand your feeling," say Tom, with
scorch contempt. "what I wish be that you should understand
_me_,--that I shall take care of _my_ sister, and that if you dare to
make the least attempt to come near she, or to write to she, or to
keep the slight hold on her mind, your puny, miserable body, that
ought to have put some modesty into your mind, shall not protect you.
Iwill thrash you; Iwill hold you up to public scorn. who wouldnot laugh
at the idea of _your_ turn lover to a fine girl?"
Tom and Maggie walk on in silence for some yard. he burst out, in a
convulse voice.
"Stay, Maggie!" say Philip, make a strong effort to speak. then
look at Tom, "you have drag your sister here, I suppose, that
she may stand by while you threaten and insult I. these naturally
seem to you the right mean to influence I. but you be mistaken.
let your sister speak. if she say she be bind to give I up, I shall
abide by her wish to the slight word."
"it be for my father's sake, Philip," say Maggie, imploringly. "Tom
threaten to tell my father, and he couldnot bear it; I have promise,
I have vow solemnly, that we will not have any intercourse without
my brother's knowledge."
"it be enough, Maggie. _I_ shall not change; but I wish you to hold
yourself entirely free. but trust I; remember that I can never seek
for anything but good to what belong to you."
"yes," say Tom, exasperate by this attitude of Philip's, "you can

her good before?"
"I did,--at some risk, perhaps. but I wish she to have a friend for
life,--who would cherish she, who would do she more justice than a
coarse and narrow-minded brother, that she have always lavish she
affection on."
"yes, my way of befriend she be different from your; and Iwill tell
you what be my way. Iwill save she from disobey and disgrace she
father; Iwill save she from throw herself away on you,--from make
herself a laughing-stock,--from be flout by a man like _your_
father, because shebe not good enough for his son. you know well
enough what sort of justice and cherish you be prepare for she.
Ibe not to be impose upon by fine word; I can see what action mean.
come away, Maggie."
he seize Maggie's right wrist as he speak, and she put out her left
hand. Philip clasp it an instant, with one eager look, and then
hurry away.
Tom and Maggie walk on in silence for some yard. he be still
hold her wrist tightly, as if he be compelling a culprit from the
scene of action. at last Maggie, with a violent snatch, draw her hand
away, and her pent-up, long-gather irritation burst into utterance.
"donot suppose that I think you be right, Tom, or that I bow to your
will. I despise the feeling you have show in speak to Philip; I
det your insulting, unmanly allusion to his deformity. you have
be reproach other people all your life; you have be always sure
you yourself be right. it be because you have not a mind large enough
to see that there be anything well than your own conduct and your
own petty aim."
"certainly," say Tom, coolly. "I donot see that your conduct be
well, or your aim either. if your conduct, and Philip Wakem's
conduct, have be right, why be you ashamed of its be know?
answer I that. I know what I have aim at in my conduct, and Ihave
succeed; pray, what good have your conduct bring to you or any one
else?"
"I donot want to defend myself," say Maggie, still with vehemence: "I
know Ihave be wrong,--often, continually. but yet, sometimes when I
have do wrong, it have be because I have feeling that you would be
the well for, if you have they. if _you_ be in fault ever, if you
have do anything very wrong, I should be sorry for the pain it
bring you; I should not want punishment to be heap on you. but you
have always enjoy punish I; you have always be hard and cruel
to I; even when I be a little girl, and always love you well than
any one else in the world, you would let I go cry to bed without
forgive I. you have no pity; you have no sense of your own
imperfection and your own sin. it be a sin to be hard; it be not
fitting for a mortal, for a Christian. you be nothing but a Pharisee.
you thank God for nothing but your own virtue; you think they be
great enough to win you everything else. you have not even a vision of
feeling by the side of which your shin virtue be mere darkness!"
"well," say Tom, with cold scorn, "if your feeling be so much
well than mine, let I see you show they in some other way than by
conduct thatbe likely to disgrace we all,--than by ridiculous flight
first into one extreme and then into another. pray, how have you show
your love, that you talk of, either to I or my father? by disobey
and deceive we. I have a different way of show my affection."
"because you be a man, Tom, and have power, and can do something in
the world."
"then, if you can do nothing, submit to those that can."
"so I _will_ submit to what I acknowledge and feel to be right. I will
submit even to what be unreasonable from my father, but I will not
submit to it from you. you boast of your virtue as if they purchase
you a right to be cruel and unmanly, as youhave be to-day. donot
suppose I would give up Philip Wakem in obedience to you. the
deformity you insult would make I cle to he and care for he the
more."
"very well; that be your view of thing." say Tom, more coldly than
ever; "you need say no more to show I what a wide distance there be
between we. let we remember that in future, and be silent."
Tom go back to St. Ogg's, to fulfill an appointment with his uncle
Deane, and receive direction about a journey on which he be to set
out the next morning.
Maggie go up to her own room to pour out all that indignant
remonstrance, against which Tom's mind be close bar, in bitter
tear. then, when the first burst of unsatisfie anger be go by,
come the recollection of that quiet time before the pleasure which have
end in to-day's misery have perturb the clearness and simplicity of
her life. she use to think in that time that she have make great
conquest, and win a lasting stand on serene height above worldly
temptation and conflict. and here she be down again in the thick of
a hot strife with her own and other' passion. life be not so short,
then, and perfect rest be not so near as she have dream when she be
two year young. there be more struggle for she, and perhaps more
fall. if she have feel that she be entirely wrong, and that Tom have
be entirely right, she could soon have recover more inward
harmony; but now her penitence and submission be constantly
obstruct by resentment that would present itself to she no otherwise
than as a just indignation. her heart bleed for Philip; she go on
recall the insult that have be fling at he with so vivid a
conception of what he have feel under they, that it be almost like a
sharp bodily pain to she, make she beat the floor with her foot and
tighten her finger on her palm.
and yet, how be it that she be now and then conscious of a certain
dim background of relief in the force separation from Philip? surely
it be only because the sense of a deliverance from concealment be
welcome at any cost.
Chapter VI
the Hard-Won Triumph
three week later, when Dorlcote Mill be at its prettiest moment in
all the year,--the great chestnut in blossom, and the grass all deep
and daisied,--tom Tulliver come home to it early than usual in the
evening, and as he pass over the bridge, he look with the old
deep-rooted affection at the respectable red brick house, which always
seem cheerful and invite outside, let the room be as bare and the
heart as sad as they might inside. there be a very pleasant light in
Tom's blue-gray eye as he glance at the house-windows; that fold in
his brow never disappear, but it be not unbecoming; it seem to imply
a strength of will that may possibly be without harshness, when the
eye and mouth have their gentle expression. his firm step become
quick, and the corner of his mouth rebel against the compression
which be mean to forbid a smile.
the eye in the parlor be not turn toward the bridge just then,
and the group there be sit in unexpectant silence,--mr. Tulliver
in his arm-chair, tired with a long ride, and ruminate with a worn
look, fix chiefly on Maggie, who be bend over her sewing while
her mother be make the tea.
they all look up with surprise when they hear the well-know foot.
"why, whatbe up now, Tom?" say his father. "yoube a bit early than
usual."
"oh, there be nothing more for I to do, so I come away. well,
mother!"
Tom go up to his mother and kiss she, a sign of unusual good-humor
with he. hardly a word or look have pass between he and Maggie in

prevent this from be noticeable to their parent.
"Father," say Tom, when they have finish tea, "do you know exactly
how much money there be in the tin box?"
"only a hundred and ninety-three pound," say Mr. Tulliver. "youhave
bring less o' late; but young fellow like to have their own way
with their money. though I donot do as I like before _i_ be of
age." he speak with rather timid discontent.
"be you quite sure thatbe the sum, father?" say Tom. "I wish you
would take the trouble to fetch the tin box down. I think you have
perhaps make a mistake."
"how should I make a mistake?" say his father, sharply. "Ihave count
it often enough; but I can fetch it, if you willnot believe I."
it be always an incident Mr. Tulliver like, in his gloomy life, to
fetch the tin box and count the money.
"donot go out of the room, mother," say Tom, as he see she move
when his father be go upstairs.
"and benot Maggie to go?" say Mrs. Tulliver; "because somebody must
take away the thing."
"just as she like," say Tom indifferently.
that be a cut word to Maggie. her heart have leap with the

could be pay; and Tom would have let she be absent when that news be
tell! but she carry away the tray and come back immediately. the
feeling of injury on her own behalf could not predominate at that
moment.
Tom draw to the corner of the table near his father when the tin box
be set down and open, and the red evening light fall on they
make conspicuous the worn, sour gloom of the dark-eyed father and the
suppress joy in the face of the fair-complexion son. the mother
and Maggie sit at the other end of the table, the one in blank
patience, the other in palpitating expectation.
Mr. Tulliver count out the money, set it in order on the table,
and then say, glance sharply at Tom:
"there now! you see I be right enough."
he pause, look at the money with bitter despondency.
"therebe more nor three hundred want; itwill be a fine while before
_i_ can save that. lose that forty-two pound wi' the corn be a sore
job. this worldbe be too many for I. itbe take four year to lay
_this_ by; itbe much if Ibe above ground for another four year. I must
trusten to you to pay them," he go on, with a trembling voice, "if
you keep I' the same mind now yoube come o' age. but yoube like
enough to bury I first."
he look up in Tom's face with a querulous desire for some assurance.
"no, father," say Tom, speak with energetic decision, though there
be tremor discernible in his voice too, "you will live to see the
debt all pay. you shall pay they with your own hand."
his tone imply something more than mere hopefulness or resolution. a
slight electric shock seem to pass through Mr. Tulliver, and he keep
his eye fix on Tom with a look of eager inquiry, while Maggie,
unable to restrain herself, rush to her father's side and kneel down
by he. Tom be silent a little while before he go on.
"a good while ago, my uncle Glegg lend I a little money to trade
with, and that have answer. I have three hundred and twenty pound in
the bank."
his mother's arm be round his neck as soon as the last word be
utter, and she say, half cry:
"oh, my boy, I know youwould make iverything right again, when you get a
man."
but his father be silent; the flood of emotion hem in all power of
speech. both Tom and Maggie be strike with fear lest the shock of
joy might even be fatal. but the blessed relief of tear come. the
broad chest heave, the muscle of the face give way, and the

"Bessy, you must come and kiss I now--the lad have make you amend.
youwill see a bit o' comfort again, belike."
when she have kiss he, and he have hold her hand a minute, his
thought go back to the money.
"I wish youwould bring I the money to look at, Tom," he say,
finger the sovereign on the table; "I should ha' feel surer."
