appoint the creditor to meet to-morrow at the Golden Lion, and he
have order a dinner for they at two o'clock. my uncle Glegg and he
will both be there. it be advertise in the 'Messenger' on Saturday."
"then Wakem know on't!" say Mr. Tulliver, his eye kindling with
triumphant fire. "ah!" he go on, with a long-draw guttural
enunciation, take out his snuff-box, the only luxury he have leave
himself, and tap it with something of his old air of defiance.
"Iwill get from under _his_ thumb now, though I _must_ leave the old
mill. I think I could ha' hold out to die here--but I can't----we've
get a glass o' nothing in the house, have we, Bessy?"
"yes," say Mrs. Tulliver, draw out her much-reduce bunch of key,
"therebe some brandy sister Deane bring I when I be ill."
"get it I, then; get it I. I feel a bit weak."
"Tom, my lad," he say, in a strong voice, when he have take some

you as get the good part o' the money. theywill see Ibe honest at last,
and ha' get an honest son. ah! Wakem 'ud be fine and glad to have a
son like mine,--a fine straight fellow,--i'stead o' that poor crooked
creatur! youwill prosper I' the world, my lad; youwill maybe see the day
when Wakem and his son 'ull be a round or two below you. youwill like
enough be ta'en into partnership, as your uncle Deane be before

hinder your get rich. and if ever yoube rich enough--mind
this--try and get th' old mill again."
Mr. Tulliver throw himself back in his chair; his mind, which have so
long be the home of nothing but bitter discontent and foreboding,
suddenly fill, by the magic of joy, with vision of good fortune.
but some subtle influence prevent he from foresee the good
fortune as happen to himself.
"shake hand wi' I, my lad," he say, suddenly put out his hand.
"itbe a great thing when a man can be proud as hebe get a good son.
Ihave have _that_ luck."
Tom never live to taste another moment so delicious as that; and
Maggie couldnot help forget her own grievance. Tom _be_ good;
and in the sweet humility that spring in we all in moment of true
admiration and gratitude, she feel that the fault he have to pardon in
she have never be redeem, as his fault be. she feel no jealousy
this evening that, for the first time, she seem to be throw into
the background in her father's mind.
there be much more talk before bedtime. Mr. Tulliver naturally want
to hear all the particular of Tom's trading adventure, and he
listen with grow excitement and delight. he be curious to know
what have be say on every occasion; if possible, what have be
think; and Bob Jakin's part in the business throw he into peculiar

under Mr. Tulliver's knowledge, be recall with that sense of

reminiscence of the childhood of great man.
it be well that there be this interest of narrative to keep under
the vague but fierce sense of triumph over Wakem, which would
otherwise have be the channel his joy would have rush into with

threat of its ultimate mastery, in sudden burst of irrelevant

it be long before Mr. Tulliver get to sleep that night; and the

"whatbe the matter, Mr. Tulliver?" say his wife. he look at she,

"Ah!--I be dream--do I make a noise?--i think Iwould got hold of

Chapter VII
a day of reckoning
Mr. Tulliver be an essentially sober man,--able to take his glass and
not averse to it, but never exceed the bound of moderation. he have
naturally an active Hotspur temperament, which do not crave liquid
fire to set it aglow; his impetuosity be usually equal to an exciting
occasion without any such reinforcement; and his desire for the
brandy-and-water imply that the too sudden joy have fall with a
dangerous shock on a frame depress by four year of gloom and
unaccustomed hard fare. but that first doubtful tottering moment
pass, he seem to gather strength with his gathering excitement;
and the next day, when he be seat at table with his creditor, his
eye kindling and his cheek flush with the consciousness that he be
about to make an honorable figure once more, he look more like the
proud, confident, warm-hearted, and warm-temper Tulliver of old
time than might have seem possible to any one who have meet he a
week before, rid along as have be his willnot for the last four year
since the sense of failure and debt have be upon him,--with his head
hang down, cast brief, unwilling look on those who force
themselves on his notice. he make his speech, assert his honest
principle with his old confident eagerness, allude to the rascal
and the luck that have be against he, but that he have triumph
over, to some extent, by hard effort and the aid of a good son; and
wind up with the story of how Tom have get the good part of the
needful money. but the streak of irritation and hostile triumph seem
to melt for a little while into purer fatherly pride and pleasure,

could hardly have be brief. he thank the gentlman for the honor

why do he not happen to meet Wakem? the want of that coincidence
vex he, and set his mind at work in an irritate way. perhaps
Wakem be go out of town to-day on purpose to avoid see or
hear anything of an honorable action which might well cause he
some unpleasant twinge. if Wakem be to meet he then, Mr. Tulliver

little by his cool, domineering impudence. he would know by and by
that an honest man be not go to serve _he_ any long, and lend
his honesty to fill a pocket already over-full of dishonest gain.
perhaps the luck be begin to turn; perhaps the Devil donot
always hold the good card in this world.
simmer in this way, Mr. Tulliver approach the yardgate of

they on a fine black horse. they meet about fifty yard from the gate,
between the great chestnut and elm and the high bank.
"Tulliver," say Wakem, abruptly, in a haughty tone than usual,
"what a fool's trick you did,--spreade those hard lump on that far
Close! I tell you how it would be; but you man never learn to farm
with any method."
"oh!" say Tulliver, suddenly boil up; "get somebody else to farm
for you, then, as'll ask _you_ to teach he."
"you have be drink, I suppose," say Wakem, really believe that
this be the meaning of Tulliver's flushed face and sparkle eye.
"no, Ihave not be drink," say Tulliver; "I want no drinking to
help I make up my mind as Iwill serve no long under a scoundrel."
"very well! you may leave my premise to-morrow, then; hold your
insolent tongue and let I pass." (Tulliver be back his horse
across the road to hem Wakem in.)
"no, I _sha'n't_ let you pass," say Tulliver, get fiercer. "I
shall tell you what I think of you first. yoube too big a raskill to
get hang--you're----"
"let I pass, you ignorant brute, or Iwill ride over you."
Mr. Tulliver, spur his horse and raise his whip, make a rush
forward; and Wakem's horse, rear and staggering backward, throw his
rider from the saddle and send he sideways on the ground. Wakem have
have the presence of mind to loose the bridle at once, and as the horse
only stagger a few pace and then stand still, he might have rise
and remount without more inconvenience than a bruise and a shake.
but before he could rise, Tulliver be off his horse too. the sight of
the long-hat predominant man down, and in his power, throw he into
a frenzy of triumphant vengeance, which seem to give he
preternatural agility and strength. he rush on Wakem, who be in the
act of try to recover his foot, grasp he by the left arm so as
to press Wakem's whole weight on the right arm, which rest on the
ground, and flog he fiercely across the back with his riding-whip.
Wakem shout for help, but no help come, until a woman's scream be
hear, and the cry of "Father, father!"
suddenly, Wakem feel, something have arrest Mr. Tulliver's arm; for
the flogging cease, and the grasp on his own arm be relaxed.
"get away with you--go!" say Tulliver, angrily. but it be not to
Wakem that he speak. slowly the lawyer rise, and, as he turn his
head, see that Tulliver's arm be be hold by a girl, rather by
the fear of hurt the girl that clung to he with all her young
might.
"oh, Luke--mother--come and help Mr. Wakem!" Maggie cry, as she
hear the long-for footstep.
"help I on to that low horse," say Wakem to Luke, "then I shall
perhaps manage; though--confound it--I think this arm be sprain."
with some difficulty, Wakem be heave on to Tulliver's horse. then he
turn toward the miller and say, with white rage, "youwill suffer for
this, sir. your daughter be a witness that youhave assault I."
"I donot care," say Mr. Tulliver, in a thick, fierce voice; "go and
show your back, and tell them I thrash you. tell them Ihave make thing
a bit more even I' the world."
"ride my horse home with I," say Wakem to Luke. "by the Tofton
Ferry, not through the town."
"Father, come in!" say Maggie, imploringly. then, see that Wakem
have ride off, and that no further violence be possible, she
slacken her hold and burst into hysteric sob, while poor Mrs.
Tulliver stand by in silence, quiver with fear. but Maggie become
conscious that as she be slacken she hold her father be begin
to grasp she and lean on she. the surprise check her sob.
"I feel ill--faintish," he say. "help I in, Bessy--i'm giddy--i've a
pain I' the head."
he walk in slowly, prop by his wife and daughter and totter
into his arm-chair. the almost purple flush have give way to paleness,
and his hand be cold.
"hadnot we well send for the doctor?" say Mrs. Tulliver.
he seem to be too faint and suffering to hear she; but presently,
when she say to Maggie, "go and seek for somebody to fetch the
doctor," he look up at she with full comprehension, and say,
"Doctor? no--no doctor. itbe my head, thatbe all. help I to bed."
Sad end to the day that have rise on they all like a beginning of
well time! but mingle seed must bear a mingled crop.
in half an hour after his father have lie down Tom come home. Bob
Jakin be with he, come to congratulate "the old master," not without
some excusable pride that he have have his share in bring about Mr.
Tom's good luck; and Tom have think his father would like nothing
well, as a finish to the day, than a talk with Bob. but now Tom
could only spend the evening in gloomy expectation of the unpleasant
consequence that must follow on this mad outbreak of his father's
long-smother hate. after the painful news have be tell, he sit in
silence; he have not spirit or inclination to tell his mother and
sister anything about the dinner; they hardly care to ask it.
apparently the mingled thread in the web of their life be so
curiously twist together that there could be no joy without a sorrow
come close upon it. Tom be deject by the thought that his
exemplary effort must always be baffle by the wrong-doing of other;
Maggie be live through, over and over again, the agony of the
moment in which she have rush to throw herself on her father's arm,
with a vague, shudder foreboding of wretched scene to come. not
one of the three feel any particular alarm about Mr. Tulliver's
health; the symptom do not recall his former dangerous attack, and
it seem only a necessary consequence that his violent passion and
effort of strength, after many hour of unusual excitement, should
have make he feel ill. rest would probably cure he.
Tom, tired out by his active day, fall asleep soon, and sleep soundly;
it seem to he as if he have only just come to bed, when he wake to
see his mother stand by he in the gray light of early morning.
"my boy, you must get up this minute; Ihave send for the doctor, and
your father want you and Maggie to come to he."
"be he bad, mother?"
"hebe be very ill all night with his head, but he donot say itbe
bad; he only say suddenly, 'bessy, fetch the boy and girl. tell them
to make haste.'"
Maggie and Tom throw on their clothe hastily in the chill gray light,
and reach their father's room almost at the same moment. he be
watch for they with an expression of pain on his brow, but with
sharpen, anxious consciousness in his eye. Mrs. Tulliver stand at
the foot of the bed, frightened and tremble, look wear and age
from disturbed rest. Maggie be at the bedside first, but her father's
glance be toward Tom, who come and stand next to she.
"Tom, my lad, itbe come upon I as I sha'n't get up again. this
world's be too many for I, my lad, but youhave do what you could
to make thing a bit even. shake hand wi' I again, my lad, before I
go away from you."
the father and son clasp hand and look at each other an instant.
then Tom say, try to speak firmly,--
"have you any wish, father--that I can fulfil, when----"
"ay, my lad--you'll try and get the old mill back."
"yes, father."
"and therebe your mother--you'll try and make her amend, all you can,
for my bad luck--and therebe the little wench----"
the father turn his eye on Maggie with a still more eager look,
while she, with a burst heart, sink on her knee, to be close to
the dear, time-wear face which have be present with she through long
year, as the sign of her deep love and hard trial.
"you must take care of she, Tom--don't you fret, my wench--there'll
come somebody as'll love you and take your part--and you must be good
to she, my lad. I be good to _my_ sister. kiss I, Maggie.--Come,
Bessy.--You'll manage to pay for a brick grave, Tom, so as your mother
and I can lie together."
he look away from they all when he have say this, and lie silent for
some minute, while they stand watch he, not dare to move. the
morning light be grow clear for they, and they could see the
heaviness gathering in his face, and the dulness in his eye. but at
last he look toward Tom and said,--
"I have my turn--I beat he. that be nothing but fair. I never want
anything but what be fair."
"but, father, dear father," say Maggie, an unspeakable anxiety
predominate over her grief, "you forgive he--you forgive every one
now?"
he do not move his eye to look at she, but he said,--
"no, my wench. I donot forgive he. whatbe forgiving to do? I cannot
love a raskill----"
his voice have become thick; but he want to say more, and move his
lip again and again, struggle in vain to speak. at length the word
force their way.
"do God forgive raskills?--but if he do, he willnot be hard wi' I."
his hand move uneasily, as if he want they to remove some
obstruction that weigh upon he. two or three time there fall from
he some break words,--
"this world's--too many--honest man--puzzling----"
soon they merge into mere muttering; the eye have cease to discern;
and then come the final silence.
but not of death. for an hour or more the chest heave, the loud, hard
breathing continue, get gradually slow, as the cold dew
gather on the brow.
at last there be total stillness, and poor Tulliver's dimly light
soul have forever cease to be vex with the painful riddle of this
world.
help be come now; Luke and his wife be there, and Mr. Turnbull have
arrive, too late for everything but to say, "this be death."
Tom and Maggie go downstairs together into the room where their
father's place be empty. their eye turn to the same spot, and
Maggie spoke,--
"Tom, forgive I--let we always love each other"; and they clung and
weep together.

_the Great Temptation_
chapter I
a Duet in Paradise
the well-furnish drawing-room, with the open grand piano, and the
pleasant outlook down a sloping garden to a boat-house by the side of
the Floss, be Mr. Deane's. the neat little lady in mourning, whose
light-brown ringlet be fall over the colored embroidery with
which her finger be busy, be of course Lucy Deane; and the fine

young lady's foot be no other than Mr. Stephen Guest, whose diamond
ring, attar of rose, and air of _nonchalant_ leisure, at twelve

large oil-mill and the most extensive wharf in St. Ogg's. there be
an apparent triviality in the action with the scissor, but your
discernment perceive at once that there be a design in it which make
it eminently worthy of a large-headed, long-limbe young man; for you
see that Lucy want the scissor, and be compel, reluctant as she

playfully down on the face that be so very nearly on a level with her

"my scissor, please, if you can renounce the great pleasure of
persecute my poor Minny."
the foolish scissor have slip too far over the knuckle, it seem,
and Hercules hold out his entrapped finger hopelessly.
"confound the scissor! the oval lie the wrong way. please draw they
off for I."
"draw they off with your other hand," say Miss Lucy, roguishly.
"oh, but thatbe my left hand; Ibe not leave-handed."
Lucy laugh, and the scissor be draw off with gentle touch from
tiny tip, which naturally dispose Mr. Stephen for a repetition _da

he may get they into his possession again.

growl again. sit up and behave properly, and then I will tell you

"what be that?" say Stephen, throw himself back and hang his

his portrait, which would have represent a rather strike young man
of five-and-twenty, with a square forehead, short dark-brown hair,
stand erect, with a slight wave at the end, like a thick crop of
corn, and a half-ardent, half-sarcastic glance from under his

"yes, very. guess."
"you be go to change Minny's diet, and give he three ratafia
soak in a dessert-spoonful of cream daily?"
"quite wrong."
"well, then, Dr. Kenn have be preach against buckram, and you
lady have all be send he a roundrobin, say, 'this be a hard
doctrine; who can bear it?'"
"for shame!" say Lucy, adjust her little mouth gravely. "it be
rather dull of you not to guess my news, because it be about something
I mention to you not very long ago."
"but you have mention many thing to I not long ago. do your
feminine tyranny require that when you say the thing you mean be one
of several thing, I should know it immediately by that mark?"
"yes, I know you think I be silly."
"I think you be perfectly charming."
"and my silliness be part of my charm?"
"I donot say _that_."
"but I know you like woman to be rather insipid. Philip Wakem betray
you; he say so one day when you be not here."
"oh, I know Phil be fierce on that point; he make it quite a personal

"by the by," say Lucy, pause in her work, "it have just occur to

"yes; that be my news, which you have forget. shebe go to leave

since her father's death; and she will stay with I a month or
two,--many month, I hope."
"and be I bind to be pleased at that news?"
"oh no, not at all," say Lucy, with a little air of pique. "_i_ be
pleased, but that, of course, be no reason why _you_ should be
pleased. there be no girl in the world I love so well as my cousin

"and you will be inseparable I suppose, when she come. there will be
no possibility of a _tete-a-tete_ with you any more, unless you can
find an admirer for she, who will pair off with she occasionally. what
be the ground of dislike to Philip? he might have be a resource."
"it be a family quarrel with Philip's father. there be very painful
circumstance, I believe. I never quite understand they, or know they

buy Dorlcote Mill, my uncle's old place, where he always live. you

"no," say Stephen, with rather supercilious indifference. "Ihave

that detach, disjoint way."

quite mistaken in many way; his mind have become embitter. but Tom

thing. they have have so much, so very much trouble. Maggie be at

think. she have be in a dreary situation in a school since uncle's
death, because she be determined to be independent, and not live with
aunt Pullet; and I could hardly wish she to come to I then, because
dear mamma be ill, and everything be so sad. that be why I want she
to come to I now, and have a long, long holiday."
"very sweet and angelic of you," say Stephen, look at she with an

quality of her mother."
"poor aunty! you be cruel to ridicule she. she be very valuable to

"yes, but in point of companionship one would prefer that she should

no agreeable proxy of that kind,--a fat, blond girl, with round blue

"oh yes!" exclaim Lucy, laugh wickedly, and clap her hand,

"no, indeed; Ibe only guess what Mrs. Tulliver's daughter must be;
and then if she be to banish Philip, our only apology for a tenor,

"but I hope that may not be. I think I will ask you to call on Philip

Stephen master the little hand that be stray toward the table,

wear off this fine edge of susceptibility; it be jasmine gather
and present in a large bouquet.

appearance and manner," say the cunning Lucy, move to reach her

have not round eye; and he be as far as possible from star at

"oh, I suppose he be like the father; he seem to be as proud as

grow my pride and happiness.'"

surely the only courtship unshaken by doubt and fear must be that in
which the lover can sing together. the sense of mutual fitness that
spring from the two deep note fulfil expectation just at the
right moment between the note of the silvery soprano, from the
perfect accord of descend third and fifth, from the preconcerte
love chase of a fugue, be likely enough to supersede any immediate
demand for less impassioned form of agreement. the contralto will not
care to catechise the bass; the tenor will foresee no embarrassing
dearth of remark in evening spend with the lovely soprano. in the
province, too, where music be so scarce in that remote time, how
could the musical people avoid fall in love with each other? even
political principle must have be in danger of relaxation under such
circumstance; and the violin, faithful to rotten borough, must have
be tempt to fraternize in a demoralizing way with a reform
violoncello. in that case, the linnet-throate soprano and the
full-toned bass singing,--
"with thee delight be ever new,
with thee be life incessant bliss,"
believe what they sing all the more _because_ they sing it.

Stephen deliver with admirable ease the deep note represent the

that he leap out and scamper under the remotest _chiffonni_, as

when he have do singing, and smile down from his tall height, with

"you will not be able to call on Philip, then? it be of no

"good-bye, then." and there be that slight pressure of the hand, and
momentary meeting of the eye, which will often leave a little lady

immediately when the door be close, and with an inclination to walk

and somewhat rank odor of egoism. even now, that she be walk up and

make two current of feeling or imagination seem simultaneous, be

that no one make any account of, she be to be surprised with the

revery about her own happy love-affair. with this thought she
walk toward the door, but pause there.
"whatbe the matter, then, Minny?" she say, stoop in answer to some
whimpering of that small quadruped, and lift his glossy head
against her pink cheek. "do you think I be go without you? come,
then, let we go and see Sinbad."
Sinbad be Lucy's chestnut horse, that she always feed with her own
hand when he be turn out in the paddock. she be fond of feed
dependent creature, and know the private taste of all the animal
about the house, delight in the little rippling sound of her
canary when their beak be busy with fresh seed, and in the small
nibble pleasure of certain animal which, lest she should appear
too trivial, I will here call "the more familiar rodent."
be not Stephen Guest right in his decide opinion that this slim
maiden of eighteen be quite the sort of wife a man would not be
likely to repent of marrying,--a woman who be love and thoughtful
for other woman, not give they Judas-kiss with eye askance on
their welcome defect, but with real care and vision for their
half-hide pain and mortification, with long ruminate enjoyment
of little pleasure prepare for they? perhaps the emphasis of his
admiration do not fall precisely on this rare quality in she;
perhaps he approve his own choice of she chiefly because she do not
strike he as a remarkable rarity. a man like his wife to be pretty;
well, Lucy be pretty, but not to a madden extent. a man like his
wife to be accomplish, gentle, affectionate, and not stupid; and
Lucy have all these qualification. Stephen be not surprised to find
himself in love with she, and be conscious of excellent judgment in
prefer she to Miss Leyburn, the daughter of the county member,
although Lucy be only the daughter of his father's subordinate
partner; besides, he have have to defy and overcome a slight
unwillingness and disappointment in his father and sisters,--a
circumstance which give a young man an agreeable consciousness of his
own dignity. Stephen be aware that he have sense and independence
enough to choose the wife who be likely to make he happy, unbiassed
by any indirect consideration. he mean to choose Lucy; she be a
little darling, and exactly the sort of woman he have always admire.
Chapter II
First Impressions
"he be very clever, Maggie," say Lucy. she be kneel on a
footstool at Maggie's foot, after place that dark lady in the large
crimson-velvet chair. "I feel sure you will like he. I hope you
will."
"I shall be very difficult to please," say Maggie, smile, and
hold up one of Lucy's long curl, that the sunlight might shine
through it. "a gentleman who think he be good enough for Lucy must
expect to be sharply criticise."
"indeed, hebe a great deal too good for I. and sometimes, when he be
away, I almost think it cannot really be that he love I. but I can
never doubt it when he be with I, though I couldnot bear any one but
you to know that I feel in that way, Maggie."
"oh, then, if I disapprove of he you can give he up, since you be
not engage," say Maggie, with playful gravity.
"I would rather not be engage. when people be engage, they begin to
think of be marry soon," say Lucy, too thoroughly preoccupy to
notice Maggie's joke; "and I should like everything to go on for a
long while just as it be. sometimes I be quite frightened lest Stephen
should say that he have speak to papa; and from something that fall
from papa the other day, I feel sure he and Mr. Guest be expect
that. and Stephen's sister be very civil to I now. at first, I
think they donot like his pay I attention; and that be natural.
it _do_ seem out of keep that I should ever live in a great place
like the Park House, such a little insignificant thing as I be."
"but people be not expect to be large in proportion to the house
they live in, like snail," say Maggie, laugh. "pray, be Mr.
Guest's sister giantess?"
"oh no; and not handsome,--that be, not very," say Lucy,
half-penitent at this uncharitable remark. "but _he_ be--at least he
be generally consider very handsome."
"though you be unable to share that opinion?"
"oh, I donot know," say Lucy, blush pink over brow and neck. "it
be a bad plan to raise expectation; you will perhaps be disappoint.
but I have prepare a charming surprise for _he;_ I shall have a
glorious laugh against he. I shall not tell you what it be, though."
Lucy rise from her knee and go to a little distance, hold she
pretty head on one side, as if she have be arrange Maggie for a
portrait, and wish to judge of the general effect.
"stand up a moment, Maggie."
"what be your pleasure now?" say Maggie, smile languidly as she
rise from her chair and look down on her slight, aerial cousin,
whose figure be quite subordinate to her faultless drapery of silk
and crape.
Lucy keep she contemplative attitude a moment or two in silence, and
then said,--
"I cannot think what witchery it be in you, Maggie, that make you look
well in shabby clothe; though you really must have a new dress now.
but do you know, last night I be try to fancy you in a handsome,
fashionable dress, and do what I would, that old limp merino would
come back as the only right thing for you. I wonder if Marie
Antoinette look all the grander when her gown be darn at the

unnoticeable. I should be a mere rag."
"oh, quite," say Maggie, with mock gravity. "you would be liable to
be sweep out of the room with the cobwebs and carpet-dust, and to find
yourself under the grate, like Cinderella. maynot I sit down now?"
"yes, now you may," say Lucy, laugh. then, with an air of serious
reflection, unfasten her large jet brooch, "but you must change
brooch, Maggie; that little butterfly look silly on you."
"but willnot that mar the charming effect of my consistent shabbiness?"

never get a well situation without more accomplishment."

sometimes at the sight of happy people. I think I get bad as I get
old, more selfish. that seem very dreadful."
"now, Maggie!" say Lucy, in a tone of remonstrance, "I donot believe

"well, perhaps it be," say Maggie, resolutely clear away the

Maggie take up the "Sketch Book," which lie by she on the table.

Lucy hurry out of the room, but Maggie do not take the opportunity

Chapter III
Confidential Moments
when Maggie go up to her bedroom that night, it appear that she
be not at all inclined to undress. she set down her candle on the
first table that present itself, and begin to walk up and down her
room, which be a large one, with a firm, regular, and rather rapid
step, which show that the exercise be the instinctive vent of
strong excitement. her eye and cheek have an almost feverish
brilliancy; her head be throw backward, and her hand be clasp
with the palm outward, and with that tension of the arm which be apt
to accompany mental absorption.
have anything remarkable happen?
nothing that you be not likely to consider in the high degree
unimportant. she have be hear some fine music sing by a fine bass
voice,--but then it be sing in a provincial, amateur fashion, such as
would have leave a critical ear much to desire. and she be conscious
of have be look at a great deal, in rather a furtive manner,
from beneath a pair of well-mark horizontal eyebrow, with a glance
that seem somehow to have catch the vibratory influence of the
voice. such thing could have have no perceptible effect on a
thoroughly well-educate young lady, with a perfectly balanced mind,
who have have all the advantage of fortune, training, and refine
society. but if Maggie have be that young lady, you would probably
have know nothing about she: her life would have have so few
vicissitude that it could hardly have be write; for the happy
woman, like the happy nation, have no history.
in poor Maggie's highly-string, hungry nature,--just come away from a
third-rate schoolroom, with all its jarring sound and petty round of
tasks,--these apparently trivial cause have the effect of rouse and
exalt her imagination in a way that be mysterious to herself. it
be not that she think distinctly of Mr. Stephen Guest, or dwelt on
the indication that he look at she with admiration; it be rather
that she feel the half-remote presence of a world of love and beauty
and delight, make up of vague, mingle image from all the poetry and
romance she have ever read, or have ever weave in her dreamy revery.
her mind glance back once or twice to the time when she have court
privation, when she have think all longing, all impatience be
subdue; but that condition seem irrecoverably go, and she
recoil from the remembrance of it. no prayer, no strive now, would
bring back that negative peace; the battle of her life, it seem, be
not to be decide in that short and easy way,--by perfect renunciation
at the very threshold of her youth.
the music be vibrate in her still,--purcell's music, with its wild
passion and fancy,--and she could not stay in the recollection of that
bare, lonely past. she be in her bright aerial world again, when a
little tap come at the door; of course it be her cousin, who enter
in ample white dressing-gown.
"why, Maggie, you naughty child, havenot you begin to undress?" say
Lucy, in astonishment. "I promise not to come and talk to you,
because I think you must be tired. but here you be, look as if
you be ready to dress for a ball. come, come, get on your
dressing-gown and unplait your hair."
"well, _you_ be not very forward," retort Maggie, hastily reach
her own pink cotton gown, and look at Lucy's light-brown hair
brush back in curly disorder.
"oh, I have not much to do. I shall sit down and talk to you till I
see you be really on the way to bed."
while Maggie stand and unplaite her long black hair over her pink
drapery, Lucy sit down near the toilette-table, watch she with
affectionate eye, and head a little aside, like a pretty spaniel. if
it appear to you at all incredible that young lady should be lead on
to talk confidentially in a situation of this kind, I will beg you to
remember that human life furnish many exceptional case.
"you really _have_ enjoy the music to-night, havenot you Maggie?"
"oh yes, that be what prevent I from feel sleepy. I think I
should have no other mortal want, if I could always have plenty of
music. it seem to infuse strength into my limb, and idea into my
brain. life seem to go on without effort, when I be fill with
music. at other time one be conscious of carry a weight."
"and Stephen have a splendid voice, havenot he?"
"well, perhaps we be neither of we judge of that," say Maggie,
laughing, as she seat herself and toss her long hair back. "you
be not impartial, and _i_ think any barrel-organ splendid."
"but tell I what you think of he, now. tell I exactly; good and bad
too."
"oh, I think you should humiliate he a little. a lover should not be
so much at ease, and so self-confident. he ought to tremble more."
"Nonsense, Maggie! as if any one could tremble at I! you think he be
conceited, I see that. but you donot dislike he, do you?"
"dislike he! no. be I in the habit of see such charming people,
that I should be very difficult to please? besides, how could I
dislike any one that promise to make you happy, my dear thing!"
Maggie pinch Lucy's dimpled chin.
"we shall have more music to-morrow evening," say Lucy, look happy
already, "for Stephen will bring Philip Wakem with he."
"oh, Lucy, I cannot see he," say Maggie, turn pale. "at least, I
could not see he without Tom's leave."
"be Tom such a tyrant as that?" say Lucy, surprised. "Iwill take the
responsibility, then,--tell he it be my fault."
"but, dear," say Maggie, falteringly, "I promise Tom very solemnly,
before my father's death,--i promise he I would not speak to Philip
without his knowledge and consent. and I have a great dread of open
the subject with tom,--of get into a quarrel with he again."
"but I never hear of anything so strange and unreasonable. what harm
can poor Philip have do? may I speak to Tom about it?"
"oh no, pray donot, dear," say Maggie. "Iwill go to he myself
to-morrow, and tell he that you wish Philip to come. Ihave think
before of ask he to absolve I from my promise, but Ihave not have
the courage to determine on it."
they be both silent for some moment, and then Lucy said,--
"Maggie, you have secret from I, and I have none from you."
Maggie look meditatively away from Lucy. then she turn to she and
say, "I _should_ like to tell you about Philip. but, Lucy, you must
not betray that you know it to any one--least of all to Philip
himself, or to Mr. Stephen Guest."
the narrative last long, for Maggie have never before know the
relief of such an outpouring; she have never before tell Lucy anything
of her inmost life; and the sweet face bent toward she with
sympathetic interest, and the little hand press her, encourage
she to speak on. on two point only she be not expansive. she do not
betray fully what still rankle in her mind as Tom's great
offence,--the insult he have heap on Philip. angry as the
remembrance still make she, she could not bear that any one else
should know it at all, both for Tom's sake and Philip's. and she could
not bear to tell Lucy of the last scene between her father and Wakem,
though it be this scene which she have ever since feel to be a new
barrier between herself and Philip. she merely say, she see now that
Tom be, no the whole, right in regard any prospect of love and
marriage between she and Philip as put out of the question by the
relation of the two family. of course Philip's father would never
consent.
"there, Lucy, you have have my story," say Maggie, smile, with the
tear in her eye. "you see I be like Sir Andrew Aguecheek. _I_ be
adore once."
"ah, now I see how it be you know Shakespeare and everything, and have
learn so much since you leave school; which always seem to I
witchcraft before,--part of your general uncanniness," say Lucy.
she muse a little with her eye downward, and then add, look at
Maggie, "it be very beautiful that you should love Philip; I never
think such a happiness would befall he. and in my opinion, you
ought not to give he up. there be obstacle now; but they may be
do away with in time."
Maggie shake her head.
"yes, yes," persist Lucy; "I cannot help be hopeful about it.
there be something romantic in it,--out of the common way,--just what
everything that happen to you ought to be. and Philip will adore you
like a husband in a fairy tale. oh, I shall puzzle my small brain to
contrive some plot that will bring everybody into the right mind, so
that you may marry Philip when I marry--somebody else. wouldnot that
be a pretty ending to all my poor, poor Maggie's trouble?"
Maggie try to smile, but shiver, as if she feel a sudden chill.
"ah, dear, you be cold," say Lucy. "you must go to bed; and so must
I. I dare not think what time it be."
they kiss each other, and Lucy go away, possess of a confidence
which have a strong influence over her subsequent impression. Maggie
have be thoroughly sincere; her nature have never find it easy to be
otherwise. but confidence be sometimes blind, even when they be
sincere.
Chapter IV
Brother and Sister
Maggie be oblige to go to Tom's lodging in the middle of the day,
when he would be come in to dinner, else she would not have find
he at home. he be not lodge with entire stranger. our friend Bob
Jakin have, with Mumps's tacit consent, take not only a wife about
eight month ago, but also one of those queer old house, pierce with
surprising passage, by the water-side, where, as he observe, his
wife and mother could keep themselves out of mischief by let out
two "pleasure-boat," in which he have invest some of his saving,
and by take in a lodger for the parlor and spare bedroom. under
these circumstance, what could be well for the interest of all
party, sanitary consideration apart, than that the lodger should be
Mr. Tom?
it be Bob's wife who open the door to Maggie. she be a tiny woman,
with the general physiognomy of a dutch doll, look, in comparison
with Bob's mother, who fill up the passage in the rear, very much
like one of those human figure which the artist find conveniently
stand near a colossal statue to show the proportion. the tiny
woman curtsy and look up at Maggie with some awe as soon as she
have open the door; but the word, "be my brother at home?" which
Maggie utter smilingly, make her turn round with sudden excitement,
and say,--
"eh, mother, mother--tell Bob!--it's Miss Maggie! come in, Miss, for
goodness do," she go on, open a side door, and endeavor to
flatten her person against the wall to make the utmost space for the
visitor.
sad recollection crowd on Maggie as she enter the small parlor,
which be now all that poor Tom have to call by the name of
"home,"--that name which have once, so many year ago, mean for both
of they the same sum of dear familiar object. but everything be not
strange to she in this new room; the first thing her eye dwelt on be
the large old Bible, and the sight be not likely to disperse the old
memory. she stand without speak.
"if you please to take the privilege o' sit down, Miss," say Mrs.
Jakin, rub her apron over a perfectly clean chair, and then
lift up the corner of that garment and hold it to her face with
an air of embarrassment, as she look wonderingly at Maggie.
"Bob be at home, then?" say Maggie, recover herself, and smile
at the bashful dutch doll.
"yes, Miss; but I think he must be wash and dress himself; Iwill
go and see," say Mrs. Jakin, disappear.
but she presently come back walk with new courage a little way
behind her husband, who show the brilliancy of his blue eye and
regular white tooth in the doorway, bow respectfully.
"how do you do, Bob?" say Maggie, come forward and put out her

come another day on purpose for that, if she will let I. but I be
oblige to come to-day to speak to my brother."
"hewill be in before long, Miss. hebe do finely, Mr. Tom be; hewill
be one o' the first man hereabouts,--you'll see that."
"well, Bob, Ibe sure hewill be indebted to you, whatever he become; he
say so himself only the other night, when he be talk of you."
"eh, Miss, thatbe his way o' takin' it. but I think the more on't when
he say a thing, because his tongue donot overshoot he as mine
do. lor! Ibe no well nor a tilt bottle, I ar'n't,--I cannot stop
mysen when once I begin. but you look rarely, Miss; it do I good to

wife,--"isn't it all come true as I say? though there benot many
sort o' good as I cannot over-praise when I set my tongue to't."
Mrs. Bob's small nose seem to be follow the example of her eye

Ibe a 'cutish chap, an' when Ihave leave off carry my pack, an' am at

"ay," say Bob, speak lower; "but do you think itbe nothing else,

Tom stand with his back against the chimney-piece, and Maggie be

"very well," say Tom, still more coldly.
but Maggie have hardly finish speak in that chill, defiant manner,

the severity of Tom's face relax a little.

that be a cruel word. Maggie's lip begin to tremble.

she be oblige to be childish; the tear would come. when Maggie be

there be a terrible cutting truth in Tom's words,--that hard rind of

she do not answer directly; her heart be too full, and she sit down,

there be a little tremor in Tom's voice as he utter the last word,

Tom smile at the eager face; his smile be very pleasant to see

"I may turn out well than you expect."
"I hope you will."

"there! be a good girl. Ihave get a great deal to think of to-day. Ibe

chapter v
show that Tom have open the oyster
"and now wehave settle this Newcastle business, Tom," say Mr. Deane,
that same afternoon, as they be seat in the private room at the
Bank together, "therebe another matter I want to talk to you about.
since yoube likely to have rather a smoky, unpleasant time of it at
Newcastle for the next few week, youwill want a good prospect of some
sort to keep up your spirit."
Tom wait less nervously than he have do on a former occasion in
this apartment, while his uncle take out his snuff-box and gratify
each nostril with deliberate impartiality.
"you see, Tom," say Mr. Deane at last, throw himself backward,
"the world go on at a smart pace now than it do when I be a
young fellow. why, sir, forty year ago, when I be much such a
strap youngster as you, a man expect to pull between the shaft
the good part of his life, before he get the whip in his hand. the
loom go slowish, and fashion donot alter quite so fast; Ihave a
good suit that last I six year. everything be on a low scale,
sir,--in point of expenditure, I mean. itbe this steam, you see, that
have make the difference; it drive on every wheel double pace, and the
wheel of fortune along with them, as our Mr. Stephen Guest say at the
anniversary dinner (he hit these thing off wonderfully, consider
hebe see nothing of business). I donot find fault with the change, as
some people do. Trade, sir, open a man's eye; and if the population
be to get thick upon the ground, as itbe do, the world must use
its wit at invention of one sort or other. I know Ihave do my share
as an ordinary man of business. somebody have say itbe a fine thing to
make two ear of corn grow where only one grow before; but, sir, itbe
a fine thing, too, to further the exchange of commodity, and bring
the grain of corn to the mouth that be hungry. and thatbe our line
of business; and I consider it as honorable a position as a man can
hold, to be connect with it."
Tom know that the affair his uncle have to speak of be not urgent; Mr.
Deane be too shrewd and practical a man to allow either his
reminiscence or his snuff to impede the progress of trade. indeed,
for the last month or two, there have be hint throw out to Tom
which enable he to guess that he be go to hear some proposition
for his own benefit. with the beginning of the last speech he have
stretch out his leg, thrust his hand in his pocket, and prepare
himself for some introductory diffuseness, tend to show that Mr.
Deane have succeed by his own merit, and that what he have to say to
young man in general be, that if they donot succeed too it be
because of their own demerit. he be rather surprised, then, when his
uncle put a direct question to he.
"let I see,--it's go on for seven year now since you apply to
I for a situation, eh, Tom?"
"yes, sir; Ibe three-and-twenty now," say Tom.
"ah, itbe as well not to say that, though; for youwould pass for a good
deal old, and age tell well in business. I remember your come
very well; I remember I see there be some pluck in you, and that be
what make I give you encouragement. and Ibe happy to say I be right;
Ibe not often deceive. I be naturally a little shy at push my
nephew, but Ibe happy to say youhave do I credit, sir; and if Iwould
have a son o' my own, I shouldnot have be sorry to see he like you."
Mr. Deane tap his box and open it again, repeat in a tone of
some feeling, "no, I shouldnot have be sorry to see he like you."
"Ibe very glad Ihave give you satisfaction, sir; Ihave do my good,"
say Tom, in his proud, independent way.
"yes, Tom, youhave give I satisfaction. I donot speak of your conduct
as a son; though that weigh with I in my opinion of you. but what I
have to do with, as a partner in our firm, be the quality youhave
show as a man o' business. ours be a fine business,--a splendid
concern, sir,--and therebe no reason why it shouldnot go on grow;
therebe a grow capital, and grow outlet for it; but therebe
another thing thatbe want for the prosperity of every concern, large
or small, and thatbe man to conduct it,--man of the right habit; none
o' your flashy fellow, but such as be to be depend on. now this be
what Mr. Guest and I see clear enough. three year ago we take gell
into the concern; we give he a share in the oil-mill. and why? why,
because Gell be a fellow whose service be worth a premium. so it
will always be, sir. so it be with I. and though Gell be pretty near
ten year old than you, there be other point in your favor."
Tom be get a little nervous as Mr. Deane go on speak; he be
conscious of something he have in his mind to say, which might not be
agreeable to his uncle, simply because it be a new suggestion rather
than an acceptance of the proposition he foresee.
"it stand to reason," Mr. Deane go on, when he have finish his new
pinch, "that your be my nephew weigh in your favor; but I donot
deny that if youhave be no relation of mine at all, your conduct in
that affair of Pelley's bank would have lead Mr. Guest and myself to
make some acknowledgment of the service youhave be to we; and, back
by your general conduct and business ability, it have make we determine
on give you a share in the business,--a share which we shall be glad
to increase as the year go on. we think thatwill be well, on all
ground, than raise your salary. itwill give you more importance, and
prepare you well for take some of the anxiety off my shoulder by
and by. Ibe equal to a good deal o' work at present, thank God; but
Ibe get older,--there's no deny that. I tell Mr. Guest I would
open the subject to you; and when you come back from this northern
business, we can go into particular. this be a great stride for a
young fellow of three-and-twenty, but Ibe bind to say youhave deserve
it."
"Ibe very grateful to Mr. Guest and you, sir; of course I feel the
most indebted to _you_, who first take I into the business, and have
take a good deal of pain with I since."
Tom speak with a slight tremor, and pause after he have say this.
"yes, yes," say Mr. Deane. "I donot spare pain when I see theywill be
of any use. I give myself some trouble with Gell, else he wouldnot
have be what he be."
"but therebe one thing I should like to mention to you uncle. Ihave
never speak to you of it before. if you remember, at the time my
father's property be sell, there be some thought of your firm buy
the Mill; I know you think it would be a very good investment,
especially if steam be apply."
"to be sure, to be sure. but Wakem outbid we; hewould make up his mind to
that. hebe rather fond of carry everything over other people's
head."
"perhaps itbe of no use my mention it at present," Tom go on,
"but I wish you to know what I have in my mind about the Mill. Ihave a
strong feeling about it. it be my father's dying wish that I should
try and get it back again whenever I could; it be in his family for
five generation. I promise my father; and besides that, Ibe attach
to the place. I shall never like any other so well. and if it should
ever suit your view to buy it for the firm, I should have a well
chance of fulfil my father's wish. I shouldnot have like to
mention the thing to you, only youhave be kind enough to say my
service have be of some value. and Iwould give up a much great
chance in life for the sake of have the Mill again,--i mean have
it in my own hand, and gradually work off the price."
Mr. Deane have listen attentively, and now look thoughtful.
"I see, I see," he say, after a while; "the thing would be possible
if there be any chance of Wakem's parting with the property. but
that I _donot_ see. hebe put that young Jetsome in the place; and he
have his reason when he buy it, Iwill be bind."
"hebe a loose fish, that young Jetsome," say Tom. "hebe take to
drinking, and they say hebe let the business go down. Luke tell I
about it,--our old miller. he say he sha'n't stay unless therebe an
alteration. I be think, if thing go on that way, Wakem might be
more willing to part with the Mill. Luke say hebe get very sour
about the way thing be go on."
"well, Iwill turn it over, Tom. I must inquire into the matter, and go
into it with Mr. Guest. but, you see, itbe rather strike out a new
branch, and put you to that, instead of keep you where you be,
which be what wehave want."
"I should be able to manage more than the Mill when thing be once
set properly go, sir. I want to have plenty of work. therebe
nothing else I care about much."
there be something rather sad in that speech from a young man of
three-and-twenty, even in uncle Deane's business-love ear.
"Pooh, pooh! youwill be have a wife to care about one of these day,
if you get on at this pace in the world. but as to this Mill, we
mustnot reckon on our chicken too early. however, I promise you to
bear it in mind, and when you come back wewill talk of it again. I be
go to dinner now. come and breakfast with we to-morrow morning, and
say good-bye to your mother and sister before you start."
Chapter VI
illustrate the Laws of Attraction
it be evident to you now that Maggie have arrive at a moment in her
life which must be consider by all prudent person as a great
opportunity for a young woman. launch into the high society of St.
Ogg's, with a striking person, which have the advantage of be quite
unfamiliar to the majority of beholder, and with such moderate
assistance of costume as you have see foreshadow in Lucy's anxious
colloquy with aunt Pullet, Maggie be certainly at a new
starting-point in life. at Lucy's first evening party, young Torry
fatigue his facial muscle more than usual in order that "the
dark-eyed girl there in the corner" might see he in all the
additional style confer by his eyeglass; and several young lady

Chapter VII
Philip re-enter
the next morning be very wet,--the sort of morning on which male
neighbor who have no imperative occupation at home be likely to pay
their fair friend an illimitable visit. the rain, which have be

nothing but an open quarrel can abbreviate the visit; latent

what can be so delightful, in England, as a rainy morning? English

down on the grass, it may lead to catarrhs. but the rain be to be
depend on. you gallop through it in a mackintosh, and presently find

"ah, Mr. Philip!" say Mr. Deane, when they enter the dining-room,

"hebe be to Mudport on business for several day," say Philip; "but

"as fond of his farming hobby as ever, eh?"

"well, well, if this youngster will keep his counsel, there willnot be

Chapter VIII
Wakem in a New Light
before three day have pass after the conversation you have just
overhear between Lucy and her father she have contrive to have a
private interview with Philip during a visit of Maggie's to her aunt
Glegg. for a day and a night Philip turn over in his mind with
restless agitation all that Lucy have tell he in that interview, till
he have thoroughly resolve on a course of action. he think he see
before he now a possibility of alter his position with respect to
Maggie, and remove at least one obstacle between they. he lay his
plan and calculate all his move with the fervid deliberation of a

his sudden genius as a tactician. his plan be as bold as it be
thoroughly calculate. have watch for a moment when his father have
nothing more urgent on his hand than the newspaper, he go behind
he, lay a hand on his shoulder, and said,--
"Father, will you come up into my sanctum, and look at my new
sketch? Ihave arrange they now."
"Ibe get terrible stiff in the joint, Phil, for climb those
stair of your," say Wakem, look kindly at his son as he lay
down his paper. "but come along, then."
"this be a nice place for you, benot it, phil?--a capital light that
from the roof, eh?" be, as usual, the first thing he say on enter

fatherly indulgence have provide the accommodation. he have be a good
father. Emily would have nothing to reproach he with there, if she
come back again from her grave.
"come, come," he say, put his double eye-glass over his nose, and
seat himself to take a general view while he rest, "youhave get a
famous show here. upon my word, I donot see that your thing benot as
good as that London artist's--whatbe his name--that Leyburn give so
much money for."
Philip shake his head and smile. he have seat himself on his

be make strong mark to counteract the sense of tremulousness. he
watch his father get up, and walk slowly round, good-naturedly

for landscape would have prompt, till he stop before a stand on
which two picture be placed,--one much large than the other, the
small one in a leather case.
"bless I! what have you here?" say Wakem, startle by a sudden
transition from landscape to portrait. "I think youwould leave off
figure. who be these?"
"they be the same person," say Philip, with calm promptness, "at
different age."
"and what person?" say Wakem, sharply fix his eye with a grow
look of suspicion on the large picture.
"Miss Tulliver. the small one be something like what she be when I
be at school with her brother at King's Lorton; the large one be not
quite so good a likeness of what she be when I come from abroad."
Wakem turn round fiercely, with a flushed face, let his

stool. but he throw himself into the armchair again, and thrust his

however. Philip do not return the look, but sit quietly watch the
point of his pencil.
"and do you mean to say, then, that you have have any acquaintance with
she since you come from abroad?" say Wakem, at last, with that vain
effort which rage always make to throw as much punishment as it
desire to inflict into word and tone, since blow be forbid.
"yes; I see a great deal of she for a whole year before her father's
death. we meet often in that thicket--the Red Deeps--near Dorlcote
Mill. I love she dearly; I shall never love any other woman. I have
thought of she ever since she be a little girl."
"go on, sir! and you have correspond with she all this while?"
"no. I never tell she I love she till just before we part, and she
promise her brother not to see I again or to correspond with I. I
be not sure that she love I or would consent to marry I. but if she
would consent,--if she _do_ love I well enough,--I should marry
she."
"and this be the return you make I for all the indulgence Ihave
heap on you?" say Wakem, get white, and begin to tremble
under an enraged sense of impotence before Philip's calm defiance and
concentration of purpose.
"no, father," say Philip, look up at he for the first time; "I
donot regard it as a return. you have be an indulgent father to I;
but I have always feel that it be because you have an affectionate
wish to give I as much happiness as my unfortunate lot would admit,
not that it be a debt you expect I to pay by sacrifice all my
chance of happiness to satisfy feeling of yours which I can never
share."
"I think most son would share their father's feeling in this case,"
say Wakem, bitterly. "the girl's father be an ignorant mad brute,
who be within an inch of murder I. the whole town know it. and
the brother be just as insolent, only in a cool way. he forbid she
see you, you say; hewill break every bone in your body, for your
great happiness, if you donot take care. but you seem to have make
up your mind; you have count the consequence, I suppose. of course
you be independent of I; you can marry this girl to-morrow, if you
like; you be a man of five-and-twenty,--you can go your way, and I
can go mine. we need have no more to do with each other."
Wakem rise and walk toward the door, but something hold he back,
and instead of leave the room, he walk up and down it. Philip be
slow to reply, and when he speak, his tone have a more incisive
quietness and clearness than ever.
"no; I cannot marry Miss Tulliver, even if she would have I, if I have
only my own resource to maintain she with. I have be bring up to
no profession. I cannot offer her poverty as well as deformity."
"ah, _there_ be a reason for your clinging to I, doubtless," say
Wakem, still bitterly, though Philip's last word have give he a

"I expect all this," say Philip. "I know these scene be often
happen between father and son. if I be like other man of my age,
I might answer your angry word by still angrier; we might part; I
should marry the woman I love, and have a chance of be as happy as
the rest. but if it will be a satisfaction to you to annihilate the
very object of everything youhave do for I, you have an advantage
over most father; you can completely deprive I of the only thing
that would make my life worth have."
Philip pause, but his father be silent.
"you know well what satisfaction you would have, beyond that of
gratify a ridiculous rancor worthy only of wandering savage."
"ridiculous rancor!" Wakem burst out. "what do you mean? damn it! be a
man to be horsewhip by a boor and love he for it? besides, therebe
that cold, proud devil of a son, who say a word to I I shall not
forget when we have the settling. he would be as pleasant a mark for a
bullet as I know, if he be worth the expense."
"I donot mean your resentment toward they," say Philip, who have his
reason for some sympathy with this view of Tom, "though a feeling of
revenge be not worth much, that you should care to keep it. I mean
your extend the enmity to a helpless girl, who have too much sense
and goodness to share their narrow prejudice. _she_ have never enter
into the family quarrel."
"what do that signify? we donot ask what a woman do; we ask whom
she belong to. itbe altogether a degrading thing to you, to think of
marry old Tulliver's daughter."
for the first time in the dialogue, Philip lose some of his
self-control, and color with anger.
"Miss Tulliver," he say, with bitter incisiveness, "have the only
ground of rank that anything but vulgar folly can suppose to belong
to the middle class; she be thoroughly refine, and her friend,
whatever else they may be, be respect for irreproachable honor and
integrity. all St. Ogg's, I fancy, would pronounce she to be more than
my equal."
Wakem dart a glance of fierce question at his son; but Philip be
not look at he, and with a certain penitent consciousness go on,
in a few moment, as if in amplification of his last words,--
"find a single person in St. Ogg's who will not tell you that a
beautiful creature like she would be throw herself away on a
pitiable object like I."
"not she!" say Wakem, rise again, and forget everything else in
a burst of resentful pride, half fatherly, half personal. "it would be
a deuced fine match for she. itbe all stuff about an accidental
deformity, when a girl's really attach to a man."
"but girl be not apt to get attach under those circumstance,"
say Philip.
"well, then," say Wakem, rather brutally, try to recover his
previous position, "if she donot care for you, you might have spar
yourself the trouble of talk to I about she, and you might have
spar I the trouble of refuse my consent to what be never likely
to happen."
Wakem stride to the door, and without look round again, bang it
after he.
Philip be not without confidence that his father would be ultimately
work upon as he have expect, by what have pass; but the scene have

determine not to go down to dinner; he couldnot meet his father again

far on in the afternoon now, Philip lock up his room and go out
for a long ramble, think he would not return until his father be

a favorite village, where he din, and linger till it be late

chapter XI
in the Lane
Maggie have be four day at her aunt Moss's give the early June

he have a half-jade, half-irritated look, such as a man get when he

chapter XII
A Family Party
Maggie leave her good aunt Gritty at the end of the week, and go to
Garum Firs to pay her visit to aunt Pullet accord to agreement. in
the mean time very unexpected thing have happen, and there be to be
a family party at Garum to discuss and celebrate a change in the
fortune of the Tullivers, which be likely finally to carry away the
shadow of their demerit like the last limb of an eclipse, and cause
their hitherto obscure virtue to shine forth in full-rounded
splendor. it be pleasant to know that a new ministry just come into
office be not the only fellow-man who enjoy a period of high
appreciation and full-blow eulogy; in many respectable family
throughout this realm, relative become creditable meet with a
similar cordiality of recognition, which in its fine freedom from the
coercion of any antecedent, suggest the hopeful possibility that we
may some day without any notice find ourselves in full millennium,
with cockatrice who have cease to bite, and wolf that no long
show their tooth with any but the blandest intention.
Lucy come so early as to have the start even of aunt Glegg; for she
long to have some undisturbed talk with Maggie about the wonderful
news. it seem, do it not? say Lucy, with her prettiest air of

creature!) be conspire now to make poor dear aunt Tulliver, and
cousin Tom, and naughty Maggie too, if she be not obstinately bent

trouble. to think that the very day--the _very day_--after Tom have

the premise at once!
it be very dreadful for that unhappy young man, but it do seem as if

think of poor aunty be in her old place again, and gradually
get comfort about she there!

"Aunt Pullet," she say, seat herself on the sofa, and caressingly

"Ihave no choice, Ibe sure, sister," say poor Mrs. Tulliver, accustom

"go into service" be the expression by which the Dodson mind

"La, Jane," say Mrs. Pullet, "it 'ud do your bed good to have
somebody to sleep in them. therebe that striped room smell dreadful
mouldy, and the glass mildew like anything. Ibe sure I think I
should be strike with death when you take I in."
"oh, there be Tom!" exclaim Lucy, clap her hand. "hebe come on
Sindbad, as I tell he. I be afraid he be not go to keep his
promise."
Maggie jump up to kiss Tom as he enter, with strong feeling, at
this first meeting since the prospect of return to the Mill have

her side. to have no cloud between herself and Tom be still a
perpetual yearning in she, that have its root deep than all change.
he smile at her very kindly this evening, and say, "well, Magsie,
howbe aunt Moss?"
"come, come, sir," say Mr. Glegg put out his hand. "why, yoube
such a big man, you carry all before you, it, seem. yoube come into
your luck a good deal early than we old folk do; but I wish you
joy, I wish you joy. youwill get the Mill all for your own again some
day, Iwill be bind. you willnot stop half-way up the hill."
"but I hope hewill bear in mind as itbe his mother's family as he owe
it to," say Mrs. Glegg. "if he havenot have they to take after, hewould
ha' be poorly off. there be never any failure, nor lawe, nor
wastefulness in our family, nor die without wills----"
"no, nor sudden death," say aunt Pullet; "allay the doctor call
in. but Tom have the Dodson skin; I say that from the first. and I
donot know what _you_ mean to do, sister Glegg, but I mean to give he
a tablecloth of all my three big size but one, besides sheet. I
donot say what more I shall do; but _that_ I shall do, and if I should
die to-morrow, Mr. Pullet, youwill bear it in mind,--though youwill be
blunder with the key, and never remember as that on the third
shelf o' the left-hand wardrobe, behind the night-cap with the broad
ties,--not the narrow-frille uns,--is the key of the drawer in the
Blue Room, where the key o' the Blue Closet be. youwill make a mistake,
and I shall niver be worthy to know it. youhave a memory for my pill
and draught, wonderful,--I'll allay say that of you,--but yoube
lose among the key." this gloomy prospect of the confusion that would
ensue on her decease be very affect to Mrs. Pullet.
"you carry it too far, sophy,--that lock in and out," say Mrs.
Glegg, in a tone of some disgust at this folly. "you go beyond your
own family. therebe nobody can say I donot lock up; but I do whatbe
reasonable, and no more. and as for the linen, I shall look out whatbe
serviceable, to make a present of to my nephey; Ihave get cloth as have
never be whiten, well worth have than other people's fine
holland; and I hope hewill lie down in it and think of his aunt."
Tom thank Mrs. Glegg, but evade any promise to meditate nightly on
her virtue; and Mrs. Glegg effect a diversion for he by ask
about Mr. Deane's intention concern steam.
Lucy have have her far-sight view in beg Tom to come on Sindbad.
it appear, when it be time to go home, that the man-servant be to
ride the horse, and cousin Tom be to drive home his mother and Lucy.
"you must sit by yourself, aunty," say that contrive young lady,
"because I must sit by Tom; Ihave a great deal to say to he."
in the eagerness of her affectionate anxiety for Maggie, Lucy could
not persuade herself to defer a conversation about she with Tom, who,
she think, with such a cup of joy before he as this rapid
fulfilment of his wish about the Mill, must become pliant and

have use his influence with his father. she have count on this

toward Philip at once, and, besides that, prove that the eld Wakem
be ready to receive Maggie with all the honor of a daughter-in-law.
nothing be want, then, but for dear Tom, who always have that
pleasant smile when he look at cousin Lucy, to turn completely
round, say the opposite of what he have always say before, and declare
that he, for his part, be delighted that all the old grievance
should be heal, and that Maggie should have Philip with all suitable
despatch; in cousin Lucy's opinion nothing could be easy.
but to mind strongly mark by the positive and negative quality
that create severity,--strength of will, conscious rectitude of
purpose, narrowness of imagination and intellect, great power of
self-control, and a disposition to exert control over others,--prejudices
come as the natural food of tendency which can get no sustenance
out of that complex, fragmentary, doubt-provoke knowledge which
we call truth. let a prejudice be bequeath, carry in the air,
adopt by hearsay, catch in through the eye,--however it may come,
these mind will give it a habitation; it be something to assert
strongly and bravely, something to fill up the void of spontaneous
idea, something to impose on other with the authority of conscious
right; it be at once a staff and a baton. every prejudice that will
answer these purpose be self-evident. our good, upright Tom Tulliver's
mind be of this class; his inward criticism of his father's fault
do not prevent he from adopt his father's prejudice; it be a
prejudice against a man of lax principle and lax life, and it be a
meeting-point for all the disappointed feeling of family and personal
pride. other feeling add their force to produce Tom's bitter
repugnance to Philip, and to Maggie's union with he; and
notwithstanding Lucy's power over her strong-willed cousin, she get
nothing but a cold refusal ever to sanction such a marriage; "but of
course Maggie could do as she liked,--she have declare she
determination to be independent. for Tom's part, he hold himself bind
by his duty to his father's memory, and by every manly feeling, never
to consent to any relation with the Wakems."
thus, all that Lucy have effect by her zealous mediation be to fill
Tom's mind with the expectation that Maggie's perverse resolve to go
into a situation again would presently metamorphose itself, as she
resolve be apt to do, into something equally perverse, but entirely
different,--a marriage with Philip Wakem.
Chapter XIII
Borne along by the Tide
in less than a week Maggie be at St. Oggbe again,--outwardly in much

Philip Wakem be a less frequent visitor, but he come occasionally in

"the thirst that from the soul doth rise
Doth ask a drink divine."

chapter I
the Return to the Mill

submissive confession; from be in the presence of those whose look

Maggie have be keep on her bed at York for a day with that
prostrate headache which be likely to follow on the terrible strain
of the previous day and night. there be an expression of physical
pain still about her brow and eye, and her whole appearance, with she
dress so long unchanged, be wear and distressed. she lift the latch
of the gate and walk in slowly. Tom do not hear the gate; he be
just then close upon the roar dam; but he presently turn, and
lift up his eye, see the figure whose worn look and loneliness
seem to he a confirmation of his bad conjecture. he pause,
trembling and white with disgust and indignation.
Maggie pause too, three yard before he. she feel the hatred in his
face, feel it rush through her fibre; but she must speak.
"Tom," she begin faintly, "I be come back to you,--I be come back
home--for refuge--to tell you everything."
"you will find no home with I," he answer, with tremulous rage.
"you have disgrace we all. you have disgrace my father's name. you
have be a curse to your good friend. you have be base, deceitful;
no motif be strong enough to restrain you. I wash my hand of you
forever. you donot belong to I."
their mother have come to the door now. she stand paralyze by the
double shock of see Maggie and hear Tom's word.
"Tom," say Maggie, with more courage, "I be perhaps not so guilty as
you believe I to be. I never mean to give way to my feeling. I
struggle against they. I be carry too far in the boat to come back
on Tuesday. I come back as soon as I could."
"I cannot believe in you any more," say Tom, gradually pass from
the tremulous excitement of the first moment to cold inflexibility.
"you have be carry on a clandestine relation with Stephen
Guest,--as you do before with another. he go to see you at my aunt
Moss's; you walk alone with he in the lane; you must have behave
as no modest girl would have do to her cousin's lover, else that
could never have happen. the people at Luckreth see you pass; you
pass all the other place; you know what you be do. you have
be use Philip Wakem as a screen to deceive Lucy,--the kind
friend you ever have. go and see the return you have make she. shebe
ill; unable to speak. my mother cannot go near she, lest she should
remind she of you."
Maggie be half stunned,--too heavily press upon by her anguish even
to discern any difference between her actual guilt and her brother's
accusation, still less to vindicate herself.
"Tom," she say, crush her hand together under her cloak, in the
effort to speak again, "whatever I have do, I repent it bitterly. I
want to make amend. I will endure anything. I want to be keep from
do wrong again."
"what _will_ keep you?" say Tom, with cruel bitterness. "not
religion; not your natural feeling of gratitude and honor. and he--he
would deserve to be shoot, if it be not----but you be ten time
bad than he be. I loathe your character and your conduct. you
struggle with your feeling, you say. yes! _i_ have have feeling to
struggle with; but I conquer they. I have have a hard life than you
have have; but I have find _my_ comfort in do my duty. but I will
sanction no such character as your; the world shall know that _i_
feel the difference between right and wrong. if you be in want, I
will provide for you; let my mother know. but you shall not come under
my roof. it be enough that I have to bear the thought of your
disgrace; the sight of you be hateful to I."
Slowly Maggie be turn away with despair in her heart. but the poor
frightened mother's love leap out now, strong than all dread.
"my child! Iwill go with you. youhave get a mother."
oh, the sweet rest of that embrace to the heart-stricken Maggie! More
helpful than all wisdom be one draught of simple human pity that will
not forsake we.
Tom turn and walk into the house.
"come in, my child," Mrs. Tulliver whisper. "hewill let you stay and
sleep in my bed. he willnot deny that if I ask he."
"no, mother," say Maggie, in a low tone, like a moan. "I will never
go in."
"then wait for I outside. Iwill get ready and come with you."
when his mother appear with her bonnet on, Tom come out to she in
the passage, and put money into her hand.
"my house be your, mother, always," he say. "you will come and let
I know everything you want; you will come back to I."
Poor Mrs. Tulliver take the money, too frighten to say anything. the
only thing clear to she be the mother's instinct that she would go
with her unhappy child.
Maggie be wait outside the gate; she take her mother's hand and
they walk a little way in silence.
"Mother," say Maggie, at last, "we will go to Luke's cottage. Luke
will take I in. he be very good to I when I be a little girl."
"hebe get no room for we, my dear, now; his wife's get so many
child. I donot know where to go, if it benot to one o' your aunt;
and I hardly durst," say poor Mrs. Tulliver, quite destitute of
mental resource in this extremity.
Maggie be silent a little while, and then said,--
"let we go to Bob Jakin's, mother; his wife will have room for we, if
they have no other lodger."
so they go on their way to St. Ogg's, to the old house by the
river-side.
Bob himself be at home, with a heaviness at heart which resist even
the new joy and pride of possess a two-months'-old baby, quite the
lively of its age that have ever be bear to prince or packman. he
would perhaps not so thoroughly have understand all the dubiousness of
Maggie's appearance with Mr. Stephen Guest on the quay at Mudport if
he have not witness the effect it produce on Tom when he go to
report it; and since then, the circumstance which in any case give a
disastrous character to her elopement have pass beyond the more
polite circle of St. Ogg's, and have become matter of common talk,
accessible to the groom and errand-boy. so that when he open the
door and see Maggie stand before he in her sorrow and weariness,
he have no question to ask except one which he dare only ask
himself,--where be Mr. Stephen Guest? Bob, for his part, hop he
might be in the warm department of an asylum understand to exist in
the other world for gentleman who be likely to be in fall
circumstance there.
the lodging be vacant, and both Mrs. Jakin the large and Mrs.
Jakin the less be command to make all thing comfortable for "the
old Missis and the young Miss"; alas that she be still "Miss!" the
ingenious Bob be sorely perplex as to how this result could have
come about; how Mr. Stephen Guest could have go away from she, or
could have let she go away from he, when he have the chance of keep
she with he. but he be silent, and would not allow his wife to ask
he a question; would not present himself in the room, lest it should
appear like intrusion and a wish to pry; have the same chivalry
toward dark-eyed Maggie as in the day when he have buy she the
memorable present of book.
but after a day or two Mrs. Tulliver be go to the Mill again for a

but she have be solitary only a little while in the old sitting-room
that look on the river, when there come a tap at the door, and
turn round her sad face as she say "come in," she see Bob enter,
with the baby in his arm and Mumps at his heel.
"wewill go back, if it disturb you, Miss," say Bob.
"no," say Maggie, in a low voice, wish she could smile.
Bob, close the door behind he, come and stand before she.
"you see, wehave get a little un, Miss, and I want'd you to look at it,
and take it in your arm, if youwould be so good. for we make free to
name it after you, and it 'ud be well for your takin' a bit o'
notice on it."
Maggie could not speak, but she put out her arm to receive the tiny
baby, while Mumps snuff at it anxiously, to ascertain that this
transference be all right. Maggie's heart have swell at this action
and speech of Bob's; she know well enough that it be a way he have
choose to show his sympathy and respect.
"sit down, Bob," she say presently, and he sit down in silence,
find his tongue unmanageable in quite a new fashion, refuse to
say what he want it to say.
"Bob," she say, after a few moment, look down at the baby, and
hold it anxiously, as if she fear it might slip from her mind and
her finger, "I have a favor to ask of you."
"donot you speak so, Miss," say Bob, grasp the skin of Mumps's
neck; "if therebe anything I can do for you, I should look upon it as
a day's earning."
"I want you to go to Dr. Kenn's, and ask to speak to he, and tell he
that I be here, and should be very grateful if he would come to I
while my mother be away. she will not come back till evening."
"eh, Miss, Iwould do it in a minute,--it be but a step,--but Dr. Kenn's
wife lie dead; shebe to be bury to-morrow; die the day I come from
Mudport. itbe all the more pity she should ha' die just now, if you
want he. I hardly like to go a-nigh he yet."
"oh no, Bob," say Maggie, "we must let it be,--till after a few day,
perhaps, when you hear that he be go about again. but perhaps he
may be go out of town--to a distance," she add, with a new sense
of despondency at this idea.
"not he, Miss," say Bob. "_hewill_ none go away. he benot one o' they
gentlefolk as go to cry at waterin'-place when their wife die; hebe
get summat else to do. he look fine and sharp after the parish, he
do. he christen the little un; an' he be _at_ I to know what I
do of a Sunday, as I donot come to church. but I tell he I be upo'
the travel three part o' the Sundays,--an' then Ibe so used to bein'
on my leg, I cannot sit so long on end,--'an' lor, sir,' say I, 'a
packman can do wi' a small 'lowance o' church; it taste strong,' say
I; 'therebe no call to lay it on thick.' eh, Miss, how good the little
un be wi' you! itbe like as if it know you; it partly do, Iwill be
bound,--like the bird know the mornin'."
Bob's tongue be now evidently loose from its unwonted bondage, and
might even be in danger of do more work than be require of it.
but the subject on which he long to be inform be so steep and
difficult of approach, that his tongue be likely to run on along the

and be silent again for a little while, ruminate much on the
possible form in which he might put a question. at last he say, in a
more timid voice than usual,--
"will you give I leave to ask you only one thing, Miss?"
Maggie be rather startled, but she answer, "yes, Bob, if it be
about myself--not about any one else."
"well, Miss, itbe this. _do_ you owe anybody a grudge?"
"no, not any one," say Maggie, look up at he inquiringly. "why?"
"oh, lor, Miss," say Bob, pinch Mumps's neck hard than ever. "I
wish you do, an' tell I; Iwould leather he till I couldnot see--I
would--an' the Justice might do what he like to I arter."
"oh, Bob," say Maggie, smile faintly, "yoube a very good friend to
I. but I shouldnot like to punish any one, even if theyhave do I

this view of thing be puzzle to Bob, and throw more obscurity than

away again to an expectant mother.
"happen youwould like Mumps for company, Miss," he say when he have take
