make no bother about it. if I tell he, hewill lie before you an'
watch you, as still,--just as he watch my pack. youwould well let I
leave he a bit; hewill get fond on you. lor, itbe a fine thing to hev
a dumb brute fond on you; itwill stick to you, an' make no jaw."
"yes, do leave he, please," say Maggie. "I think I should like to
have Mumps for a friend."
"Mumps, lie down there," say Bob, point to a place in front of
Maggie, "and niver do you stir till yoube speak to."
Mumps lie down at once, and make no sign of restlessness when his
master leave the room.
Chapter II
St. Ogg's Passes Judgment
it be soon know throughout St. Ogg's that Miss Tulliver be come

Guest,--at all event, Mr. Stephen Guest have not marry she; which

judge other accord to result; how else?--not know the process
by which result be arrive at. if Miss Tulliver, after a few month

Maggie's pride be too intense for she not to feel that sting, even in

she be show up at once, after be announce, into Dr. Kenn's

in rather break sentence, and with some effort at first, but soon

"your prompt to go to your near friends,--to remain where all
the tie of your life have be formed,--i a true prompting, to which
the Church in its original constitution and discipline respond,
open its arm to the penitent, watch over its child to the
last; never abandon they until they be hopelessly reprobate. and
the Church ought to represent the feeling of the community, so that
every parish should be a family knit together by Christian brotherhood

schismatic; and if I be not support by the firm faith that the
Church must ultimately recover the full force of that constitution
which be alone fit to human need, I should often lose heart at
observe the want of fellowship and sense of mutual responsibility

"the only thing I want be some occupation that will enable I to get

can go on lodge where I be."
"I must think over the subject maturely," say Dr. Kenn, "and in a few

which make the consent to the marriage a desecration to she; her

the great problem of the shift relation between passion and duty be

case. the casuist have become a byword of reproach; but their
perverted spirit of minute discrimination be the shadow of a truth to
which eye and heart be too often fatally sealed,--the truth, that
moral judgment must remain false and hollow, unless they be check
and enlighten by a perpetual reference to the special circumstance
that mark the individual lot.
all people of broad, strong sense have an instinctive repugnance to
the man of maxim; because such people early discern that the
mysterious complexity of our life be not to be embrace by maxim, and
that to lace ourselves up in formula of that sort be to repress all
the divine prompting and inspiration that spring from grow
insight and sympathy. and the man of maxim be the popular

patience, discrimination, impartiality,--without any care to assure

earn estimate of temptation, or from a life vivid and intense enough
to have create a wide fellow-feeling with all that be human.

"Maggie,--I believe in you; I know you never mean to deceive I; I
know you try to keep faith to I and to all. I believe this
before I have any other evidence of it than your own nature. the
night after I last part from you I suffer torment. I have see

through all the suggestion--almost murderous suggestion--of rage

I be sure that you mean to cleave to I, as you have say; that
you have reject he; that you struggle to renounce he, for

fatal for _you;_ and that dread shut out the very thought of

believe then, as I believe now, that the strong attraction which
draw you together proceed only from one side of your character,
and belong to that partial, divided action of our nature which
make half the tragedy of the human lot. I have feel the vibration
of chord in your nature that I have continually feel the want of
in his. but perhaps I be wrong; perhaps I feel about you as the
artist do about the scene over which his soul have brood with

never believe that it could bear for another all the meaning and
the beauty it bear for he.
"I dare not trust myself to see you that morning; I be fill
with selfish passion; I be shatter by a night of conscious

promise of such deep joy as would give a new and blessed meaning to
the foregoing pain,--the promise of another self that would lift my
ache affection into the divine rapture of an ever-spring,

"but the misery of that night have prepare I for what come
before the next. it be no surprise to I. I be certain that he
have prevail on you to sacrifice everything to he, and I wait
with equal certainty to hear of your marriage. I measure your love
and his by my own. but I be wrong, Maggie. there be something
strong in you than your love for he.
"I will not tell you what I go through in that interval. but even
in its utmost agony--even in those terrible throe that love must
suffer before it can be disembody of selfish desire--my love for
you suffice to withhold I from suicide, without the aid of any
other motive. in the midst of my egoism, I yet could not bear to

by the end of the week Dr. Kenn have make up his mind that there be

the last conflict
in the second week of September, Maggie be again sit in her
lonely room, battle with the old shadowy enemy that be forever
slay and rise again. it be past midnight, and the rain be beat
heavily against the window, drive with fitful force by the rushing,
loud-moan wind. for the day after Lucy's visit there have be a
sudden change in the weather; the heat and drought have give way to
cold variable wind, and heavy fall of rain at interval; and she have
be forbid to risk the contemplated journey until the weather
should become more settled. in the county high up the Floss the
rain have be continuous, and the completion of the harvest have be
arrest. and now, for the last two day, the rain on this low
course of the river have be incessant, so that the old man have shake
their head and talk of sixty year ago, when the same sort of
weather, happen about the equinox, bring on the great flood,
which sweep the bridge away, and reduce the town to great misery. but
the young generation, who have see several small flood, think
lightly of these sombre recollection and foreboding; and Bob Jakin,
naturally prone to take a hopeful view of his own luck, laugh at his
mother when she regret their have take a house by the riverside,
observe that but for that they would have have no boat, which be
the most lucky of possession in case of a flood that oblige they to
go to a distance for food.
but the careless and the fearful be alike sleep in their bed
now. there be hope that the rain would abate by the morrow;
threatening of a bad kind, from sudden thaw after fall of snow,
have often pass off, in the experience of the young one; and at
the very bad, the bank would be sure to break low down the river
when the tide come in with violence, and so the water would be
carry off, without cause more than temporary inconvenience, and
loss that would be feel only by the poor sort, whom charity would
relieve.
all be in their bed now, for it be past midnight; all except some
solitary watcher such as Maggie. she be seat in her little parlor
toward the river, with one candle, that leave everything dim in the
room except a letter which lie before she on the table. that letter,
which have come to she to-day, be one of the cause that have keep she
up far on into the night, unconscious how the hour be go,
careless of seek rest, with no image of rest come across her
mind, except of that far, far off rest from which there would be no
more wake for she into this struggle earthly life.
two day before Maggie receive that letter, she have be to the
Rectory for the last time. the heavy rain would have prevent she
from go since; but there be another reason. Dr. Kenn, at first
enlighten only by a few hint as to the new turn which gossip and
slander have take in relation to Maggie, have recently be make more
fully aware of it by an earnest remonstrance from one of his male
parishioner against the indiscretion of persist in the attempt to
overcome the prevalent feeling in the parish by a course of
resistance. Dr. Kenn, have a conscience void of offence in the
matter, be still inclined to persevere,--was still averse to give way
before a public sentiment that be odious and contemptible; but he be
finally work upon by the consideration of the peculiar
responsibility attach to his office, of avoid the appearance of
evil,--an "appearance" that be always dependent on the average quality
of surround mind. where these mind be low and gross, the area of
that "appearance" be proportionately widen. perhaps he be in danger
of act from obstinacy; perhaps it be his duty to succumb.
conscientious people be apt to see their duty in that which be the
most painful course; and to recede be always painful to Dr. Kenn. he
make up his mind that he must advise Maggie to go away from St. Ogg's
for a time; and he perform that difficult task with as much delicacy
as he could, only state in vague term that he find his attempt to
countenance her stay be a source of discord between himself and his
parishioner, that be likely to obstruct his usefulness as a
clergyman. he beg she to allow he to write to a clerical friend of
his, who might possibly take she into his own family as governess;
and, if not, would probably know of some other available position for
a young woman in whose welfare Dr. Kenn feel a strong interest.
Poor Maggie listen with a trembling lip; she could say nothing but a
faint "thank you, I shall be grateful"; and she walk back to her
lodging, through the drive rain, with a new sense of desolation.
she must be a lonely wanderer; she must go out among fresh face, that
would look at she wonderingly, because the day do not seem joyful to
she; she must begin a new life, in which she would have to rouse
herself to receive new impression; and she be so unspeakably,
sickeningly weary! there be no home, no help for the erring; even
those who pity be constrain to hardness. but ought she to
complain? ought she to shrink in this way from the long penance of
life, which be all the possibility she have of lighten the load to
some other sufferer, and so change that passionate error into a new
force of unselfish human love? all the next day she sit in her lonely
room, with a window darken by the cloud and the drive rain,
thinking of that future, and wrestle for patience; for what repose
could poor Maggie ever win except by wrestle?
and on the third day--this day of which she have just sit out the
close--the letter have come which be lie on the table before she.
the letter be from Stephen. he be come back from Holland; he be at
Mudport again, unknown to any of his friend, and have write to she
from that place, enclose the letter to a person whom he trust in
St. Ogg's. from beginning to end it be a passionate cry of reproach;
an appeal against her useless sacrifice of he, of herself, against
that pervert notion of right which lead she to crush all his hope,
for the sake of a mere idea, and not any substantial good,--_his_
hope, whom she love, and who love she with that single overpowering
passion, that worship, which a man never give to a woman more than
once in his life.
"they have write to I that you be to marry Kenn. as if I should
believe that! perhaps they have tell you some such fable about I.
perhaps they tell you Ihave be 'travel.' my body have be drag
about somewhere; but _i_ have never travel from the hideous place
where you leave I; where I start up from the stupor of helpless rage
to find you go.
"Maggie! whose pain can have be like mine? whose injury be like
mine? who besides I have meet that long look of love that have burn
itself into my soul, so that no other image can come there? Maggie,
call I back to you! call I back to life and goodness! I be banish
from both now. I have no motif; I be indifferent to everything. two
month have only deepen the certainty that I can never care for life
without you. write I one word; say 'come!' in two day I should be
with you. Maggie, have you forget what it be to be together,--to
be within reach of a look, to be within hearing of each other's
voice?"
when Maggie first read this letter she feel as if her real temptation
have only just begin. at the entrance of the chill dark cavern, we turn
with unworn courage from the warm light; but how, when we have tread
far in the damp darkness, and have begin to be faint and weary; how,
if there be a sudden opening above we, and we be invite back again
to the life-nourishing day? the leap of natural longing from under the
pressure of pain be so strong, that all less immediate motif be
likely to be forget--till the pain have be escape from.
for hour Maggie feel as if her struggle have be in vain. for hour
every other think that she strove to summon be thrust aside by the
image of Stephen wait for the single word that would bring he to
she. she do not _read_ the letter: she hear he utter it, and the
voice shake she with its old strange power. all the day before she have
be fill with the vision of a lonely future through which she must
carry the burthen of regret, uphold only by cling faith. and here,
close within her reach, urge itself upon she even as a claim, be
another future, in which hard endurance and effort be to be
exchange for easy, delicious lean on another's love strength!
and yet that promise of joy in the place of sadness do not make the
dire force of the temptation to Maggie.
it be Stephen's tone of misery, it be the doubt in the justice of
her own resolve, that make the balance tremble, and make she once
start from her seat to reach the pen and paper, and write "Come!"
but close upon that decisive act, her mind recoil; and the sense of
contradiction with her past self in her moment of strength and
clearness come upon she like a pang of conscious degradation. no, she
must wait; she must pray; the light that have forsake she would come
again; she should feel again what she have feel when she have flee away,
under an inspiration strong enough to conquer agony,--to conquer love;
she should feel again what she have feel when Lucy stand by she, when
Philip's letter have stir all the fibre that bind she to the
calm past.
she sit quite still, far on into the night, with no impulse to change
her attitude, without active force enough even for the mental act of
prayer; only wait for the light that would surely come again. it
come with the memory that no passion could long quench; the long
past come back to she, and with it the fountain of self-renounce
pity and affection, of faithfulness and resolve. the word that be
mark by the quiet hand in the little old book that she have long ago
learn by heart, rush even to her lip, and find a vent for
themselves in a low murmur that be quite lose in the loud driving of
the rain against the window and the loud moan and roar of the wind. "I
have receive the Cross, I have receive it from Thy hand; I will bear
it, and bear it till death, as Thou hast lay it upon I."
but soon other word rise that could find no utterance but in a
sob,--"forgive I, Stephen! it will pass away. you will come back to
she."
she take up the letter, hold it to the candle, and let it burn slowly
on the hearth. to-morrow she would write to he the last word of
parting.
"I will bear it, and bear it till death. but how long it will be
before death come! I be so young, so healthy. how shall I have
patience and strength? be I to struggle and fall and repent again? have
life other trial as hard for I still?"
with that cry of self-despair, Maggie fall on her knee against the
table, and bury her sorrow-stricken face. her soul go out to the
Unseen Pity that would be with she to the end. surely there be
something be teach she by this experience of great need; and she
must be learn a secret of human tenderness and long-suffering, that
the less erring could hardly know? "o God, if my life be to be long,
let I live to bless and comfort----"
at that moment Maggie feel a startling sensation of sudden cold about
her knee and foot; it be water flow under she. she start up;
the stream be flow under the door that lead into the passage. she
be not bewilder for an instant; she know it be the flood!
the tumult of emotion she have be endure for the last twelve hour
seem to have leave a great calm in she; without scream, she
hurry with the candle upstairs to Bob Jakin's bedroom. the door be
ajar; she go in and shake he by the shoulder.
"Bob, the flood be come! it be in the house; let we see if we can make
the boat safe."
she light his candle, while the poor wife, snatch up her baby,
burst into scream; and then she hurry down again to see if the
water be rise fast. there be a step down into the room at the
door lead from the staircase; she see that the water be already on
a level with the step. while she be look, something come with a
tremendous crash against the window, and send the leaded pane and the
old wooden framework inward in shiver, the water pour in after it.
"it be the boat!" cry Maggie. "Bob, come down to get the boat!"
and without a moment's shudder of fear, she plunge through the water,
which be rise fast to her knee, and by the glimmering light of the
candle she have leave on the stair, she mount on to the window-sill,
and creep into the boat, which be leave with the prow lodging and
protrude through the window. Bob be not long after she, hurrying
without shoe or stocking, but with the lanthorn in his hand.
"why, theybe both here,--both the boat," say Bob, as he get into
the one where Maggie be. "itbe wonderful this fastening benot break
too, as well as the mooring."
in the excitement of get into the other boat, unfasten it, and
master an oar, Bob be not strike with the danger Maggie incur.
we be not apt to fear for the fearless, when we be companion in
their danger, and Bob's mind be absorb in possible expedient for
the safety of the helpless indoor. the fact that Maggie have be up,
have wake he, and have take the lead in activity, give Bob a vague
impression of she as one who would help to protect, not need to be
protect. she too have get possession of an oar, and have push off,
so as to release the boat from the overhang window-frame.
"the water's rise so fast," say Bob, "I doubt itwill be in at the
chamber before long,--th' house be so low. Ihave more mind to get
Prissy and the child and the mother into the boat, if I could, and
trusten to the water,--for th' old house be none so safe. and if I let
go the boat--but _you_," he exclaim, suddenly lift the light of
his lanthorn on Maggie, as she stand in the rain with the oar in her
hand and her black hair stream.
Maggie have no time to answer, for a new tidal current sweep along the
line of the house, and drive both the boat out on to the wide water,
with a force that carry they far past the meeting current of the
river.
in the first moment Maggie feel nothing, think of nothing, but that
she have suddenly pass away from that life which she have be
dread; it be the transition of death, without its agony,--and she
be alone in the darkness with God.
the whole thing have be so rapid, so dreamlike, that the thread of
ordinary association be break; she sink down on the seat clutch
the oar mechanically, and for a long while have no distinct conception
of her position. the first thing that wake she to full
consciousness be the cessation of the rain, and a perception that the
darkness be divide by the faint light, which part the
overhang gloom from the immeasurable watery level below. she be
drive out upon the flood,--that awful visitation of God which she
father use to talk of; which have make the nightmare of her childish
dream. and with that thought there rush in the vision of the old
home, and Tom, and her mother,--they have all listen together.
"o God, where be I? which be the way home?" she cry out, in the dim
loneliness.
what be happen to they at the Mill? the flood have once nearly
destroy it. they might be in danger, in distress,--her mother and
her brother, alone there, beyond reach of help! her whole soul be
strain now on that thought; and she see the long-love face look
for help into the darkness, and find none.
she be float in smooth water now,--perhaps far on the overflooded
field. there be no sense of present danger to check the outgoing of
her mind to the old home; and she strain her eye against the
curtain of gloom that she might seize the first sight of she
whereabout,--that she might catch some faint suggestion of the spot
toward which all her anxiety tend.
oh, how welcome, the widening of that dismal watery level, the gradual
uplifting of the cloudy firmament, the slowly define blackness of
object above the glassy dark! yes, she must be out on the field;
those be the top of hedgerow tree. which way do the river lie?
look behind she, she see the line of black tree; look before
she, there be none; then the river lie before she. she seize an oar
and begin to paddle the boat forward with the energy of waken hope;
the dawning seem to advance more swiftly, now she be in action; and
she could soon see the poor dumb beast crowd piteously on a mound
where they have take refuge. onward she paddle and row by turn in
the grow twilight; her wet clothe clung round she, and her
streaming hair be dash about by the wind, but she be hardly
conscious of any bodily sensations,--except a sensation of strength,
inspire by mighty emotion. along with the sense of danger and
possible rescue for those long-remember being at the old home,
there be an undefined sense of reconcilement with her brother; what
quarrel, what harshness, what unbelief in each other can subsist in
the presence of a great calamity, when all the artificial vesture of
our life be go, and we be all one with each other in primitive
mortal need? Vaguely Maggie feel this, in the strong resurgent love
toward her brother that sweep away all the later impression of hard,
cruel offence and misunderstanding, and leave only the deep,
underlie, unshakable memory of early union.
but now there be a large dark mass in the distance, and near to her
Maggie could discern the current of the river. the dark mass must
be--yes, it be--St. Ogg's. ah, now she know which way to look for the
first glimpse of the well-know tree--the gray willow, the now
yellowing chestnut--and above they the old roof! but there be no
color, no shape yet; all be faint and dim. More and more strongly the
energy seem to come and put themselves forth, as if her life be
a store-up force that be be spend in this hour, unneede for any
future.
she must get her boat into the current of the Floss, else she would
never be able to pass the Ripple and approach the house; this be the
thought that occur to she, as she imagine with more and more
vividness the state of thing round the old home. but then she might
be carry very far down, and be unable to guide her boat out of the
current again. for the first time distinct idea of danger begin to
press upon she; but there be no choice of course, no room for
hesitation, and she float into the current. swiftly she go now
without effort; more and more clearly in the lessening distance and
the grow light she begin to discern the object that she know must
be the well-know tree and roof; nay, she be not far off a rushing,
muddy current that must be the strangely alter Ripple.
great God! there be float mass in it, that might dash against
her boat as she pass, and cause she to perish too soon. what be
those mass?
for the first time Maggie's heart begin to beat in an agony of dread.
she sit helpless, dimly conscious that she be be float along,
more intensely conscious of the anticipated clash. but the horror be
transient; it pass away before the oncoming warehouse of St. Ogg's.
she have pass the mouth of the Ripple, then; _now_, she must use all
her skill and power to manage the boat and get it if possible out of
the current. she could see now that the bridge be break down; she
could see the mast of a strand vessel far out over the watery
field. but no boat be to be see move on the river,--such as have
be lay hand on be employ in the flooded street.
with new resolution, Maggie seize her oar, and stand up again to
paddle; but the now ebb tide add to the swiftness of the river,
and she be carry along beyond the bridge. she could hear shout
from the window overlook the river, as if the people there be
call to she. it be not till she have pass on nearly to Tofton
that she could get the boat clear of the current. then with one
yearn look toward her uncle Deane's house that lie far down the
river, she take to both her oar and row with all her might across
the watery field, back toward the Mill. color be begin to awake
now, and as she approach the Dorlcote field, she could discern the
tint of the tree, could see the old Scotch fir far to the right,
and the home chestnuts,--oh, how deep they lie in the water,--deeper
than the tree on this side the hill! and the roof of the Mill--where
be it? those heavy fragment hurry down the ripple,--what have they
mean? but it be not the house,--the house stand firm; drown up to
the first story, but still firm,--or be it break in at the end
toward the Mill?
with pant joy that she be there at last,--joy that overcome all
distress,--maggie near the front of the house. at first she hear no
sound; she see no object move. her boat be on a level with the
upstairs window. she call out in a loud, pierce voice,--
"Tom, where be you? mother, where be you? here be Maggie!"
soon, from the window of the attic in the central gable, she hear
Tom's voice,--
"who be it? have you bring a boat?"
"it be I, tom,--maggie. where be mother?"
"she be not here; she go to Garum the day before yesterday. Iwill
come down to the low window."
"alone, Maggie?" say Tom, in a voice of deep astonishment, as he
open the middle window, on a level with the boat.
"yes, Tom; God have take care of I, to bring I to you. get in
quickly. be there no one else?"
"no," say Tom, step into the boat; "I fear the man be drown; he
be carry down the Ripple, I think, when part of the Mill fall with
the crash of tree and stone against it; Ihave shout again and
again, and there have be no answer. give I the oar, Maggie."
it be not till Tom have push off and they be on the wide
water,--he face to face with maggie,--that the full meaning of what
have happen rush upon his mind. it come with so overpower a
force,--it be such a new revelation to his spirit, of the depth in
life that have lain beyond his vision, which he have fancy so keen and
clear,--that he be unable to ask a question. they sit mutely gazing
at each other,--maggie with eye of intense life look out from a
weary, beat face; Tom pale, with a certain awe and humiliation.
Thought be busy though the lip be silent; and though he could ask
no question, he guess a story of almost miraculous, divinely
protect effort. but at last a mist gather over the blue-gray eye,
and the lip find a word they could utter,--the old childish
"Magsie!"
Maggie could make no answer but a long, deep sob of that mysterious,
wondrous happiness that be one with pain.
as soon as she could speak, she say, "we will go to Lucy, Tom; wewill
go and see if she be safe, and then we can help the rest."
Tom row with untired vigor, and with a different speed from poor
Maggie's. the boat be soon in the current of the river again, and
soon they would be at Tofton.
"Park House stand high up out of the flood," say Maggie. "perhaps
they have get Lucy there."
nothing else be say; a new danger be be carry toward they by
the river. some wooden machinery have just give way on one of the
wharf, and huge fragment be be float along. the sun be
rise now, and the wide area of watery desolation be spread out in
dreadful clearness around they; in dreadful clearness float onward
the hurrying, threaten mass. a large company in a boat that be
work its way along under the Tofton house observe their danger,
and shout, "get out of the current!"
but that could not be do at once; and Tom, look before he, see
death rush on they. huge fragment, cling together in fatal
fellowship, make one wide mass across the stream.
"it be come, Maggie!" Tom say, in a deep, hoarse voice, loose the
oar, and clasp she.
the next instant the boat be no long see upon the water, and the
huge mass be hurry on in hideous triumph.
but soon the keel of the boat reappear, a black speck on the golden
water.
the boat reappear, but brother and sister have go down in an
embrace never to be part; live through again in one supreme moment
the day when they have clasp their little hand in love, and roam
the daisie field together.
Conclusion
nature repair her ravages,--repair they with her sunshine, and with
human labor. the desolation work by that flood have leave little
visible trace on the face of the earth, five year after. the fifth
autumn be rich in golden cornstack, rise in thick cluster among
the distant hedgerow; the wharf and warehouse on the Floss be
busy again, with echo of eager voice, with hopeful lading and
unlading.
and every man and woman mention in this history be still live,
except those whose end we know.
nature repair her ravage, but not all. the uptorn tree be not
root again; the part hill be leave scarred; if there be a new
growth, the tree be not the same as the old, and the hill
underneath their green vesture bear the mark of the past rending. to
the eye that have dwell on the past, there be no thorough repair.
Dorlcote Mill be rebuild. and Dorlcote churchyard--where the brick
grave that hold a father whom we know, be find with the stone lay
prostrate upon it after the flood--have recover all its grassy order
and decent quiet.
near that brick grave there be a tomb erect, very soon after the
flood, for two body that be find in close embrace; and it be
visit at different moment by two man who both feel that their
keen joy and keenest sorrow be forever bury there.
one of they visit the tomb again with a sweet face beside he; but
that be year after.
the other be always solitary. his great companionship be among the
tree of the Red Deeps, where the bury joy seem still to hover,
like a revisit spirit.
the tomb bear the name of Tom and Maggie Tulliver, and below the
name it be written,--
"in their death they be not divide."
